- [ARCHIVED] (monetization, S) Best hotels in Tel Aviv guide (/best-hotels-tel-aviv) — touristisrael.com has BOTH "Best Area to Stay in Tel Aviv" AND a separate "Best Hotels in Tel Aviv 2026" page; israel-taxi.com, thebrokebackpacker.com, misstourist.com, travellingking.com all rank for "best hotels Tel Aviv" with specific property names. Our tel-aviv-neighborhoods-guide.md (SHIPPED) covers neighborhood character and atmosphere — NOT specific hotel recommendations with Booking.com affiliate CTAs. Companion monetization page: hotel picks at 3 tiers organized by area. Budget (₪300-550/night; Old North guesthouses, Florentin boutique hostels + budget hotels like Alray Boutique, generators near beach); Mid-range (₪550-1100/night; Montefiore Hotel, Brown TLV Urban Hotel, Rothschild 22 Boutique); Luxury (₪1200+/night; The Norman (Bauhaus landmark, Nahalat Shiva; visiting diplomats + celebs), The Setai (Ottoman fortress above Jaffa Port; infinity pool; historic setting), David Kempinski (beachfront; largest luxury hotel on the Tayelet), Diaghilev LIVE ART hotel (Rothschild Blvd), Renoma (Trumpledor Beach boutique)). Selection guide: for beach: Setai/Renoma; for culture + White City: The Norman; for nightlife proximity: Brown TLV/Florentin options; for families: Sheraton/Dan/Leonardo beachfront; for business: Crowne Plaza TLV. HONESTY: rates change daily — link Booking.com; no fabricated review scores; "Tel Aviv's most polished luxury" language only from cited reviews. Monetization: Booking.com hotel CTA at each tier; GYG "TLV walking tour" secondary CTA. Dense cross-links: tel-aviv-neighborhoods-guide, tel-aviv-food-guide, tel-aviv-things-to-do, israel-accommodation-guide. SEO: "best hotels Tel Aviv 2026", "where to stay Tel Aviv", "Tel Aviv luxury hotels guide". Sources: touristisrael.com/best-hotels-in-tel-aviv, israel-taxi.com/best-hotels-tel-aviv, secrettelaviv.com/best-hotels, misstourist.com/where-to-stay-tel-aviv. [iter240 research] status: ready

- [P3] (seo-content+monetization, S) Tel Aviv Pride annual guide (/tel-aviv-pride-guide) — DISTINCT from lgbtq-travel-israel.md (year-round general guide). touristisrael.com has 2 dedicated annual Pride pages (parade + events week); misterbandb.com, eurogaytravel.com, gayborhood.com, gaytravel4u.com, rainbowindex.com all rank for "Tel Aviv Pride" year-round. Largest Pride event in the Middle East (250,000+ attendees); parade = Friday of the 2nd full week of June each year (2026: June 12; 2027: ~June 11). Content: dates + route (Gan Meir Park → beachfront; after-party Ganei Yehoshua Park = free, family-friendly afternoon wave); accommodation (book 3–6 months ahead; prices 2–3× usual Pride week; Dizengoff/Hilton Beach area = optimal; Old North + Florentin also well-positioned); beach scene (Hilton Beach = designated gay beach; Metzitzim Beach mixed but welcoming; arrive 9am for good spot); circuit parties (sell out weeks ahead; buy before flying; Playground, Pappas Fritas, Culture Club rotate year to year — always link current lineup); practical tips (June in TLV = 30–32°C on exposed parade route; bring reusable water bottle + SPF 50 + hat; start at Gan Meir end to follow parade to sea; Pride week events extend Thu–Sun with bar specials and film screenings). Year-round evergreen angle: Tel Aviv as LGBTQ+ destination 52 weeks/year (dense cross-link to lgbtq-travel-israel). HONESTY: party lineup changes year to year — always link official Tel Aviv Gay Center / Pride org for current program; hotel prices are illustrative (2–3× multiplier is typical but varies); Hilton Beach designation for LGBTQ+ community is social/cultural, not officially designated. Monetization: Booking.com Pride-week hotel CTA + GYG "Tel Aviv LGBTQ Tour" CTA. Cross-links: lgbtq-travel-israel, tel-aviv-nightlife, jaffa-travel-guide (when built). SEO: "Tel Aviv Pride guide 2027", "Tel Aviv Gay Pride dates parade route", "Tel Aviv Pride festival travel tips accommodation". Sources: touristisrael.com/tel-aviv-gay-pride-parade, misterbandb.com/gay-events/israel/tel-aviv-yafo/gay-pride, gayborhood.com/events/tel-aviv-pride, eurogaytravel.com/en/trending-stories/tel-aviv-gay-guide. Kibbutz Kfar Aza = restricted military zone as of research — access not public, must verify current status before publication. CRITICAL: This content requires HUMAN EDITORIAL REVIEW before autonomous implementation: (a) victim memorial content demands sensitive, accurate, carefully framed copy — not suitable for autonomous authoring; (b) some sites may still have military-zone access restrictions; (c) security context for Gaza border area requires current assessment and evergreen framing; (d) political/historical framing complexity exceeds autonomous editorial judgment. - [SHIPPED iter446] Muslim-friendly Israel travel guide (/muslim-travel-israel) → 1e412eb. Previously: [P2] (seo-content+monetization, M) Bein Harim has dedicated "Israel for Muslim travelers" page; we have ZERO dedicated content (only brief fragments in holy-sites-dress-code + first-time-in-israel); large global Muslim tourism market (OIC countries 57 member states). Content: Al-Aqsa Mosque + Dome of the Rock context (Temple Mount / Haram al-Sharif = Islam's third holiest site; Muslim-only access to interiors; non-Muslims may visit plaza during restricted weekday hours via Mughrabi Gate only — coordinate in advance with Islamic Waqf; hours change frequently); key Islamic sites beyond Temple Mount (Al-Jazzar Mosque in Akko — Ottoman era, open to visitors outside prayer; Hassan Bek Mosque in Jaffa (Tel Aviv); White Mosque in Nazareth; Cave of Machpelah/Ibrahim Mosque in Hebron — NOTE: Hebron is explicitly out-of-scope per PROJECT.md; do NOT include); halal food in Israel (widely available in East Jerusalem Muslim Quarter, Akko, Nazareth, Jaffa — Arab-Israeli mixed cuisine is predominantly halal; Nazareth has a vibrant Arab restaurant scene); Ramadan practical tips (most tourist sites open normal hours; Muslim Quarter restaurants have iftar hours; many Muslim-owned businesses close during prayer times — plan itinerary accordingly; hotel accommodation in East Jerusalem preferred for proximity to Al-Aqsa); prayer logistics (qibla direction finding; many East Jerusalem hotels provide prayer mats; apps like Muslim Pro or Al-Aqsa app for prayer times); Muslim pilgrim vs tourist experience (distinct visitor patterns — pilgrims focus on Al-Aqsa + prophetic footsteps; tourists may also explore Crusader history in Akko, mixed culture of Jaffa, northern Galilee). HONESTY: Al-Aqsa/Temple Mount access policy is managed by Jordanian-controlled Islamic Waqf and Israeli security, changes frequently and sometimes without notice — never guarantee access; never describe Dome of the Rock interior (not open to non-Muslims; do not describe what you cannot verify); Hebron explicitly excluded from scope; "halal" claim requires verification — frame as "Arab-owned restaurants are typically halal; look for halal certification or ask staff"; Ramadan dates shift 11 days earlier each year — link to Islamic calendar, don't hardcode. Content: what is a nahal water hike + safety intro (best season spring/autumn — after winter rains, before summer heat; avoid entering ANY wadi during or after upstream rainfall — flash flood risk is fatal and the sky above can be blue while rain falls kilometres uphill); top 6 sites: (1) Nahal HaKibbutzim / Spring Valley Park near Beit She'an — most accessible for families; water can reach chest depth; natural water slides + swimming holes; (2) Nahal Arugot at Ein Gedi — adjacent to the main Ein Gedi Nature Reserve trail; multiple waterfalls + deep pools; 6.6km round; wet and dry route options; enters existing Dead Sea day trip; (3) Ein Gedi Wadi David — the most popular; David's Waterfall pools; 1-hr loop; families + beginners; (4) Nahal Kziv in northern Galilee — forested canyon; enchanting pools; flowing Oct–May; best in spring; (5) Wadi Qelt near Jerusalem (Ein Prat Nature Reserve) — desert gorge from Judean Hills to Jericho; ancient Byzantine aqueducts + cliff monastery (St George's); more scenic walk than swim (water levels variable); great for photographers; (6) Banias waterfalls (Golan Heights) — Israel's tallest waterfall (10m); suspended wooden walkway; Banias Spring (spring of ancient Caesarea Philippi); NOT a swim site but dramatic water feature. Practical gear: water shoes essential; change of clothes; waterproof bag; National Parks pass valid for Nahal Arugot + Ein Gedi; entrance fees ~₪28–30 adult where applicable. HONESTY: water levels are rainfall-dependent — "typically flowing Oct–May; dry in summer in many sites — verify before visiting"; flash flood risk must be prominently stated; not all sites accessible by public transport (car needed for Nahal HaKibbutzim, Wadi Qelt). Distinct angle: pure HOW-TO for first-time Dead Sea visitors. Content: floating science (34.2% dissolved salts = 10× Mediterranean; buoyancy is automatic — no swimming skill required; floating technique: walk in to knee depth, squat, lean back — DO NOT try to swim forward); safety (15–20 min limit per dermatologist recommendation; eye contact = severe stinging — keep head back; rinse immediately with fresh water if eyes get wet; cuts/shaving same day = burns; remove jewelry beforehand = salt tarnish); beaches (Kalia Beach: northernmost, public beach, ₪75 entry includes changing/showers/chairs/fresh water; Ein Gedi Beach: free public beach, basic facilities, no shade; Ein Bokek: resort strip, hotel beaches mostly for guests, some public access; Dead Sea Spa resort vs public beach comparison); mud ritual (black Dead Sea mineral mud — slather on, wait 15 min, rinse; available at beach vendors or naturally in shallow areas at Ein Gedi); what to bring (old swimsuit — salt will bleach/damage it; water shoes for sharp salt crystals on shoreline; 2 bottles fresh water to rinse; change of clothes; sunscreen SPF 50+ — water reflects UV; no floaties needed); photography (sunrise = golden light on flat black water — book first check-in night near Ein Bokek or Kalia; reflection photography tips); getting there (Jerusalem → Dead Sea: 90min via Route 1 east + Route 90 south; Tel Aviv → Kalia: ~80min; no direct bus to specific beaches — shared taxi/organized tour recommended or rental car); overnight vs day-trip (Ein Bokek = resort strip with 10+ hotels; Kalia = no accommodation; overnight = sunset/sunrise + spa hotel = premium experience); combining with Masada (cross-link masada-dead-sea-day-trip.md). HONESTY: Dead Sea is shrinking ~1m/year (documented scientific fact, publicly reported); frame as "unique natural phenomenon with environmental context" + link Dead Sea Commission; brine pools/salt crystal formations = beautiful but sharp-edged underfoot; 15-20 min limit is health recommendation, not law; "minerals cure disease" claims are not medically verified — frame as "widely claimed therapeutic benefits" not guaranteed. Monetization: Booking.com Dead Sea hotel filter CTA + GYG/Viator Dead Sea day tour from TLV/Jerusalem. City of Kabbalah context: Safed was the global centre of Jewish mysticism in the 16th century; Rabbi Isaac Luria (the Ha'Ari) and Rabbi Joseph Karo (author of Shulchan Aruch) lived and taught here; 900m elevation — highest city in Israel + Galilee. Content: Ha'Ari Sephardic Synagogue (functioning synagogue built near where Luria taught; guided visits; modest dress + women's section separate); Caro Synagogue (Rabbi Joseph Karo's beit midrash; Renaissance-era wooden Ark; active for prayer); Abuhav Synagogue (beautiful 15th-century Kabbalistic design; named for Rabbi Isaac Abuhav; functioning synagogue); Artists' Quarter (stone-alley galleries, ceramic art, candle-making studios, outdoor sculpture; open-air Safed art market); Tzfat Visitors Center + underground 16th-century excavations + rooftop views; Old Cemetery (kabbalist sages' tombs — Ha'Ari's grave + Beit Yosef grave; active pilgrimage site); Sukkot in Safed festival (annual, major cultural event, multi-day, Sukkot holiday); Shabbat atmosphere (Safed is heavily observant; Kabbalat Shabbat singing at the synagogues; Friday afternoon city closes early); Safed candle-making (distinctive braided Havdalah candles, local craft); where to eat (small city — hummus + Middle Eastern; kosher options only; Shabbat closures apply); getting there (bus from Haifa ~75min; from Tiberias ~45min; no train; day trip or overnight at a B&B in the old city); half-day vs full-day planning. HONESTY: synagogues are living houses of worship — do NOT visit during prayer services without permission; modest dress (covered shoulders/knees for men and women); photography inside synagogues requires checking specific rules with staff; cobblestones + steep alleys make Safed challenging for wheelchair users; Safed is observant — most businesses + restaurants close Shabbat (Friday sunset–Saturday night). Dense cross-links: galilee region, jewish-heritage-israel, bar-bat-mitzvah-israel (links back), day-trips-from-tel-aviv (Safed is viable but long day), hiking-in-israel (Galilee section). Creating /tel-aviv-white-city-bauhaus would be duplicate content. CLOSED. — UNESCO World Heritage Site proclaimed 2003 ("an outstanding example of new town planning and architecture in the early 20th century"); Tourist Israel ("The White City - Tel Aviv"), Bein Harim ("UNESCO Site: The White City of Tel-Aviv"), Atlas Obscura ("Guide to the Bauhaus Architecture of the 'White City' of Tel Aviv"), ArchDaily ("6 Bauhaus Buildings to See in the White City"), mywanderlust.pl ("Bauhaus in Tel Aviv — A Guide to the UNESCO listed White City"), UNESCO itself, Bauhaus Center (official site) all rank. We have zero architecture / UNESCO White City content. Unique angle: 4,000+ International Style buildings (commonly called Bauhaus; technically International Style with Bauhaus influence) = world's largest collection in any single city; built by German-Jewish architects who fled Nazi Germany to British Mandate Palestine from 1930s onward, bringing modernism with them. WHY it matters to tourists: historic context + visual beauty + self-guided walking = perfect free half-day for architecture/design travelers; SERP for "Tel Aviv Bauhaus" brings in design/architecture tourist segment not otherwise captured. Content: three key areas (Rothschild Boulevard and side streets — the most photogenic, tree-lined; Dizengoff Square and surrounding area — incl. Bauhaus Center + Frishman St; Bialik Street near Allenby — City Hall + Rubin Museum); must-see buildings (Engel House 1933 — Zeev Rechter's first "pilotis" building in Israel, rounded balconies, 84 Rothschild Blvd; Cinema House cultural center — 1939, former Esther Cinema; Beit Hadar building — white curves + porthole windows; Oneg Shabbat building); Bauhaus Center at 77 Dizengoff Street — mini-museum, design shop, books, posters; guided tours in English Fri + Sun at 10:00am starting from Bauhaus Center (free; 2hr; HONESTY: verify current schedule at bauhaus-center.com, format has changed); White City biennial events; self-guided walk (GPSMyCity has a Bauhaus TLV trail, or print the city's own map); bike-friendly route (connects naturally to cycling guide). Practical: self-guided walk 2–3 hours; best morning (shade limited on open boulevards; get there before 11am in summer); combine with Rothschild Boulevard café culture. HONESTY: the term "Bauhaus" is popular usage — technically "International Style" or "Modern Movement" is the correct architectural term per UNESCO and Bauhaus scholars; note this distinction briefly; avoid overstating "pure Bauhaus" when the style was adapted; guided tour schedule at Bauhaus Center changes seasonally — always link official site. Dense cross-links: tel-aviv region page, cycling guide (White City = perfect cycling neighborhood), craft beer guide (Schnitt Brewery in Bauhaus building), israel-with-kids (design exhibits at Bauhaus Center). Monetization: GYG "White City Bauhaus walking tour" CTA. SEO: "Tel Aviv Bauhaus guide", "White City Tel Aviv tour", "Tel Aviv UNESCO heritage", "Tel Aviv International Style buildings". Keeping any Israel entry record out of your passport can simplify later travel to certain countries"); a full standalone guide captures significant long-tail and is useful for multi-country Middle East travelers. Content: Ben Gurion Airport policy (no stamp by default since ~2013; paper entry slip issued instead — KEEP throughout trip, hotels + internal checkpoints may ask to see it; also keeps Israel entry record out of passport); land border exceptions (Jordan: Allenby Bridge / King Hussein Bridge crossing = Jordanian stamp typically placed by Jordan on Jordanian side noting the crossing point; Eilat / Wadi Araba crossing and Taba/Egypt crossings may similarly result in stamps from the neighbouring country side; travellers can REQUEST paper only at land crossings — usually honoured but not guaranteed); what to do if you receive a stamp (separate paper insert, not a direct-in-passport stamp — removable; keep it for the trip then remove after departure); countries where Israel evidence causes problems (policy changes constantly — HONESTY: NEVER list specific countries as absolutes; link reader's home-country government travel advisory + FCDO/State Dept; frame as "a group of countries, primarily in the Arab League and parts of Southeast Asia, may restrict entry where evidence of Israel travel is present — always check current policy with your home government before travelling" — do NOT name specific countries as a guarantee of refusal); countries where no concern (Jordan, Egypt, UAE, Bahrain, Morocco, Oman — all have normalisation or existing peace treaties; Israel visits explicit in itineraries are routine); practical traveler tips (visit Israel last on a multi-country Middle East/North Africa trip; at land border ask for slip not stamp; be aware that Egyptian/Jordanian side may stamp independently; phones and search history may be checked at some borders — this is a documented reality, frame without sensationalism); digital footprint note (social media, apps on phone are occasionally inspected at some border crossings — brief mention without specific claims about countries). HONESTY: this topic evolves with geopolitics; never state a specific country "will" refuse or "will" allow; link readers to check their home government's current travel advisory; frame entire guide as "general guidance; always verify current policy from official sources". Dense cross-links: visa-information, border-crossings, petra-from-israel (many readers visit both), is-israel-safe. ZERO sustainability content on site. Growing 2026 travel trend: eco-conscious travelers seek environmental and community-based experiences. Content: Kibbutz Lotan (Southern Arava desert near Eilat; eco-cooperative founded 1980s by American olim; permaculture + organic farming + solar energy + natural building; meditation + mindfulness programs; short stays available alongside longer programs; birding + nature; composting + low-impact tourism); Succah in the Desert (central Negev highlands near Mitzpe Ramon; eco-accommodation; handcrafted bamboo/wood structures; minimum footprint; no electricity/running water in original structures; desert tranquility + stargazing); Ariel Sharon Park (TLV metro area: former Hiriya municipal landfill transformed into 2,000-acre metropolitan park; walking + cycling trails; guided tours of the reclamation project — ecological restoration storytelling; one of Israel's largest green infrastructure achievements); Clil eco-village (northern Israel, Galilee; intentional community; healing practitioners + basket-weavers + sustainable cooks; guest accommodation); Ein Gedi Kibbutz (Dead Sea region; organic botanical garden + date palm plantation; sustainable Dead Sea-adjacent accommodation; cross-link dead-sea-guide); water conservation context (Israel recycles ~90% of wastewater for agriculture — global leader; National Water Carrier; drip irrigation invented in Israel — Netafim); responsible wildlife (Coral Beach Eilat — do NOT touch coral, marine protected area; no starfish/shells removal; Agamon HaHula cranes — stay on observation platforms); Hula Valley birdwatching + KKL-JNF wetland restoration (drained 1950s → negative ecological consequences → partially re-flooded 1990s → restored ecosystem + world-class birding); low-impact hiking tips (marked trails only; Israel Hiking Trail association's leave-no-trace; dry-toilet facilities at select nature reserves); sustainable accommodation options (Green List at booking.com; Isrotel "Green Inn" certification; SPNI Israel's society for protection of nature). HONESTY: Israel's sustainability record is mixed — water recycling is genuinely world-class BUT the country has other environmental challenges (Dead Sea shrinkage, coastal development); frame factually, not as pure "green paradise"; Kibbutz Lotan volunteer programs: post-2023 significant changes — focus on hotel stays and day-visit eco-programs, NOT work-exchange volunteer programs; frame accordingly. Revenue: lower direct monetization (Booking.com eco-hotel filter CTA; Abraham Tours nature day-trip CTA); value is SEO trust + niche eco-traveler segment + inbound links from sustainability-focused travel communities. TLV is "Vegan Capital of the World" (~5% of Israelis vegan = world's highest per capita; 400+ vegan eateries). Middle Eastern staples (hummus, falafel, shakshuka) are naturally vegan; kashrut creates "accidentally vegan" meat restaurants (parve menu = no meat/dairy). Top TLV restaurants: Meshek Barzilay, Falafel Sumsum, Green Cat (vegan pizzeria), Opa (high-end vegan). Jerusalem vegan scene (Machane Yehuda market stalls). Israel beyond TLV (Haifa, Galilee brief mention). Shabbat closure planning (Fri-Sat most restaurants closed). Reading menus: parve = no meat/dairy; chalov nochri/halav yisrael distinction for vegans avoiding dairy traces. - [SHIPPED iter483] Rosh Hanikra sea caves guide (/rosh-hanikra-guide) → 17b876d — KKL-JNF (official kkl-jnf.org/rosh-hanikra), Tourist Israel (Rosh Hanikra page), Laidback Trip ("Rosh Hanikra: Complete Guide"), Bein Harim (day trip coverage), TripAdvisor (top Haifa day trip 2026) all rank. Cable car restored and reopened in 2026 after restoration. Our akko-acre-guide.md mentions Rosh Hanikra as a further day trip possibility; day-trips-from-haifa BACKLOG item mentions it; road trip BACKLOG item lists it; haifa-travel-guide BACKLOG mentions it. But NO standalone guide exists or is planned. Short topical page captures dedicated "Rosh Hanikra guide" queries; fits northern itinerary cap. Content: Rosh Hanikra = Israel's northernmost point on Mediterranean coast (Lebanese border 100m north); chalk cliff sea caves formed by wave erosion over millions of years; UNESCO nomination-worthy geological site. Practical: cable car (restored 2026; 90-second descent of 70m cliff to grotto level; kkl-jnf.org for current hours; closed Shabbat and Jewish holidays — plan accordingly); cave system (accessible grottoes via wooden boardwalks; dramatic blue/white water surges through arched caves; best effect in swell conditions); border observation point (Israel-Lebanon demarcation line visible; old Ottoman-era railway tunnel through cliff at border); former Mandatory Palestine railway connection (Haifa–Beirut rail passed through here pre-1948 — sealed tunnel visible). Getting there: 45 min by car from Haifa or Akko; bus from Nahariya (20 min) then taxi/sherut 5 km; car recommended; Rosh Hanikra is NOT accessible on Shabbat (KKL-JNF site closes). Combining: classic northern day: Akko AM (2h) + Rosh Hanikra PM (1.5h experience) + Nahariya beach. HONESTY: cave access is via the cable car ONLY (not hikable independently); cable car capacity = 40 persons, queues on weekends — early arrival strongly advised; "Lebanese border" framing — avoid security speculation, link is-israel-safe; hours/prices change — always link kkl-jnf.org. Dense cross-links: akko-acre-guide, haifa-travel-guide (when built), day-trips-from-haifa (when built), transportation, israel-national-parks-pass (KKL sites have separate admission — clarify INPA pass does NOT cover Rosh Hanikra). Monetization: GYG "Haifa + Rosh Hanikra" day tour CTA. SEO: "Rosh Hanikra sea caves guide 2026", "Rosh Hanikra cable car Israel", "visiting Rosh Hanikra tips", "Israel northern border sea caves". Sources: kkl-jnf.org, touristisrael.com/rosh-hanikra, laidbacktrip.com, beinharimtours.com. [iter85 research] status: ready

Museum travelers = distinct segment often overlooked in Israeli travel content. Content: Tel Aviv Museum of Art (Rothschild Blvd; Israel's largest art museum; 5 pavilions incl. Helena Rubinstein + Amir Building; strong Israeli modernism + Impressionist collection + contemporary; admission ₪60 adult; Tue eve extended hours); Eretz Israel Museum (Ramat Aviv campus; multi-building complex including ancient glass, ceramics, numismatics, folklore pavilions; open-air Philistine Well excavation; planetarium; TelToday — live archaeological dig you can watch); ANU Museum of the Jewish People (Clore Center, Tel Aviv University campus; world's largest Jewish genealogy database + diaspora communities + contemporary Israeli art; recently renovated; "Golden Path" central exhibition + genealogy research desk — pre-book); Ben-Gurion House (David Ben-Gurion's personal TLV home 1931–1968; free guided tours in English Tue/Thu 2pm + Sun 11am; 2,000-volume personal library preserved; intimate connection to Israel's founding narrative; one of few truly free TLV cultural experiences); Design Museum Holon (nearby, 20 min from TLV by bus/car; Ron Arad + Frank Gehry-inspired building; changing contemporary design exhibitions; widely regarded as Israel's most architecturally striking museum; check current exhibition before visiting — Holon.il); Independence Hall / Balfour House (Rothschild Blvd; 1948 Declaration of Independence site; free entry; 30-min guided presentation; powerfully moving for Jewish visitors; call ahead for English tour times). Planning tips: Tel Aviv Museum of Art + Ben-Gurion House = free-to-cheap half-day in Rothschild/TLV center; Eretz Israel Museum + ANU Museum = full Ramat Aviv day; Design Museum Holon = add to Jaffa day trip (same direction from center). HONESTY: museum hours change — always link official sites; ANU genealogy desk requires advance appointment; Ben-Gurion House English tour times change — call ahead; Design Museum exhibitions are temporary — check holon.il before visiting. - [P3] (seo-content, S) Jerusalem Festival of Light dedicated guide (/jerusalem-festival-of-light) — Tourist Israel ("Jerusalem Festival of Light: Mid-June 2026": touristisrael.com/jerusalem-festival-of-light/1054); visitjlm.com (official Jerusalem municipality tourism); Bein Harim; Time Out Israel; TripAdvisor (annual event reviews, 250k+ visitor event) all rank; our events-festivals.md has a single line about the festival in the June entry — NO dedicated guide; the festival draws 200,000–250,000 visitors annually (typically 10 nights in early-to-mid June); it's one of the most spectacular annual light-art events in the Middle East and a major SEO opportunity during planning season (April–May) and during the festival itself (June). Content: what it is (international light artists — from France, Germany, USA, UK, Israel — use projection mapping, LED sculpture, and light installations to illuminate the Old City's streets, alleys, churches, mosques, synagogues, and 2,000-year-old stone walls; free admission for the main walking route; one of the world's largest urban light festivals set in a UNESCO World Heritage Site); location and route (~3km walking route through Old City: Christian Quarter, Muslim Quarter, Jewish Quarter, Armenian Quarter, Tower of David + Jaffa Gate area; most iconic projections typically on Tower of David = largest lit canvas; route changed each year — check festivaloflight.co.il for current year map); free vs premium (main street route = FREE — no ticket required; Tower of David premium show = separate ticketed event; check festivaloflight.co.il for current premium offerings); timing (festival operates nightly ~9pm–midnight during the 10-night run; best visiting: weekday evening for lower crowds; first and last nights typically less crowded than weekends); practical tips (dress for Jerusalem evening temperatures — 18–22°C in June, layers needed for midnight; flat shoes for cobblestones; no strollers in narrow Old City alleys; no glass bottles; arrive by 8:30pm for a good starting position; Jaffa Gate area = main entry concentration — consider entering via Zion Gate or Damascus Gate for dispersed crowd entry); photography (long exposure mode; smartphone Night Mode handles the low light well; the illuminated stone walls + colored projections = some of the most Instagram-worthy images in Israel; tripod helpful for crisp shots); combining: Old City daytime sightseeing + Festival of Light evening = perfect full day; from Tel Aviv — day train + late night Egged bus back OR book Jerusalem accommodation for 2 festival nights (book 3–6 months ahead for festival period accommodation). HONESTY: festival program and specific artists/venues change annually — ALWAYS link official festivaloflight.co.il for current year schedule + map; free route is longstanding policy but verify; "typically early-to-mid June" for evergreen accuracy — exact 2027+ dates not known at time of writing; accommodation prices surge during festival period — book early. Dense cross-links: jerusalem region, christian-pilgrimage, first-time-in-israel, events-festivals, holy-sites-dress-code (Old City entry), israel-evening-activities (when built). Monetization: Abraham Tours / Bein Harim "Jerusalem evening + Festival of Light" guided tour CTA; Booking.com Jerusalem hotels CTA (festival surge). SEO: "Jerusalem Festival of Light 2026 guide", "Jerusalem Festival of Light guide", "Old City Jerusalem light festival", "things to do Jerusalem June", "festival of light Israel". Sources: touristisrael.com/jerusalem-festival-of-light/1054, visitjlm.com, beinharimtours.com Jerusalem events, timeout.com/israel Jerusalem. [iter100 research] status: ready
- [P3] (seo-content, S) Haifa food guide (/haifa-food-guide) — BackpackIsrael ("The Best Restaurants in Haifa: Wadi Nisnas and Beyond"), Fodor's (10 Best Restaurants in Haifa and Northern Coast), TripAdvisor ("10 Best Haifa Restaurants — Updated April 2026"), Evendo.com (10 best restaurants Wadi Nisnas 2026 + German Colony 2026), debbestfood.com ("Tastes, Smells and Sights of Wadi Nisnas"); we have tel-aviv-food-guide.md (pre-loop) and jerusalem-food-guide (P2, M, backlog) — but NO Haifa food guide; Haifa is Israel's 3rd city + cruise port + culinary crossroads of Jewish + Arab + Druze + German-Templar food traditions; distinct from haifa-travel-guide (P2, general destination guide — this is a food-focused companion); distinct from druze-villages-carmel (P2, the Carmel Druze village food experience — this covers Haifa city food). Content: Wadi Nisnas neighborhood (Arab-Christian neighborhood in lower Haifa; Falafel Ha'zkenim on Wadi Street — family-owned since 1950, one of Haifa's most beloved falafel shops; Em Sabri Bakery — fresh pita with jibni white soft cheese + mahamra red pepper paste; kanafeh + baklava from local confectioneries; the neighborhood market character = unlike any TLV/Jerusalem market equivalent; open Shabbat since Arab-Christian-owned); German Colony (Ben Gurion Boulevard terraced restaurants; Fattoush — sharing plates, hummus topped with mushrooms + garlic, cooked okra, spiced lamb — neighbourhood institution; Hanamal 24 — modern French-Israeli fusion, smoked aubergine + calamari + ground lamb truffles; Nabila — koba specialty; evening terraces along the tree-lined boulevard); Haifa Port / downtown (Mul HaYam seafood on harbor; port-adjacent restaurants for cruise passengers); distinctive Haifa food character (Haifa = Israel's "working city" = less tourist-packaged food scene than TLV/Jerusalem; Arab-Jewish-Druze food traditions create more genuine culinary diversity; Haifa hummus = warmer + more olive oil than Jerusalem style — food traveler's nuance worth noting); Carmelit + logistics (Wadi Nisnas and German Colony are both in lower Haifa — 15 min walk between them; Carmelit funicular connects lower city to upper Carmel for contrasting restaurant districts); practical tips (most Wadi Nisnas stalls open Sun–Fri 8am–dusk; German Colony restaurants peak Fri evening + Sat night; Wadi Nisnas open Shabbat = rare in Israel). HONESTY: restaurant scene changes — "verify current hours and status before visiting"; specific vendor names from 2026 research — qualify as "consistently cited" rather than guaranteed; no fabricated ratings or reviews; "best hummus" claims are contested — frame as "widely recommended" not absolute superlative. Dense cross-links: haifa-travel-guide (when built), akko-acre-guide (food context from adjacent city), cruise-shore-excursions (Haifa cruise port context), druze-villages-carmel (when built — complementary Carmel food experience). Monetization: GYG "Haifa food tour" CTA (verify available operator). - [ARCHIVED stale iter70/130 backlog duplicate of iter117] Israeli street food guide (/israeli-street-food) — touristisrael.com has dedicated "fast-food-street-food-in-israel" page; nextleveloftravel.com, tasteatlas.com, eater.com all rank for "Israeli street food" / "best street food Israel"; tel-aviv-food-guide.md + israeli-food-cuisine-guide.md exist but NEITHER is a street food–specific guide (Israeli street food = its own high-intent niche with dedicated audience, distinct from sit-down dining); gap confirmed. Content: format = vendor-focused street food guide with dish profiles. Core dishes: (1) Falafel — chickpea fritters fried fresh, pita wrap, toppings bar (Israeli salad + pickled cabbage + amba mango sauce + tahini); what makes Israeli falafel distinctive (herb blend, crispy shell); top vendors: HaKosem ("The Magician") on King George TLV, Falafel Gabai Jerusalem Machane Yehuda, any tiny counter with queue on Dizengoff; (2) Shawarma — slow-spit lamb/turkey/chicken shaved to order; distinguished from falafel by meat-based filling; Jerusalem-style vs TLV-style differences (Jerusalem = more spiced, TLV = aioli-forward); lafa flatbread vs pita choice; (3) Sabich — Iraqi-Jewish sandwich: fried eggplant + hard-boiled egg + amba + tahini + Israeli salad in pita; distinct from falafel (egg + eggplant base); named after the inventor Oved Sabich; best in Givataim + TLV; Sabich Frishman on Frishman St TLV = benchmark; (4) Bourekas — Sephardic-origin flaky pastry filled with cheese, potato, spinach, or mushroom; sold at bakeries + markets (Carmel Market + Mahane Yehuda); best eaten fresh from oven; sesame-seed crust signals quality; (5) Jerusalem bagel (ka'ak al-quds) — large oval sesame bread sold by street vendors in Old City Jerusalem; eat with za'atar + olive oil dip; iconic Old City morning snack; (6) Laffa — oversized Iraqi-Jewish flatbread; best eaten as wrap for shawarma; Mahane Yehuda + south TLV bakeries; (7) Knafeh — Arab sweet cheese pastry in sugar syrup; best in Muslim Quarter Jerusalem (Jaffar Sweets near Damascus Gate) + Akko; eaten hot at breakfast; brilliant contrast to savory food walk. Market contexts (Carmel Market TLV, Machane Yehuda Jerusalem, Akko market = three great street food hubs with distinct character and overlap-free dish profiles). Practical tips (most street food = cash-preferred; sabich vendors often have no English signage — point and gesture; best times = morning fresh falafel + market hours, not late evening when stalls wind down; falafel and sabich are typically parve = vegan-friendly except egg component in sabich; shwarma is meat — separate kashrut considerations). HONESTY: specific vendor names change (HaKosem TLV and Falafel Gabai are longstanding institutions but frame as "among the most consistently recommended" not "the absolute best"; no invented reviews or ratings; prices are typical street-food range ₪20–40/item in 2026 = illustrative not guaranteed). Dense cross-links: tel-aviv-food-guide, israeli-food-cuisine-guide, kosher-food-guide, carmel-market-guide (when built), vegan-vegetarian-guide (when built), first-time-in-israel. Monetization: GYG "Tel Aviv street food tour" + "Jerusalem food walk" CTAs (strong 5-star products on GetYourGuide). SEO: "Israeli street food guide", "best street food Israel", "falafel guide Israel", "sabich guide Tel Aviv", "Israel food tour guide". Sources: touristisrael.com/fast-food-street-food-in-israel, nextleveloftravel.com/street-food-israel, tasteatlas.com/israel-street-food, eater.com/israel-food-guide. HONESTY: prices are editorial ranges — always check restaurant directly; MICHELIN star was awarded as of 2025–2026 — verify current star status at guide.michelin.com; "La Liste 2026" rankings are that year's list — link La Liste for current year; don't fabricate "best restaurant in Israel" superlatives; Machneyuda advance booking = 4–8 weeks typical (link their booking page). HONESTY: reading room access requires registration/appointment — frame clearly; "5 million books" is the cited library figure from official NLI statistics; building ownership/opening date — frame as "opened 2023–2024 per NLI communications" with link; exhibition rotation means specific displayed items change — link NLI website for current exhibitions; not a children-focused attraction (frame appropriately). - [STALE — iter397 confirmed israel-wine-wineries.md already exists as comprehensive hub (DONE iter14)] (seo-content+monetization, M) Israel wine tourism hub (/israel-wine-regions) — WineTourism.com/wine-country/israel, ottoisrael.com, golanwinetour.com, goisrael.com/wine all rank for "Israel wine regions" and "wine tourism Israel"; we have golan-heights-wineries (BACKLOG, Golan-only) and judean-hills-wine-trail (BACKLOG, Judean Hills-only) but NO comprehensive all-regions hub. Israel has 5 distinct regions: Golan Heights (volcanic basalt 700–1,200m; Yarden, Chateau Golan, Assaf, Pelter), Galilee (Gamla, Galil Mountain, Dalton), Judean Hills (Clos de Gat, Flam, Castel, Tzora), Shomron/Samaria (Tishbi winery near Zichron Yaakov), Negev highlands (desert viticulture pioneer; Carmey Avdat — UNESCO Nabataean wine heritage). Content: regional comparison table (region, signature varietals, flagship winery, visitor-friendly?, season, nearest city); top wineries per region; 2-day Golan circuit + 1-day Judean Hills from Jerusalem + day-trip Haifa → Zichron Yaakov itinerary stubs. HONESTY: Israel wine internationally recognized but niche — frame as "growing recognition" with concrete examples; advance booking REQUIRED at boutique producers; no fabricated ratings; Golan status — state wines are Israeli-produced without political commentary. Monetization: GYG/Viator "Israel wine tour" CTA + Booking.com wine-country accommodation (Zichron Yaakov guesthouses, Golan kibbutz hotels). Dense cross-links: golan-heights-wineries (when built), car-rental-israel, israel-national-parks-pass, golan-druze-villages. SEO: "Israel wine regions guide", "wine tourism Israel", "Israeli wineries visitor guide". Sources: winetourism.com/wine-country/israel, ottoisrael.com/blog/israel-wine-regions. - [SHIPPED iter397 → f635bde] (seo-content+monetization, M) Northern Israel self-drive circuit guide (/northern-israel-road-trip) — Nomadic Matt, Tourist Israel, Wanderlog, travelingisrael.com rank for "Northern Israel road trip" and "Galilee self-drive itinerary"; /israel-road-trip (BACKLOG) covers the full national loop; this is DISTINCT: 3–4 day Galilee + Golan circuit for travelers skipping the long Negev-Eilat drive. Circuit: TLV → Caesarea (45 min) → Haifa/Akko (Bahá'í + Crusader walls, 1 day) → Rosh Hanikra (chalk grottoes, 30 min from Akko) → Sea of Galilee/Tiberias (boat + baptism sites, 1 day) → Nazareth (Church of Annunciation) → Golan Heights (Banias, Nimrod Fortress, Bental, winery, 1 day) → TLV. Driving logistics: ~500km total; Route 65 + Route 77 core; Waze essential; Shabbat-safe routing (fuel Friday PM); no West Bank roads required; car essential (no practical public transit for Golan attractions). HONESTY: Rosh Hanikra safe tourist-standard; do not enter security zone; link gov.il for current border framing. Monetization: discovercars.com + Booking.com (Akko boutique hotels, Tiberias lakefront, Golan kibbutz). Dense cross-links: car-rental-israel, israel-road-trip (national tour), caesarea-guide, akko-acre-guide, golan-druze-villages, hiking-in-israel. SEO: "Northern Israel road trip itinerary", "Galilee Golan self-drive route", "North Israel 3 days by car". Sources: nomadicmatt.com, touristisrael.com, wanderlog.com. - [SHIPPED iter476 → 671007c] (seo-content+monetization, M) Tel Aviv bachelorette / hen party guide (/tel-aviv-bachelorette) — DISTINCT from tel-aviv-nightlife.md (year-round bar/club guide) and lgbtq-travel-israel.md; targets the specific pre-wedding group-travel planning query ("bachelorette party Tel Aviv", "hen party Tel Aviv") which has HIGH commercial intent; competitors cover generic nightlife but NO Israel site has a dedicated bachelorette format. Tel Aviv is a recognized top bachelorette destination: LGBTQ+-friendly, Mediterranean beach setting, diverse dining, accessible from European hubs. Content: 3-night/4-day bachelorette weekend structure; accommodation (boutique hotels Florentin/Hayarkon beach strip vs. Airbnb apartment; areas: TLV Port/Hilton Beach area for beach proximity; Florentine for hipster vibe); Day 1 daytime (private beach setup with portable speaker + drinks cooler at Gordon/Banana Beach — no reservation needed but arrive 8am for umbrellas; alternatively: Tel Aviv White Night or Carmel Market food + wine walk); Day 1 evening (Florentin cocktail bar crawl: Teder.fm + Sputnik + Imperial Craft + Briciola — all walkable; do NOT name specific clubs as "still operating" without linking current listings); Day 2 daytime (spa option: luxury spa day at David Intercontinental or boutique TLV spa — book 2–4 weeks ahead for group rooms; alternatively: Israeli cooking class at TLV kitchen — handles groups 8–20, advance booking required); Day 2 evening (dinner at rooftop restaurant Jaffa strip → Haoman 17 or Block TLV for dancing — note: entry policies change, link current event listings via Spotimeapp or club Facebook); Day 3 options (Dead Sea day trip — 2h drive, Ein Bokek spa hotel day pass ₪80–150; Caesarea day trip — Roman ruins + aqueduct beach; Jaffa flea market + Old Jaffa afternoon); Group logistics: Gett minibus for group transport within city; sherut for Dead Sea; Avis/Sixt for day trip car rental. HONESTY: nightclub entry policies change frequently — NEVER promise "no queue" or specific party nights; spa group bookings require advance confirmation + deposit; "boutique Airbnb" quality varies significantly — use Airbnb Plus filter for vetted options; cooking class times limited (typically Fri morning or Sun morning — book 3+ weeks ahead for 8+ people); Israeli law: alcohol sales to under-18 prohibited. Monetization: GYG "Tel Aviv private tour" CTA (multiple hen-party-friendly tour operators listed) + boutique hotel booking.com CTA + cooking class operator direct link. Dense cross-links: tel-aviv-nightlife, tel-aviv-food-guide, israel-cost-budget, lgbtq-travel-israel, israel-wellness-spa (when built), eilat-tours-compared. SEO: "bachelorette party Tel Aviv Israel 2026", "hen party Tel Aviv", "girls trip Tel Aviv", "Tel Aviv party weekend guide". Sources: touristisrael.com, secrettelaviv.com, timeout.com/israel, spotimeapp.com event listings. [iter105 research] status: ready

- [P2] (seo-content, M) Muslim travel guide to Israel (/israel-muslim-travel-guide) — DISTINCT from kosher-food-guide.md (Jewish dietary laws); growing high-value international segment; muslimtravelgirl.com, halfawayaroundtheworld.com, thewanderlustwithin.com, touristisrael.com all publish Muslim travel content for Israel; NO equivalent page on site; Muslims from Malaysia, Indonesia, Turkey, USA, UK, Europe are a significant and growing visitor segment. Content: visiting Al-Aqsa Mosque + Dome of the Rock (Jerusalem Old City compound; Muslim Quarter entrance via Lion's Gate or Cotton Merchants' Gate; non-Muslims admitted Mon–Thu + Sat 7:30–11am + 1:30–2:30pm — NOT during prayer times; modest dress essential: women = hijab + long sleeves + long skirt; men = long trousers; free entry; no photography inside mosques; crowd management: Fri = Jumu'ah prayer, compound access for Muslim worshippers only; check Waqf schedule for closures — Ramadan, Eid Al-Fitr, Eid Al-Adha can restrict non-Muslim access; political sensitivity: refer to compound as "Al-Haram Al-Sharif" in Muslim context; PAIRED naming required when describing same site in English context per project rules); halal food logistics (Israel is NOT uniformly halal-certified; pork appears in many TLV restaurants; certified halal restaurants cluster in East Jerusalem Muslim Quarter, Arab neighborhoods Haifa/Nazareth/Jaffa/Akko, Israeli-Arab towns; Wolt + Google Maps "halal" filter increasingly reliable in Arabic neighborhoods; large hotel chains often have halal options on request — confirm ahead; HONESTY: halal certification not universal even in Arab restaurants — verify with restaurant; "kosher" ≠ "halal" though both prohibit pork and require animal slaughter method); prayer facilities (Al-Aqsa = primary; mosque in Jaffa, Akko, Nazareth, Haifa, Beer Sheva, Eilat all reachable; prayer times vary seasonally — download Muslim Pro app; direction of prayer toward Mecca = slightly NW in Israel); Ramadan travel (Israel's Muslim population observes Ramadan; Arab towns become festive after Iftar; restaurants in Arab neighborhoods close during daylight hours in Ramadan — plan accordingly; Jewish areas unaffected); ETA-IL and visa (nationals of most Muslim-majority countries require standard tourist visa, not ETA-IL — link visa-information page; travelers with Pakistani or Saudi passports face additional scrutiny — state factually, link official Israeli MFA information); border considerations (travelers who have previously visited certain countries — link visa-information for official notes; border security questions may be detailed — frame factually, see is-israel-safe); community (Muslim-majority Abraham Hostel Nazareth branch + local guides from Arab families). CRITICAL HONESTY: political context — phrase Al-Aqsa/Temple Mount/Haram Al-Sharif using paired naming + neutral framing; do NOT take political sides on sovereignty; security situation changes — always link home government current travel advisory + is-israel-safe; this is among the most politically sensitive pages on the site — extra review before shipping. Dense cross-links: holy-sites-dress-code-etiquette, is-israel-safe, visa-information, kosher-food-guide, jewish-heritage-israel, first-time-in-israel. Monetization: Abraham Tours private Jerusalem tour CTA + Bein Harim (Muslim quarter focus) + Booking.com halal-friendly hotel filter. SEO: "Muslim travel Israel guide 2026", "halal food Israel", "Al-Aqsa mosque visit guide tourist", "Muslim visitor Israel tips". Sources: muslimtravelgirl.com, halfawayaroundtheworld.com, touristisrael.com/muslim-travelers, thewanderlustwithin.com. [iter105 research] status: ready
- [P3] (seo-content, S) Israel drone photography regulations guide (/israel-drone-photography) — DISTINCT from photography-guide (best-spots editorial; already in backlog as P2 M); targets a specific technical regulatory query: photographers and travel content creators who research "can I fly a drone in Israel" before booking. Civil Aviation Authority of Israel (CAAI) has specific, strict rules; multiple travel forums (TripAdvisor, reddit r/travel, DJI forums) show tourists confused about Israeli drone rules; competitors have only brief 1-paragraph mentions (touristisrael, roughguides) — NO competitor has a dedicated regulatory guide. Content: CAAI permit requirements (drones >250g require CAAI permit for non-Israeli residents; apply via caai.gov.il before departure; processing can take weeks; permit is site-specific in some cases; recreational vs. commercial use have different permit types); no-fly zones (within 5km of military bases, airports, government facilities + Knesset + all settlements/IDF outposts; entire Old City Jerusalem = restricted airspace; Ben Gurion Airport + all Israeli airports obvious; Gaza border region; Golan Heights military areas; HONESTY: exact restricted zones change — always verify current map via caai.gov.il interactive restriction map); areas where flying is generally permitted with permit (Negev open desert, Ramon Crater overlooks, Sea of Galilee north shore, Arava Valley; verify permit before any flight); what to expect at security (Ben Gurion Airport: drone equipment is declared but not prohibited; batteries must be carry-on; DJI controllers with no illegal frequencies pass normally; inspection at airport possible); photography restrictions at holy sites (Western Wall — drones prohibited; Dome of the Rock compound — drone prohibited; Church of Holy Sepulchre — no drones; ground photography generally allowed in most tourist areas, but do NOT photograph military personnel, IDF vehicles, or security infrastructure — can result in detention); Wailing Wall Tunnel interior — no photography of specific areas. HONESTY: laws and restrictions change — caai.gov.il is the single authoritative source; never publish specific GPS coordinates of restricted zones (these change); "no-fly list" is a guide, not legal advice — link CAAI official resource prominently; operating an unregistered drone = criminal offence in Israel. Dense cross-links: photography-guide (when built), is-israel-safe, first-time-in-israel, israel-travel-apps (CAAI official app if available). SEO: "drone Israel guide 2026", "can you fly drone Israel", "Israel drone laws tourist", "DJI drone Israel rules". Sources: caai.gov.il, tripadvisor.com Israel drone forum threads, DJI community forum, roughguides.com. [iter105 research] status: ready

- [P2] (seo-content, M) Israel National Trail (Shvil Yisrael) section hiking guide (/israel-national-trail-hiking) — DISTINCT from hiking-in-israel.md (focuses on day hikes + adventure hiking overview) which mentions the INT as a 45–60-day thru-hike only; THIS targets the much larger audience of tourists who want to do 3–7 days of the INT as a section hike or bucket-list experience. National Geographic named INT one of world's greatest trails; TING manages trail-angel network. Content: what makes the INT unique (1,100km from Eilat to Tel Dan; entire length of the country; trail-angel network = Israeli families who open their homes to hikers at no charge — genuine cultural institution, uniquely Israeli hospitality); accessible section options for tourists (Eilat → Timna: 3 days, desert/copper mines, easy access; Carmel section: 4–5 days, forest + coast, accessible from Haifa; Galilee section: 5–7 days, Mount Meron + Nahal Amud; Jerusalem Hills section: 3–4 days, biblical landscape + settlement-free scenic zones); practical logistics per section (start/end transport, water sources, camping/hostel options, trail-angel registration via TING app); gear essentials (3L water capacity minimum; head-torch essential for dawn starts; the INTrail app for offline navigation); best season (November to April for most sections; desert sections October–March; Galilee best March–April for wildflowers); thru-hike culture notes (orange/white/blue trail markers; the TING stamp booklet; completing all = "Shvil Hiker" community). HONESTY: trail-angel system is voluntary — never guaranteed; some sections pass through or near sensitive military areas in Golan Heights — link TING current trail notes; trail conditions change after floods/fires — always check TING trail alerts; camping at unapproved sites is prohibited in INPA-managed sections. Dense cross-links: hiking-in-israel, national-parks-pass, negev-stargazing, mitzpe-ramon-guide (when built), rosh-hanikra-guide (northern terminus). Monetization: discovercars affiliate (many section hikers rent a car for logistics) + Booking.com for trailhead-area guesthouses. SEO: "Israel National Trail section hike", "Shvil Israel hiking guide", "INT hiking Israel tourist", "Israel national trail logistics". Sources: israeltrail.net (TING official), hikeisrael.com, outdooractive.com/Israel-National-Trail, supergan.co.il. [iter110 research] status: ready
- [P2] (seo-content+monetization, S) Jordan River kayaking & Galilee river adventure guide (/israel-kayaking) — DISTINCT from water-hiking-nahal-swimming (springs + wading ≠ paddling; different operators + skill set); and adventure-sports (no kayaking coverage). Key operators: Kfar Blum (Upper Jordan River, north Galilee; HaGoshrim kibbutz area; 3–8km routes; guided + self-guided Apr–Sep; prices ₪70–150/person; group packages); HaGoshrim Kayaks (adjacent kibbutz on Jordan River; gentler current suitable for families + young children). Content: what to expect (Jordan River here = clear Upper Jordan, NOT the polluted Lower Jordan section near Jericho; route flows through riparian nature with lush vegetation; 1.5–3h float depending on route; no white-water rafting — calm river, class I); practical logistics (drive from Tiberias 40 min, from TLV 2.5h; public transport = Egged to Kiryat Shmona then taxi; advance booking recommended Jul–Aug + Israeli holidays — sells out; bring old shoes + change of clothes; waterproof phone case essential); best season (April–September, peak July–August = very busy; May–June = optimal — less crowded, pleasantly warm water); age/size limits (typically 6+ years; life jackets provided; non-swimmers must use life jacket — state this); extension options (combine with Galilee region sightseeing: Banias waterfall, Nimrod Fortress, Sea of Galilee boat tour). HONESTY: Upper Jordan River ≠ "the biblical River Jordan baptism site" — link jordan-river-baptism-sites for clarification (baptism sites are southern Lower Jordan near Jericho, a completely different river stretch); prices seasonal — link operator directly. Monetization: GYG/Viator "Galilee kayaking" CTA + Booking.com Sea of Galilee accommodation. Dense cross-links: galilee, tiberias-guide (planned), water-hiking-nahal, day-trips-from-tel-aviv. SEO: "Jordan River kayaking Israel", "kayaking Kfar Blum guide", "Israel river adventure", "kayak Upper Jordan". Sources: kfarblum.co.il, igoogledisrael.com/where-to-go-kayaking-in-israel, galilandgolan.com/kfar-blum-kayaks. [iter110 research] status: SHIPPED iter457

- [P2] (seo-content+monetization, M) Judean Hills winery day-trip guide from Jerusalem (/judean-hills-wine-trail) — DISTINCT from israel-wine-wineries.md (covers all wine regions in overview; Judean Hills = one paragraph only); THIS targets "Jerusalem wine trail" + "Judean Hills winery day trip" queries for Jerusalem-based tourists, which are completely different from Golan/Galilee winery visits. 40+ boutique wineries within 30–50 min drive of Jerusalem (western hills direction). Content: why Judean Hills (Israel's growing fine-wine region since 1990s; Mediterranean climate + altitude 400–900m = natural temperature variation; complex limestone-clay soils; international recognition growing); top wineries with visit logistics (Tzora: biodynamic; award-winning; vineyard tours + tasting ₪60–120; book ahead; Shoresh area; Flam: family winery, 20+ years, Bordeaux-variety focus; tasting room open Fri-Sun by appt; Beit Shemesh area; Psagot: panoramic Judean Hills views, newer but praised; winery tour + tasting; Gush Etzion area — note location sensitivity: Gush Etzion is within West Bank; frame factually and link is-israel-safe; Nevo Winery: small boutique; appointment-only; Sphera: organic, natural wine approach); self-drive day-trip route from Jerusalem (rental car recommended; 30–45 min from city center to first winery; plan 2–3 wineries per day with 1h visit each; pair with Ein Kerem village coffee + lunch before wine circuit; option to add Beit Guvrin caves or Nahal Sorek nature walk). HONESTY: Gush Etzion wineries are within the West Bank — link is-israel-safe and note Israeli government considers area under Israeli jurisdiction while Palestinian Authority and international bodies dispute this; tourist access standard; frame factually, no editorializing; winery opening hours change — ALWAYS link winery website; tasting prices approximate; many wineries require advance appointments (especially small boutique ones — cannot "just show up"); Israeli wine is kosher in most cases — confirm with winery if important to visitor. Monetization: discovercars.com affiliate (self-drive essential) + ByFood / GYG private guided wine tour CTAs + Booking.com Jerusalem hotels. Dense cross-links: israel-wine-wineries, jerusalem-food-guide (planned), day-trips-from-jerusalem, car-rental-israel, driving-in-israel. - [P2] (seo-content+monetization, S) Sarona Market & Tel Aviv indoor food halls guide (/sarona-market-tel-aviv) — DISTINCT from tel-aviv-food-guide (restaurant recommendations overview), carmel-market-guide (outdoor produce + street market = different experience), and street-food-guide (vendor-hunting + falafel spots); Sarona = indoor curated culinary-and-design destination in a restored German Templar colony (WWII-era Bauhaus-influenced buildings, heritage designation). Content: what Sarona is (former Templar agricultural colony 1871–1948; preserved and converted 2014 into upscale complex; 90+ food vendors + restaurants + bars + design boutiques + cultural spaces); key vendors and zones (Taizu: celebrated modern Asian; Nini HaSasson: Israeli comfort food institution; gourmet beer hall; Israeli craft ice cream + chocolate vendors; artisan bakeries; wine bar selection; bookshops + design stores in courtyard buildings); practical visit guide (open Sun–Thu 10am–10pm, Fri 10am–3pm, Sat closed — ALWAYS verify hours since Shabbat closure pattern standard; entry free; parking in adjacent Azrieli lot or walking from Habima Square; Sarona vs Carmel Market: Sarona = all-weather indoor, structured, upscale; Carmel = outdoor, chaotic, budget; recommendation: visit both on same TLV day); the "rainy day" hook (best indoor food destination in TLV for inclement weather = practical advice many guides miss); heritage architecture angle (Templar-colony buildings have guided heritage walk available — GAN SARONA heritage park surrounds market). HONESTY: specific restaurant names change; focus on the market format/concept rather than specific operators that may close; Sarona hours and opening calendar = verify on saronacenter.co.il before visiting; pricing at Sarona is typically higher than Carmel Market or street food — set expectation. Monetization: GYG "TLV food hall + Sarona" tour CTA + Booking.com TLV accommodation. Dense cross-links: tel-aviv-food-guide, carmel-market-guide (planned), street-food-guide, rainy-day-activities, white-city-bauhaus (Templar architecture angle). - [SHIPPED iter253] (seo-content+monetization, S) Masada access guide: Snake Path vs Cable Car vs sunrise hike (/masada-visitor-guide) → e1d30be — DISTINCT from masada.md (attraction info page), masada-dead-sea-day-trip.md (day trip logistics from TLV/Jerusalem) and national-parks-pass-calculator; THIS targets "Masada snake path or cable car" / "Masada sunrise hike" — consistently high-volume query cluster that all major competitors own. Content: three access methods compared (Snake Path: 1.5–2km ascent, 300m elevation, 45 min up, departs east base at 5am for sunrise, moderate fitness, closed daily 10am–3pm Jul–Aug due to heat; Cable Car: runs from east visitor center, ₪92 round-trip, opens 8am, closes 1h before park closing, advance ticket purchase recommended May–Sep via masada.org.il, no physical exertion; Sunrise Hike: arrive Masada east car park 4am, park gates open 4am, hike Snake Path in darkness with torch, reach summit at dawn for one of Israel's most iconic spectacles — Masada fortress in first light over Dead Sea); who should choose what (families with children or limited mobility = cable car; moderate hikers = Snake Path; bucket-list dawn experience = sunrise hike); top sights once inside fortress (Northern Palace terrace = standout viewpoint + Herod's bath complex; snake path Byzantine gate entry; storage rooms with Roman siege wall visible below; synagogue = one of world's oldest; Byzantine church mosaic floor; lookout platform over Dead Sea and Jordan); Masada Sound+Light Show (seasonal Mar–Oct, Tue+Thu evenings ~9pm at west side of fortress; ticket ₪75–110; check masada.org.il for current schedule and exact dates — varies by season + demand); National Parks Pass compatibility (Masada = INPA site; Orange/national parks pass valid — cross-link to calculator tool); practical tips (bring 2L water minimum; cable car cannot be booked on-site on busy days — advance booking essential; sunrise hike in summer = arrive before heat; bring head-torch + charged phone; Israel National Parks app for offline map; wear sturdy shoes even for cable car since fortress surface is uneven stone). HONESTY: sunrise time varies by season — link our sunrise/golden-hour calculator (planned tool); cable car capacity is genuinely limited and can sell out May–Sep — frame as booking necessity not alarmist; Masada INPA closes Snake Path in extreme heat for safety — this changes; always verify current schedule at masada.org.il. Monetization: GYG "Masada sunrise tour" CTA (popular 5-star tour product) + national-parks-pass-calculator link + Booking.com Dead Sea hotel CTAs. Dense cross-links: national-parks-pass, dead-sea-practical (planned), masada-dead-sea-day-trip, jordan-river-baptism-sites, israel-sunrise-calculator (planned tool). SEO: "Masada snake path guide", "Masada cable car or hike", "Masada sunrise hike guide", "how to visit Masada Israel". Sources: touristisrael.com/masada, masada.org.il (official INPA site), beinharimtours.com/masada-sunrise. [iter110 research] status: ready
- [SHIPPED iter488 → e781683] Israel music & arts festivals experience guide (/israel-music-festivals) — DISTINCT from israel-events-festivals.md (calendar format, monthly listing with 1–2 lines per event); THIS is an experience guide: what each major festival is LIKE to attend, logistics, tickets, camping, what makes each unique. Content: 7 flagship events covered (Meteor Festival: electronic + ambient music, Negev desert / Ramon Crater setting = one of world's most atmospheric festival venues; September typically; camping on crater rim; tickets ₪450–900 3-day; international artists; link Meteor official site — never hardcode lineup; Tamar Festival: classical + world music at Dead Sea salt flats, outdoor stage, October; tickets ₪150–400; acoustics unique in desert silence; combine with Dead Sea float; Jerusalem International Film Festival: July, Cinematheque + Sacker Park open-air screen; international competition + Israeli premieres; free open-air screenings + ticketed screenings ₪30–80; Tel Aviv International Jazz Festival: May, multiple TLV clubs + auditoriums + outdoor stage; free street performances + ticketed venues; Jerusalem Festival of Light: June, free walking route through Old City alleyways with light-art installations; 200k+ visitors; best viewed 9pm–midnight; no ticket — free but crowded; arrive early evening; Eilat Chamber Music Festival: December, indoor venues + Red Sea waterfront stages; classical focus; Purim TLV street celebration: February/March, Dizengoff Square main stage, free, costume culture, day-party atmosphere unique globally). Practical cross-guide advice: how to buy tickets for each (festival-specific sites; no single Israel ticket hub); camping at desert festivals (Meteor: own tent required; Israeli desert night = cold even September — bring sleeping bag; no open fire); transportation to Negev festivals (shared shuttle buses from TLV, Jerusalem — check festival site for official shuttles; rental car = only independent option; no public transport to crater rim). HONESTY: festival dates shift 2–4 weeks annually depending on Jewish calendar interactions (Rosh HaShanah, Sukkot affect October events); ALWAYS link each festival's official site for current dates; ticket prices above are historical estimates from public sources — may change; Meteor camping capacity is limited — sold out 2024 + 2025; lineup announced usually 4–6 weeks before event — cannot name specific artists. - [ARCHIVED] (seo-content+monetization, S) Sea of Galilee boat tour & "Jesus Boat" sailing experience (/sea-of-galilee-boat-tour) — DISTINCT from galilee-tours-compared.md (compares multi-day tour operators; no dedicated boat/sailing section), tiberias-guide (city logistics ≠ on-water experience), and nazareth-sea-of-galilee-day-trip.md (day-trip logistics from TLV ≠ experience guide for on-water activities); multiple operators run traditional wooden-style boat tours from Tiberias and Kibbutz Ein Gev on the Sea of Galilee; the "Jesus Boat" replica vessel at Kibbutz Ginnosar museum (Yigal Alon Museum) is a separate museum artifact; competitor touristisrael.com has one paragraph on boat tours; GetYourGuide lists 12+ "Sea of Galilee boat" products; ZERO dedicated page on site. Content: two experience types covered (1. Traditional-style boat tour: Kinneret Sailing Company from Tiberias pier; ~1h Tiberias–Ein Gev crossing or circular lake tour; ₪50–90 per person; live performance of Hava Nagila on most tours; combine with Ein Gev kibbutz lunch/dinner; book at Tiberias waterfront pier or GYG; seasonal Apr–Oct peak; 2. "Jesus Boat" replica sailing at Ein Gev or Kibbutz Nof Ginosar: 40-min private or semi-private wooden sailboat; flag-raising ceremony to traveler's home nation; highly Instagrammable; advance booking required; ₪300–500 for semi-private group); Yigal Alon Museum context (the REAL ancient boat = 1st-century CE wooden vessel excavated from lake bed in 1986; on permanent display; museum entry ~₪25; separate from the boat tours); practical logistics (Tiberias pier 5 min walk from bus station; Egged buses 430/431 from TLV; combo: morning museum visit + afternoon boat tour + Ein Gev evening = full Galilee day); Christian pilgrim angle (Lake of Galilee where Gospels set Calling of the Disciples, Walking on Water, Storm Stilling; boat tour with devotional reading option = significant for Christian visitors — cross-link christian-pilgrimage). HONESTY: the replica wooden boats on tours are NOT the actual 1st-century vessel; make this distinction clear; boat tour operator pricing changes seasonally — link ginnosaur.co.il and eingevfarm.co.il for current rates; weather affects schedule — Galilean storms are real, tours can cancel; never claim a "guaranteed religious experience". Monetization: GYG/Viator "Sea of Galilee boat tour" CTA (5-star products available) + Booking.com Tiberias hotel CTAs. Dense cross-links: tiberias-guide (planned), galilee-tours-compared, christian-pilgrimage-holy-land, nazareth-sea-of-galilee-day-trip, nazareth-travel-guide. SEO: "Sea of Galilee boat tour guide", "Jesus boat Galilee", "Tiberias boat tour Israel", "Kinneret sailing experience". Sources: kinneret-sailing.co.il, touristisrael.com/sea-of-galilee, ginnosaur.co.il, getyourguide.com/sea-of-galilee-l109609. [iter120 research] status: ready

- [P2_ARCHIVED] (seo-content+monetization, M) Timna Park visitor guide — copper mines, desert art & stargazing (/timna-park-guide) — DISTINCT from eilat-diving-snorkeling.md (marine/Red Sea activities), negev (regional overview page), and national-parks-pass (calculator tool; brief Timna mention only); Timna National Park = Israel's most spectacular desert landscape after Mitzpe Ramon; 3,000-year-old copper mines, dramatic sandstone formations (Solomon's Pillars, Mushroom Rock), biblical smelting sites; 30km from Eilat; INPA national park; featured by National Geographic and Lonely Planet but NO dedicated guide on site; competitor touristisrael.com has 400-word Timna section; site has zero dedicated Timna content. Content: what makes Timna unique (ancient copper mining active since Chalcolithic/Egyptian periods; Egyptian temple of Hathor; King Solomon's Mines = biblical resonance + archaeological reality; dramatic Cambrian sandstone formations eroded to pillars and arches; accessible Israel's most geological diverse site); key features (Solomon's Pillars: 50m red sandstone columns, most photographed; Mushroom Rock: erosion-formed giant sandstone mushroom; Timna Lake: artificial freshwater lake in desert heart = kayaking + pedal boats; Egyptian Temple of Hathor reconstructed; Copper Mine Trail: 4km loop through ancient smelting sites + mine shafts; night desert bike trails Nov–Mar); practical logistics (30km north of Eilat on Route 90; own car or Egged bus from Eilat then 3km walk/taxi to gate; entrance ~₪40, INPA pass valid — cross-link national-parks-pass-calculator; open 8am–4pm winter, 8am–5pm summer; best arrived 8am before heat builds; avoid July–August 11am–3pm); stargazing angle (Timna = minimal light pollution, 30km from Eilat; INPA runs seasonal dark-sky events; Cooper Sky stargazing tours from Eilat run night-time Timna astronomy experiences Mar–Nov); camping within park (designated camping area; tents ₪100–150/night; advance booking via parks.org.il; Shabbat operations reduced). HONESTY: "King Solomon's Mines" name = historical/archaeological nickname; actual attribution to Solomon's era disputed by some archaeologists — frame as "Bronze Age mines" with historical context; Timna temperatures Jun–Aug = 40°C+ making midday exploration dangerous — frame heat warnings clearly; kayaking on Timna Lake is the artificial park lake, not a natural feature; prices above are estimates — link parks.org.il. Monetization: discovercars.com affiliate (car essential) + GYG "Eilat desert tour" CTA + national-parks-pass-calculator cross-link. Dense cross-links: eilat-diving-snorkeling, petra-from-eilat-vs-amman, national-parks-pass, best-time-to-visit-israel, negev. SEO: "Timna Park visitor guide Israel", "Solomon's Pillars Timna", "Timna National Park guide 2026", "Eilat desert day trip". Sources: touristisrael.com/timna, parks.org.il/timna, israeladventure.com/timna-park, roughguides.com/israel/timna. [iter120 research] status: ready

- [ARCHIVED] (seo-content+monetization, S) Beer Sheva visitor guide — Negev capital, Bedouin market & Abraham's city (/beer-sheva-guide) — DISTINCT from negev (regional overview), driving-in-israel (transit route), and any road-trip itinerary mentions; Beer Sheva = fourth-largest Israeli city; Israel's "Negev capital"; Bedouin Thursday Market = largest market in the Middle East; UNESCO-listed Ottoman Old City quarter; biblical Abraham's Well (Be'er Sheva = "Well of the Oath"); strong day-trip market from Tel Aviv (90 min by train); competitors touristisrael.com and thetravelbite.com have Beer Sheva content; ZERO dedicated page on site. Content: what makes Beer Sheva worth visiting (Beer Sheva = overlooked gateway to Negev; 203k population; modern tech hub — Ben-Gurion University anchors cyber-security cluster; Israeli Army Intelligence based here; old Ottoman quarter UNESCO-listed 2012 as part of "White City" heritage towns); key attractions (Bedouin Market: Thu 7am–2pm, largest Israeli market; rugs, spices, traditional Bedouin crafts + food; Negev Brigade Monument: iconic Moore-influenced 1968 sculpture; Abraham's Well Heritage Site: excavated biblical-era well complex, visitor center; UNESCO Old City quarter: Ottoman-era buildings, architecture walk; BGU campus: modern Negev desert campus architecture worth 30-min walk; Beersheba Observatory: views from atop old water tower; Hanegev Museum: desert archaeology + Bedouin culture); day-trip logistics from TLV (HaShalva/Merkavim trains from Tel Aviv University station, ~1h30; hourly service; Beer Sheva Central station = heart of old city district; Rami Levy or Shufersal for Bedouin market supplement groceries); practical tips (Bedouin Market = cash-only, arrive early, bargaining expected; Old City quarter = 10-min walk from station; Abraham's Well = small entrance fee ₪15–25; full visit = 5–6h from TLV). HONESTY: Bedouin Market status/location can change — always verify current schedule via Beer Sheva tourism authority or local Facebook groups before visit; Abraham's Well reconstruction is partial — not the original well; Beer Sheva Old City is undergoing gentrification — some blocks still rough, frame accurately; market authenticity is genuine but tourist crowds thin vs. Tel Aviv sites — set accurate expectations. Monetization: discovercars.com affiliate (Negev day-trip extension possible after Beer Sheva) + Booking.com Beer Sheva hotels for overnight. Dense cross-links: negev, israel-cost-budget, day-trips-from-tel-aviv, driving-in-israel, bedouin-culture (planned). SEO: "Beer Sheva travel guide", "Beersheba Israel visitor guide", "Bedouin market Beer Sheva", "Beer Sheva day trip Tel Aviv". Sources: touristisrael.com/beersheba, thetravelbite.com/beer-sheva, visitbeer-sheva.co.il, roughguides.com/beersheba. [iter120 research] status: ready
