- [P3] (seo-content+tools, S) Guided vs self-guided touring Israel: comparison & decision guide (/guided-vs-self-guided-israel) — a conversion/decision page targeting top-of-funnel "how should I tour Israel?" intent; NO competitor travel editorial site has a genuinely balanced comparison (Tourist Israel + Abraham Tours + Bein Harim naturally promote guided tours; lonely planet + rough guides list both but don't compare; nomadicmatt "Budget Israel Road Trip" is firmly self-guided); touristisrael.com, abrahamtours.com, seagulltours.com, and beinharimtours.com all have "why use a guide" pages but NO balanced editorial comparison exists anywhere. Distinct from: private-tours-israel.md (specifically private guided packages), best-tours-in-israel.md (ranked list of operators), car-rental-israel.md (one option only). This is the pre-decision page for visitors arriving at "how do I see Israel?" without a prior opinion. Content: WHY Israel is unusual (licensed Ministry of Tourism guides required at Western Wall Tunnels, many holy sites, and heritage sites — factor this into planning); guided pros (group dynamics at sites like Masada where guide access = sunrise slot management; language barrier at some Arab-market sites; private security logistics in sensitive areas; local insight on religious customs at multiple-faith sites); self-guided pros (Shabbat flexibility, no group pace, spontaneous falafel stops, cheaper for couples and solo travelers, rental car + INPA pass = greatest flexibility); hybrid model (base: rent a car and do regions independently; hire a licensed guide for ONE day in Jerusalem Old City or ONE day in Galilee — this maximises knowledge at the highest-value site while preserving independence); cost comparison table (private guide $150–300/day; shared tour $50–120/person; rental car $40–80/day + fuel; INPA pass ₪170 covers 35+ parks); matching by traveler type (first-timer → guided for Jerusalem + self-drive for Dead Sea/Negev; religious pilgrim → guided throughout; repeat visitor → fully self-guided; budget traveler → hostel group tours + self-drive); licensed guide booking (Ministry of Tourism licensed guide directory; qualifications required; IMPORTANT: anyone calling themselves a "guide" without the Tourism Ministry license is unlicensed — note distinction). HONESTY: private guide daily rate ranges are estimates — always quote live from operator; licensed vs unlicensed distinction is factually accurate under Israeli law; self-guided in Gaza border area or sensitive security zones requires current briefing — cross-link is-israel-safe; "Israel is generally very safe for self-guided travel" = accurate but context-dependent and should cross-link is-israel-safe rather than a blanket assertion. Monetization: discovercars.com CTA (self-guided) + GYG private licensed guide CTAs (guided). Dense cross-links: private-tours-israel, best-tours-in-israel, car-rental-israel, driving-in-israel, first-time-in-israel, is-israel-safe. SEO: "guided tour vs self-guided Israel", "do I need a guide in Israel", "private tour Israel vs independent travel", "Israel travel planning how to tour", "licensed guide Israel". ZERO equivalent editorial on site. Content: dates + atmosphere (entire Arab-Israeli community fasts sunrise→sunset; Arab-majority cities Jaffa, Nazareth, Umm al-Fahm, Acre transform after sunset into festive iftar street-food markets — Jaffa clock tower square is especially vibrant; Nazareth Ramadan night markets rival Sukkot for festive atmosphere; Acre/Akko old city comes alive after sunset); Al-Aqsa context (Ramadan = holiest month for Muslims; nightly Tarawih prayers at Al-Aqsa attract hundreds of thousands; Israeli authorities historically restrict non-Muslim entry to the Temple Mount/Haram al-Sharif complex during Ramadan prayer times — confirm via WAQF/Israeli police announcements before visit; Muslims from Israel + Palestinian Territories and international visitors all converge; women dress conservatively; photography inside mosques = sensitive — ask first); logistics for non-Muslim tourists (most tourist sites in Jewish areas function normally during daytime; some Arab-owned restaurants + shops in mixed cities close during the day or keep unusual hours; Ben Gurion Airport: unaffected; Jewish-sector restaurants/cafes: fully open; Arab-sector restaurants: typically open from sunset; Israeli hotels: unaffected; souk shopping in Arab markets: best after sunset / Ramadan evenings = lively); what NOT to miss (iftar feast shared in Arab home — cultural exchange programs like coexistence NGOs sometimes offer iftar invitations; Jaffa flea market evening atmosphere; Ramadan lanterns and decorations in Arab neighborhoods). HONESTY: access to Haram al-Sharif/Temple Mount for non-Muslims during Ramadan changes annually based on Israeli security decisions — NEVER assert access will be available; always cross-link is-israel-safe and direct readers to confirm current WAQF + Israeli police policy near their visit; Ramadan dates are lunar-calendar and shift yearly; framing must be respectful (this is a holy month, not a tourist attraction — context matters); avoid framing as "what's closed" (negative frame) — lead with what's special and open. Monetization: Booking.com Jaffa/Nazareth hotel CTAs; GYG "Jaffa by night" tour CTA. Cross-links: jaffa-travel-guide, kosher-food-guide (contrast with halal food), is-israel-safe, holy-sites-dress-code-etiquette, akko-acre-guide, nazareth-travel-guide. - [SHIPPED iter283 → 78c7ff7] (seo-content, M) Tel Aviv beaches complete guide (/tel-aviv-beach-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: 0 backlog mentions for dedicated Tel Aviv beach guide (existing BACKLOG iter5 item = "Israel beaches guide" covering the entire country; this is a dedicated TLV-only beach guide); timeoutisrael.com, secrettelaviv.com, touristisrael.com, theworldpursuit.com, and thebrokebackpacker.com all have dedicated Tel Aviv beach guides; high commercial value because beach-adjacent hotels (Isrotel Tower, Sheraton, Dan Tel Aviv, Norman) are premium bookings. Tel Aviv's 14km of beachfront = the most distinctive asset of the city for leisure travelers; beaches are all free public with dramatically different personalities. DISTINCT from general "Israel beaches" guide (that covers Eilat, Mediterranean coast, Sea of Galilee, etc.); this guide targets "Tel Aviv beach" + "best beach in Tel Aviv" keywords exclusively. Content: named beaches north→south (Gordon Beach — most central; city's social hub; beach volleyball courts; saltwater pool on the boardwalk for lap swimmers; good sunbed/umbrella rental; best for first-timer; Hilton Beach — THREE distinct zones: north = Independence Park = LGBTQ+ beach, one of the most famous gay beaches globally; middle = dog-friendly zone; south = surf zone with modest wave break; Frishman Beach — family-oriented; gently sloping sandy entry; closest to hotel strip; kids' play area; Banana Beach / Bograshov — young local party crowd; outdoor beach bar; volleyball; busiest on weekends; popular with surfers; Jerusalem Beach / Geula Beach — best surf break in TLV; multiple surf schools (TLV Surf Club); board and wetsuit rental ~₪80/hr; beginners welcome; Alma Beach — quietest; southernmost beach easily accessible; local neighbourhood feel; smaller vendors; Nordau Beach — gender-separated Orthodox beach; women's section one day, men's section the next according to a rotating schedule published by the municipality — always verify current schedule before visit); logistics and access (all beaches free; lifeguards May–Oct daily 7am–7pm; red-flag system (no swimming when red), yellow (swim with caution), white (safe); blue flag (EU eco-quality); disabled access: long continuous boardwalk wheelchair-accessible, beach wheelchairs available via Tel Aviv municipality for free — must pre-book via municipality app; lockers available at Gordon and Frishman for fee; showers at all main beaches; parking = extremely limited, arrive by public transport: bus routes 4/104 from city centre; light rail Green Line stops near Hilton/Gordon); seasonal notes (year-round mild; July–Aug = crowded + jellyfish season — Israeli Med coast gets jellyfish blooms Aug–Oct; check daily jellyfish alert app "Medusozoa" or ask lifeguards; water temperature 28–29°C Aug peak; May–June + Sep–Oct = ideal beach conditions). HONESTY: Nordau gender-separation schedule is published by Tel Aviv municipality — always link/cross-check as it changes seasonally; jellyfish alerts are real and important — include seasonal warning without overstating; LGBTQ+ designation at Hilton north end is de facto (not officially designated) — frame accurately; lifeguard hours are seasonal and may shift — direct to municipality site for current times. Monetization: Booking.com Tel Aviv beachfront hotel CTA (Sheraton, Dan, Isrotel all direct beach access); GetYourGuide "Tel Aviv bike rental" or "Tel Aviv beach party tour" CTAs. - [P2] (seo-content, S) Yom HaZikaron & Yom Ha'atzmaut experience guide (/yom-haatzmaut-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: 0 backlog mentions; israel-events-festivals.md (SHIPPED iter55) covers both holidays briefly in a calendar list; no dedicated standalone guide; touristisrael.com, itraveljerusalem.com, jnf.org, and israelforever.org all have dedicated Yom HaZikaron + Yom Ha'atzmaut content; genuinely unique experience unlike anything in other countries — the whiplash transition from solemn national mourning to joyful celebration within 25 hours is disorienting and profound for visitors. 2027 dates: Yom HaZikaron Apr 29 evening–Apr 30 daytime; Yom Ha'atzmaut Apr 30 evening–May 1 daytime. Content: Yom HaZikaron (Memorial Day for fallen IDF soldiers + terror victims — NOT "Veterans Day" in the American sense; more similar to UK Remembrance Day but MORE communal; entire country stops for 2 sirens: evening siren (1 min) at 8pm, and morning siren (2 min) at 11am; during the morning siren traffic stops on highways — drivers exit vehicles and stand — this is among the most striking sights in Israel for visitors; all entertainment venues / restaurants / cinemas closed the evening and full day of Yom HaZikaron; the mood is deeply subdued — public celebrations or loud activities are considered deeply disrespectful; military cemeteries hold formal ceremonies — Har Herzl in Jerusalem is the national ceremony; open to public; live broadcast; flag at half-mast throughout; television broadcasts consist of memorial programs and tributes); transition ceremony (the official state ceremony at Har Herzl moves from Yom HaZikaron memorial to Yom Ha'atzmaut celebration — the moment the flag is raised from half-mast to full and fireworks start is genuinely dramatic; usually ~9pm on the evening of Ha'atzmaut); Yom Ha'atzmaut (Independence Day — 78th in 2026; mass street parties from evening onward in all Israeli cities; Tel Aviv Yarkon Park free concert = biggest gathering; Jerusalem Safra Square municipal party; Ben Gurion Airport resumes normal operations after Yom HaZikaron closure to Ben Gurion Airport midnight check; barbecues (mangalim) in every park from midday — the national Yom Ha'atzmaut tradition; families and groups claim park spots from morning with charcoal; cities issue bbq permits and designated zones; Independence Day air show (Heyl Avir): jet formations over Tel Aviv seafront — visible from the beach, typically mid-afternoon; fireworks at major parks; flags everywhere — expect traffic and reduced parking). HONESTY: restaurants/bars in Jewish areas that close for Yom HaZikaron have genuinely no exceptions (this is a cultural/legal norm, not a business choice); Arab-Israeli restaurants may stay open (it is NOT their national holiday); visitors attempting to party on Yom HaZikaron evening will find themselves alone and creating social friction — be very clear; Ha'atzmaut BBQ = very smoky in parks, not ideal for respiratory sensitivities; political context — Yom Ha'atzmaut is also the day Palestinian communities commemorate Nakba Day — framing should acknowledge this RESPECTFULLY without false equivalence or erasure; sourcing for political context: Al-Monitor, Times of Israel, Haaretz provide balanced coverage. Cross-links: israel-events-festivals, jewish-heritage-israel, is-israel-safe, ben-gurion-airport-guide, traveling-israel-jewish-holidays. SEO: "Yom Ha'atzmaut guide tourist", "Israel Independence Day guide", "Yom HaZikaron experience tourist", "visiting Israel Yom Ha'atzmaut 2027", "Israel Memorial Day tourist". [iter170 research] status: ready
- [P3] (seo-content, S) Israeli supermarket & grocery shopping guide (/israel-supermarket-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: 0 backlog mentions; kosher-food-guide.md (SHIPPED) covers EATING kosher in restaurants; israeli-food-cuisine-guide.md (SHIPPED) covers food culture broadly; NO guide covers navigating Israeli supermarkets as a tourist (understanding labels, finding which chains exist, what to take home as food souvenirs). secrettelaviv.com, timeout.com/israel, seriouseats.com Israel features, Saveur magazine Israel guides, and multiple food-blogger posts on "Israeli supermarket tour" queries all rank. Medium SEO value but high engagement / social shareability (lists of "things to buy at Israeli supermarket" consistently go viral in travel communities). Distinct from both shipped food guides. Content: chain overview (Shufersal / שופרסל = largest national chain, blue branding, locations everywhere including airport; Rami Levy / רמי לוי = deep-discount chain, popular in settlements and lower-income areas, popular with budget travelers; Mega = mid-tier, good fresh produce sections; AM:PM = 24h convenience chain in TLV/Jerusalem city centres, open Shabbat = critical for travelers who need basics on Saturday; Tiv Ta'am = non-kosher chain, stocks pork products and non-kosher items = the go-to for travelers from pork-eating countries; Victory = supermarket chain in coastal cities; Yeinot Bitan = wine-focused supermarket chain); kosher certification labels explained (every product has a hechsher if kosher: Badatz = strictest (black diamond + Hebrew letters); Mehadrin = second tier strict; regular Rabbinate kashrut seal = standard; Chalav Yisrael on dairy = milk supervised from cow onward; meat products: look for glatt kosher label; products without any Hebrew certification seal = not certified kosher (check for imported items in some chains); practical for travelers: if you see a "Tiv Ta'am" you are in a non-kosher store — acts as a useful shortcut); what to buy / take home as food souvenirs (tahini brands: Har Bracha, Al Arz — the best; buy 2–3 jars; silan / date honey: pure Medjool date extract, syrup form, excellent sweetener + exotic souvenir; Bamba peanut snack: Osem brand corn puffs with peanut butter — iconic Israeli snack since 1964, loved by all ages; Halva: sesame-seed candy block, sold by weight at deli counters, many flavors; za'atar spice blend: dried wild thyme + sumac + sesame + olive oil — buy loose from spice stalls or pre-packed Pereg brand in supermarket; pomegranate products (pomegranate molasses, pomegranate seeds dried, pomegranate concentrate); Israeli chocolate: Elite brand (Elite bitter chocolate = underrated; Elite Pesek Zman bar = Israeli institution); Turkish coffee: Elite Elitte brand or Cafe Ahla — pre-ground for moka pot; instant coffee: Nes cafe Elite is a cultural artifact; Israeli wine: supermarkets stock Golan Heights Winery, Carmel, Yarden, and Barkan labels at much cheaper than export prices); Shabbat hours (most non-convenience supermarkets close Friday 3–4pm and reopen Sunday morning — critical planning: stock up Thursday evening; AM:PM and Tiv Ta'am and some Arab-area markets remain open Friday evening and Saturday); self-checkout systems (common in Shufersal — bring cash or card, no language barrier needed for self-checkout). HONESTY: chain locations, hours, and kosher certifications change — always link to the chain's own website; kashrut certification system is complex and the above is a simplified tourist-level overview — direct readers to the Rabbinate's official site for full information; pricing in supermarkets fluctuates; do NOT give specific prices for products. Cross-links: kosher-food-guide, israeli-food-cuisine-guide, israel-money-guide, shabbat-guide. SEO: "Israeli supermarket guide tourist", "what to buy Israeli grocery store", "kosher labels Israel explained", "Shufersal tourist guide", "Israeli food souvenirs to take home". [iter170 research] status: ready

- [P2] (seo-content, S) Israel water parks & summer family attractions guide (/israel-water-parks) — iGoogledIsrael.com ("Water parks in Israel: the ESSENTIAL guide"), TravelTriangle ("9 Most Thrilling Water Parks in Israel"), TripAdvisor (top 10 updated 2026) all rank; ZERO water park guide on site (israel-with-kids.md is a general family guide; zero overlap with water park-specific queries); Israeli families pack water parks July–August; many tourists traveling in summer want this. Main parks: (1) Shefayim Water Park (between TLV + Haifa on Route 2; Israel's largest, most accessible from Tel Aviv; iconic Israeli summer institution; wave pool + slides + lazy river; Kibbutz Shefayim accommodation option); (2) Luna Gal (Golan Heights, southeast Sea of Galilee shore at Ramot; Israel's most famous; best sea view setting; summer-only; very crowded Israeli holidays); (3) Aqua Kef (Tiberias/Ganim Beach; adjacent to Sea of Galilee; 3 challenge levels; accessible for families with young kids); (4) Mango Park (Hod HaSharon; smaller; family-oriented; suitable for young children); (5) Kibbutz pool access (dozens of kibbutzim open their pool to paying day visitors in summer; local secret; much less crowded; prices ₪30–60/person). Seasonal context: most parks open Apr/May–Sept; Israeli domestic families dominate July–August; arrive by 10am for parking; summer heat = essential during heatwave weeks. HONESTY: admission prices change annually — link each park's official website; no fabricated ratings; "crowded in August" is accurate editorial framing. Cross-links: israel-with-kids, galilee region, sea of galilee (tiberias), best-time-to-visit-israel. - [P2] (seo-content+monetization, S) Israel military heritage sites for tourists (/israel-military-heritage) — DISTINCT from jewish-heritage-israel.md (culture/religion focus), is-israel-safe.md (current security), and bar-bat-mitzvah-israel.md (lifecycle ceremony context). Military history tourism is a significant niche (IDF service = national conversation; Oct 7 memorial context makes IDF-history content highly relevant in 2026). Main sites: (1) Latrun Armored Corps Museum (between TLV + Jerusalem on Route 1; hundreds of tanks + armored vehicles from 1948–present including captured Egyptian/Syrian; Yitzhak Rabin's name on the wall; Holocaust survivor unit flag display; excellent English signage; free entry with parking; 2.5h; great stop en route Jerusalem); (2) Palmach Museum Tel Aviv (Ramat Aviv; cinematic multi-room experience following a fictional Palmach fighter; advance booking required; unmissable for Israel-history depth; 90min; admission ₪50-70); (3) IDF History Museum Tel Aviv (Bauhaus building; visual narrative of Israel's military history from pre-1948 through recent decades; free; 1.5h); (4) Ammunition Hill Jerusalem (1967 Six-Day War pivotal battle site near Hebrew University; original trench system preserved + on-site museum; 1.5h; emotionally intense; connects to Jewish Quarter/Western Wall); (5) Israel Air Force Museum, Hatzerim (near Beer Sheva; largest IAF aircraft collection; Phantoms, Skyhawks, captured MiGs; requires advance registration via iaf.org.il; 3h+; unique globally); (6) Kibbutz Yad Mordechai (near Ashkelon; 1948 battle memorial + statue of Warsaw Ghetto Uprising; Holocaust survivor fighter context; museum + outdoor battle reenactment display). HONESTY: Latrun museum open Sun–Thu (check hours); IAF Museum requires pre-registration + some days closed due to operational use; no fabricated entry prices — link each site; respectful framing — avoid sensationalism around Oct 7 content; this is HERITAGE not security tourism. Monetization: GYG Jerusalem + Ammunition Hill day tour CTA + discovercars.com CTA (Latrun en-route car rental). Cross-links: jewish-heritage-israel, first-time-in-israel, jerusalem, ben-gurion-airport-guide (Latrun near airport area), israel-national-parks-pass (some sites INPA adjacent). SEO: "Israel military museums tourists", "Latrun armored corps museum guide", "Ammunition Hill Jerusalem", "IDF museum Israel", "Palmach Museum Tel Aviv". Sources: touristisrael.com/latrun-armored-corps-museum, iaf.org.il/Galleries/Aviation-Museum, palmachmuseum.com, tripadvisor.com/Attraction_Review-g294071-d593836-Ammunition_Hill. [iter175 research] status: ready

- [SHIPPED iter458] Israel tourist visa extension guide (/israel-visa-extension) — visa-information.md covers ENTRY + ETA-IL; does NOT cover what happens when tourists want to STAY LONGER than their authorized 90-day period. High intent for gap-year students, families visiting relatives, remote workers on extended stays, born-Jewish visitors exploring Aliyah. Content: official extension process (apply at Population and Immigration Authority office — Misrad HaPnim; bring: passport + ETA-IL + evidence of funds + onward ticket; fee ₪200–300; typically grants 3-month extension; not guaranteed); who qualifies (tourists with legitimate purpose — studying Hebrew, religious study, visiting family — have highest approval; "tourist" extensions increasingly scrutinized); timing (apply before original authorized stay expires; allow 1-2 weeks before expiry); border run strategy (exit Israel and re-enter — technically resets 90-day clock; legal gray area — some travelers refused re-entry after multiple consecutive runs; NOT recommended as a routine strategy; frame factually with official guidance caveat); student/volunteer options (ulpan programs and recognized volunteer placements sometimes include visa status; MASA Israel program = government-affiliated pathway for longer stays up to 10 months); conversion/Aliyah research stays (process differs; contact Jewish Agency). HONESTY: visa extension is entirely at the discretion of the immigration officer — this guide cannot guarantee approval; "border run" as a strategy exists in legal gray area — disclose this; no overstay advice; link piba.gov.il as the only authoritative source. Cross-links: visa-information.md, first-time-in-israel.md, israel-after-birthright.md (post-birthright extended stays). SEO: "extend tourist visa Israel 2026", "Israel 90 day extension", "how to stay longer than 90 days Israel", "Israel visa extension process tourist". Sources: piba.gov.il, expat.com/en/guide/middle-east/israel/immigration, masaisrael.org, il.usembassy.gov/visas-immigration. [iter175 research] status: ready

- [P2] (seo-content+monetization, M) Golan Heights complete travel guide (/golan-heights-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: golan-druze-villages.md (SHIPPED) covers DRUZE VILLAGES ONLY (Majdal Shams, Masade, Buqata); NO comprehensive Golan Heights guide exists. The Golan is one of Israel's most diverse regions: alpine-style forests, volcanic basalt plateaux, wineries, national parks, ancient ruins, and geopolitical complexity. Main attractions: (1) Nimrod Fortress National Park (most spectacular Crusader/Mamluk castle in Israel; 13th-century fortifications + vaulted halls + towers + cisterns; panoramic Golan valley view; INPA pass valid; 2h); (2) Banias Nature Reserve (spring + waterfall + Roman-era Caesarea Philippi ruins; lush mossy canyon fed by Jordan River source; two trails: Banias Spring circuit 1h or full waterfall hike 2h; Israel's most dramatic waterfall 10m; INPA pass valid); (3) Yehudiya Nature Reserve (basalt gorge + natural swimming pools — Hexagon Pool (Breichat HaMeshushim); summer swim site; moderate hike; flash-flood risk Oct–Apr — seasonal closure); (4) Golan Heights Winery, Katzrin (Israel's most internationally recognized winery; Yarden + Gamla + Golan labels exported to 25+ countries; visitors' center + barrel tastings; book ahead for tours); (5) Gamla Nature Reserve (ancient Jewish fortress that fell to Rome 67CE; parallel "Jewish Masada" story; Israel's most important griffon vulture colony visible from observation platform; moderately challenging hike 2–3h); (6) Mount Hermon / Neve Ativ (Israel's only ski area in winter; hiking + cable car in summer; highest point in Israel 2,224m; military zone nearby — follow trail markers); (7) Katzrin (Golan's capital; ancient synagogue archaeological park; Golan Archaeological Museum; Dolmen field). Political context (FRAME FACTUALLY, NO ADVOCACY): captured from Syria in 1967 Six-Day War; administered by Israel since; Druze population has Syrian citizenship or Israeli residency; US recognized Israeli sovereignty 2019 — unique among Western nations; most of world still considers it occupied Syrian territory; ongoing civil war in Syria makes current situation complex; Syrian communities in north still face cross-border family separation; safety — no active conflict in Israeli-administered sector; standard FCDO/State Dept advisory "exercise normal caution." Competitor coverage: takeyourbackpack.com/backpacking-in-israel/visit-golan-heights, Frommers (Upper Galilee and Golan), TripAdvisor (THE 15 BEST Things to Do in Golan Heights 2026), Expedia (Golan Heights Travel Guide), private-tours-in-israel.com (Ultimate Guide), shalomisraeltours.com. HONESTY: Yehudiya swimming only safe in dry months (May–Oct); Nimrod Fortress / Gamla / Banias all INPA entry fees — link pass calculator; political context described factually, balanced; no advocacy; skiing at Hermon closed in off-season (May–Oct) — state seasonal clearly; Golan wine = genuinely internationally recognized (accurate editorial). Monetization: GYG "Golan Heights ATV + Wine Tour" CTA + Israel National Parks Pass CTA (3 major INPA sites). Dense cross-links: golan-druze-villages, galilee-tours-compared, tiberias-guide, water-hiking-israel, israel-national-parks-pass, israel-wine-wineries, day-trips-from-tel-aviv. - [SHIPPED iter226] 1-day Tel Aviv itinerary (/1-day-tel-aviv-itinerary) → 9b5fd01 the natural companion is a Tel Aviv equivalent. tel-aviv-neighborhoods-guide.md + jaffa-travel-guide.md + tel-aviv-white-city.md all exist but NOT a curated single-day flow guide. Tourist Israel, Nomadic Matt, The Culture Trip, Time Out, Travel + Leisure all rank for "one day in Tel Aviv 2026." High-intent query for cruise passengers, connecting travelers, and first-timers with limited time. Format: same structure as 1-day-jerusalem-itinerary.md (time-stamped, practical, affiliate-wired). Content: 7:30am Jaffa (walk the port at sunrise, Jaffa Clock Tower, HaPishpishim flea market from 9am on Fridays — skip if not Friday and do White City first); 9:30am–11:30am White City Bauhaus walk (Rothschild Blvd → Dizengoff Square Agam Fountain → Bialik Square → UNESCO Bauhaus Centre; tel-aviv-white-city.md cross-link); 11:30am–1pm Carmel Market lunch (hummus at a stall or sit-down; fresh fruit, cheeses, street food; tel-aviv-carmel-market.md cross-link); 1:30pm–4pm beach time (Gordon Beach or Hilton Beach — 1.5km promenade walk from Carmel; rent sun lounger; Mediterranean swim); 4pm–6pm Neve Tzedek (wander boutiques on Shabazi St, Suzanne Dellal dance centre courtyard, Nahum Gutman Museum); 7pm+ dinner and evening (Florentin neighbourhood bars + street art if nightlife; or Rothschild Blvd rooftop dining). Optional upgrades: add Old Jaffa walking tour (1.5h, GYG CTA); skip beach for Tel Aviv Museum of Art (Tue/Thu open to 9pm). HONESTY: Carmel Market + Flea Market closed Shabbat (Friday sunset–Saturday end); plan timing accordingly; summer heat = beaches best before 10am or after 4pm; no fabricated distances (measure from real map). - [P3] (seo-content, S) Israel markets & shuk hub guide (/israel-markets-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: tel-aviv-carmel-market.md (SHIPPED) covers Carmel Market specifically; israel-food-tours-cooking-classes.md references markets; NO hub guide covers Israel's diverse market scene. Competitor coverage: iGoogledIsrael, Timeout Israel, Secret Tel Aviv, Tripadvisor all feature comprehensive "best markets in Israel" or "best shuk" content. High engagement content (markets = authentic travel experience + social-shareability). Key markets: (1) Machane Yehuda, Jerusalem ("the Shuk", 250 stalls, fruit/veg/spice/meat/dairy vendors + artisan food, Thursday/Friday = busiest + freshest stock, Thursday evening = "Night Market" with wine bars + pop-up restaurants in the stalls, Friday closes ~3pm for Shabbat — tip: arrive Thursday 5pm for dual market + bar experience; tourist-accessible; free entry; adjacent: Mahane Yehuda neighbourhood with bakeries + coffee); (2) Levinsky Market, Tel Aviv (South TLV near Florentine; SPICE market speciality — distinct from Carmel Market's produce; oud + dried fruit + Persian-Jewish flavors; Azrieli of spice merchants; smaller scale, intense aromas; Cafe Levinsky 41 = coffee landmark; foodie pilgrimage); (3) Jaffa Flea Market / Shuk HaPishpishim (Old Jaffa, Fri–Sat busiest; antiques, vintage clothing, furniture, art, handmade jewelry; hip independent galleries interspersed; adjacent to Nahalat Binyamin craft fair Tue/Fri); (4) Haifa Wadi Nisnas market (Arab neighbourhood; vegetables, spices, street food — knafeh, sfeeha pastries; Museum of the Unknown Artist embedded in market street; Hanukkah lights installation in December — Wadi Nisnas hosts Arab Christmas celebrations + multicultural festival in December/January — "Holiday of Holidays"); (5) Nazareth Old City souk (covered market alley; spices, pottery, hand-embroidered Palestinian textiles, olive wood carvings; authentic Arab medina feel; less touristy than Jaffa; vendors negotiate). HONESTY: Machane Yehuda Night Market only Thu; do NOT present Fri as "Night Market night" (market closes midday Fri); Jaffa flea market has expanded artisan/café scene but still scattered vintage finds not curated antique market; all market hours are variable — link individual market websites; Haifa Wadi Nisnas "Holiday of Holidays" = December festival, not year-round. Cross-links: tel-aviv-carmel-market, jaffa-travel-guide, jerusalem-food-guide, tel-aviv-food-guide, haifa-travel-guide (when built), nazareth-food-guide (when built). SEO: "best markets in Israel 2026", "Machane Yehuda market guide", "Levinsky Market Tel Aviv", "Israel shuk guide", "Jaffa flea market guide". - [P3] (technical+seo, S) Israel power plug & electricity guide (/israel-power-plug) — From COMPETITORS.md iter170 (confirmed gap), never added to BACKLOG. Type H plug (unique 3-pin triangular layout used ONLY in Israel + Palestinian Territories — found NOWHERE else; physical description: 3 round pins arranged in a triangle, no grounding sleeve, looks like an upside-down "Y"); voltage 220V/50Hz (NOT compatible with 110V US appliances without a converter — note: modern electronics like phone chargers, laptops, USB-C chargers, cameras are all dual-voltage 100–240V and require only a plug adapter); European Type C plugs (standard 2-pin round) FIT Israeli Type H sockets backwards — they work! This means European travelers with standard Type C adapters don't need anything; UK travelers (Type G 3-pin rectangular) need a UK→Israel adapter or UK→EU C adapter + C adapts to H; US travelers (Type A/B) need a US→Israel adapter (₪20–40 at Ben Gurion Airport duty-free, supermarkets, or Amazon pre-trip); what to buy (recommended: a universal adapter covering Type H + has USB-A/C ports built in; link amazon.com via affiliate if configured; or buy at Ben Gurion Airport arrival hall shops). Practical tips: USB-C chargers = no problem worldwide (direct plug type doesn't matter if you carry a USB-C cable + any international port hub); only risk = older appliances with fixed plugs (US-style hairdryers, UK shavers) — either buy Israeli-compatible on arrival or use hotel hairdryer. Airport and hotels: Type H outlets throughout Israel's hotels; USB-A/USB-C ports now common in newer hotel rooms. HONESTY: claim that "European C plugs work in Israel" is TRUE but verify with current socket — some older Israeli installations use slightly tighter sockets; modern C plugs fit standard; do NOT claim compatibility as guaranteed for all sockets; always recommend a dedicated Type H adapter for certainty. Cross-links: ben-gurion-airport-guide, first-time-in-israel, israel-esim (tech travel gear context). SEO: "Israel plug type", "Israel power adapter", "electric plug Israel guide", "do I need an adapter for Israel", "Type H plug Israel". Sources: wikitravel.org/en/Israel, voltagedb.com/israel, tripadvisor.com/ShowTopic-g293977-Israel-Power-adaptor. [iter180 research] status: ready

- [P3] (seo-content, S) Nazareth food & Arab-Israeli cuisine guide (/nazareth-food-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: nazareth-travel-guide.md (SHIPPED) is a city overview with brief restaurant mentions; israeli-food-cuisine-guide.md (SHIPPED) covers Israeli food culture broadly; NO dedicated Nazareth food guide exists; jerusalem-food-guide.md + tel-aviv-food-guide.md both exist — a Nazareth equivalent completes the trifecta of Israeli culinary cities. Nazareth is uncontroversially the capital of Arab-Israeli cuisine: Middle Eastern Arab cooking at its most refined (not falafel clichés — sophisticated mezze, stuffed vine leaves, maqluba rice dishes, lamb preparations, knafeh). Competitor coverage: Timeout Israel, Haaretz travel, Aleph Israel Travel, Culture Trip, TripAdvisor "Best restaurants in Nazareth." Content: culinary context (Nazareth Arab-Christian city; ~77,000 residents; Israel's largest Arab city; food scene reflects Levantine Arab Christian traditions mixed with Galilean produce + Ottoman culinary heritage); Diana restaurant (family-run institution, widely considered Nazareth's finest, Arab-Israeli mezze + grilled meats, reservations required, owner Daoud Bulus legendary host, consistently in top Israeli restaurant lists — "most authentic Arab hospitality"); El Babour mill restaurant (converted flour mill in the old market, romantic setting, Eastern European + Middle Eastern fusion, noted for spice selection, often cited alongside Diana); Abu Ashraf (simple family lunches, grape leaves, lamb, fresh-squeezed pomegranate juice; lunch-only); the Nazareth market (spice merchants in the old city souk: za'atar, sumac, whole spices sold by weight — better selection + lower price than TLV/Jerusalem tourist markets); sweets (knafeh = iconic Palestinian/Levantine cheese pastry + orange blossom syrup + semolina — buy at any sweets shop on main street; Halawet el jibn = cheese-cream roll in rosewater; baklava shops sell by the kilo); coffee culture (Arabic cardamom coffee = qahwa in traditional homes; espresso culture also strong in modern Nazareth cafés); wine (Nazareth is in the Lower Galilee wine region; several nearby Christian-owned wineries). HONESTY: Diana and El Babour require advance reservations, especially weekends; Abu Ashraf closes early afternoon; "best Arab food in Israel" is genuine editorial consensus based on multiple sources but frame as "widely considered"; knafeh quality varies by shop — frame as "a highlight" not "the only good knafeh"; no fabricated prices — link individual restaurant websites. Cross-links: nazareth-travel-guide, galilee-tours-compared, day-trips-from-haifa, day-trips-from-tel-aviv, kosher-food-guide (halal vs kosher note), israel-food-tours-cooking-classes. SEO: "best restaurants Nazareth Israel 2026", "Nazareth food guide", "Arab-Israeli cuisine guide", "where to eat Nazareth", "Diana restaurant Nazareth". Sources: timeout.com/israel, tripadvisor.com/Restaurants-g297702-Nazareth, haaretz.com/israel-news/travel, aleph-travel.com/israel/regions/nazareth. [iter180 research] status: ready

- [SHIPPED iter191] Yad Vashem complete visitor guide (/yad-vashem-visitor-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: src/content/attractions/jerusalem-yad-vashem.md is a 91-line stub; NO standalone visitor guide exists. Tourist Israel has /yad-vashem-holocaust-museum/409/; Lonely Planet has a dedicated attraction page; Bein Harim offers Yad Vashem combination tours. The site receives 1M+ visitors/year — highest-traffic single attraction in Jerusalem after the Old City; massive search demand for practical visit logistics. Content: visit sequencing through 9 chronological galleries (Shoah history from 1933 → Liberation); Children's Memorial (separate building, 5 candles reflected via mirrors to create 1.5M points of light — one per child victim; stark, moving, 10-min standalone visit); Avenue + Garden of the Righteous Among the Nations (10,000+ trees honouring rescuers; outdoor walk pre/post main museum); Hall of Names (rotunda with 600 names + photos, 3M+ documented; database searchable at yadvashem.org); Photography absolutely prohibited inside main museum (allowed outdoors and in some memorial spaces — check signage); no backpacks inside (free lockers at entrance); recommended for visitors age 10+; groups of 6+ must pre-book via yadvashem.org (mandatory); individuals can enter without advance booking; allow 3–4 hours for full site; arrive by 10am (crowds peak 11am–2pm); access: Jerusalem Light Rail to Mount Herzl station + free shuttle every 20 min (or bus 27 from city centre); entry is FREE; last admission 1 hour before closing. Monetisation: GYG "Jerusalem + Yad Vashem guided tour" CTA (contextually appropriate — many visitors prefer guided interpretation of Holocaust history; guided tours add depth and personal testimony context). HONESTY: do not fabricate survivor names, exhibit content descriptions, or casualty sub-counts beyond well-established historical record; frame emotional preparation as personal — "many visitors find the experience deeply moving; allow time to process"; exit gardens/café provide reflection space. Cross-links: jerusalem-city-guide, 1-day-jerusalem-itinerary, jerusalem-old-city-walking-tour, israel-itinerary-2-weeks. - [P2] (seo-content, M) Mount of Olives complete visitor guide (/mount-of-olives-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: src/content/attractions/jerusalem-mount-of-olives.md is an 84-line stub; NO standalone visitor guide exists. Tourist Israel has a dedicated page; Bein Harim has 3 separate Mount of Olives pages. The ridge overlooking the Old City contains Jerusalem's most photographed viewpoint and 5+ major Christian + Jewish pilgrimage sites; extremely high search volume from Christian pilgrims and first-time Jerusalem visitors. Content: The Viewpoint (top of Mount of Olives road, above Intercontinental Hotel; iconic Jerusalem Old City panorama — Dome of the Rock centre, Old City walls, Jewish Quarter in foreground; single best photograph in Jerusalem; best 30 min before sunrise (golden light hits Temple Mount directly) and at sunset — avoid midday flat light; Bus 75 from Damascus Gate or taxi ~₪35–45 from city centre; street vendors at viewpoint sell postcards and sfeeha); Church of All Nations / Basilica of the Agony (Franciscan; gold mosaic facade of Christ presenting the world to God; interior has remarkable dim amber light from coloured windows; Rock of the Agony = bare rock inside church where Jesus is believed to have prayed before arrest; free entry; hours 8am–12pm + 2pm–5/6pm seasonal — check board at entrance; no photography inside church); Dominus Flevit Church ("the Lord Wept" — teardrop-shaped chapel designed by Antonio Barluzzi 1955; exterior looks like a stone tear-drop; interior window above altar frames the entire Old City perfectly = one of Jerusalem's best architectural photographs; free entry; hours 8am–11:45am + 2:30pm–5pm; Franciscan); Garden of Gethsemane (adjacent to Church of All Nations; 8 ancient olive trees — carbon-dated to approximately 900 years old, though grove may have been cut and regrown from older roots; scientific uncertainty about 2,000-year age claim — frame as "ancient grove" or "some of Jerusalem's oldest trees"; free to walk the perimeter; photography of trees allowed; interior of garden fenced off; quiet, shaded refuge); Church of Mary Magdalene (Russian Orthodox; 7 golden onion domes visible from anywhere in Old City; exterior photography ideal; interior access extremely limited — typically open Tue + Thu 10am–12pm only; photogenic from Gethsemane path); Mount of Olives Jewish Cemetery (150,000+ burials; one of world's oldest continuously used Jewish cemeteries; tombs of prophets Haggai, Zechariah, Malachi in tradition; burial plots here command premium prices due to proximity to Temple Mount and belief in priority at resurrection; open to visitors; respectful dress required; no guided tours available — self-guided only); top-to-bottom walking route: Start at Viewpoint → Church of All Nations → Dominus Flevit → Garden of Gethsemane → Tomb of the Virgin Mary (cave chapel, Greek Orthodox + Armenian custody, free entry) → exit to Kidron Valley; total ~2km, ~1.5 hours walking time without site visits. HONESTY: olive tree age scientifically disputed — do NOT claim "2,000-year-old trees" without caveat; frame as "among Jerusalem's oldest olive trees"; church hours seasonal and subject to change on religious feast days — link official parish sites; Dominus Flevit view is genuinely iconic but only from inside — clarify it is accessible free; no fabricated pilgrimage statistics. Cross-links: jerusalem-old-city-walking-tour, 1-day-jerusalem-itinerary, jerusalem-city-guide, church-holy-sepulchre-guide (when built). - [P2] (seo-content+monetization, M) Israel Mediterranean diving & Caesarea Underwater Archaeological Park guide (/israel-mediterranean-diving) — CONFIRMED GAP: caesarea-guide.md (SHIPPED iter111) has exactly one sentence about "an underwater snorkel archaeology park (summer only)" with zero dive logistics; eilat-diving-snorkeling.md (SHIPPED) covers the Red Sea entirely. No Mediterranean dive guide exists. Caesarea Underwater National Park is the world's first underwater archaeological park (designated 1994): 4 color-coded rope-marked trails over the submerged Herodian harbor (20 BCE), including the massive sea wall, anchors, columns, statues, coins; snorkel trails accessible without certification (1–4m depth); dive trails reach 6–12m; PADI Old Caesarea Diving Center on-site (only operator with NPA permit for underwater park; rents full kit; guided dives + snorkel tours; open Apr–Oct seasonally). Beyond Caesarea: Achziv (northern coast near Nahariya; marine reserve; seahorses, octopus, small wrecks; Achziv National Park land-side with permits); Rosh Hanikra grottos above-water but underwater inspection accessible by snorkel in calm conditions; Dor Habonim beach (rocky coves south of Haifa; accessible shore dive; no guide needed; good for PADI-certified independent divers). Competitor coverage: caesarea-diving.com (the on-site dive operator's booking page ranks for "Caesarea diving Israel"); traveltriangle.com ("Best Scuba Diving In Israel 2026"); snorkeling-report.com/israel (dedicated Israel snorkel guide); PADI dive shop listing for Old Caesarea Diving Center. HONESTY: park is seasonal (closed Nov–Mar due to winter seas — do NOT present it as year-round); entry to Caesarea National Park required (link Caesarea.com ticket page); snorkel trails open to all ages but confirm swimming ability required; no fabricated depth/visibility claims — cite operator estimates (underwater visibility typically 6–12m in summer). Monetization: GYG "Caesarea snorkel tour" CTA + israelnationalparks.org/caesarea pass link. Cross-links: caesarea-guide, israel-national-parks-pass, eilat-diving-snorkeling, water-hiking-israel. SEO: "Caesarea underwater park Israel", "Mediterranean diving Israel guide", "snorkeling Caesarea Israel", "underwater archaeology park Israel", "Israel scuba diving Red Sea vs Mediterranean". Sources: caesarea-diving.com, traveltriangle.com/blog/scuba-diving-in-israel, padi.com (Old Caesarea Diving Center listing), israelnationalparks.org/caesarea. [iter190 research] status: ready

- [P2] (seo-content, M) Israel historic synagogues visitor guide (/israel-synagogue-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: jewish-heritage-israel.md (SHIPPED) covers broad Jewish cultural and historical sites; safed-tzfat-guide.md (SHIPPED) mentions a few Safed synagogues briefly in a city overview; NO dedicated multi-site synagogue visitor guide exists. High intent from Jewish diaspora visitors, heritage travelers, and any tourist exploring Jerusalem Old City (Jewish Quarter). Key sites: (1) Hurva Synagogue, Jerusalem Old City Jewish Quarter (neo-Byzantine; twice destroyed, rebuilt 2010 with stunning white dome visible from Western Wall plaza; named "Hurva" = ruin; free exterior; interior admission ₪35; guided tours yadvashem-style walk-around; open Sun–Thu 9am–5pm, Fri 9am–1pm; 15 min from Western Wall — include on Old City walk); (2) Four Sephardic Synagogues complex, Jewish Quarter (Ben Zakkai / Ramban / Emtzai / Istanbul — four historic Sephardic congregations in interconnected courtyards; 17th-century; active for prayers + tourism; entry ₪30; closed Shabbat + Jewish holidays); (3) Great Synagogue Jerusalem (King George St; 1982 neo-classical; one of world's largest active synagogues; free guided tours available through Israel Tour Guide School; Shabbat services particularly atmospheric with a large choir; non-Jewish visitors respectfully welcome at services + tours); (4) Abuhav Synagogue, Safed (16th-century Kabbalistic; attributed to Rabbi Yitzchak Abuhav; Torah scrolls said to be self-referentially miraculous in local tradition — frame as community belief; active congregation; women's gallery; open Sun–Fri mornings; free entry; deeply atmospheric — whitewashed stone, gilded woodwork); (5) Italian Synagogue (Hechal Shlomo museum, Jerusalem; Baroque interior salvaged from Conegliano Veneto, Italy (17th century) and reassembled in Jerusalem 1952; Italian Jewish Museum adjacent; admission ₪30; particularly appealing for art + architecture visitors); (6) Beit Alpha Synagogue National Park (Lower Galilee, near Beit She'an; 6th-century Byzantine-era floor mosaic = best-preserved ancient synagogue mosaic in Israel; stunning zodiac wheel + Ark of the Covenant + Abraham/Isaac scene; INPA entry fee; excellent for families — mosaic is dramatic visual + accessible story; no photography restrictions outdoors). HONESTY: synagogue hours subject to prayer schedules — always link official synagogue site; Hurva + Four Sephardic verified hours via official Jewish Quarter site; visiting active synagogues requires modest dress (covered shoulders + knees for all; men cover head — kippahs provided at most; women may need to sit separately at Orthodox services — explain respectfully in guide); Abuhav Torah scroll tradition = community belief, not historical fact — frame carefully. Monetization: GYG "Jerusalem Jewish Quarter + synagogues tour" CTA + israel-national-parks-pass (Beit Alpha). Cross-links: jewish-heritage-israel, jerusalem-old-city-walking-tour, safed-tzfat-guide, 1-day-jerusalem-itinerary, israel-national-parks-pass. SEO: "synagogues to visit in Israel", "Hurva Synagogue guide Jerusalem", "historic synagogues Israel tour", "Jewish Quarter Jerusalem synagogues", "Safed Kabbalistic synagogues". - [P3] (seo-content+monetization, S) Israel scenic helicopter tours guide (/israel-helicopter-tours) — CONFIRMED GAP: luxury-travel-israel.md (SHIPPED) has exactly one sentence: "Helicopter charter — several Israeli operators offer private charter flights between sites (Jerusalem to Masada, or Tel Aviv to Eilat); a fast-track option for multi-site itineraries in limited time." Grep for "helicopter" across all content files confirms only this single mention. No standalone guide exists. Competitor coverage: GYG has "Israel helicopter tour" listings (GetYourGuide); Viator has helicopter Israel listings; israelinsider.com has helicopter overview; heliisrael.com (operator website). Key operators and routes: Israel Sightseeing Helicopters (ISH; based Herzliya Airport; operated with certified civilian pilots; most prominent tourism operator); scenic routes include Jerusalem Old City flyover (bird's-eye Temple Mount + Dome of the Rock = extraordinary aerial view), Mediterranean coast (Old Jaffa + Tel Aviv skyline from sea side), Sea of Galilee + Golan Heights, Masada + Dead Sea (most dramatic single aerial view in Israel — Masada plateau floating above salt flats), Negev crater views (Machtesh Ramon from air). Typical experience: 15–30 min scenic flights; ₪800–1,500 per person (group rates available); minimum 2 passengers most flights; doors-on or doors-off options (doors-off = better photography but louder + cooler); helicopter pads at Herzliya, Tel Aviv Sde Dov site (check current availability), Eilat; some operators offer point-to-point charter (TLV → Eilat ~35 min vs 3h driving). Also available: private charter for groups (minibus helicopter, 6 pax) for multi-site days (Jerusalem AM → Dead Sea → Masada → Eilat PM = achievable in a single day by helicopter). HONESTY: prices are estimates — link operator booking sites for current rates; route availability changes seasonally and based on airspace; Israeli airspace near Jerusalem has restrictions (Temple Mount military zone); specific operators should be verified current — do not name a specific operator as "the best" without recent verification; no fabricated review counts. Monetization: GYG helicopter tours CTA + Viator helicopter Israel CTA. Cross-links: luxury-travel-israel, masada-tours-compared, eilat-tours-compared, galilee-tours-compared. SEO: "helicopter tours Israel", "helicopter flight over Jerusalem", "aerial tour Israel", "Israel helicopter sightseeing", "helicopter Masada Dead Sea". Sources: getyourguide.com/israel-l93/helicopter-tours-tc228, viator.com/Israel/d723 (helicopter filter), heliisrael.com, israelinsider.com/helicopter-tours. [iter190 research] status: ready

- [P2] (seo-content+monetization, S) Mount Tabor complete visitor guide (/mount-tabor-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: Grep for "mount tabor" + "tabor" across all content files shows only one mention — israel-adventure-sports.md line 130 in a hot air balloon context ("dawn flights drift over the Sea of Galilee, the Jezreel Valley and Mount Tabor at first light"). No standalone guide exists and no backlog entry exists. High intent from Christian pilgrims (Transfiguration site), Christian tour groups visiting Nazareth + Galilee (Mount Tabor is 10km from Nazareth), and general visitors wanting the famous panoramic Jezreel Valley view. Key facts: rises 575m above the Jezreel Valley (dramatic isolated dome shape visible from 30km); Transfiguration of Jesus tradition (Matthew 17:1–9, Mark 9:2–8, Luke 9:28–36) — "Mount Tabor" as Transfiguration site identified by 3rd-century church tradition; most Christian denominations accept this identification though Mount Hermon is the competing scholarly location — frame clearly as "traditional site" without false certainty; Basilica of the Transfiguration (Franciscan; 1924; architect Antonio Barluzzi — same architect as Tabgha's Church of the Multiplication and Dominus Flevit; striking twin-tower facade + dramatic interior arched nave + Transfiguration mosaic; free entry; open daily 8am–12pm + 2pm–5pm); Greek Orthodox Church of the Transfiguration (separately accessed on summit; traditionally the "Moses and Elijah" chapel; open to visitors but limited hours); Byzantine + Crusader fortification ruins beneath and around basilica (13th-century Crusader fortress of Thabor; partially excavated; walk the walls for panorama); Summit road logistics: personal cars CANNOT drive to the summit — the narrow spiral road is licensed exclusively to servees (shared minibuses) and taxis from the village of Daburiyya at the base; park at Daburiyya (free) + take servee/taxi to summit (₪20–30 one-way, ₪40–60 return; drivers wait at summit); allow 1.5–2 hours total visit. Combine with: Nazareth (10km NW — half-day combo); Megiddo National Park (20km SW — archaeology); Beit She'an (30km SE — Roman ruins). HONESTY: Transfiguration site tradition = 3rd century CE identification, not first-century archaeology — frame as "traditionally identified as the Transfiguration site"; Greek Orthodox church hours irregular — confirm locally; servee/taxi logistics are the ONLY option for personal vehicles — must state this clearly to prevent frustrated visitors driving up and turning back; no fabricated entry prices for the basilica (entry currently free but donations requested). Monetization: GYG "Galilee + Nazareth + Mount Tabor" day tour CTA + discovercars.com (Nazareth/Galilee car rental context). Cross-links: christian-pilgrimage-holy-land, galilee-christian-sites-circuit, nazareth-travel-guide, galilee-tours-compared, israel-national-parks-pass. SEO: "Mount Tabor guide Israel", "Transfiguration site Israel", "Mount Tabor Nazareth day trip", "Basilica Transfiguration Mount Tabor", "Mount Tabor visitor information". Sources: touristisrael.com/mount-tabor, franciscan-cyberspot.org/tabor (Basilica of Transfiguration), biblewalks.com/Tabor, tripadvisor.com/Attraction_Review-g297702-Mount_Tabor. [iter190 research] status: ready

- [P2] (seo-content+monetization, M) Tel Megiddo (Armageddon) standalone visitor guide (/tel-megiddo-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: Megiddo appears only in cluster guides: hidden-gems hub backlog (line 66, as 1 of 10+ sites), UNESCO World Heritage hub backlog (line 324, as 1 of 9 sites = "Biblical Tels: Megiddo, Hazor, Beer-Sheba"), archaeology dig guide backlog (line 317, as a "dig for a day" option). A standalone guide is DISTINCT from all three: it provides dedicated visitor logistics, full site interpretation, and the "Armageddon" popular culture hook that drives massive secular + Christian pilgrim search volume. Competitor coverage: parks.org.il/megiddo (official INPA page); biblewalks.com/Megiddo (most comprehensive English walk-through by photos + narrative); touristisrael.com (Megiddo National Park guide); Lonely Planet (Megiddo World Heritage listing); Frommer's (Upper Galilee chapter includes Megiddo). Key content: Name + biblical hook (Hebrew "Har Megiddo" → Greek "Armageddon" → Revelation 16:16 site of the final battle; this is the main draw for secular visitors who arrive saying "so THIS is where Armageddon is supposed to happen" — lean into it with accurate scholarly context); 26 strata of occupation spanning 7,000 years (4500 BCE–332 BCE; 20 distinct cities each built on the ruins of the last; archaeological layer cake visible in the sides of the exposed tel); UNESCO World Heritage Site 2005 (jointly designated with Tel Hazor + Tel Beer Sheva as "Biblical Tels — Megiddo, Hazor, Beer-Sheba" for demonstrating Bronze/Iron Age urban civilization); Site highlights: (a) Solomonic Gate (Iron Age; 10th century BCE; 6-chamber gate attributed to King Solomon's building campaign; parallel gate at Hazor + Gezer; 1 Kings 9:15 — frame attribution as "traditionally attributed to Solomon; similar in form to other 10th-century BCE gates"); (b) Stables / Storehouses (Iron Age; once thought to be Solomon's stables — now more often interpreted as storehouses or administrative buildings from the Omri dynasty period; interpret carefully — scholarly debate ongoing); (c) Ahab's Water Tunnel (9th-century BCE; 75m underground shaft + 50m horizontal tunnel cut through solid rock to bring water from outside the city walls into the Tel; can walk through the tunnel = one of the most impressive ancient engineering feats visible in Israel; low ceiling — note for claustrophobic visitors + children); (d) Bronze Age Temples (3500–1200 BCE; circular altar and Canaanite high-place area; pre-Israelite layer); (e) Jezreel Valley panorama from tel crest (360° view across the Valley of Armageddon — vast agricultural plain flanked by Mount Carmel ridge to west, Mount Gilboa to east, Mount Tabor to north; the scale of the "final battle" imagery makes geographic sense from this vantage); Site museum at entrance (good orientation before or after walking the tel; scale model of the city layers; Megiddo Ivories display — some of the finest Bronze Age carved ivory objects found anywhere; allow 20 min); logistics: INPA national park, Israel National Parks Pass valid; entry ₪35 adults/₪19 children; 9am–5pm (winter 4pm); allow 1.5–2.5 hours; 30 min from Haifa by car via Route 70; combined route: Haifa → Megiddo → Mount Tabor (20km) → Nazareth (20km further NW). HONESTY: "Armageddon" etymology and Revelation reference = accurate; "final battle" site is Christian apocalyptic tradition, NOT a historical prediction — frame clearly as theological tradition; Solomonic attribution is working scholarly consensus but debated — frame as "traditionally attributed to"; Stables vs Storehouses = genuine scholarly debate — present both interpretations; water tunnel walk = genuinely accessible (INPA maintains it) but confirm with current site before publication; no fabricated UNESCO ranking. Monetization: GYG "Megiddo National Park guided tour" CTA + Israel National Parks Pass CTA. Cross-links: israel-national-parks-pass, day-trips-from-haifa, galilee-tours-compared, mount-tabor-guide, jewish-heritage-israel. SEO: "Tel Megiddo visitor guide", "Armageddon Israel site guide", "Megiddo National Park tips", "Biblical Tels UNESCO Israel", "Megiddo archaeological site walk-through". Sources: parks.org.il/megiddo, biblewalks.com/Megiddo, touristisrael.com/megiddo-national-park, lonelyplanet.com/israel/lower-galilee-and-jezreel-valley/tel-megiddo. [iter190 research] status: ready

- [ARCHIVED] (seo-content+monetization, S) Jordan Pass complete guide (/jordan-pass-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: petra-from-israel.md (SHIPPED) covers border logistics for a Petra day-trip; petra-from-eilat-vs-amman.md (SHIPPED, i18n pending) covers the crossing comparison; petra-tours-compared.md (SHIPPED) covers Petra tour operators. NONE of these explain the Jordan Pass product itself — the single-purchase ticket (jordanpass.jo) that bundles entry to 40+ Jordanian sites including Petra with a tourist visa waiver, saving visitors JD30–50 vs. paying separately. High search: "Jordan Pass is it worth it", "Jordan Pass Israel", "how to buy Jordan Pass", "Jordan Pass Petra". Competitor coverage: nomadicmatt.com, earth-trekkers.com, and every Israel+Jordan travel blog has a Jordan Pass explainer; ZERO equivalent on our site. Content: What the Jordan Pass is (single online purchase from jordanpass.jo; 3 tiers by Petra visits: 1 day/2 days/3+ days; includes Petra entrance fee + waives the JD40 tourist visa for eligible nationalities; valid for 5 days in Jordan; 70+ sites included); Cost math (Jordan tourist visa JD40 + Petra 1-day JD60 = JD100 separately; Jordan Pass 1-day Petra ~JD70 = saves JD30 minimum; 2+ Petra days = always worth it; spending ≥1 night = cost-positive); How to buy (online-only at jordanpass.jo; need passport number + planned entry date; instant PDF/QR download; present at land border AND at Petra ticket office); What it does NOT include (accommodation, food, horse/donkey ride inside Petra, guide fees, internal transport); Land border activation (Wadi Araba/Eilat → Aqaba: present pass on Jordanian side; Allenby Bridge → Amman: same; both cases: Jordan side immigration stamps the pass); When NOT worth it (if visiting Jordan fewer than 2 nights and only 1 day at Petra AND your nationality pays no visa fee separately — rare case for GCC + certain nationalities); current pass coverage for non-Petra sites (Jerash, Aqaba, Madaba, Mount Nebo, Kerak Castle, Wadi Rum facilities, etc. — all covered). HONESTY: Jordan Pass prices change annually — always link jordanpass.jo for current price; NEVER hardcode price ranges as they shift; pass validity period may change — link official site; some nationalities are visa-exempt from Jordan independently (verify at moi.gov.jo — pass still saves on Petra entrance only in those cases, but the saving may be less); do NOT state any specific country "cannot" use the pass — direct to official site for nationality-specific eligibility; Petra horse ride from entrance gate = NOT included, typically JD5–10, optional; guide fees NOT included (recommended for Treasury/High Place/Monastery routes). Monetization: Booking.com Petra/Wadi Musa hotels CTA + GYG "Petra from Eilat" tour CTA (for visitors who want a guided day-trip without self-organizing). Dense cross-links: petra-from-israel.md, petra-from-eilat-vs-amman.md, petra-tours-compared.md, israel-jordan-itinerary (when built), border-crossings.md. SEO: "Jordan Pass guide 2026", "is Jordan Pass worth it Israel visitors", "how to buy Jordan Pass", "Jordan Pass Petra entry fee", "Jordan Pass cost savings calculator". Sources: jordanpass.jo (official), earth-trekkers.com/jordan-pass, nomadicmatt.com/travel-guides/jordan/jordan-pass, petra.gov.jo. [iter195 research] status: ready

- [P2] (seo-content+monetization, M) Arab-Israeli culture & food tourism guide (/arab-israeli-culture-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: muslim-travel-guide BACKLOG (iter65/105) is specifically for MUSLIM TRAVELERS navigating prayer times, Al-Aqsa access, halal food; druze-villages-carmel.md (SHIPPED) = Druze-specific; jaffa-travel-guide.md (SHIPPED) + nazareth-travel-guide.md (SHIPPED) + haifa-travel-guide.md (SHIPPED) each cover their city broadly. NO guide exists for ALL tourists who want to experience Arab-Israeli culture, food, and communities specifically — a growing segment (cultural tourism, coexistence travel, food tourism). 21% of Israel's citizens are Arab-Israeli (Christian, Muslim, Druze); their food, architecture, and culture are distinctive and genuinely outstanding. Competitor gap: Tourist Israel and Bein Harim have no dedicated "Arab-Israeli culture for tourists" guide; GYG has "Nazareth food tour" + "Jaffa Arab market tour" (confirmed on-site). Content: Five Arab-Israeli cultural experiences across Israel: (1) Wadi Nisnas, Haifa (Arab-Israeli arts neighborhood; covered market + traditional coffee roasters + falafel stands; Street Art Museum with murals by Arab-Israeli + Jewish artists; "House of Art" free Thursday cultural events; "Museum Without Walls" = murals across neighborhood walls; Christmas celebrations in December by Arab-Christian community = unique cross-cultural event often covered by Israeli media); (2) Nazareth Old City + market (largest Arab city in Israel 77k pop; Old City souq lanes; Mahroum Sweets = knafeh bakery on main street, considered best knafeh in Israel by multiple Israeli food critics; Fauzi Azar Inn = restored Ottoman mansion boutique guesthouse run by Arab-Israeli family, offers walking tours of Old City Arab community life; Evening Night Market Thu in summer; Al-Bishara Church restaurant = panoramic view + Arab-Israeli mezze); (3) Old Jaffa Arab cultural sites (al-Bahr Mosque at sea's edge — oldest mosque in Israel, active, visitors welcome between prayers; Abulafia bakery 7 Yefet St = 24/7 Arab bread + pastries + sfinge donuts = landmark TLV experience; Jaffa flea market Sunday = Arab antique dealers + vintage + creative market; Puaa restaurant = cheap Arab-Israeli breakfast institution on Yefet St); (4) Akko Old City Arab Quarter (the bazaar between the Khan and the sea gate; Uri Buri restaurant = James Beard-endorsed Arab-Israeli seafood; Al-Fanar at the sea wall; coffee roasters doing strong cardamom-spiced Arabic coffee; Old City Muslim Quarter evening lantern atmosphere); (5) Abu Gosh hummus pilgrimage (Palestinian-Israeli village 10km west of Jerusalem via Road 1; two restaurants famously served as the best hummus in Israel by multiple Israeli magazines + Haaretz: Abu Shukri-style family operations; Village kitchen; Crusader church in center; 15-min bus from Jerusalem; biannual Abu Gosh Vocal Music Festival in Crusader courtyard — spring and autumn). Cultural context (appropriate for tourists): note that Arab-Israeli citizens are full citizens of Israel; this is NOT a guide to the Palestinian West Bank (explicitly out of scope: Hebron excluded); Druze = separate community with own guide pages; Arab-Israeli restaurants typically halal or assume halal by community practice — ask staff to confirm; most Arab-Israeli commercial neighborhoods welcoming; Arabic greeting "ahlan wa sahlan" appreciated; Friday afternoon = Jumu'ah prayers, some businesses close briefly. HONESTY: "Arab-Israeli" = citizens of Israel (do NOT use "Palestinian" for this guide — this guide is specifically about citizens); West Bank is out of scope per project rules; "halal" claim = ask the restaurant — do not assume; Abu Gosh hummus reputation = widely attested by Israeli media (verifiable via Haaretz.co.il, Ynet, Timeout Israel) = can state with confidence but NOT fabricate awards or rankings; some of these restaurants/businesses change hours frequently — always link Google Maps for current hours. Monetization: GYG "Nazareth food tour" CTA (legitimate operator with verified reviews) + Abraham Tours "Interfaith Old City" tour CTA. Dense cross-links: haifa-travel-guide, jaffa-travel-guide, nazareth-travel-guide, akko-acre-guide, druze-villages-carmel, kosher-food-guide (contrast — Jewish dietary law vs Arab-Israeli cuisine). SEO: "Arab Israeli food culture guide", "Nazareth food tour", "Jaffa Arab market guide", "Haifa Wadi Nisnas visit", "coexistence tourism Israel", "Abu Gosh hummus guide". Sources: fauziazarinn.com (Nazareth community tourism), haaretz.co.il/food (Abu Gosh hummus feature), timeout.co.il/wadi-nisnas, tripadvisor.com/Nazareth food + Haifa Arab quarter, getyourguide.com/nazareth (food tour listings). [iter195 research] status: ready

- [SHIPPED iter271] Israel tour operators compared: who to book with (/israel-tour-operators-guide) → e097ed7  // CONFIRMED GAP (trimmed): masada-tours-compared.md, galilee-tours-compared.md, eilat-tours-compared.md, tel-aviv-tours-compared.md, petra-tours-compared.md, jerusalem-tours-compared.md — all SHIPPED — compare TOURS by destination. NO guide exists comparing the OPERATORS themselves (Abraham Tours vs Bein Harim vs GetYourGuide vs Viator vs private IMTA guides). High search: "Abraham Tours review Israel", "Bein Harim Tours review", "best Israel tour company 2026", "GetYourGuide vs Viator Israel", "should I use Abraham Tours". A buyer's guide to operators captures high-intent pre-booking traffic with direct affiliate revenue. Operator landscape: (1) Abraham Tours (Jerusalem/TLV/Haifa/Eilat hostels; budget-to-mid small-group day tours; TripAdvisor Certificate of Excellence; strong backpacker/millennials following; organizes from hostel hubs; culturally diverse guides; best for: solo travelers, budget-conscious, group social vibe; not ideal: private/quiet experience seekers); (2) Bein Harim Tours (large-fleet coach operator; biggest Israel inbound operator; set departure times; mostly Jerusalem/Galilee/Dead Sea standard circuits; popular with senior groups + cruise passengers + larger families; budget-friendly group rates; best for: cruise passengers, large groups, standard itineraries; not ideal: independent travelers wanting flexibility); (3) GetYourGuide (marketplace; thousands of Israel experiences from 50+ operators; cancellation guarantee; payment security; best filter: "free cancellation" + "rating ≥ 4.5"; best for: activity-by-activity booking, flexibility; not ideal: package itinerary seekers); (4) Viator (TripAdvisor-owned marketplace; largest global review base; slightly higher prices than GYG; premium operator verification; best for: verified reviews on contested-quality operators, booking luxury/specialist tours; not ideal: budget bookings); (5) Keshet Tours / Egged Tours (Israel's established group tour operators; affordable; set package itineraries; English-guided; best for: structured packages from Israel with hotel); (6) IMTA-licensed private guides (Israel's Ministry of Tourism licensed; individual specialists; Hebrew/Arabic/Christian-site expertise; charge ₪800–1,500/day; INGUIDE platform lists them; best for: deep-dive Biblical sites, Holocaust education, personalized family itineraries — worth every shekel at heritage sites); use-case matrix: best for budget solo → Abraham; best for cruise ship → Bein Harim; best for flexibility → GYG; best for verified quality → Viator; best for depth → IMTA private guide. HONESTY: no fabricated review counts or stars; link TripAdvisor + GYG listing pages for current verified reviews; operator quality varies by GUIDE assigned not just company — state clearly; prices change seasonally — link booking page for current pricing; do NOT rank these operators ordinally from "best" to "worst" — present as "right for different needs"; Bein Harim + Abraham have thousands of TripAdvisor verified reviews — can reference quality with confidence. Monetization: HIGHEST revenue potential of any single guide — GYG affiliate link (commission per booking) + Viator affiliate link + Abraham Tours direct link + Bein Harim inquiry link; a well-converting operator guide typically outperforms destination guides 3–5× per page on affiliate revenue. Dense cross-links: best-tours-in-israel.md, all tours-compared pages (masada, galilee, eilat, tel-aviv, jerusalem, petra), first-time-in-israel. SEO: "Abraham Tours review 2026", "Bein Harim Tours Israel review", "best Israel tour company guide", "GetYourGuide Israel vs Viator", "which tour company for Israel trip". Sources: abrahamtours.com, beinharimtours.com/about, tripadvisor.com/Attraction_Review-d619513-Abraham_Tours, tripadvisor.com/Attraction_Review-g293977-d590177-Bein_Harim_Tours, getyourguide.com/israel (Israel category), viator.com/Israel/d723. - [P2] (seo-content, M) Israel in Spring: March, April & May travel guide (/israel-in-spring) — CONFIRMED GAP: best-time-to-visit-israel.md (SHIPPED) covers all 12 months briefly; israel-in-autumn guide (BACKLOG iter195) was added as a seasonal deep-dive. NO equivalent spring guide exists, despite spring (March–May) being the single most recommended season and April being Israel's highest-traffic tourist month (Passover + Easter overlap). The complement to the Autumn guide in our seasonal series. High search: "visiting Israel in spring", "Israel in April guide", "Israel March April travel", "Israel spring weather", "Easter in Israel 2027". Content: Why spring is Israel's peak season (weather at its best: Jerusalem 18–24°C, TLV 20–26°C, Negev hiking ideal before summer heat; wildflower bloom peaks in Feb–Mar in Negev, Mar–Apr in Galilee; Mediterranean sea not yet swimming temperature but pleasant coastal walking); The Passover + Easter convergence window (typically late March–late April; most impactful overlap in tourism: Jewish Passover = synagogue Seders + matzah everywhere + Old City extra-crowded around Kotel; Easter = Armenian/Roman Catholic/Greek Orthodox on different dates — link church-holy-sepulchre-guide; Good Friday Via Dolorosa procession = deeply moving, free to observe; Easter Sunday at Galilee churches; Greek Orthodox Easter fire ceremony at Holy Sepulchre = uniquely spectacular); Spring-specific highlights: (1) Red anemone / wildflower season peaks Feb–Mar (link israel-spring-wildflowers when built; Darom Adom festival Eshkol region — cross-link); (2) Galilee and Golan Heights lush and green (cross-link golan-heights-guide when built; Sea of Galilee best in spring — less crowded than summer, refreshingly clear water); (3) Masada and desert hiking season in full swing before heat (April = ideal Dead Sea + Masada day trips, cross-link masada-dead-sea-day-trip, dead-sea-guide); (4) Tel Aviv outdoor culture (outdoor café season in full bloom; Rothschild Boulevard tree canopy green; Dizengoff Square filled with evening activity; TLV restaurants open their street-facing terraces; Independence Day celebrations in April = fireworks + parks full); (5) Jerusalem's spring atmosphere (Mahane Yehuda market spring produce; Wohl Rose Garden in Givat Ram blooms; Mea Shearim Passover preparations = fascinating urban ethnography; post-Passover quiet for the week after holiday = lower crowds, lower prices); Practical planning: book accommodation 3–6 months ahead for April (Passover + Easter = highest annual hotel prices and availability crunch); Passover restaurant logistics (first and last days of Passover most restaurants close or serve chametz-free menus; mid-chol-hamoed = mostly normal in non-religious neighborhoods; Tel Aviv restaurants largely unaffected; Jerusalem Old City area = more observance-driven closures); Easter week church logistics (Western churches Dec–Apr, Orthodox typically 1–2 weeks later; always check specific church for English service times); Hebrew calendar caveat: Passover shifts each Gregorian year — link chabad.org/holidays. What to pack (light layers + rain jacket for Jerusalem; sandals + hats for Negev; swimsuit for indoor hotel pools + maybe Dead Sea; formal layer for Shabbat/religious sites). Budget: April = highest prices (book early or target first 2 weeks of March for spring conditions at shoulder prices). HONESTY: "April = best month" is editorial recommendation — not guaranteed (occasional heat waves in April do occur; rain possible in Jerusalem into mid-March); Passover restaurant closures complex — frame as "varies by neighborhood and restaurant; call ahead for Passover week"; Easter dates shift annually — link to correct 2027 dates via Wikipedia/church calendar, never hardcode; Dead Sea swimming technically permitted in spring but some visitors find it cold before May. Dense cross-links: best-time-to-visit-israel, israel-in-autumn, christmas-in-israel (when built), israel-events-festivals, masada-dead-sea-day-trip, dead-sea-guide, church-holy-sepulchre-guide, 1-day-jerusalem-itinerary, israel-spring-wildflowers (when built), golan-heights-guide (when built), shabbat-guide. Monetization: Booking.com CTA (book early spring message) + GYG Easter/Passover experience tours + GYG "Sunrise at Masada" spring experience CTA. - [P2] (seo-content+monetization, M) Golan Heights practical travel guide (/golan-heights-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: golan region hub page exists (regions/golan.md) but provides brief overview. NO standalone practical destination guide exists for the Golan Heights as a multi-day destination — comparable to the druze-villages-carmel.md (SHIPPED) + haifa-travel-guide.md (SHIPPED) + nazareth-travel-guide.md (SHIPPED) format. Mount Hermon skiing guide = separate BACKLOG item (iter60/iter145). This guide covers the Golan as a whole destination for self-drive explorers and day-trippers. Competitors: Tourist Israel ("How to Visit the Golan Heights"), Backpack Israel ("How to Plan a Trip to the Golan Heights"), Timeout Israel ("Best Attractions in the Golan Heights"), Private Tours Israel ("Golan Heights Ultimate Guide"), Frommers (Upper Galilee and Golan chapter), TakeyourBackpack, Isrotel Magazine all have comprehensive Golan guides; ZERO equivalent on our site beyond a region hub. Six anchor experiences: (1) Banias (Hermon Stream) Nature Reserve — ancient Caesarea Philippi / Pan's spring cave sanctuary (Greco-Roman temple to Pan built over the Jordan's source; significant New Testament site — "gates of Hades" passage in Matthew 16:18 understood to reference this pagan shrine — relevant cross-link to christian-pilgrimage-holy-land); Israel's most powerful waterfall (Banias Falls, 10m, suspended wooden walkway over the gorge); easy 2km lower trail + more strenuous upper trail to the falls; lush forested canyon; best spring and autumn when water flow strongest; (2) Gamla Nature Reserve — the "Masada of the North" (Jewish Revolt city destroyed by Romans 67 CE, one year before Jerusalem; dramatic ridge-top archaeological site overlooking the Sea of Galilee; Gamla Waterfall trail; the Golan Heights' most significant archaeological site for historical drama; griffon vulture colony on cliffs = best raptor viewing in Israel, organized by INPA); (3) Golan Heights Winery & wine region (Katzrin, the Golan's capital; Golan Heights Winery = Israel's most internationally awarded winery since 1983; high-altitude volcanic basalt soil + cool nights = ideal viticulture; tasting room open daily except Shabbat, ₪40–60/tasting; buy direct vs retail; winery tours available by appointment; key wines: Yarden, Gamla, Golan labels; cross-link israel-wine-wineries.md); (4) Nimrod Fortress National Park — largest Crusader/Mamluk castle in Israel (13th-century Crusader stronghold expanded by Ayyubids; dramatic position on ridge above Banias; towers accessible; INPA card valid; stunning views over Upper Galilee; 45 min drive from Tiberias); (5) Majdal Shams — Druze town on the slopes of Mount Hermon; Shouting Hill (HaMoshav HaMichabel) — observation point overlooking the Syrian side of the fence where separated Druze families historically shouted across to each other (fence is still there; the shouting hill has become symbolic; intense and moving to visit); local Druze restaurants and artisan food stalls; cross-link druze-villages-carmel for background on Druze culture; distinct from Carmel Druze (closer to Syrian Druze, maintain different political status); (6) Tel Dan Nature Reserve — northernmost tip of Israel; one of Israel's most beautiful nature walks (bamboo jungle + crystal-clear Dan River fed by Hermon snow melt; ancient Tel Dan = Canaanite/Israelite city where Jeroboam set up golden calves — significant Biblical archaeology; stele inscribed "House of David" = earliest non-Biblical reference to King David; easy 2h loop trail). Getting around: car essential (public bus service extremely limited; Bus 55/58 from Kiryat Shmona to Majdal Shams on some days; no direct bus to Banias, Gamla, Nimrod Fortress without private vehicle; renting from Tiberias or Haifa = access hub); staying overnight: Kibbutz guest houses + Golan Druze B&Bs + Katzrin (largest town); Neve Ativ ski village near Hermon for winter. HONESTY: Golan Heights is Israeli-administered but its permanent international legal status is disputed; our existing is-israel-safe guide covers safety context — cross-link, don't editorialize; border area security from INPA/gov.il warnings only; Banias Roman temple ruins = active archaeological excavation area; some Gamla trails require fitness + footwear (state clearly); Majdal Shams "Shouting Hill" is now accessible via normal visitor protocols — the dramatic fence crossing scenes of the past are from an era that ended post-2024 Syrian political transition (confirm current status at trip time — political situation evolving); wine tasting tours require advance booking at Golan Heights Winery. Dense cross-links: golan region hub, mount-hermon-skiing (when shipped), druze-villages-carmel, christian-pilgrimage-holy-land (Banias/Caesarea Philippi), israel-wine-wineries, hiking-in-israel, day-trips-from-haifa (Banias + Nimrod reachable from Haifa in long day), day-trips-from-tel-aviv (Golan as overnight destination), israel-national-parks-pass (Gamla + Banias + Nimrod = INPA card valid). Monetization: GYG/Viator Golan Heights day tour CTA (from TLV/Tiberias; multiple operators) + Booking.com Kiryat Shmona/Tiberias filter (Golan gateway accommodation). SEO: "Golan Heights travel guide 2026", "things to do Golan Heights Israel", "Golan Heights day trip from Tel Aviv", "Banias waterfall Israel guide", "Gamla nature reserve visit", "Golan Heights wine tasting". Sources: touristisrael.com/visit-golan-heights/12401, timeout.com/israel/attractions/the-best-attractions-in-the-golan-heights, backpackisrael.com/destinations/how-to-plan-a-trip-to-the-golan-heights, private-tours-in-israel.com/guides/golan-heights-ultimate-guide, frommers.com/destinations/upper-galilee-and-the-golan-heights/things-to-do, takeyourbackpack.com/backpacking-in-israel/visit-golan-heights.
