- [P2] (seo-content, S) Western Wall Tunnels tour guide (/western-wall-tunnels-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: jerusalem-old-city-walking-tour.md (SHIPPED iter186) covers the Old City at street level; church-holy-sepulchre-guide.md (SHIPPED) covers one major underground/architectural site. The Western Wall Tunnels = entirely separate underground experience that reveals the full extent of Herod's Western Wall (the visible praying section is only 57m of an original 488m wall) and includes excavated Herodian street running beneath the modern Muslim Quarter. One of Jerusalem's most popular paid experiences: GYG shows 500+ reviews for various Western Wall Tunnel tour operators; TripAdvisor #3-5 most-booked Jerusalem attraction depending on season; consistently cited in "Jerusalem bucket list" content. Competitors: Tourist Israel ("Western Wall Tunnels — All You Need to Know"), Danny The Digger ("The Western Wall Tunnels: 2026 Tour Guide"), itraveljerusalem.com (Western Wall Tunnels page), seetheholyland.net, yeshiva.co/western-wall-heritage-foundation all have guides. Content: What the Tunnels are (Western Wall Heritage Foundation (WWHF)-managed underground excavation running north under the Muslim Quarter alongside the full length of the original Herodian Western Wall; exposure of an ancient Hasmonean/Herodian street at the base of the wall; Warren's Gate = sealed ancient gateway into the Temple Mount visible from inside the tunnels; Struthion Pool — ancient Roman cistern — visible in sections; massive foundation stone (Western Stone) = largest stone in the Herodian wall structure, 14m × 3.5m × 4.5m and ~570 tonnes; guided tours only — no self-guided access); Tour logistics (advance booking ESSENTIAL — weeks to months ahead for morning/peak time slots; book at english.thekotel.org only; 60–70 minute tours in English depart regularly; exit via Via Dolorosa in the Muslim Quarter rather than returning the way you came — important logistical note; combined tunnel + Kotel visit = 2.5h minimum); Who it's for (history-focused, archaeologically curious visitors; strong Biblical interest; NOT suitable for claustrophobic visitors or those with mobility issues — narrow passages with stairs and uneven surfaces; stairs unavoidable); Context provided on tour (Herod's original intention for the wall; 2,000-year-old Hasmonean conduit; the significance of Warren's Gate — the closest physical point to the Temple's Holy of Holies that a visitor can legally access in the modern era — major significance for observant Jewish visitors). HONESTY: tours are guided-only and require advance booking (do NOT imply walk-in access); passage IS narrow — state mobility/claustrophobia context clearly; group sizes capped; Exit via Muslim Quarter = surprise for visitors who expect to emerge where they entered — state explicitly; "closest point to the Holy of Holies" claim is geographically accurate but requires honest contextual framing (Temple itself was destroyed; Waqf controls Temple Mount access above); pricing from WWHF site (do not fabricate; link thekotel.org for current prices). Dense cross-links: jerusalem-old-city-walking-tour, church-holy-sepulchre-guide, 1-day-jerusalem-itinerary, jewish-heritage-israel, christian-pilgrimage-holy-land (significance for Christian pilgrims re: Second Temple), first-time-in-israel. Monetization: GYG "Western Wall Tunnels tour" CTA (multiple operators; WWHF-licensed; book via GYG for peace of mind re: cancellation). SEO: "Western Wall Tunnels tour Jerusalem guide", "Kotel tunnels how to book", "Western Wall Tunnels visitor guide 2026", "underground Jerusalem tour". Sources: touristisrael.com/western-wall-tunnels, dannythedigger.com/western-wall-tunnels, english.thekotel.org (WWHF official), itraveljerusalem.com/western-wall-tunnels. [iter200 research] status: SHIPPED iter207

- [P3] (seo-content, S) Tel Aviv street art & urban culture walking guide (/tel-aviv-street-art) — CONFIRMED GAP: tel-aviv-neighborhoods-guide.md (SHIPPED iter157) covers Florentin briefly as "hipster-bohemian"; jaffa-travel-guide.md (SHIPPED) mentions Jaffa flea market; zero dedicated street art content anywhere on site. Tel Aviv has one of the world's most vibrant street art scenes — specifically Florentin district (south TLV, 10-minute walk from Levinsky Market + Central Bus Station area) and Neve Tzedek's back streets. Growing SEO/link-bait niche: "street art Tel Aviv", "Florentin district Tel Aviv guide", "urban art Tel Aviv walk" rank well with blog and social audiences; Atlas Obscura, Time Out, Lonely Planet, multiple photo-bloggers (seesaw.media, midsommarkansen.com, flickr gallery communities) all have dedicated TLV street art content. Content: Three districts + context: (1) Florentin (bounded by Salame Road + Florentin St + Levinsky St + Kibbutz Galuyot; the epicenter; dense murals on every alley wall; key walls: Florentin 21 (multi-artist building facade gallery), "Wall of Fame" near parking at corner of Florentin + Matalon, Stern Street fire-escape murals; local artists: Broken Fingaz Crew (TLV's most internationally recognized street art collective; internationally shown at Stroke Urban Art Fair Munich, appeared in Vans x Broken Fingaz collab); Nespoon (European lace-pattern muralist who has done TLV residency works); Solomon Souza (figurative portraits); (2) Tel Aviv Port / HaTachana area (gentrified areas where street art meets commercial installation; Bauhaus murals on concrete pillars near the train station HaTachana complex — cross-link tel-aviv-white-city); (3) Jaffa (al-Ajami and flea market surroundings; coexistence-themed murals near the Arab-Jewish interface; cross-link jaffa-travel-guide); Self-guided walk option (Florentin: 1.5–2h loop covering 20–30 murals; downloadable/Google Maps pin approach); Photography tips (morning best for softer light + no crowds; Florentin murals on south-facing walls = afternoon shadow; best focal lengths + composition advice for wall art); Context: Tel Aviv street art scene emerged from early 2000s as city began tolerating (then celebrating) murals that had started as underground work; current city-commissioned public art program expanded since 2015; pieces update regularly (new murals replace older work — HONESTY: specific pieces change; guide should frame as "the Florentin district" not specific mural locations that may have been painted over). HONESTY: specific murals change as new work is painted; never claim a specific mural "is there" — frame as "as of [year] the district features..."; Broken Fingaz Crew's presence is verified via their own website + multiple press features; guide captures a type of experience, not a guaranteed specific inventory. No monetization (pure SEO/link-bait). Dense cross-links: tel-aviv-neighborhoods-guide, jaffa-travel-guide, tel-aviv-white-city-bauhaus (when built), free-things-to-do-israel. SEO: "Tel Aviv street art guide", "Florentin district street art Tel Aviv", "urban art walk Tel Aviv", "best street art Israel". Sources: atlasoscura.com/places/tel-avivs-street-art (multiple TLV entries), timeout.com/israel/art/street-art-in-tel-aviv, broken-fingaz.com (artist collective), lonely planet Tel Aviv street art section. - [P2] (seo-content, M) Israel in winter: January & February travel guide (/israel-in-winter) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "israel.in.winter|winter.*israel|january.*israel|february.*israel" — backlog has /christmas-in-israel (iter045, covers Dec 24-25 Christian Christmas + Jan 6 Orthodox + Jan 18 Armenian) and /israel-in-spring (iter200, covers Mar-May) and /israel-in-autumn (SHIPPED iter195, covers Sep-Nov). January-February specific content is genuinely distinct: those months fall AFTER Christmas and have entirely different attractions. Competitors: backpackisrael.com has a "winter in Israel" guide (~600 words, no affiliate hooks). Content: Why visit January-February (cheapest flights + accommodation of the year — shoulder to low season except Jewish holidays; most attractions without summer crowds; Negev + Dead Sea hiking at peak conditions — 15-20°C versus brutal 40°C summer); Eilat whale sharks (January-February is peak whale shark season in the Red Sea; snorkeling with whale sharks at Eilat is a globally unique experience; Dolphin Reef + underwater observatory open year-round; GYG whale shark snorkeling tours; this is the ONLY time whale sharks consistently appear near Eilat — critical Jan-Feb specific draw); Dead Sea in winter (most comfortable temperatures for floating; 22-25°C air, 33°C water; Ein Gedi hiking at its best in winter with greenery; Masada: winter sunrise ascent without summer heat exhaustion — Masada Snake Path is brutal in July, manageable in January); Negev hiking peak (Makhtesh Ramon: best hiking Oct-Mar; Avdat National Park; Sde Boker; Israel National Trail section; wildflowers begin appearing in the Negev in late February); Jerusalem possible snow (rare but spectacular — happens roughly 1-3 years out of 10 in January; Hermon ski resort open Jan-Feb if snowfall; ski lift + basic ski rental; ≤30-min drive from Kiryat Shmona in the north; Temple Mount + Dome of the Rock with snow = iconic photography; HONESTY: snow in Jerusalem not guaranteed — frame as "occasionally possible, last major snowfall was 2015"); Northwest coast birdwatching (Hula Valley bird migration — winter is peak season for tens of thousands of cranes, pelicans, storks at Agamon HaHula; INPA park; bird hide photography; organized safaris available); Practical: what's closed in winter (some Galilee + Golan attractions reduce winter hours; Jordan River rafting closes November-March; water parks closed); packing (layers + waterproofs — Dec-Feb is Israel's only real rainy season; Jerusalem gets cold at night 3-8°C; Eilat stays warm 20-25°C day). HONESTY: Israel in winter = "budget-friendly + crowd-free" frame is accurate; avoid claiming all prices are low (some winter activities like Hermon skiing can be expensive); Jerusalem snow framing must be explicitly uncertain; whale shark snorkeling — sharks are wild animals, not guaranteed on every dive, frame as "best window" not "guaranteed"; rainfall data from Israel Meteorological Service. Dense cross-links: eilat-travel-guide, dead-sea-guide, masada-guide, hiking-in-israel, israel-in-spring. Monetization: GYG Eilat whale shark snorkeling tour CTA + Booking.com Eilat winter-deals hotel CTA (shoulder-season pricing angle) + Booking.com Dead Sea winter resort CTA. SEO: "Israel in winter", "Israel January travel guide", "Israel February travel guide", "Eilat whale sharks winter", "Israel winter holidays". Sources: backpackisrael.com/winter-israel, goisrael.com (tourism board seasonal guides), dolphin-reef.co.il, inpa.gov.il (Hula Valley). [iter205 research] status: ready

- [P3] (seo-content, S) Shavuot in Israel: festival of weeks, all-night Torah study & dairy traditions (/shavuot-in-israel) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "shavuot|shavuoth|feast.of.weeks|pentecost" across backlog — returned EMPTY. jewish-holidays-guide.md (SHIPPED iter160) covers Passover + Sukkot + High Holidays + Purim + Hanukkah. All other major Jewish holidays now have standalone guides or are in backlog: Passover iter155, Sukkot iter160, Rosh HaShanah + Yom Kippur iter160, Purim iter160, Hanukkah = this iter205. Shavuot is the final gap completing the Jewish holiday set. Content: What Shavuot is (Festival of Weeks; Chag HaShavuot; 7 weeks after Passover; commemorates receiving Torah at Mount Sinai AND agricultural first-fruits harvest; 2-day holiday in diaspora, 1 day in Israel; 2027 dates: May 21-22; generally late May-early June annually — check Jewish calendar); Tikkun Leyl Shavuot (all-night Torah study sessions — entire night before Shavuot morning; synagogues nationwide host open study sessions; non-Jewish visitors welcome at many liberal synagogues; Jerusalem: dozens of open sessions including Reform kehillot; some in English specifically for tourists and English-speakers; distinctive custom: stay awake all night to "receive Torah" at sunrise); Western Wall sunrise service (4am+ massive gathering — tens of thousands of Jews walk to Western Wall for the sunrise Shacharit morning service; most spectacular Jewish pilgrimage gathering visible to tourists after Yom Kippur Ne'ilah; free public gathering; plan to be at Jaffa Gate by 3:30am to walk down with the crowds; atmosphere: singing in streets, families in holiday clothes, sense of sacred collective movement; security present but public welcome); Kibbutz first-fruits ceremonies (kibbutzim celebrate agricultural origins of Shavuot with first-fruits (bikkurim) processions; children in white clothes carrying baskets of first-fruit produce; secular interpretation of the holiday; some kibbutzim allow tourist observers — check specific kibbutz websites; Kibbutz Kfar Blum in Upper Galilee known for open Shavuot celebrations); Dairy food tradition (the Jewish tradition of eating only dairy on Shavuot = nationwide holiday food culture; cheesecake, blintzes/crepes with cheese, quiche, labneh, cheese bourekas, dairy-stuffed pastries; bakeries + cafés prep special Shavuot dairy menus; no need to go to religious establishments — secular restaurants and bakeries observe the tradition; recommended: Lehem Erez bakeries in TLV for Shavuot cheesecakes; Mahane Yehuda Jerusalem market for local cheese + pastry); Public holiday logistics (Shavuot = full public holiday in Israel — all government offices, many shops closed; public transport severely reduced or stopped on Shavuot day itself; plan for limited buses on the day; erev Shavuot = very busy at Western Wall; Shavuot itself from sundown to sundown the following day). HONESTY: 2027 dates listed as May 21-22 based on Jewish calendar calculation — always verify with a Jewish calendar site (hebcal.com) as dates shift annually; Western Wall sunrise crowd size can vary year to year; specific kibbutz events change annually — frame as "check kibbutz directly"; tikkun leyl sessions vary by denomination and year — link Chabad.org or local synagogue calendars rather than guaranteeing specific sessions; dairy food = cultural tradition not religious law for most secular Israelis (frame accurately). Dense cross-links: jewish-holidays-guide, western-wall-guide, israel-in-spring (late May shoulder of spring guide), kibbutz-experience-guide. Monetization: Low direct commercial intent; possible Viator "Shavuot experience Jerusalem" tour if available; internal cross-link value to jerusalem-old-city-walking-tour + western-wall-guide more valuable here than direct monetization. SEO: "Shavuot in Israel", "Shavuot Jerusalem guide", "Israel Shavuot celebrations 2027", "Western Wall Shavuot sunrise". Sources: touristisrael.com/shavuot-in-israel, chabad.org/shavuot (custom explanations), hebcal.com (date calculation), reformjudaism.org.il (open tikkun sessions). [iter205 research] status: ready

- [P3] (seo-content, S) Mount Carmel hiking guide: Nahal Me'arot caves, Elijah's Cave & forest trails (/mount-carmel-hiking-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "mount.carmel.*hik|carmel.*hik|carmel.*park|carmel.*trail|nahal.mearot|carmel.*cave" across backlog — returned EMPTY (Carmel Market TLV appeared but not Mount Carmel the mountain). hiking-in-israel.md (SHIPPED) mentions Carmel ridge briefly as part of Israel National Trail summary; haifa-travel-guide (in backlog) covers city attractions (Baha'i Gardens, Wadi Nisnas, Carmel Center neighborhoods) but NOT the nature/hiking dimension of Mount Carmel. No dedicated Mount Carmel nature/hiking guide anywhere on site. Competitors: hike-israel.com lists 8 Carmel-area trails; inpa.gov.il has full Nahal Me'arot UNESCO nature reserve info. Content: What is Mount Carmel (mountain range running northwest from Haifa to Mediterranean coast; ridge rises to 546m; covered in Mediterranean forest — pine, oak, carob, mastic; part of Israel National Trail; biblical significance as site of Elijah vs. Baal prophets contest; UNESCO World Heritage site at its northwest tip for the cave complex); Nahal Me'arot Nature Reserve and UNESCO World Heritage Site (Carmel Caves — 4 prehistoric cave sites: Tabun Cave, Skhul Cave, el-Wad Cave, Jamal Cave; 500,000+ years of human occupation evidence; Neanderthal + early Homo sapiens remains found here; UNESCO inscribed 2012 as "Sites of Human Evolution in the Mount Carmel Caves"; INPA management; INPA annual pass valid; guided tours available on site; entry: check inpa.gov.il for current fees; location: Route 4 near Zikhron Ya'akov, 30km south of Haifa); Elijah's Cave (Mearath Eliyahu; cave on the lower slope of Carmel at Haifa sea level; tradition holds that Elijah sheltered here before his Mount Carmel contest; triple-sacred: Jewish shrine + Christian chapel + Muslim wali in same cave complex; free entry; modest dress required strictly; no photography inside — note for visitors; accessible via city bus from Haifa center); Stella Maris Monastery (Carmelite Order world headquarters — the Carmelite Order takes its name from Mount Carmel; spectacular monastery at the top of the Carmel ridge above Elijah's Cave; panoramic views of Haifa port + Mediterranean sea; church open to visitors; small museum on Carmelite Order history; free; often combined with Elijah's Cave visit — 5-minute cable car OR 20-minute steep walk between them); Muhraka (Arab name for "place of burning" = Elijah's altar fire contest; site near Daliyat el-Carmel Druze village; small Carmelite monastery marks the traditional site; balcony view over entire Jezreel Valley — one of Israel's best views; clear days see from Mediterranean coast to Jordan hills; cross-link druze-villages-guide); Forest trails: Carmel Forest and National Park (Israel's largest forest park — pine forests covering the ridge; multiple INPA-managed trail access points; most popular: Beit Oren → Haifa cycling/walking path along ridge; Nahal Kelah gorge walk; Adam Bahar trail; section of Israel National Trail runs through Carmel; Carmel was devastated by 2010 fire — current forest is regrowth); Practical: best from Haifa base (all Carmel sites accessible as day trips from Haifa; car recommended for Nahal Me'arot and Muhraka; Elijah's Cave + Stella Maris accessible by bus 25/115 from Haifa Merkaz); INPA annual card covers Nahal Me'arot but NOT Elijah's Cave (free) and NOT Muhraka (monastery, free). HONESTY: no photography inside Elijah's Cave — mention prominently as visitor will be surprised; Muhraka view quality is weather-dependent; INPA card applicability — only Nahal Me'arot Nature Reserve; Nahal Me'arot guided tour content quality varies; Carmel forest is regrowth from 2010 fire — "lush forest" marketing language is slightly optimistic but recovery is genuine and the trail experience is pleasant; Stella Maris monastery dress code enforced. Dense cross-links: haifa-travel-guide, hiking-in-israel, druze-villages-guide, israel-national-trail-guide. Monetization: GYG "Mount Carmel hiking tour from Haifa" or "Druze villages + Carmel hike" day tour CTA; lower commercial intent but strong INPA card cross-sell opportunity. SEO: "Mount Carmel hiking Israel", "Nahal Me'arot caves guide", "Elijah Cave Haifa", "Carmel trails Israel", "hiking near Haifa Israel". Sources: hike-israel.com/mount-carmel (trail listings), inpa.gov.il (Nahal Me'arot UNESCO), carmel.org.il (Carmelite Order), myhaifa.co.il (local city guide). [iter205 research] status: ready

- [SHIPPED iter211 d367fb7] Israeli breakfast guide: shakshuka, labneh & aruchat boker (/israeli-breakfast-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "israeli.*breakfast|breakfast.*israel|aruchat|shakshuka.*guide" — israeli-food-cuisine-guide.md (SHIPPED, covers general food culture: falafel, hummus, knafeh, etc.); tel-aviv-food-guide.md (SHIPPED, restaurant-focused, covers dinner + lunch options); israeli-street-food-guide (in backlog — covers falafel, sabich, shawarma = classic street foods eaten at lunch/dinner). None cover the Israeli breakfast meal specifically. "Israeli breakfast" (aruchat boker, אֲרוּחַת בֹּקֶר) is internationally recognized — exported as a dining format; hotel breakfast in Israel is globally famous; strong search intent from tourists who heard about it and want to seek it out. Competitors: timeout.com/israel has a "best breakfasts in Israel" restaurant list; numerous food bloggers cover shakshuka specifically. Content: What is an Israeli breakfast (aruchat boker = morning meal; distinct from continental European or English breakfast; mezze-style spread with multiple small dishes; traditionally served at kibbutz as large communal morning meal post-work; now a restaurant format nationwide; components: Israeli salad (finely diced tomato + cucumber + olive oil + lemon + parsley; must be fresh; never use the word "Palestinian salad" — politically charged in this context), labneh (strained yogurt dip + olive oil + za'atar), hummus (plain + with olive oil), bourekas (savory pastry filled with cheese/potato/mushroom — Sephardic Jewish heritage; best from bakeries not cafés), fresh cheeses (Bulgarian white cheese/gvina levana + yellow cheese + cottage cheese varieties), tahini, olives, bread (challah or pita or gluten-free options), jams + honey, fresh orange juice; warm elements: eggs in multiple styles); Shakshuka (eggs poached in spiced tomato sauce — Libyan Jewish heritage; Israel's most famous exported breakfast dish; classic (tomatoes + peppers + onion + cumin + paprika + eggs), green shakshuka (tomatillo or herb-based variation), white shakshuka (Israeli cheese sauce variant)); Key breakfast restaurants: Benedict TLV (24/7 breakfast; original location Rothschild Blvd; multiple branches across Israel; the quintessential Israeli upscale breakfast experience), Dr. Shakshuka Jaffa (historic Libyan-Jewish restaurant in Jaffa's flea market area; owner Bino Gabso; multiple shakshuka variants; excellent for the Jaffa atmosphere), Café Meshek Barzilay (vegan Israeli breakfast pioneer; Florentin TLV; good for dairy-free version of the spread), Sarona Market Tel Aviv (food hall; morning hours; multiple Israeli breakfast stalls), Mahane Yehuda Market Jerusalem (morning market atmosphere; Azura restaurant for traditional Iraqi-Jewish breakfast including kuba soup); Hotel breakfast vs restaurant (Israel hotel breakfast = same format delivered to you; recommended: King David Hotel Jerusalem breakfast buffet; Mamilla Hotel rooftop breakfast; Isrotel chain consistently good breakfast included rates — angle for Booking.com "breakfast included" filter); Regional variations (Galilean breakfast: more Druze and Arab pastry influence including malawach from Yemenite tradition; Eilat hotels: more international buffet style, less authentic); Kosher considerations (most Israeli breakfast places are kosher dairy — no meat; some are kosher-parve; very few are non-kosher; practically: the breakfast format IS the kosher dairy meal format, so this works naturally for observant visitors). HONESTY: "Israeli breakfast" as a concept is debated — some food historians credit it to kibbutz culture, others to Yemenite and Levantine traditions; avoid stating a single origin; specific restaurant pricing changes — frame as budget tier rather than exact prices (Benedict = mid-range, Dr. Shakshuka = budget-mid); shakshuka was NOT "invented in Israel" — it has North African and Levantine antecedents; credit Libyan Jewish immigrants for popularizing it in Israel; "Israeli salad" vs "Arab salad" vs "Palestinian salad" naming controversy — call it "Israeli salad" throughout as most common name in tourist context without comment, OR briefly note the naming is contested (safer: just use "finely diced salad of tomato and cucumber" in body text). Dense cross-links: israeli-food-cuisine-guide, tel-aviv-food-guide, jaffa-travel-guide (Dr. Shakshuka), tel-aviv-neighborhoods-guide (Benedict Rothschild). Monetization: GYG "Israeli breakfast food tour Tel Aviv" or "Mahane Yehuda morning market tour" CTA + Booking.com "breakfast included" filter deep-link for Israel hotels (high conversion — breakfast is a purchase trigger for Israel accommodation). SEO: "Israeli breakfast guide", "what is an Israeli breakfast", "shakshuka Israel", "aruchat boker restaurants Tel Aviv", "best Israeli breakfast Tel Aviv Jerusalem". Sources: timeout.com/israel/restaurants/best-breakfasts-in-israel, benedict.co.il (restaurant), drshakshuka.com (Jaffa restaurant), savoringtheglobe.com (Israeli breakfast overview). [iter205 research] status: ready

- [P2] (seo-content+monetization, M) Pet-friendly Israel: traveling with dogs & cats (/pet-friendly-israel) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "pet.*friendly|dog.*friendly|cat.*israel|travel.*dog|pet.*import|traveling.*pet" across full BACKLOG — returned EMPTY. No entry anywhere in backlog or shipped guides covers pets. Tel Aviv is consistently ranked among the world's most dog-friendly cities (Hilton Dog Beach, Yarkon Park dog areas, Sarona Market dog-welcome cafés); diaspora and aliyah travelers routinely bring pets; Israel MOA pet import requirements are strict and time-sensitive (minimum 3-month prep). Competitors: zero dedicated pet-travel pages on touristisrael.com or backpackisrael.com; thepointsguy.com and dogfriendly.com have generic international content, not Israel-specific. Content: Pet import requirements into Israel (Ministry of Agriculture: microchip ISO 15444, up-to-date rabies vaccination + 30-day wait + titre test + health certificate from licensed vet; apply to MOA 30+ days before travel — NOT a last-minute process; cats + dogs under 8kg CAN travel El Al cabin; larger dogs cargo hold with El Al + Arkia; 6-month quarantine risk if docs incomplete — warn prominently); Dog-friendly areas TLV (Hilton Dog Beach on TLV promenade — fenced, off-leash, free, with water; Yarkon Park designated dog areas; many Sarona Market + Dizengoff Center cafés are pet-welcoming; Rothschild Boulevard dog-friendly café scene); Restrictions (Old City Jerusalem — pets prohibited except guide dogs; INPA national parks — most prohibit dogs; Ben Gurion Airport green space — small designated relief area near Terminal 3 arrivals; Egged buses do not permit dogs; shared taxis/sheruts — driver's discretion); Accommodation (Booking.com "pets allowed" filter in Israel — use for TLV, Haifa, Eilat hotels; many boutique TLV hotels accept pets; Airbnb pet-friendly filter); Vet access (24-hour emergency veterinary clinics: TLV area — Hachoter clinic, Refuah clinic; Jerusalem: Shaare Tzedek Vet; Eilat — limited, plan ahead); Summer heat protocols (Israel summer = dangerous for brachycephalic breeds; never leave pets in cars; early-morning / late-evening walks only in July-August; Negev + Dead Sea area contraindicated July-August for pets without serious precaution); Water access (tap water safe for dogs; bottled water for sensitive stomachs). HONESTY: import requirements change — always direct to official gov.il/moa source, not summarized; 6-month quarantine is a REAL RISK — be unambiguously clear that incomplete docs = quarantine, not a brief inconvenience; "dog-friendly" café culture in TLV is real but informal — no legal "service animal only" rule for cafés so individual owner discretion applies. Dense cross-links: tel-aviv-complete-guide, tel-aviv-beaches-guide, israel-parks-pass-calculator (Booking.com CTA for pet-friendly hotels). Monetization: Booking.com "pets allowed" hotels in TLV + Haifa + Eilat CTA (high intent — traveler has already committed to bringing pet = must book pet-friendly). SEO: "traveling to Israel with a dog", "Israel pet import requirements", "dog-friendly Tel Aviv", "Israel pet-friendly hotels", "can I bring my cat to Israel". Sources: gov.il/en/departments/moa (official pet import), el-al.com/en/flying-with-pets (airline policy), dogfriendly.com/israel (partial), hiltonbeach.co.il/en (dog beach). [iter210 research] status: ready

- [P2] (seo-content, S) Traveling to Israel with a baby or infant (/israel-with-baby) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "baby|infant|0.*18.*month|newborn|nursing.*room|formula.*israel|stroller.*jerus" across full BACKLOG — returned EMPTY. Note: israel-with-kids.md guide exists as SHIPPED content covering 2–12 age range (children's museums, water parks, interactive sites); BACKLOG contains "Israel with teenagers" item confirming teens as separate niche. 0-18 month infants present DISTINCT concerns: ETA-IL for infants (requires own biometric passport — many parents don't realize this; no infant exemption), Israeli formula brands (Materna/Similac-type products available at pharmacies + supermarkets; breastfeeding culture: generally accepted in cafés + restaurants), nursing rooms at Ben Gurion Terminal 3 (available; location: Level 3 near security), stroller logistics in Jerusalem Old City (cobblestone streets + steep steps + no ramps at many entrances = umbrella stroller essential; baby carrier/sling often better for Kotel plaza + Cardo); Israeli car seat law (mandatory for all children; infant rear-facing car seats available to rent with hire cars — pre-book); summer heat for infants (Israel July-August temperatures 30–40°C; heat stroke risk is acute for infants — serious safety warning); kosher baby food available at major supermarkets; 24-hour pediatric clinics (Terem chain nationwide — English-speaking staff; EHIC/travel insurance required for non-residents; Tel Aviv: Ichilov Pediatric ER; Jerusalem: Shaare Zedek). Content sections: Before you go (ETA-IL for infants — own passport; travel insurance with pediatric emergency coverage), Ben Gurion Airport with baby (nursing room locations; baby-change facilities; family security lane; stroller through security — fold required at X-ray; baby food/formula in carry-on: unlimited quantity for flight, but will be tested by security), Getting around Israel with baby (rental car with infant seat: pre-book — HONESTY: availability not guaranteed without advance reservation; baby carriers + slings: ideal for Jerusalem Old City, markets, Haifa Baha'i steps; taxis: child seats not required in taxis by law but recommended — bring your own or Yango/Gett app sometimes has car-seat vehicles), Jerusalem with baby (Old City: umbrella stroller minimum, carrier better; Kotel Plaza is accessible; Israel Museum has stroller-accessible route; Yad Vashem not recommended for infants — disturbing content; Mahane Yehuda market: passable with stroller but narrow + crowded + loud), Tel Aviv with baby (beach: completely stroller accessible; Yarkon Park: flat, shade, ideal; Dizengoff Center: nursing rooms; accommodation: most hotels have cots on request), Medical (Terem clinics: English-speaking, walk-in, nationwide; closest pediatric ER from Old City: Shaare Zedek; closest from TLV: Ichilov; formula: Materna Forte / Similac / Humana available at major pharmacies + Super-Pharm chain; bring car-seat adaptor for Israeli car models if using travel car seat — or rent locally). HONESTY: Israel in July/August with an infant is genuinely challenging due to heat — don't soften this; cobblestones in Old City are a REAL barrier and not all routes have ramp access — be specific about which areas are accessible; Terem clinics are excellent but not free for tourists without travel insurance — lead with "get good travel insurance"; formula availability at Super-Pharm is good but brand match to home-country formula is uncertain — bring 1-week extra supply. Dense cross-links: ben-gurion-airport-guide, jerusalem-old-city-walking-tour, israel-with-kids (SEE ALSO — older children), tel-aviv-beaches-guide. SEO: "Israel with a baby travel guide", "traveling to Israel with infant", "Jerusalem with baby stroller", "baby formula in Israel", "ETA Israel for babies". Sources: government.gov.il/en/eta-il (infant passport rules), bgu-airport.co.il (Terminal 3 facilities), terem.com (English medical), super-pharm.co.il (baby formula availability). [iter210 research] status: ready

- [P3] (seo-content, S) Israel by train: intercity rail guide (/israel-by-train) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "intercity.*rail|israel.*rail|rav.kav|rail.co.il|train.*haifa|train.*jerusalem|train.*beer.sheva" — returned reference to tel-aviv-light-rail.md (SHIPPED iter112, covers TLV metropolitan light rail only = Red/Green/Purple lines within TLV metro area). Searched "intercity.*train|national.*rail|transport.*guide" — returned transportation.md (SHIPPED, covers all transport modes briefly as overview). NO dedicated intercity Israel Railways guide anywhere. Israel's national rail network is a genuinely under-used resource by tourists: TLV→Jerusalem takes 28 minutes via Ben Gurion Airport tunnel (fastest route between cities); the coastal line connects TLV→Netanya→Haifa→Akko→Nahariya in ~90 minutes. Key content gaps: tourists often don't realize the airport train connects directly to Jerusalem; Rav-Kav card works for trains (distinct from bus-only Rav-Kav setup that many assume). Competitors: rome2rio.com and seat61.com/israel have generic rail info but no in-depth Israel-specific guide. Content: Network overview map (TLV Hub: Central, HaShalom, Savidor Center, University; Airport: Ben Gurion Terminal → direct trains to Jerusalem + TLV; Jerusalem: Yitzhak Navon station downtown + Malha station; Haifa: HaMifratz + Bat Galim + Hof HaCarmel; Beer Sheva: Be'er Sheva North station; Nahariya terminus); Key routes (TLV→Jerusalem Navon: 28 min; TLV→Ben Gurion Airport: 18 min; TLV→Haifa Merkaz: 55 min; TLV→Be'er Sheva: 70 min; TLV→Nahariya (via Netanya + Haifa): 90 min); Rav-Kav card for trains (Rav-Kav card works for Israel Railways intercity trains — same card as buses; charged as stored value; season passes available for heavy users; available at all stations + online; student discounts; note: senior discounts require Israeli ID for Israeli citizens — foreign tourists do not qualify); Cities NOT on rail network (Eilat — no rail, must fly or bus; Safed/Tzfat — no rail, bus from Akko or Tiberias; Tiberias — no rail, bus from Haifa or TLV; Masada — no rail, bus from Beer Sheva or Dead Sea junction; Nazareth — no direct rail, take bus from Haifa or Afula); Ben Gurion Airport connection (Terminal 3: underground train station, walk 10 min from arrivals; trains to Jerusalem Navon: every 30 min approx; trains to TLV Central: every 15 min approx; first train: 5:30am approx; last train: midnight approx on most nights; HONESTY: no trains on Shabbat — Friday evening to Saturday night); Booking trains (rail.co.il in English and Hebrew; online booking with credit card; no surcharge; print ticket or mobile QR; no assigned seats except business class on some routes; busy times: Sunday morning + Thursday evening + Motzei Shabbat (Saturday night) = very crowded); Accessibility (most stations fully accessible; newer Navon station Jerusalem is fully accessible + has lift to street level; bike carriage: allowed but must book bike space). HONESTY: Israel Railways is state-owned and was historically criticized for reliability; punctuality has improved significantly since 2015 electrification but delays on Hebrew Shabbat-eve (Thursday-Friday) remain common; no overnight trains in Israel; no station at Masada/Dead Sea — this confuses many tourists; Shabbat shutdown is CRITICAL and non-obvious to non-Jewish visitors. Dense cross-links: transportation.md (overview), tel-aviv-light-rail.md (TLV metro only), netanya-guide (coastal train stop), haifa-travel-guide, be'er-sheva-negev-gateway (train from TLV). Monetization: lower direct affiliate potential — no GYG/Tiqets for train tickets; instead cross-sell Booking.com accommodation CTAs at destination stations; INPA parks pass cross-sell (train = how you get to trailheads). SEO: "Israel train guide", "Israel Railways tourist", "Ben Gurion airport train Jerusalem", "Rav Kav train Israel", "intercity trains Israel". Sources: rail.co.il/en (official English), seat61.com/israel.htm (independent overview), rome2rio.com/israel-train. [iter210 research] status: ready

Competitor coverage: lonelyplanet.com Jerusalem chapter, timeout.com/israel Jerusalem, frommers.com/destinations/jerusalem/neighborhoods all cover East Jerusalem. Content: Damascus Gate and souk (main entrance from West Jerusalem; Arab market corridor; practical logistics); Salah al-Din Street (Palestinian commercial hub; shops, cafés, Arab-owned restaurants); Zedekiah's Cave / Solomon's Quarries (massive underground cave 80m below Damascus Gate; free entry; remarkable acoustic + archaeological space; often missed by tourists); Rockefeller Archaeological Museum (1930s British Mandate building; world-class Levant archaeology; often free on Shabbat); Garden Tomb (Protestant alternative to Holy Sepulchre — Gordon's Calvary; quiet Anglican gardens; free entry; most-visited Protestant pilgrimage site in Jerusalem); American Colony Hotel (1902 Ottoman mansion, historic neutral-ground hotel for journalists and diplomats, beautiful courtyard, afternoon tea, worth visiting for architecture even non-guests). HONESTY: East Jerusalem political status complex — frame as practical visitor guidance; Israeli law governs (Basic Law 1980); international community largely does not recognize annexation; tourist areas generally safe; exercise normal urban awareness; no political advocacy. - [P2] (seo-content+monetization, S) Beit Guvrin / Maresha UNESCO caves visitor guide (/beit-guvrin-caves-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "/beit-guvrin", "beit.guvrin.caves.guide", "beit.guvrin.*\[P" — only matches are (1) Beit Guvrin mentioned in hidden-gems hub cluster as one of several items, (2) Beit Guvrin mentioned in archaeology dig entry; no standalone guide. Bein Harim has dedicated Beit Guvrin tour page; touristisrael.com/beit-guvrin; UNESCO World Heritage Site 2014. Content: UNESCO context (Caves of Maresha and Bet Guvrin in Judean Lowlands — 2014 WHS; 3,500 underground chambers spanning multiple civilisations); Bell Caves (unique bell-shaped chambers 10–20m high, Ottoman chalk quarrying, 800 bell caves, deeply photogenic — the Instagram draw); Sidonian Burial Caves (Hellenistic Greek era; rare surviving painted murals — hunting scenes, peacocks, musicians; exceptional historical significance — access via separate ticket, 15 min walk); Roman amphitheater remains; columbaria (ancient dove-cotes, Roman period); olive presses; practical visit (Route 35, ~60 min from Jerusalem / ~90 min from TLV; INPA National Parks Pass valid; 1.5–2h visit; hours Sun–Thu+Sat 8am–5pm, Fri 8am–4pm); summer advantage (cave interiors naturally cool — heat escape angle); combining with Maresha painted tombs (short walk, separate ticket). HONESTY: cave interiors dark — bring torch/phone light; Sidonian tombs access — verify current INPA booking requirements before writing; admission range ₪29/₪15 (verify before publishing — INPA prices change). Monetization: GYG Beit Guvrin day tour from Jerusalem/TLV + INPA National Parks Pass affiliate. Cross-links: jerusalem-day-trips, masada-dead-sea-day-trip, hidden-gems. - [P2] (seo-content, M) Temple Mount / Haram al-Sharif visitor guide (/temple-mount-visitor-guide) — The most-asked Jerusalem visitor question is "Can non-Muslims visit the Temple Mount / Dome of the Rock?" church-holy-sepulchre-guide.md and jerusalem-old-city-walking-tour.md mention it in passing; no comprehensive standalone guide exists anywhere on site. PAIRED NAMING REQUIRED throughout: "Temple Mount / Haram al-Sharif". Access facts: non-Muslim tourists enter only via Mughrabi Gate (adjacent to Western Wall plaza); access is to the open-air PLAZA ONLY — mosque and Dome of the Rock interiors are NEVER accessible to non-Muslims; hours Mon–Thu + Sat approx 7:30–11am and 1:30–2:30pm (times change without notice, may close for Islamic holidays, Ramadan, Fridays, and security events — NEVER promise specific-day access); strict dress code (full cover including hair for women, no bare arms or legs for anyone); no prayer or religious gestures by non-Muslims (legally enforced by WAQF + Israeli police); no bags with religious items; photography of exterior OK but restrictions near prayer areas. CRITICAL HONESTY framing: frame the visit as an exterior view of an active sacred Islamic site — not a tourist attraction in the conventional sense; access closures happen without advance warning; mosque interiors = zero access for non-Muslims (no exceptions, no special permits); the "sacred to three faiths" framing is theologically accurate but must not imply equal non-Muslim access — be precise. Cross-links: jerusalem-old-city-walking-tour, western-wall-tunnels-guide, church-holy-sepulchre-guide, jewish-heritage-israel, christian-pilgrimage-holy-land, muslim-travel-israel (backlog). SEO: "visiting Temple Mount non-Muslim 2026", "Dome of the Rock visitor guide tourists", "Temple Mount access hours", "Haram al-Sharif non-Muslim access". Sources: tripadvisor.com (Temple Mount Q&A thread), timeout.com/israel/jerusalem, lonelyplanet.com/israel/jerusalem, roughguides.com/destinations/middle-east/israel/jerusalem. [iter220 research] status: ready

- [P2] (seo-content+monetization, S) Ashdod cruise port excursions: day trips to Jerusalem, Dead Sea & Tel Aviv (/ashdod-cruise-port-excursions) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "/ashdod-cruise", "ashdod.*cruise", "ashdod.*port.*excursion", "ashdod.*port.*day.*trip" across full backlog — EMPTY. cruise-shore-excursions-israel.md (SHIPPED iter32) covers both Haifa and Ashdod ports generically (~300 words each port); Ashdod-specific standalone guide is distinctly valuable: (1) Jerusalem is only 1h from Ashdod vs Haifa's 2h — distinct excursion pattern; (2) Viator has 15+ Ashdod-specific products; (3) "Ashdod cruise excursions" is high commercial-intent with clear affiliate opportunity. Ashdod = Israel's primary cruise port (Western Mediterranean routes; larger ships). Content: Port logistics (Ashdod Port pier to city ~5 min taxi; typical dock 7am–6pm; ship-excursion vs independent options); Jerusalem day trip (1h by coach; Mughrabi Gate + Western Wall + Old City; self-guided Route 405 bus OR organized tour; 3h minimum before return); Dead Sea trip (1.5h from port; Ein Bokek beach free; combined Jerusalem + Dead Sea ~8-9h tour common); Tel Aviv visit (30 min from port; Jaffa + Carmel Market + beach; very feasible independently); Bethlehem option (adds 45min; Palestinian Authority — passport required; authorized guide needed — do NOT suggest independent walk-across); Practical: taxi to Jerusalem ~₪250 one-way; Gett/Yango may not work at port; strict return-time buffer (2h minimum for port re-entry). HONESTY: Dead Sea + Jerusalem in one day is a rush — frame honestly; Bethlehem = Palestinian Authority territory requiring licensed guide; Haifa cruise port separately covered in cruise-shore-excursions-israel.md. Monetization: GYG "Jerusalem from Ashdod cruise port" CTA (highest-converting cruise excursion term); Viator Ashdod excursion; Booking.com TLV hotel (for passengers extending stay). - [SHIPPED iter226] /1-day-tel-aviv-itinerary (duplicate iter225 entry) → 9b5fd01 "1.day.*tel.aviv|one.day.*tel.aviv|tel.aviv.*1.day|tel.aviv.*itinerary", "/1-day-tel-aviv" across full backlog — EMPTY. 1-day-jerusalem-itinerary.md (SHIPPED) is a proven format; TLV equivalent is entirely missing. Target audiences: (1) Ashdod cruise passengers with 6–8h in TLV; (2) Transit visitors with long Ben Gurion layover; (3) Day-trippers from Jerusalem (45 min by train); (4) Travelers on 2–3 day Israel trip. Format (mirror 1-day Jerusalem): timed morning/midday/afternoon/evening blocks with transport notes + optional detours. TLV 1-day structure: Morning (Jaffa — Clock Tower + Andromeda's Rock + flea market; arrive before 9am); Late morning (promenade walk/e-scooter from Jaffa Port to Gordon Beach; Neve Tzedek detour); Lunch (Carmel Market area or Sarona Market for shade); Afternoon (White City Bauhaus walk — Rothschild Boulevard + Ben Gurion House; Dizengoff Square); Late afternoon (Gordon or Hilton Beach; optional Museum of Art); Evening (Florentin street art + dinner; rooftop sunset bar). Practical: Gordon Beach area as base; Lime/Bird e-scooter for promenade; last train to Jerusalem midnight (day-trip buffer). HONESTY: Carmel Market closed Shabbat + public holidays; Jaffa flea market open Sun–Fri only; e-scooter rules may change; itinerary is a recommendation not a fixed "must-do." Monetization: GYG "Tel Aviv walking tour" or "Jaffa free tour" CTA + Booking.com TLV hotel CTA. Cross-links: jaffa-travel-guide, tel-aviv-carmel-market, tel-aviv-white-city, tel-aviv-neighborhoods-guide, ashdod-cruise-port-excursions. Israel has a globally distinguished music scene: Israel Philharmonic Orchestra ranks among top 10 globally (conductor Lahav Shani); Idan Raichel, Subliminal, Mashina, Balkan Beat Box internationally known; strong festival circuit. Competitors: timeout.com/israel/music, tlvcity.com/guides/music, citiesatsunrise.com/israel all rank for live music queries with no competition from our site. HONESTY: Festival dates change annually — link official sites; IPO tickets can sell out months ahead — book via ipo.co.il; Meteor Festival is 18+ electronic/trance culture; Red Sea Jazz lineup quality varies. - [SHIPPED iter232] Ein Gedi Nature Reserve complete visitor guide (/ein-gedi-guide) → 9afd319 — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "ein.gedi" across full backlog — ZERO standalone guide entry. water-hiking-israel.md (SHIPPED iter67) covers Ein Gedi's Wadi David + Nahal Arugot in ~200 words as two of six water-hike sites; no standalone destination guide exists. Competitors with full guides: deadsea.com/ein-gedi, hike-israel.com/ein-gedi, touristisrael.com/ein-gedi, takeyourbackpack.com/ein-gedi, israel-in-photos.com/ein-gedi — all rank for "Ein Gedi guide" with full visitor planning content. INPA manages the site: inpa.gov.il/content_page/ein-gedi. Ein Gedi is Israel's most-visited nature reserve (Dead Sea region, ~1.5h from both Jerusalem and TLV); visited by nearly all Israel trip itineraries doing a Dead Sea day. SEO gap: "Ein Gedi visitor guide", "Ein Gedi trails", "Ein Gedi Dead Sea trip", "Ein Gedi spring pools". Content: two distinct areas of the reserve — (1) Ein Gedi Nature Reserve (INPA-managed; Wadi David trail to David's Waterfall + Ein Gedi Spring upper pools, 3–5km loop; Nahal Arugot trail to Hidden Waterfall, 4km; entrance fee ~₪28 adult INPA; NP pass valid; hours 8am–4pm winter, extended summer; closes earlier on holidays); (2) Ein Gedi Field School + Kibbutz (eco-lodge and campground adjacent to reserve; Ein Gedi Spa on Dead Sea shore — a separate paid facility with pool and free beach access; dates from kibbutz agriculture); getting there (bus 444/487 Egged from Jerusalem Central Bus Station ~1.5h; car from Jerusalem 1h via Route 1 to Dead Sea junction; no Uber/Gett at the reserve — arrange return in advance or trust the bus schedule); combining with Dead Sea (Ein Gedi to Ein Bokek beach: 20 min south by car or ~40 min bus; Dead Sea float is natural addition); 2026 CURRENT STATUS: Wadi David accessible to first waterfall on the paved accessible path only — May 2026 flooding damaged upper trail; Nahal Arugot fully open as of June 2026 (verify via INPA before visiting); plants and animals: ibex graze freely around visitor facilities (do not approach or feed; they are wild animals); Rock Hyrax commonly seen sunbathing on boulders; Desert Kingfisher, Little Owls; leopards live in the reserve (rarely seen; camera traps confirm resident population — a genuine highlight mention). HONESTY: summer heat in Ein Gedi is extreme (38–42°C Jul-Aug; hike before 8am only in summer; carry 3L+ water; INPA sometimes closes trails above 39°C by 9am); 2026 flood damage makes Wadi David upper section inaccessible — must frame as "check INPA website before visiting" (not as permanently damaged); "waterfall swimming" in Wadi David pool is possible at lower sections only; no fabricated "best season" rankings — spring (Mar–May) and autumn (Oct–Nov) are generally safer temperature windows. Monetization: GYG/Abraham Tours "Dead Sea + Ein Gedi day trip from Jerusalem" or "from Tel Aviv" CTA (high-volume affiliate product — both Viator and GYG list it). Cross-links: dead-sea-guide, water-hiking-israel, masada-dead-sea-day-trip, car-rental-israel (car recommended for flexibility), national-parks-pass. SEO: "Ein Gedi guide 2026", "Ein Gedi visitor information", "Ein Gedi trails hike", "Ein Gedi Dead Sea day trip", "Ein Gedi ibex". Sources: inpa.gov.il/content_page/ein-gedi, deadsea.com/ein-gedi, hike-israel.com/ein-gedi, touristisrael.com/ein-gedi, takeyourbackpack.com/ein-gedi. [iter230 research] status: ready

- [P2] (seo-content, M) Israel for Gulf & Abraham Accords travelers: UAE, Bahrain & Morocco visitors guide (/israel-for-gulf-travelers) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "gulf.*travel", "gulf.*visitor", "abraham.*accords.*guide", "uae.*israel.*guide" across full backlog — ZERO standalone guide. first-time-in-israel.md (SHIPPED) is generic; no guide targeting Gulf/Arab-world visitors navigating the specific practical + cultural context of visiting Israel post-normalization. The Abraham Accords (Sept 2020: UAE, Bahrain; Dec 2020: Morocco, Sudan) created a new inbound tourist segment: Emirati, Bahraini, and Moroccan visitors to Israel. Ministry of Tourism has run dedicated UAE/Bahrain outreach campaigns. Competitors: Business Traveller magazine (UAE-Israel tourism feature 2024), Abraham Accords Peace Institute (editorial on tourism growth), Times of Israel (dozens of UAE visitor articles), and goisrael.com/en/articles/uae all cover the topic, but no competitor has a COMPREHENSIVE standalone editorial visitor guide specifically targeting Gulf travelers. Gap is unique to our site: we have content for Christian pilgrims, solo female travelers, seniors, LGBTQ travelers, and families — but zero content for this new and growing Arab-world visitor segment. SEO: "Israel travel guide UAE visitors", "Bahrain to Israel travel", "Gulf traveler Israel guide 2026", "UAE citizens visiting Israel", "Tel Aviv from Dubai guide". Content: Visa + ETA-IL context (UAE, Bahrain, and Morocco passport holders can apply for ETA-IL online — link israel-eta-guide; no in-person embassy required; normal stamp policy; clarify that Palestinian Authority territories are separate and not visited via regular tourist entry); Flights (direct El Al TLV-Dubai (DBX), TLV-Abu Dhabi (AUH), TLV-Casablanca (CMN); FlyDubai, Etihad, Royal Air Maroc all fly direct in 2026 — check current schedules; ~3-4h from UAE); Language + customs context (Israeli Arabic is widely spoken — Arab-Israeli quarter in every major city; Arabic-speaking taxi drivers common; Arabic signage at many sites; Israeli culture = warm + informal; handshake culture OK; haggling expected in markets); Food + halal context (halal certification in Israel is complex — Arab-Israeli restaurants are typically halal by community practice but may not carry official halal certificates; kosher dairy restrictions apply in Jewish restaurants; best bet: Arab-Israeli neighborhoods like Wadi Nisnas Haifa, Nazareth, Jaffa Arab quarter, East Jerusalem for reliable halal options; McDonald's, Burger King have halal Israel branches confirmed; hummus culture shared; mansaf, knafeh, Arabic coffee all widely available; date palms = Israeli agricultural export = familiar food); Prayer context (mosques: Al-Aqsa Compound is accessible to Muslim visitors on designated visitor hours for non-prayer visiting — PAIRED NAMING "Temple Mount / Haram al-Sharif"; Al-Bahr Mosque Jaffa (oldest mosque in Israel, active); Hassan Bek Mosque TLV (landmark, active); Friday Jumu'ah prayers maintained at all active mosques; prayer apps like Muslim Pro work in Israel; qibla direction same in Jerusalem as in Gulf cities); Top attractions for Gulf visitors (Al-Aqsa / Haram al-Sharif Old City Jerusalem — significant Muslim pilgrimage site; Dome of the Rock; Abraham's Mosque Hebron — NOTE: Hebron is specifically excluded per project rules — do NOT include); Shopping + experience (TLV's beaches and nightlife appeal to Gulf leisure travelers; Tel Aviv duty-free electronics; Carmel Market; Sarona Market; Eilat Red Sea diving); Driving + transport (car rental easy; UAE license accepted with IDP; Israeli roads = right-hand drive, same as UAE); Currency (₪ new shekel; ATMs everywhere; credit cards universal; NFC pay widely accepted — Apple Pay works in Israel); Golf (Caesarea Golf Club — 18-hole, one of few in the region); Practical cultural note (Israelis are aware of the Abraham Accords context; Arab-speaking Israelis = warm welcome; political discussions can arise — traveler's choice whether to engage); Gulf-to-Israel travel packages (Israel MOT working with UAE tour operators; Abercrombie & Kent, Cox & Kings offer UAE-Israel tours 2026). HONESTY: halal certification in Israel is genuinely inconsistent — do NOT claim food is halal without the traveler verifying directly; Al-Aqsa access for Muslim non-prayer visiting is permitted in general but hours and access change with security conditions (DO NOT guarantee access); the political context is acknowledged but this is a practical guide — frame diplomatically without taking political positions; Western Wall / Al-Aqsa are physically adjacent — describe geography factually; Hebron is excluded per project content policy. Monetization: GYG Jerusalem Muslim quarter guided tour CTA + Booking.com TLV Bauhaus hotel CTA (target luxury segment). Cross-links: israel-eta-guide, kosher-food-guide (contrast section), holy-sites-dress-code-etiquette, first-time-in-israel, jerusalem-old-city-walking-tour, akko-acre-guide, arab-israeli-culture-guide (when built). SEO: "Israel travel guide for UAE visitors 2026", "Emirati visiting Israel", "Gulf Arab tourism Israel", "Israel from Dubai travel guide", "halal food in Israel guide". Sources: businesstraveller.com/uae-israel-tourism, abrahamaccords.com/tourism, timesofisrael.com/uae-visitors-israel, goisrael.com/en/articles/gulf-visitors, visit.io/israel-uae-tourism-report-2024. [iter230 research] status: ready

- [P2] (seo-content, M) Israel Christian monasteries & convents circuit guide (/israel-monasteries-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "monasteri", "convent.*circuit" across full backlog — only match was "multiple denomination hospices + monasteries" as a passing mention in the Jerusalem neighborhoods entry (not a standalone guide). christian-pilgrimage-holy-land.md (SHIPPED iter94) covers New Testament narrative sites (Bethlehem, Nazareth, Sea of Galilee, Jerusalem entry points); galilee-christian-sites-circuit.md (SHIPPED iter221) covers Galilee-specific sites (Nazareth, Tabgha, Capernaum, Mount of Beatitudes, Cana). Neither covers the distinct circuit of living MONASTIC communities in the Judean Wilderness and surrounding areas — contemplative monasteries accessible to visitors but not primarily New Testament pilgrimage sites. Competitors: secret-israel.com/monasteries, viator.com (Monastery circuit tours), toursbylocals.com/Israel/monasteries, holylandtourstravel.com/judaean-desert-monasteries, danny-the-digger.com/wadi-qelt-monastery all rank for monastery circuit searches with no equivalent on our site. SEO: "Judean Desert monasteries", "St George monastery Israel", "Mar Saba monastery guide", "Israel monastery circuit", "Latrun monastery Israel". Four core monasteries: (1) St George's Monastery / Deir Mar Jirjis (Wadi Qelt, Greek Orthodox; suspended in cliff face of Wadi Qelt gorge; 5th century CE; inhabited by Greek Orthodox monks today — typically 3–5 resident monks; open to visitors Sun–Fri ~8am–1pm, 3–5pm — no entry Sat; modest dress essential (women must wear a skirt or long wrap provided at entrance); accessible from the western end of Wadi Qelt via the Ein Prat Nature Reserve (INPA) trail — a 3–5km walk into the gorge OR 15-min drive on sealed road to the top-side viewpoint; AREA A/B/C note: Wadi Qelt approaches from the Jericho-side are in Area C (Israeli civil + military) while the trailhead at Almon junction is in Israeli control — most tours approach from the Jerusalem side; no Palestinian Authority area crossing required); (2) Mar Saba Monastery (Great Lavra; Eastern Judean Desert, 12km from Bethlehem; Greek Orthodox; founded 483 CE by St Sabas; one of the oldest inhabited monasteries in the world and largest monastery in the Judean Wilderness; dramatically situated in the Kidron Valley gorge; CRITICAL ACCESS NOTE: Women are NOT permitted inside the monastery — the monastery has maintained this rule for 1,500 years; women can view from the Women's Tower across the gorge — a dedicated stone tower was built historically for women pilgrims; men may enter with modest dress + 45-min guided explanation + ₪10 donation requested; car required — no public transport; road from Bethlehem crosses Area A (Palestinian Authority territory); independently navigating Bethlehem area = passport + awareness; many tours approach via authorized licensed guide from Jerusalem; monastery open Sun–Fri approx 9am–3pm, closed noon); (3) Latrun Trappist Monastery / Notre-Dame de Sept Douleurs (Latrun, between Jerusalem and TLV via Route 1; Cistercian Trappists; founded 1890; Crusader fortress (Castrum Emmaus / Le Toron des Chevaliers) on monastery grounds; monks produce Latrun wine (Chardonnay, Cabernet, Merlot) and olive oil at on-site winery shop; free to visit winery shop Mon–Sat 8am–5:30pm, Sun 9am–noon; wine bottle prices ₪50–120 range; monastery church open to visitors; no internal guided tours but self-guided grounds; easy stop between Jerusalem and TLV; accessible by car — Exit 293 off Route 1; mini-Israel park is adjacent — family trip combo option); (4) Abu Gosh Benedictine Monastery / Dormition Monastery at Abu Gosh (Abu Gosh village, 10km west of Jerusalem on Route 1; Benedictine monks from Beuron Germany; 10th-century Crusader church built over a Byzantine church; remarkable intact Crusader capitals + frescoes; free entry; Gregorian chant sung at specific service times — check current schedule); Additional monastery options (Stella Maris Carmelite Monastery (Haifa, Carmel Ridge; founded 12th century; Elijah's Cave beneath; panoramic Haifa views; free entry; covered in haifa-travel-guide but worth cross-linking); Monastery of the Cross, Jerusalem (Greek Orthodox; Jerusalem valley between Israel Museum and city center; named as site where the wood for the cross was grown per tradition; free to enter Mon–Sat; mosaic floor, 11th-century structure); Ein Kerem Franciscan sites (two Franciscan churches in the Ein Kerem village of west Jerusalem — Church of the Visitation + Church of St John the Baptist; pilgrimage + local residential neighborhood; accessible by bus from Jerusalem)). Practical circuit planning (full circuit requires car; Mar Saba = half-day from Jerusalem; St George + Latrun can be combined in one day from Jerusalem; Abu Gosh is 15 min from Jerusalem — easy add-on to day in TLV or Dead Sea circuit; spring and autumn are ideal — Judean Desert heat in summer extreme). HONESTY: Mar Saba women access policy is a 1,500-year-old rule observed strictly by the monks — do NOT suggest exceptions; do NOT fabricate opening hours (direct travelers to call or check monastery websites; hours change for fasts, church feast days, and construction); Area C / Palestinian Authority territory context must be accurate — frame honestly that some approach routes go through PA-administered areas (Area A/B) and solo independent access requires awareness; Mar Saba access is typically via guide from Jerusalem or via the Bethlehem side (Area A) requiring a licensed Palestinian guide; frame clearly. Monetization: GYG "Judean Desert monasteries day tour from Jerusalem" CTA (Viator lists this product) + Booking.com Jerusalem hotel for overnight circuit base. Cross-links: christian-pilgrimage-holy-land, galilee-christian-sites-circuit, jerusalem-old-city-walking-tour, hiking-in-israel (Wadi Qelt section), water-hiking-israel (Wadi Qelt gorge walk). - [P3] (seo-content, S) Akko (Acre) food & restaurant guide (/akko-food-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "akko.*food", "acre.*food" across full backlog — ZERO standalone food guide entry (matches found are: akko-acre-guide in SHIPPED metadata and as a cross-link destination in haifa-food-guide + arab-israeli-culture-guide entries). akko-acre-guide.md (SHIPPED iter72) is a destination guide (history, Crusader halls, Templar tunnel, what to see) with minimal food depth; this is a distinct food-focused companion, parallel to jaffa-food-guide.md (SHIPPED iter216) and mahane-yehuda-market-guide.md (SHIPPED iter217). Akko (ancient Acre) has one of Israel's most distinctive food scenes — Arab-Israeli seafood + Levantine home cooking in an atmospheric Old City. James Beard-endorsed chef Uri Jeremias built his reputation there. Competitors: timeout.com/israel/akko/restaurants, lonelyplanet.com/israel/northern-israel/akko (food section), Danny the Digger's Akko food guide, and various foodie blogs all rank but no major competitor has a dedicated standalone Akko food guide page. SEO: "Akko restaurants guide", "Acre Israel food", "where to eat in Akko Israel", "best seafood Akko". Content: Uri Buri (ha-Ha'Ahlai 11, Port; James Beard-recognized chef Uri Jeremias; Arab-Israeli seafood; creative preparations of locally caught fish + shrimp; lunch and dinner; reservations essential; ₪150–250 mains — frame as range not exact; one of Israel's most celebrated restaurants — widely attested in Israeli food media + international press); Al-Marsa (ha-Ha'Ahlai 17, Port; Uri Jeremias's more casual sibling restaurant; grilled fish + mezze; lower price point than Uri Buri; reservations recommended); Al-Fanar (al-Zeituna Square; atmospheric Old City courtyard; traditional Palestinian/Arab dishes; mezze, ful, knafeh; indoor + outdoor seating); the Old City souk / Khan el-Umdan (Ottoman caravanserai; fresh produce + spices + pickles; cardamom-heavy Arabic coffee from roasters; sesame-ring ka'ak pastry; knafeh sellers on main lane; shopping for spice blends unique to Akko); hummus at Hummus Sa'id (near Clock Tower; one of the best-known hummus shops in the north; open mornings only, closes when sold out — arrive before 10am; serves hummus with whole chickpeas + olive oil + ful; cash only); sweets (knafeh + baklava from confectioneries in the souk; Lebanese influence visible in sweet varieties); where to eat by time of day (morning: Hummus Sa'id or souk bakeries; lunch: Al-Marsa for fish; dinner: Uri Buri or Al-Fanar); practical tips (Old City walking is required — no cars inside; Akko fish market adjacent to harbour Fri mornings; all Old City restaurants close or reduce on Jewish public holidays — call ahead; Shabbat: Arab-Christian-owned restaurants typically open; Uri Buri is closed on Jewish holidays; most restaurants kosher or non-kosher — check individually); combining Akko food with sightseeing (morning: Hummus Sa'id + souk + Templar tunnel + Crusader halls; lunch: Al-Marsa; afternoon: boat tour of harbour + Pisan harbour walls; evening: Uri Buri dinner + overnight option at an Akko boutique hotel). HONESTY: specific restaurant recommendation qualification — "consistently cited in Israeli food press as of 2026 research; verify current operation and hours before visiting as restaurants change"; Uri Buri prices = estimate range, book via official website for accurate pricing; "James Beard recognition" is factual and widely reported but Uri Jeremias has not won the James Beard Award — frame as "James Beard-noted" or "recognized by the James Beard community" (verify exact recognition level before publishing); no fabricated ratings or review counts. Monetization: Booking.com Akko hotel CTA + GYG "Akko Old City tour with food" CTA (verify GYG product availability). Cross-links: akko-acre-guide, haifa-food-guide (when built), arab-israeli-culture-guide (when built), day-trips-from-haifa, haifa-travel-guide. SEO: "Akko food guide restaurants", "Acre Israel where to eat", "Uri Buri restaurant Akko guide", "best seafood restaurant Israel Akko", "Akko Old City food". Sources: timeout.com/israel/akko, lonelyplanet.com/israel/northern-israel/akko (dining section), danny-the-digger.com/akko-food, haaretz.co.il (Uri Buri features), uriburi.co.il. - [P3] (seo-content, S) Ramla & Lod: ancient cities near Tel Aviv guide (/ramla-lod-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "ramla", "Ramla", "/ramla", "/lod-guide", "/lod-ramla", "ramla-lod" across full backlog — ZERO matches. Two Arab-Israeli cities in central Israel (30–40 min from TLV; 15–20 min from Ben Gurion Airport) that are historically significant but almost completely overlooked by the tourist circuit. No competitor has a dedicated guide for this pair: touristisrael.com has a one-paragraph Ramla section in a Central Israel overview; Danny the Digger mentions Ramla briefly; lonelyplanet.com has a 2-page spread but it's rarely visited content. Our site has zero coverage. Audience: historically curious travelers, Christian pilgrims (Lod = biblical Lydda / Acts narrative), Jewish heritage travelers, day-trip-from-TLV seekers. SEO: "Ramla Israel guide", "Lod ancient city Israel", "day trips near Ben Gurion airport", "White Tower Ramla Israel". Ramla section: Ramla (Arabic: الرملة; Hebrew: רמלה) is the only city in the Holy Land founded by Muslim conquerors — established 716 CE by Umayyad Caliph Suleiman ibn Abd al-Malik as capital of Bilad al-Sham's southern province; unique historical significance as it was capital of the region for 500 years until Crusader conquest; key sites: (1) White Tower / Tower of the Forty (Burj al-Arba'in; Mamluk-era minaret built 1318, adjacent to an earlier Abbasid mosque now ruined; accessible to visitors; climb to panoramic view of the city and surrounding plain; HONESTY: the "Tower of the Forty" name refers to 40 Muslim martyrs per tradition — note respectfully); (2) Great Mosque of Ramla (interior is former Crusader Cathedral of St John the Baptist, itself built over an earlier Abbasid-era mosque; the mosque still functions as an active place of worship; exterior viewable, interior access for Muslim worshippers during prayer times + potentially for respectful visitors outside prayer — inquire at entrance; beautiful pointed Gothic arches surviving from Crusader construction); (3) Pool of St Helena / Birket al-Anaziyya (underground cistern from Abbasid period, 8th century; accessed by small rowboat — the boatman poles a flat-bottomed boat through the arched underground halls; one of the few places in Israel you can take a genuine underground boat ride; entrance fee ~₪15; open daily; one of the true hidden gems of the region; HONESTY: verify current hours and access at Ramla Tourism office — pool access can be seasonal); (4) Ramla Hospitality Center + Local Market (Ramla's market is a working-class mixed Arab-Jewish bazaar; fresh produce, fabric, cheap restaurants — Ramla has an excellent local food scene little known to tourists; ful + hummus in the market area). Lod section: Lod (Lydda; biblical city of Diospolis; Acts 9:32–35 records St Peter healing a paralysed man there — significant for Christian pilgrims; Crusaders knew it as Saint George's town): (1) Church of St George (one of the original George-and-the-Dragon connection sites; a Greek Orthodox church stands on the reputed site of St George's burial place — the original church was Crusader-period; modern structure rebuilt multiple times; the dedicated tomb below = pilgrimage site for Eastern Christians, specifically important for Greek Orthodox and Roman Catholic pilgrims; modest dress; donations appreciated; active Orthodox parish); (2) Al-Omari Mosque (former Crusader Cathedral of St George, same site as the church — the mosque + church are built in immediate adjacency sharing the compound; the two communities maintain their adjacent spaces; a rare co-existence site visible to tourists; PAIRED NAMING where applicable: Church of St George / Al-Omari Mosque); (3) Lod Mosaic (discovered 1996 during road construction; extraordinary Roman mosaic floor (3rd century CE) depicting animal and fish hunting scenes across 180m²; displayed in a purpose-built museum called the Shelby White and Leon Levy Lod Mosaic Archaeological Center; notable: this mosaic toured the Metropolitan Museum of Art, Louvre, and Israel Museum before being installed here; open Sun–Fri; ~₪30 admission; genuinely world-class archaeological exhibit). Practical combining Ramla + Lod: 3km apart by road or 10-min bus (Route 431); full circuit takes 3–4 hours; best base is Lod Mosaic → Lod St George Church → drive to Ramla Pool of St Helena → White Tower → Great Mosque → market lunch; car recommended (Ramla bus from Tel Aviv Central Station; Lod is on the national rail line — Lod Station is on Jerusalem-TLV intercity rail route); accessible as a day trip from Jerusalem (50 min) or TLV (25 min). HONESTY: Ramla and Lod are Arab-Jewish mixed cities with some difficult history (1948 displacement of much of their Arab population) — frame historical context accurately; do NOT fabricate "picturesque medieval quarter" if urban renewal has altered the character — describe what exists factually; Pool of St Helena boat ride is a genuinely distinctive experience but hours/access can be inconsistent — always qualify; White Tower opening hours from municipal tourism pages, verify. Monetization: Lower direct affiliate opportunity; Booking.com Jerusalem/TLV hotel CTA for overnight base; potential GYG "Hidden Israel: Ramla & Lod historical tour" if operator exists — verify. Cross-links: jaffa-travel-guide (comparative Arab-Israeli urban history angle), akko-acre-guide (Crusader sites comparison), day-trips-from-tel-aviv, christian-pilgrimage-holy-land (Lod/Lydda Acts reference). SEO: "Ramla Israel travel guide", "Lod Mosaic museum Israel", "White Tower Ramla", "St George Church Lod Israel", "day trips near Ben Gurion airport", "hidden gems near Tel Aviv Israel". Sources: touristisrael.com/central-israel (Ramla section), danny-the-digger.com/ramla, lonelyplanet.com/israel/central-israel/ramla, lodmosaic.org, israel-in-photos.com/ramla. [iter230 research] status: ready

- [P3] (seo-content, M) India to Israel travel guide: flights, visa & tips for Indian visitors (/india-to-israel-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "india", "indian" across full backlog — ZERO matches. israel-eta-guide.md (SHIPPED iter222) covers ETA-IL generically; first-time-in-israel.md (SHIPPED) is general-market. India is an emerging Israel tourism market with genuine infrastructure now in place: El Al launched Tel Aviv–Mumbai (BOM) direct route in 2024 (3x/week, ~9h); Air India is evaluating a Delhi–Tel Aviv route; Israel MOT ran "Choose Israel" campaigns in India in 2023–2024. Indian visitors to Israel are primarily: (a) Jewish diaspora (Bene Israel community ~50k in India; Cochin/Malabar Jews; Baghdadi Jews; ancestral community visiting for identity tourism); (b) Christian pilgrims (large Indian Christian community including Syrian Orthodox, Malankara Catholic; Israel's New Testament sites appeal directly); (c) General leisure tourists (Dead Sea, TLV beach + nightlife; Petra extension via Jordan). Competitors: touristisrael.com has no India-specific guide; incredibleindia.org has an Israel feature; Indian travel blogs (traveltriangle.com/israel, holidify.com/places/israel, thrillophilia.com/tours/israel) have content but no comprehensive Israel MOT-backed editorial guide for Indian visitors. SEO: "Israel travel guide for Indians 2026", "India to Israel trip", "Israel visa for Indian passport", "Tel Aviv from Mumbai guide". Content: ETA-IL for Indian passport holders (Indian citizens require ETA-IL since Jan 2025 — same as all non-exempt countries; apply online at eta.gov.il, ~₪20 fee, usually approved within 24–72h; link israel-eta-guide for full step-by-step; no prior ETA-IL = boarding denial — must obtain before travel); Direct flights (El Al TLV-BOM currently 3x/week; Air India possible future route; connecting alternatives via Abu Dhabi (Etihad), Dubai (Emirates/FlyDubai), Istanbul (Turkish Airlines), Paris (Air France), Frankfurt (Lufthansa)); Vegetarian food (Israel is one of the most vegan/vegetarian-friendly countries in the world — over 400 vegan restaurants in TLV alone; hummus, falafel, salads, shawarma with falafel, Israeli breakfast culture are inherently vegetarian; Indian visitors will find abundant options; Israeli supermarkets stock produce familiar to Indian cooking; note that onion and garlic are standard in Israeli cooking; HONESTY: not all vegetarian food is "sattvic" by stricter Hindu standards — Indian visitors should inspect ingredients); Indian community in Israel (Bene Israel communities primarily in Ramat Gan, Rishon LeZion, Beer Sheva; Synagogue Magen David in Ramat Gan is Bene Israel-founded; Cochin Jewish heritage in the area of Moshav Nevatim and Kibbutz Shahar); Currency + payments (Indian Rupee not directly exchangeable at Israeli banks — convert INR to USD/EUR before arriving; forex at Ben Gurion Airport + city exchange bureaus; Forex rate is typically better at city bureaus than airport; credit cards universally accepted; UPI does NOT work in Israel); Tel Aviv attractions for Indian visitors (White City Bauhaus = architectural parallel to art deco Mumbai; Dead Sea = unique in the world; Old Jaffa clock tower = Ottoman architecture familiar visual language; TLV nightlife = Bollywood diaspora events occasionally; Israel is "mini India in some ways" — diversity, food culture, entrepreneurialism); Climate + packing (Mediterranean climate in TLV/Jerusalem; hot summers, mild winters; modest dress for holy sites — same logic as Indian temple dress codes applies; women should pack a scarf for holy sites); Language (Hebrew + Arabic official; English widely spoken in TLV, tourist sites; Hindi/Urdu NOT spoken — some Israelis familiar with Bollywood but no significant Hindi-speaker community). HONESTY: India-Israel political stance has historically been nuanced (India voted against some UN Israel resolutions pre-2014; Indian tourists should be aware their nationality may spark genuine political conversations from some Israelis — frame diplomatically); do NOT claim Hindi is spoken in Israel; vegetarian-friendly claims must be qualified ("for non-meat-eaters, not necessarily sattvic"); ETA-IL approval is not guaranteed but typically granted for tourism purposes — do not guarantee approval; El Al flight frequency may change — direct readers to airlines.com for current schedules. Monetization: Booking.com TLV hotel CTA + GYG "TLV City tour" or "Dead Sea day trip" CTA (Indian tourists = high Dead Sea interest); consider advertising disclosure if partnering with El Al affiliate links. Cross-links: israel-eta-guide, first-time-in-israel, dead-sea-guide, tel-aviv-beaches-guide (when built), christian-pilgrimage-holy-land. SEO: "Israel travel guide for Indians", "Israel from India 2026", "Israel ETA Indian passport", "Mumbai to Tel Aviv direct flight El Al", "best of Israel for Indian tourists". - [P2] (seo-content+monetization, S) SkyField Extreme Park Jaffa visitor guide (/skyfield-extreme-park-jaffa) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "skyfield", "bloomfield.*extreme", "extreme.*park.*jaffa" — ZERO matches. Tourist Israel + TripAdvisor + Jerusalem Post + Yahoo News all cover the opening; zero dedicated guide on our site. Israel's first fully accessible extreme park opened 2025 on the rooftop of Bloomfield Stadium in Jaffa — 5 multi-level Via Ferrata climbing routes + a 35-meter bungee jump + a giant swing; all activities designed fully wheelchair-accessible for visitors aged 12+ using mobility aids; panoramic Mediterranean Sea views. DISTINCT from adventure-sports.md (that covers hiking/rappelling in Negev; this is an urban adventure park in Jaffa). Content: What SkyField is (three ticket tiers: Beginner ₪120 / 3hr session = all 5 Via Ferrata routes; Brave ₪360 = all Via Ferrata + 1 bungee or swing; All Inclusive ₪495 = everything; closed shoes mandatory — no sandals; ages 12+; advance booking recommended weekends; rooftop setting with sea view); location + access (Bloomfield Stadium, Yigal Allon St, Jaffa — 10-min walk from Clock Tower + Old Jaffa; bus 40/41 from TLV center; Jaffa Port 15-min walk; combine with Jaffa visit: flea market Sat morning then SkyField afternoon or Old Jaffa walking tour + SkyField in same visit); practical safety info (all activities harnessed with safety instructor oversight; Via Ferrata = assisted climbing on cliff/wall with fixed rope and rungs; beginner route accessible to non-climbers with no prior experience; bungee = supervised single jump from 35m platform; professional staff on all routes; full accessibility = disabled guests can use Via Ferrata routes via wheelchair/mobility aid — unique in Israel). HONESTY: prices are from 2025/2026 opening data — link official SkyField/OPA website for current rates and booking; weather can affect rooftop operations — check on-the-day status; closed shoes = genuinely mandatory (do not arrive in sandals or flip-flops — will be turned away); "fully accessible" per official reports but verify specific accessibility needs directly with venue; no age limit on bungee beyond the 12+ rule — adult supervision for teens. Dense cross-links: jaffa-travel-guide, adventure-sports-israel, israel-with-kids, tel-aviv-things-to-do. Monetization: GYG/Viator adventure activities CTA (if SkyField available on platform — verify at publication) + Booking.com Jaffa/southern TLV hotel CTA.
