- [P2] (seo-content, S) Schottenstein National Campus for the Archaeology of Israel visitor guide (/schottenstein-campus-jerusalem) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "schottenstein", "national campus.*archaeol", "iaa.*museum.*2026" — ZERO matches; the rainy-day activities backlog item mentions it briefly as "IAA National Campus expected 2026" and the israel-travel-2026 backlog item lists it as a bullet — neither is a standalone guide. The Jay and Jeanie Schottenstein National Campus for the Archaeology of Israel is the Israel Antiquities Authority's new purpose-built home opening 2026 in Jerusalem — designed by Moshe Safdie (architect of Jerusalem's Supreme Court, Yad Vashem) in a three-staggered-courtyard design mimicking an archaeological excavation; 36,000m² campus on 20,000m² site between the Israel Museum and Bible Lands Museum. Major cultural event of 2026 Jerusalem tourism; will draw Dead Sea Scrolls enthusiasts, Jewish heritage travelers, archaeology devotees globally. Content: What it is (IAA's entire collection — nearly 2 million archaeological objects from Israel's State Treasury — under one roof for the first time; 15,000 Dead Sea Scroll fragments [the "published scrolls" are ~900; the IAA holds the remaining 15,000 fragments including unpublished pieces] — largest collection globally; National Archaeological Conservation and Restoration Laboratories open to public observation windows for the first time — visitors can watch conservators work on ancient artifacts in real time; National Archaeological Library and Archives with rare maps + historical documents; major gold, bronze, ceramic, mosaic, and textile finds from every archaeological period of Israel's history); Moshe Safdie's design (three tiered courtyards open to the sky, descending like an excavation; Jerusalem stone cladding ties building to city visual language; the design intentionally creates the physical sensation of descending into history); location + logistics (Givat Ram cultural campus, adjacent to Israel Museum and Bible Lands Museum; arriving: Bus 9 or 17 from Jerusalem city center; Givat Ram cultural campus morning = Israel Museum + National Library + Schottenstein Campus; full day program for culture-lovers; car parking available; ~20-min walk to Old City); practical visit planning (opening 2026 — verify exact date + admission pricing at iaa.gov.il; expect significant demand and advance booking systems; anticipated ~3h visit for full campus). HONESTY: "expected to open 2026" = IAA's published timeline as of mid-2026; ALWAYS link iaa.gov.il/NCampus for confirmed open date before publishing this page; admission pricing not confirmed at time of writing; "15,000 Dead Sea Scroll fragments" is the IAA's cited figure from official publications — do not conflate with the 900 published scrolls (these are mostly unpublished fragments); building scale/gallery layout may shift from plans to completion; Moshe Safdie = internationally recognized architect (brief bio accurate per public record). Dense cross-links: israel-museum-jerusalem (when built), jewish-heritage-israel, qumran-guide, israel-rainy-day-activities (where it is briefly mentioned), dead-sea-guide. SEO: "Schottenstein Campus Jerusalem 2026 guide", "IAA archaeology museum Jerusalem", "Israel National Campus Archaeology visitor guide", "Dead Sea Scroll fragments museum 2026 Jerusalem", "Moshe Safdie IAA building". Sources: timesofisrael.com/view-ancient-treasures-at-jerusalems-new-national-archeology-campus, friendsofiaa.org/our-home, safdiearchitects.com/projects/national-campus-for-the-archaeology-of-israel, wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Campus_for_the_Archaeology_of_Israel, leonlevyfoundation.org/grantees/national-campus. [iter235 research] status: ready

- [P3] (seo-content, S) Midburn Festival Israel — Israel's Burning Man guide (/midburn-festival-israel) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "midburn" — ZERO matches; searched "burning man.*israel" — ZERO matches. Midburn is Israel's official Burning Man regional event; the second-largest Burning Man regional globally (after Africa Burns); has been held annually since 2014 in the Negev desert near Be'er Sheva; 10,000+ participants; Tourist Israel + Secret Tel Aviv + World Jewish Travel + TakeYourBackpack all cover it; zero guide on our site. CRITICAL DISTINCTION: Midburn is NOT a music festival — no official stages, no ticketed performances — it is an art-expression-community event (theme camps host events organically, art installations dominate the open playa, the wooden Temple burns on the final night in a collective ceremony); DISTINCT from Meteor psytrance festival (music-focused) and from InDNegev (indie music festival). Audience: creative travelers, global Burning Man community, Israeli locals + diaspora; English workable. Content: What Midburn is (five-day temporary city in the Negev desert; built and dismantled entirely by participants with zero trace left; structured on the 10 Principles of Burning Man — gifting, decommodification, radical self-reliance, radical self-expression, communal effort, civic responsibility, leaving no trace, participation, immediacy, inclusion; the only thing sold on the playa is ice — everything else is gifted between camps; theme camps host art performances, workshops, dinners, pool parties; central "playa" has large-scale art installations; the Temple — a wooden structure into which participants write hopes/grief/prayers — burns on the final night in a profound communal ceremony; mutant art cars travel the playa); dates (late November annually; typically a Thursday–Monday; check midburn.org for exact year dates as they shift with the Hebrew calendar); location (Negev desert, Be'er Sheva area; exact GPS coordinates released closer to date on midburn.org — this is intentional design); tickets (advance purchase via midburn.org essential; no gate sales; early-bird tickets typically go on sale April-May; all-event + single-day passes; children welcome in dedicated areas but this is an adult-oriented event); packing essentials (tent + sleeping bag mandatory — November Negev nights drop to 10°C; daytime 22-24°C pleasant; sun protection for daytime desert; closed-toe shoes; costume/playa wear encouraged; camelbak + electrolytes; midburn.org has official full packing list — link, don't reproduce); getting there (car strongly recommended; organized shuttle from Be'er Sheva Central Bus Station sometimes available — check midburn.org closer to date; no public transport to site); first-Midburn etiquette (gifting culture means bringing something to give — handmade items, skills, food, art, not commercial goods; leave-no-trace is enforced — you pack in + pack out everything; arrive Thursday for best camping position + orientation to the space; the burning is Saturday night — the emotional climax of the event). HONESTY: Midburn has strong drug subculture associations (as all Burning Man events) — do NOT endorse, describe, or imply; Israel's desert November can be cold + rainy in rare years — genuine self-reliance required; "Israel's Burning Man" framing is accurate (official Burning Man regional per burningman.org); dates change annually — link midburn.org as sole authoritative source; exact playa location revealed close to event by design; Israeli crowd-dominant with growing international participation — English is workable but Hebrew dominates some social contexts; children's areas exist but overall event has adult-oriented art/expression — state clearly. Dense cross-links: negev region, adventure-sports-israel, israel-events-festivals, best-time-to-visit-israel (November). - [P3] (seo-content, S) TLVFest — Tel Aviv International LGBTQ Film Festival visitor guide (/tlvfest-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "tlvfest" — ZERO matches; searched "tel aviv.*film.*festival" — only within the music festivals guide content (not as standalone page). TLVFest is the annual LGBTQ+ film festival held at the Tel Aviv Cinematheque; 2026 dates: Oct 22–31, 2026; established 2000; 60+ films from 30+ countries; competitive Israeli film program; winner's cash prize; Q&A screenings with directors; panel discussions. DISTINCT from Tel Aviv Pride (June, street party + outdoor events); DISTINCT from lgbtq-travel-israel.md (SHIPPED — year-round general guide); DISTINCT from Israel music festivals guide (film, not music); DISTINCT from Jerusalem International Film Festival (separate event July at Jerusalem Cinematheque). Audience: LGBTQ+ film enthusiasts, cultural tourists, lgbtq community travelers, cinephiles. Content: What TLVFest is (founded 2000; Tel Aviv Cinematheque, 2 Sprinzak St; 10-day event with screenings of non-commercially-distributed LGBTQ cinema from 60+ countries; competitive "Israeli Competition" program with cash prize for best Israeli LGBTQ short or feature; international competition; panel discussions on LGBTQ cinema + human rights; retrospective screenings of LGBTQ cinema classics; Q&A sessions with visiting filmmakers; the festival has premiered work that later won international festival awards); practical info (Tel Aviv Cinematheque address: 2 Sprinzak St, near Shuk HaCarmel/Carmel Market; buses 18, 82, 82A from city center; individual screening tickets ~₪50-70; festival pass available — link tlvfest.com for current pricing; popular screenings sell out 1–2 weeks ahead — book early; some screenings in Hebrew without subtitles — check program details before booking; English-language and subtitled films are plentiful; Cinematheque has 3 screens); combining with TLV visit (October is excellent TLV weather: 22-26°C, low crowds relative to summer; morning at Carmel Market adjacent to Cinematheque; TLV beach still swimmable in October; afternoon museum visits; evening screenings; Jerusalem day-trip viable between screening days; Hilton Beach LGBTQ+ community beach nearby for rest day). HONESTY: festival program announced ~6 weeks before start; link tlvfest.com for current year program; not all films have English subtitles — check per-screening details; October hotel bookings: no Pride-week surge but general advance booking advisable; "60+ films from 30+ countries" is cited figure from festival marketing — frame as "typically" since program size varies slightly year to year; festival founded 2000 per Wikipedia + official sources. - [P3] (seo-content, S) InDNegev Music Festival — Israel's largest indie festival guide (/indnegev-festival) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "indnegev", "in.d.negev", "indie.*negev.*festival" — ZERO matches. InDNegev (In-D-Negev) is Israel's largest independent music and arts festival; 2026 dates: Oct 15–17, 2026 at Mitzpe Gvulot, Kibbutz Gvulot, western Negev; 100+ acts over 3 days; genres: rock, folk, electronic, groove — broad indie spectrum DISTINCT from Meteor (psytrance only); camping festival with art installations; founded to showcase Israel's independent music scene; secrettelaviv.com + touristisrael.com + worldjewishtravel.org + carnifest.com all cover it; zero page on our site. Audience: indie music enthusiasts, festival campers, international travelers combining Israel trip with festival. Content: What InDNegev is (Israel's premier independent music festival founded to break the dominance of commercial pop on Israeli radio; annual October event; Mitzpe Gvulot = flat desert plain near Kibbutz Gvulot, western Negev — near Sderot area; 100+ acts across multiple stages including rock, folk, electronic, groove, jazz; immersive art installations between stages; strong food vendor presence; camping integral to experience; strongly Israeli crowd with growing international attendance; mix of Hebrew and English acts; English widely spoken by festivalgoers; very family-present in the afternoon — distinct adult atmosphere takes over at night); 2026 dates (Oct 15–17, 2026; check indnegev.co.il for lineup, tickets, and transport); tickets (advance purchase strongly advised; sells out; single-day and 3-day camping passes; indnegev.co.il + secrettelaviv.com/tickets); camping (bring your own tent; October Negev nights: 10–15°C — sleeping bag essential; days 22-24°C; flat desert terrain = easy tent setup; communal outdoor vibe); getting there (car recommended; organized shuttle buses from Be'er Sheva and sometimes from Tel Aviv — check indnegev.co.il closer to date; limited site parking fills fast); music experience (lineup announced August-September; mix of veteran Israeli indie acts + emerging artists + occasional international headliners; no single dominant sound — something for rock, folk, electronic listeners alike; live music runs from afternoon into early morning); food and amenities (food stalls with diverse Israeli + international street food; vegetarian + vegan options well-represented; ATM on site in recent years — verify; basic facilities). HONESTY: festival location is in the western Negev near Sderot — MUST cross-link is-israel-safe for current safety context of this region; security situation has improved significantly as of 2026 but check government travel advisory for this specific area at time of booking; lineup changes annually — link indnegev.co.il; "100+ acts" is consistently cited figure — verify via official lineup announcement; October festival: weather excellent but desert nights cold — prominently advise sleeping bag; Israeli audience dominant, Hebrew used for announcements — most festivalgoers speak English. Dense cross-links: negev region, is-israel-safe (western Negev context), beer-sheva-guide, israel-events-festivals, israel-music-festivals (when built). - [P3] (seo-content, S) The Israel Festival Jerusalem — performing arts guide (/israel-festival-jerusalem-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "israel festival.*performing", "sultan.*pool.*festival", "jerusalem.*performing.*arts.*festival" — ZERO matches. The Israel Festival is the oldest annual performing arts festival in Israel, established 1961; 2026 dates: July 16–August 1, 2026; held at Sultan's Pool open-air amphitheater (5,000 seats; built into ancient stone cistern below the Old City walls, with the Jaffa Gate and Tower of David as backdrop — one of the world's most dramatic outdoor performance venues) + Jerusalem Theatre (two indoor halls). Covers international + Israeli dance, theatre, circus, opera, and world music. Tourist Israel has a dedicated page (touristisrael.com/the-israel-festival-jerusalem-israel/26350); our israel-events-festivals.md (SHIPPED) mentions it in a single line; our planned israel-music-festivals guide (backlog iter110) lists 7 festivals but NOT the Israel Festival (that guide covers Meteor/Tamar/Film Festival/Jazz/Festival of Light/Chamber Music/Purim). This is a genuinely separate standalone event worth a dedicated guide. Audience: cultural tourists, performing arts enthusiasts, summer visitors to Jerusalem. Content: What the Israel Festival is (est. 1961; the oldest and most prestigious performing arts festival in Israel; curated by professional artistic director; international performers in dance, physical theatre, circus, opera, and world music alongside leading Israeli productions; known for commissioning unique Israel premieres; the Jerusalem setting — stone terraces, ancient walls, night sky — gives performances a unique physical context); Sultan's Pool (ancient Ottoman-era reservoir below the Jaffa Gate / Hinnom Valley; outdoor amphitheater seating ~5,000 on stone tiered seating; The Tower of David and Old City walls form the backdrop on clear nights — bucket-list visual experience; open-air performances typically free or low-cost ₪30–80; best seats early arrival for free events; evening air in Jerusalem July = cool 18-22°C at 700m elevation — bring a light layer; stone seating uncomfortable after 2h — bring a small cushion or rent from vendors); Jerusalem Theatre (indoor air-conditioned halls; ticketed ₪80–250 via tickets.co.il or israelifestival.co.il; advance booking essential for popular productions); logistics (Sultan's Pool: 5-min walk south of Jaffa Gate; public parking limited in Old City area — arrive by Egged bus or Light Rail; combine with Old City daytime visit + Israel Festival evening = perfect Jerusalem day; from Tel Aviv: intercity train + Light Rail or Egged bus from Jerusalem Central; program released April/May — check israelifestival.co.il before booking train); who attends (Jerusalem-based Israelis + cultural tourists; mixed age; international visitors attending specifically for festival; the festival features English-language productions + subtitled Israeli works). HONESTY: festival program published April–May for July dates — do NOT list specific performers (they change annually); link israelifestival.co.il for current year program and tickets; Sultan's Pool events are typically free but some years include paid performances in the open-air venue — verify; Jerusalem July evenings: light layer always needed; July 16–August 1 is the 2026 specific date — use "typically mid-July through early August" for evergreen framing in body text with the 2026 specific dates in a sidebar/CTA; not all productions are accessible to visitors without Hebrew — check language of performance before booking. Dense cross-links: jerusalem region, israel-in-summer, israel-events-festivals, holy-sites-dress-code-etiquette (Old City vicinity), 1-day-jerusalem-itinerary (when built). SEO: "Israel Festival Jerusalem 2026 guide", "Sultan's Pool Jerusalem events", "Jerusalem performing arts festival July", "outdoor concert Jerusalem Israel summer". - [P3] (seo-content, S) Tel Aviv on a budget: free & cheap things to do (/tel-aviv-budget-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "tel.aviv.*budget.*guide", "/tel-aviv-budget", "budget.*tel.aviv.*guide", "free.*cheap.*tel.aviv" across full backlog — ZERO standalone guide entry. free-things-to-do-israel.md (SHIPPED iter73) is a 10-experience NATIONAL hub (covers TLV only as 2 of 10 experiences: beaches + Greeters); israel-cost-budget.md (SHIPPED) is a COST OVERVIEW (accommodation tiers, daily budget ranges, transport cost breakdown) — NOT a "what to do" guide for budget travelers; travel-cost-by-style BACKLOG (iter145, P3) is a style-comparison narrative. None of these is a dedicated "free and cheap Tel Aviv experiences" guide, which is a high-intent niche: budget travelers planning a TLV visit who want specific actionable recommendations. Competitors: thebrokebackpacker.com ("The Top 10 FREE Things to Do in Tel Aviv"), nomadicmatt.com/israel/tel-aviv (budget section), backpackisrael.com ("Free Things to Do in Tel Aviv"), budget-travel.net/israel all rank specifically for "free things to do Tel Aviv" with zero competition from our site. DISTINCT from all existing content as above. Content: free experiences first (TLV beaches: 14km of public Mediterranean beaches — Gordon/Frishman/Hilton/Banana all free; arrive early for umbrella spot; restrooms + outdoor showers at no charge; Hilton Beach = designated dog + LGBTQ+ community beach; White City Bauhaus self-guided walk: 2–3h loop on Rothschild Blvd + Dizengoff area; print city map or use Bauhaus Center printed guide free; zero admission; Ben-Gurion House: free guided tours Tue/Thu 2pm + Sun 11am in English; founding PM's personal home preserved since 1968; books + letters + historic artifacts; Greeters TLV: volunteer local guides offer free 2-3h walking tours by neighborhood theme; book via greeters.co.il; limited slots — book 2 weeks ahead; HaYarkon Park: 3.5km riverside park north TLV; walking/cycling trails, paddle boats, rose garden, amphitheater outdoor concerts some seasons; free entry; Florentin street art walk: 1.5h self-guided; densely muraled neighborhood; zero cost; Old Jaffa: Clock Tower area + Andromeda's Rock + old alleyways free to walk; Jaffa flea market: free to browse, great photography, food stalls cash only); cheap experiences (Carmel Market: ₪20–40 for a full falafel or sabich lunch at market stalls; Shuk HaNamal: Levinsky Spice Market area — cheaper than Sarona, authentic spice + olive browsing; Tel Aviv Museum of Art: ₪60 adult but free Sat 10am–2pm and Tue eve 4–8pm for Israeli residents only — note for guide: visitors NOT free; national parks pass: INPA card covers several TLV-area sites if combining with rest of country; Egged bus + Tel-O-Fun bike: ₪8.50/trip or 30-min free Tel-O-Fun rides with Rav-Kav; Tel Aviv port sunset: free evening; street food circuit: falafel + sabich ₪20–35 per item — budget 1-day eating ₪100–120); saving money tips (eat at Carmel Market vs restaurant = 60-70% cheaper; travel Mon-Thu: beaches less crowded; use light rail vs taxi; Gett app cheapest taxi when needed; supermarket Shufersal/Rami Levy for apartment cooking if self-catering; Happy Hour at beach-adjacent bars 5–7pm: cocktails ₪35–45 vs ₪65–80 full price; Tel-O-Fun dock-to-dock unlimited 30-min rides = nearly free city cycling). HONESTY: museum free hours are Israeli resident discounts only in most cases — clearly distinguish "free for all" vs "discounted/free for residents"; beaches are genuinely free; park entrance is genuinely free; no fabricated ratings; "cheapest" city in Israel = not accurate (TLV is Israel's most expensive city — frame as "how to do TLV affordably" not "TLV is cheap"); Greeters availability is limited — book well ahead. Dense cross-links: free-things-to-do-israel (national hub), israel-cost-budget, tel-aviv-neighborhoods-guide, jaffa-travel-guide, cycling-in-israel, tel-aviv-carmel-market. Monetization: GYG "free walking tour Tel Aviv" CTA (zero cost tour = high conversion click) + Abraham Hostel (budget accommodation category). - [SHIPPED iter247] Dead Sea hotels guide (/dead-sea-hotels-guide) → 5401635 — Ein Bokek resort strip: luxury (David Dead Sea Resort & Spa, Herods Dead Sea, Isrotel Dead Sea Hotel), mid-range (Isrotel Ganim, Lot Spa Hotel, Leonardo Plaza), honest no-budget framing, Ein Gedi Kibbutz Guest House alternative, seasonal pricing table, day-trip section. ORIGINAL BACKLOG ENTRY (archived): CONFIRMED GAP: searched "dead.sea.*hotel.*guide", "dead.sea.*where.*stay", "dead.sea.*hotels" across full backlog + DONE — ZERO standalone guide entry (dead-sea-guide.md SHIPPED iter86 is a general destination guide; eilat-hotels-guide SHIPPED iter241 and best-hotels-tel-aviv SHIPPED iter243 make this the natural third companion in the hotels-guide series). Competitors dominate: tourist israel ("Where to Stay at the Dead Sea – Best Hotels at Ein Bokek"), israel-taxi.com ("Best Dead Sea Hotels – Where To Stay At Ein Bokek In 2026"), secrettelaviv.com ("Best Hotels in the Dead Sea 2026"), deadsea.com ("Dead Sea Israel Hotels: Complete Guide"). Ein Bokek is Israel's only resort strip at the Dead Sea — virtually all accommodation clusters here: Luxury tier ₪1,400+ (Kayma Hotel ~44 boutique rooms in a salt-crystal aesthetic; Herods Dead Sea; Herbert Samuel Milos; David Dead Sea Resort & Spa ~606 rooms — the largest property); Mid-range ₪700–1,400 (Isrotel Ganim; Royal Rimonim Dead Sea Hotel; Hod Dead Sea Hotel). Budget note: Ein Bokek has almost NO budget options (it is an isolated resort zone with zero hostels or cheap guesthouses) — honest framing needed: budget travelers should day-trip from Jerusalem (90-min bus) or plan for mid-range minimum; genuine budget option is En Gedi Kibbutz Guest House (~₪480–600) 20 km north. Seasonal best: spring (Mar–May) and autumn (Oct–Nov) = ideal temperatures and water clarity; summer (Jul–Aug) = scorching 40°C+ but lowest hotel rates; winter (Dec–Feb) = mild weather and cheapest rates (~₪600–800 avg). Booking tips: Dead Sea resorts book out Israeli holiday weeks (Passover, Sukkot, Hanukkah, Yom Ha'atzmaut) 8–10 weeks ahead; many require 2-night minimum on weekends; Israeli domestic tourism heavy Fri–Sat. Content: zone overview (Ein Bokek 3.5 km resort strip — all within walking distance; beach access included with hotel stay at private Dead Sea beach; lifeguard hours posted at each hotel; minimum depth ~1.5m required for floating — safe for all adults); hotel picks at 3 tiers with honest price ranges; spa focus (virtually every hotel has a spa — Dead Sea minerals = authentic therapeutic draw; not a gimmick; psoriasis / joint condition benefits clinically documented); day-trip alternative (Egged direct bus 486 Jerusalem → Ein Bokek ~90 min; returns last bus ~17:00; plan timing carefully — Dead Sea water burns eyes on contact, budget 2h minimum); visa/checkpoint note (Ein Bokek is within Israel proper — no checkpoint; distinct from West Bank territory). Monetization: 2× Booking.com CTAs (luxury pick + value pick) + 1× GYG Dead Sea day tour CTA (for day-tripper variant). Dense cross-links: dead-sea-guide.md, eilat-hotels-guide (compare resort zones), masada-guide, israel-cost-budget. Sources: touristisrael.com/dead-sea-hotels, israel-taxi.com/dead-sea-hotels, secrettelaviv.com/dead-sea-hotels, deadsea.com/hotels. [iter245 research] status: ready

- [DUPLICATE — SHIPPED iter213; re-added by iter245 research; cleaned up iter247] Jericho day trip from Jerusalem guide (/jericho-day-trip-from-jerusalem) → 1109630. ORIGINAL BACKLOG ENTRY: CONFIRMED GAP: searched "jericho.*day.*trip", "jericho.*jerusalem", "jericho.*guide" across full backlog + DONE.md — ZERO entries (Bethlehem day trip SHIPPED iter87; Hebron is EXPLICITLY excluded per PROJECT.md; Jericho is NOT excluded). Jericho is the world's oldest continuously inhabited city (oldest permanent settlement dating to ~10,000 BCE; UNESCO-listed Tell Jericho); located in Palestinian Authority territory (Area A) on the Jordan River plain; 45 min by road from Jerusalem; accessible on all organized tours without security concerns (tourist zone; standard PA checkpoint entry — guide handles paperwork on tours). Competitors: GYG offers dozens of Jericho day tours; Viator ("Day Trip to Jericho from Jerusalem" from $35/pp); touristisrael.com dedicated Jericho page; GetYourGuide; Abraham Tours ("Jericho & Jordan Valley Day Tour"). All confirm strong tour demand. Key sites: Tell Jericho / Tel es-Sultan (the actual archaeological mound with Neolithic tower dating 8000 BCE — one of the oldest structures on Earth; Kathleen Kenyon excavation site; excellent on-site signage in English); Mount of Temptation / Jabal al-Qurunṭul (the 350m cliff where Jesus was said to have been tempted; cable car ₪60–90 one-way; monastery carved into the cliff face visible from cable car — spiritual + dramatic landscape; cable car hours 8am–5pm; Fri often reduced); Hisham's Palace / Khirbet al-Mafjar (2km north of Jericho town; 8th-century Umayyad winter palace; the mosaic "Tree of Life" floor in the bath house is one of the finest Byzantine-Umayyad mosaics in the Middle East; ₪25 entry; often uncrowded); Sycamore Tree (traditional tree Zacchaeus climbed; a living tree in the town centre + small plaza; free to visit; 5 min stop); Jericho city centre market (Palestinian street food — knafeh, fresh-squeezed pomegranate juice, dates; safe for tourists; cash only). Logistics (MUST get right): only safe/practical access is via organized tour (private car with Israeli plates cannot enter Area A; East Jerusalem Arab taxis can enter; most visitors use organized tours from Jerusalem — Abraham Tours, GYG operators, Viator); typical half-day 4–5h; full-day adds Jordan Valley (Qumran Caves + Baptism Site = ~8h); cost ₪120–220/pp on small-group tour. Checkpoint framing: Israeli citizens / most tourist groups enter via Allenby Bridge crossing note only if doing Jordan connection; Jericho day trip from Jerusalem uses the direct Jericho road with PA-administered checkpoint — organized tour guide manages; foreign tourists virtually never stopped; Israeli nationals may not enter Area A as per Israeli law — note if Israeli-citizen readers exist. HONESTY: "world's oldest city" claim requires careful wording — Tell Jericho is one of the oldest CONTINUOUSLY INHABITED sites; Damascus and Çatalhöyük also make this claim — use "among the world's oldest" or "one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities"; no fabricated ticket prices (use "check current admission fee" + linked source); checkpoint language: informative not alarmist — "organized tours manage the checkpoint crossing; foreign tourists experience minimal delay". Monetization: GYG Jericho day tour CTA (primary) + optional Booking.com Jerusalem hotel (overnight context). Dense cross-links: bethlehem-day-trip, dead-sea-guide (day-combine option), masada-guide, is-israel-safe (West Bank framing). SEO: "Jericho day trip from Jerusalem", "Jericho Israel tourist guide", "Mount of Temptation cable car Jericho", "Tell Jericho archaeological site guide". Sources: GetYourGuide Jericho tours, Abraham Tours Jericho day trip, touristisrael.com/jericho, Viator Jericho from Jerusalem. [iter245 research] status: ready

- [P3] (seo-content, S) Beer Sheva / Beersheba city guide (/beer-sheva-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "beer.sheva.*guide", "beer.sheva.*travel", "beersheba", "/beer-sheva" across full backlog + DONE.md — ZERO standalone entry (multiple backlog items reference "beer-sheva-guide (when built)" as a cross-link target: negev-incense-route, south-israel-itinerary, indnegev-festival [iter235], and the planned Bedouin market content). Beer Sheva (Be'er Sheva / Beersheba) is the capital of the Negev and Israel's 4th-largest city (~220,000 residents); major transport hub for all Negev tourism; Ben-Gurion University of the Negev (45,000+ students; relevant for digital-nomad + academic tourism angles). Key sites: Tel Be'er Sheva (UNESCO World Heritage Site; ancient Israelite city from 10th century BCE with classic "four-room houses"; excellent excavated remains and museum; 30 min from city centre); Thursday Bedouin market (largest in the Middle East — though the original Thursday livestock market has diminished, the Abu Mahar / Daled market still draws ~30,000 visitors weekly; authentic Palestinian/Bedouin craft, produce, and livestock; best visited before noon Thursday); Old City neighbourhood (Ottoman-era grid laid by the Turks 1900–1914; some restored houses; small museum; historic mosque); Negev Brigade Monument (Dani Karavan 1963 — a significant work of land art and memorial; UNESCO-recognized as Outstanding Universal Value for Beer Sheva World War I graves nearby); Israel Air Force Museum (Hatzerim base; approx 150 historic aircraft; largest air museum in the Middle East; Tue–Sat only; ₪50 adult). Practical role: Beer Sheva is the natural base for southern Negev excursions — Mitzpe Ramon (90 min), Sde Boker/Ben-Gurion tomb (40 min), Petra day trip departure point (3h); accessible by frequent train from Tel Aviv (1h10 direct, ₪42); light rail within city. Content: city overview, top sites (Tel Be'er Sheva UNESCO, IAF Museum, Thursday Bedouin market, Dani Karavan monument), 1-day itinerary template, day-trip hub function (Negev gateway), Abraham's Well biblical connection (Beer Sheva = "Well of the Oath" in Genesis — seven wells dug by Abraham; small well site in city centre). HONESTY: Thursday Bedouin market is authentic but smaller than its reputation suggests (the livestock section has contracted substantially since the 1990s; manage expectations — frame as atmospheric and interesting, not overwhelming); Tel Be'er Sheva UNESCO site: check current opening days before visiting (has had irregular seasonal hours). Dense cross-links: negev-incense-route (when built), mitzpe-ramon-guide, israel-best-hikes, south-israel-itinerary, indnegev-festival. SEO: "Beer Sheva travel guide", "things to do in Beer Sheva Israel", "Beersheba Negev capital guide", "Tel Beer Sheva UNESCO site". Sources: touristisrael.com/beer-sheva, go2israel.com, wikitravel.org/en/Beersheba, israel21c.org. [iter245 research] status: ready

- [P2] (seo-content+monetization, M) Israel birdwatching & migration guide (/israel-birdwatching) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "birdwatch|birding|hula.*valley" — ZERO matches. hike-israel.com, ecoisraeltours.com, aardvarkisrael.com, natureisrael.org, buckettripper.com, touristisrael.com/hula-valley/2540 all rank; zero guide on our site. BBC Wildlife declared Hula Lake one of the world's top 10 birdwatching sites; 500+ species transit Israel (bridge migration corridor between Europe/Africa/Asia); 2M+ birds/day at Hula Valley peak. Two annual festivals: Eilat Bird Festival (March, spring migration, raptor spectacle at Eilat), Hula Valley Birds Festival (November, cranes and pelicans, Agamon Hula safari-wagon sunrise tours Fri/Sat). Content: Hula Valley (Agamon Hula reserve — crane overwintering mid-Oct through Feb; pelicans, storks, raptors; safari wagon, bicycle hire, walking trails; open daily; ₪40 entry); Eilat (International Birding and Research Center, Eilat Mountains for raptor migration March and October); coastal birding (Caesarea, Mediterranean shorebirds); Negev spring bloom + associated passerines (March–April). Best season: November for Hula crane spectacle; March for Eilat spring migration; avoid August (heat + migration gap). HONESTY: exact bird counts change annually; no fabricated species counts beyond established published figures; festival dates shift — link official sites. Monetization: ecoisraeltours.com / GYG "birdwatching Israel" tour CTA + Booking.com Rosh Pina / Upper Galilee accommodation (closest base for Hula). Dense cross-links: hula-valley (when built), eilat-travel-guide, galilee region, israel-nature-guide (when built). - [ARCHIVED-DUPE iter250 research error] Haifa neighborhoods & where to stay guide (/haifa-neighborhoods-guide) — already SHIPPED iter223 → b60e624; iter250 research failed to check DONE.md before adding. Key neighborhoods: German Colony (best location — Ben Gurion Blvd restaurants, cafes; walking distance to Bahá'í Gardens lower terrace; Colony Hotel; boutique accommodation; short cable car or walk to port); Upper Carmel (affluent residential; panoramic views; Carmel Center shopping; Carmeliya neighbourhood; most mid/upper hotels clustered here); Bat Galim (beachfront; seaside promenade; lower cable car terminus; quieter, residential feel; fewer dining options); Hadar (mid-slope; local neighbourhood; Madatech Science Museum; budget guesthouses and mid-range hotels; authentic Haifa street life); Downtown/Port (cruise port access; Wadi Nisnas Arab quarter adjacent; Paris Square architecture; less accommodation but growing hostel scene). Cross-neighborhood logistics: Carmelit underground funicular runs German Colony → Hadar → Upper Carmel (6 stops, ₪7.50, Sunday–Thursday + Saturday night); cable car Bat Galim → Upper Carmel. Bahá'í Gardens logistics: upper terrace (Upper Carmel end, free self-guided) + lower terrace tour (German Colony end, free guided tours Sunday–Thursday). HONESTY: no fabricated hotel prices; booking.com CTA by neighborhood; German Colony boutique options limited — book ahead. Monetization: Booking.com Haifa hotel CTA segmented by neighborhood tier. - [SHIPPED iter251] Israel stargazing & dark sky astronomy guide (/israel-stargazing) → 9c7cb08 — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "stargazing|astrono" — ZERO matches. DISTINCT from mitzpe-ramon-guide (P2 backlog — general destination guide; stargazing is one attraction among many). Mitzpe Ramon is the first International Dark Sky Park (IDA-certified) in the Middle East; dedicated stargazing operators include desert-prime.com, deserttimeisrael.com, deepdesertisrael.com, astronomyisrael.com. Tours: ~2h guided sessions, 9:30pm start, laser pointer constellation tour + telescope, ~4 nights/week (weather/moon dependent). Events: Summer of Stars (August — Perseid meteor shower, Mitzpe Ramon town dims lights collectively; a unique community astronomy event); annual Negev dark sky gatherings. Beyond Mitzpe: Negev highlands generally, Golan Heights away from Katzrin/city lights, Judean Desert east of Jerusalem. Best season: October–May (clear sky nights dominant; cooler temp for night outings); summer possible but hotter; avoid full-moon week (wash-out). Content: what makes Israel ideal (minimal longitude — no hour-change jet lag from Europe; ultra-dry Negev + Golan = exceptional seeing conditions; compact geography = accessible from TLV day-return or Jerusalem overnight); dark sky park intro (IDA certification = measured light pollution below Bortle 3; specific park location: Mitzpe Ramon crater rim); tour booking practicalities (advance book 3–7 days; cancellation if clouds or full moon — good operators refund; no prior astronomy knowledge needed; family-friendly); self-guided options (bring binoculars + Stellarium app; park overnight camping near crater rim; Mitzpe Ramon hostel + eco-lodge options nearby). HONESTY: dark sky certification depends on current light pollution levels — link IDA for current status; moon phase governs experience dramatically — mention it; "best in the Middle East" framing is accurate per IDA certification. Monetization: GYG "Mitzpe Ramon stargazing" tour CTA + Booking.com eco-lodge/zimmer Mitzpe Ramon. Dense cross-links: mitzpe-ramon-guide (when built), negev region, israel-adventure-sports, glamping-israel (when built). SEO: "stargazing Israel guide", "Mitzpe Ramon dark sky park", "Israel astronomy tourism 2026", "best stargazing Israel Negev". Haifa: Dan Carmel Hotel terrace (Mount Carmel + Haifa Bay panorama). - [SHIPPED iter257] Tel Aviv specialty coffee & café culture guide (/tel-aviv-coffee-guide) → 979c59f — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "tel.aviv.*coffee.*guide", "/tel-aviv-coffee", "specialty.*coffee", "nahat.*coffee", "cafelix", "caffe tamati", "third.wave.*coffee" across full BACKLOG — ZERO standalone guide entry (only match for "tel.aviv.*coffee" was in Jaffa flea market entry = neighbourhood context, not a café guide). DISTINCT from: tel-aviv-food-guide.md (SHIPPED — restaurants/cuisines broadly; no specialty coffee depth); israel-craft-beer (SHIPPED iter236 — beer, not coffee); vegan-vegetarian-israel (SHIPPED — dietary preferences, not café culture). touristisrael.com has dedicated "Best Coffee in Tel Aviv" page; secrettelaviv.com/food-and-drink/coffee ranks; coffeescouts.com/tel-aviv lists 20+ roasters; Wanderlog "Best Coffee Shops in Tel Aviv." Tel Aviv's third-wave specialty coffee scene is internationally recognized — Israeli baristas have placed at European Championship of Coffee competitions; ~60+ specialty roasters and independent cafés as of 2026. DISTINCT EDITORIAL ANGLE: Israeli coffee spans two traditions — traditional botz (unfiltered Turkish-style black coffee, ubiquitous in Arab-Israeli cafés and older establishments) and the internationally acclaimed third-wave specialty scene that emerged 2010–2020; the contrast is the hook. Key roasters and cafés: Nahat (Dizengoff St + Rothschild Blvd; direct-trade sourcing + seasonal espresso + filter), Cafelix (3 locations; French-press service + precise single-origin espressos), Caffe Tamati (Carmel Market area; blend of market energy and specialty technique), Way Cup (Florentin; neighborhood institution; natural-process Ethiopians), Mae (Bograshov area; compact; exceptional milk steaming), Origine (Neve Tzedek; boutique roaster with tasting-menu approach), Jera (Gordon Beach area; beach + quality espresso combination). Neighbourhood coffee circuit: Florentin → Neve Tzedek → Carmel Market → Rothschild → Gordon Beach. Coffee culture context: Israeli café culture = sit for hours with a single coffee; most specialty cafés open from 7am daily INCLUDING Shabbat (cafés are not kosher-obligated to close — KEY practical fact different from most Israeli restaurants); credit cards universal; tip 10% at table service; iced coffee (café kar) universally available year-round. HONESTY: specific café recommendations change rapidly — frame as "consistently cited in Israeli food media as of 2026 research; verify current operations before visiting"; do NOT fabricate ratings, social media follower counts, or competition placement claims beyond verified sources; "award-winning" framing only for documented competitions; prices are "typically ₪18–28 for a specialty espresso drink" — frame as range, advise verification. Monetization: Booking.com TLV boutique hotel CTA; GYG "Tel Aviv food and market tour" CTA (cafés on food tour circuits). Cross-links: tel-aviv-food-guide, tel-aviv-neighborhoods-guide, tel-aviv-carmel-market, vegan-vegetarian-israel. - [P2] (seo-content+monetization, S) Bahá'í World Centre complete visitor guide (/bahai-world-center-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "bahai.*world.*centre", "bahai.*center.*guide", "/bahai-world", "shrine.*bab.*guide", "shrine.*bahaullah", "baha.*world", "bahai.*haifa.*guide", "bahai.*akko.*guide" across full BACKLOG — ZERO standalone entry (only match for "baha.*world" was in Mount Carmel hiking entry = single passing mention of Bahá'í Gardens as a viewpoint). DISTINCT from: haifa-travel-guide.md (SHIPPED — 2 lines on Terraced Gardens); haifa-neighborhoods-guide.md (SHIPPED iter223 — German Colony section notes garden access; not a visitor guide); akko-acre-guide.md (SHIPPED — Bahjí in 1 line); bahai-gardens-haifa (referenced as "when built" cross-link target in several entries = no standalone page exists). The Bahá'í World Centre receives approximately 1 million visitors annually across the Haifa and Akko sites combined — one of Israel's most-visited attractions; UNESCO World Heritage Site 2008. Competitors: bahai.org.il (official English guide), lonelyplanet.com/israel/haifa (full Bahá'í section), bbc.com/travel ("Haifa's most beautiful gardens"), seetheholyland.net/bahai-world-centre, GPSMyCity Haifa Bahá'í walking route — all rank; ZERO equivalent on our site. Haifa sites — Bahá'í Terraced Gardens: 19 manicured terraces on north slope of Mount Carmel; UNESCO WHS 2008; free entry to lower terraces daily 9am–5pm; upper terrace free guided tours available Monday–Thursday mornings via advance registration at bahai.org.il (must book 24h+ ahead; no walk-in access to upper terraces); dress code: modest clothing required (shoulders + knees covered; follow guide's instruction on footwear); photography of gardens permitted; photography inside the Shrine of the Báb NOT permitted. Shrine of the Báb: gold-domed octagonal structure at midpoint of terraces; burial place of the Báb (founder-herald of the Bahá'í Faith, 1819–1850); interior access = Bahá'ís and formally invited guests only; exterior and surroundings accessible during garden tours. Bahá'í Faith overview for visitors: founded 19th-century Persia; the Báb (Ali Muhammad, 1819–1850) proclaimed a new religious cycle; Bahá'u'lláh (1817–1892) = central figure; 5–8 million adherents worldwide; Haifa and Akko became World Centre due to Bahá'u'lláh's Ottoman-era exile and imprisonment. Akko sites — Shrine of Bahá'u'lláh at Bahjí: holiest site in the Bahá'í Faith (burial place of Bahá'u'lláh); 7km north of Akko city; surrounded by formal Persian gardens; open to all faiths daily 9am–5pm EXCEPT Friday afternoon; gardens accessible; shrine interior = pilgrimage for Bahá'ís only; free entry; car or taxi from Akko (~15 min); no regular public bus. Ridvan Garden: island garden on the Na'aman River where Bahá'u'lláh spent time during imprisonment; peaceful; open to visitors. Mansion of Mazra'ih: where Bahá'u'lláh resided 1877–1879; open on specific days — check bahai.org.il for current public schedule. Practical circuit: 2 days for full circuit (Day 1: Haifa Terraced Gardens + German Colony; Day 2: Akko Bahjí + Ridvan Garden + akko-acre-guide attractions). German Colony = ideal base (walkable garden access + boutique accommodation). HONESTY: upper-terrace guided tour MUST be pre-booked via bahai.org.il — walk-in unavailable; Shrine of the Báb interior = Bahá'ís only (clarify clearly — many travel sites imply otherwise and disappoint visitors); Shrine of Bahá'u'lláh interior = Bahá'ís only; gardens are active religious sites, not public parks — respectful behaviour expected and monitored; dress code enforced at entrance; Friday afternoon closure at Bahjí is a firm religious closure. Monetization: Booking.com Haifa German Colony hotel CTA; GYG "Haifa city tour including Bahá'í Gardens" CTA (verify product availability). Cross-links: haifa-travel-guide, haifa-neighborhoods-guide, akko-acre-guide, day-trips-from-haifa. SEO: "Bahá'í World Centre visitor guide", "Haifa Bahá'í Gardens tour guide", "Shrine of the Báb Haifa visit", "Shrine of Bahá'u'lláh Akko Bahjí guide", "Bahá'í Gardens UNESCO Israel 2026". Sources: bahai.org.il, whc.unesco.org/en/list/1220, lonelyplanet.com/israel/haifa, bbc.com/travel/article/20180329-haifas-most-beautiful-gardens, seetheholyland.net/bahai-world-centre. [iter255 research] status: SHIPPED iter256

- [P2] (seo-content+monetization, M) Tel Megiddo (Armageddon) & Jezreel Valley visitor guide (/megiddo-jezreel-valley-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "/megiddo.*guide", "megiddo.*visitor", "armageddon.*israel.*guide" across full BACKLOG — ZERO standalone guide entry (8 "megiddo" matches: 6 in SHIPPED day-trips-from-haifa where Megiddo is one stop among five, 2 in hidden-gems hub where listed as a single bullet among 15+ items; no dedicated guide). DISTINCT from: day-trips-from-haifa.md (SHIPPED iter162 — Megiddo as 1 of 5 day-trip options; brief); israel-hidden-gems hub (P2 backlog — general hub; Megiddo listed but not given guide-depth coverage). Tel Megiddo is a UNESCO World Heritage Site (2005, "Biblical Tels — Megiddo, Hazor, Beer-sheba"). The name "Armageddon" derives from the Hebrew "Har Megiddo" (Mount of Megiddo) and appears in the Book of Revelation (16:16) as the site of the apocalyptic final battle — this name recognition generates substantial SEO value from Christian, secular history, and pilgrimage travelers globally. Competitors: inpa.gov.il/megiddo (official), touristisrael.com/megiddo (full standalone guide), beinharimtours.com/megiddo, christiansintheland.com/megiddo-armageddon, holylandtourstravel.com, biblicalarchaeologysociety.org — all rank for "Tel Megiddo visitor guide" / "Armageddon Israel" with zero competition from our site. What to see — Archaeological Tel: 29 overlapping settlement layers 7000 BCE–600 BCE; one of the densest archaeological records on earth; marked excavation zones with English signage. Canaanite temples: two circular altars and a temple structure from ~3000 BCE — among the best-preserved Canaanite sacred architecture in Israel. The Stables: large ashlar stone enclosures in Iron Age Stratum IVA; traditionally called "Solomon's Stables" (1 Kings 9:15) but most archaeologists now associate them with King Ahab of Israel (9th-century BCE) — frame honestly as both attributions with the scholarly context; the debate is itself interesting. Ahab's Water Tunnel: 9th-century BCE underground shaft descending 36 metres into bedrock with a 70-metre horizontal tunnel at the bottom; walk-through visitor path; one of the most impressive engineering feats of ancient Israel. Summit panorama: overlooks the entire Jezreel Valley — vast fertile plain contested throughout history; Thutmose III's 1457 BCE battle (among the earliest reliably documented battles in history) was fought here; subsequently: Deborah vs. Sisera, Gideon vs. Midianites, King Josiah vs. Pharaoh Necho 609 BCE. Visitor centre: ground level; curated finds (carved ivory, Egyptian scarabs, cuneiform tablets; well-presented in English). Jezreel Valley circuit (natural 1-day combination): Megiddo (2h) → Gan HaShlosha / Sachne warm spring pools (30 min south; INPA site; 28°C freshwater pools; swimming + picnic; open year-round; one of Israel's most pleasant outdoor sites) → Beit Alfa Byzantine synagogue (15 min from Sachne; 6th-century CE mosaic floor with zodiac wheel in exceptional preservation; ₪14 entry; 20 min visit) → optional Mount Tabor (15 min north; Transfiguration site; winding road to summit; Franciscan Basilica; panoramic Galilee view). Practical: INPA-managed; NP Pass valid; car required (no public transport); 1h from Haifa via Route 65, 1.5h from Tel Aviv, 45 min from Nazareth; hours approx. 8am–5pm (verify inpa.gov.il); admission ~₪29 adult (verify); parking on site; pack lunch (no restaurant at site). "Armageddon" framing: the Revelation reference is factual and appropriate; do NOT sensationalize; frame as: "the site that gave us the word Armageddon and why." HONESTY: "earliest documented battle" = Thutmose III's 1457 BCE Megiddo engagement is consistently cited as among the earliest reliably documented battles per Biblical Archaeology Society and academic consensus — not fabricated; attribution of "Solomon's Stables" = present traditional attribution AND note modern scholarly consensus re Ahab period; INPA admission and hours approximate — direct readers to inpa.gov.il; Gan HaShlosha and Beit Alfa details from INPA sources — verify before publishing. Monetization: GYG "Megiddo day tour from Tel Aviv" or "Galilee history + Megiddo tour" CTA; Booking.com Haifa or Nazareth hotel CTA (overnight base for circuit); INPA National Parks Pass cross-sell. Cross-links: day-trips-from-haifa, national-parks-pass-calculator, galilee-christian-sites-circuit, nazareth-travel-guide, israel-hidden-gems, beit-shean-guide (when built). SEO: "Tel Megiddo visitor guide", "Armageddon Israel site guide", "Megiddo UNESCO Israel", "Tel Megiddo archaeological tour", "Jezreel Valley day trip Israel 2026". Sources: inpa.gov.il/megiddo, touristisrael.com/megiddo, beinharimtours.com/megiddo, whc.unesco.org/en/list/1108 (Biblical Tels UNESCO), christiansintheland.com/megiddo-armageddon, biblicalarchaeologysociety.org/online-archive/topic/megiddo. [SHIPPED iter258]

- [P3] (seo-content, S) Israel summer night hiking guide (/israel-night-hiking) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "night.hik", "/israel-night", "night.*trail.*israel", "summer.*hike.*night" across full BACKLOG — ZERO matches. DISTINCT from: hiking-in-israel.md (SHIPPED — year-round day hiking; no night-specific content); israel-adventure-sports.md (SHIPPED — multi-sport hub; no night hiking section); israel-evening-activities.md (SHIPPED — cultural evening activities: museums, markets, sunset cruises — not wilderness hiking); israel-stargazing (SHIPPED iter251 — astronomy focus; mentions night-time outdoors but not as a hiking guide). hike-israel.com has dedicated "Night Hiking in Israel" article; timeout.com/israel covers night hiking in summer activity round-ups; INPA Israel promotes specific summer night trail openings. Night hiking is a documented Israeli summer adaptation: daytime 35–40°C+ in July–August makes day hiking dangerous; night temperatures of 22–26°C are ideal; INPA opens certain trail sections at night specifically during summer (announced annually via inpa.gov.il and INPA social media). Routes (representative; direct readers to INPA for current night-approved trail list as it changes annually): Upper Galilee / Nahal Amud (forest trail to Kinneret; fireflies in June–July; 6km one-way; no permit required); Nahal Yael near Ein Gedi (desert stream; full moon walk to waterfall; 3km round-trip; check INPA fire/access restrictions); Sataf Springs (Jerusalem Hills; family-friendly; terraced ancient farmland + springs; 2km loop; cool night air); Yehudia Forest Reserve, Golan Heights (wooded; cooler than coast in summer); Eilat Mountains / Nachal Hatira (dry riverbed with dramatic coloured sandstone visible by headlamp; 4–8km options). Urban night walks: HaYarkon Park TLV (lit promenade, 4km loop); Old Jaffa coastal path (lit, 2km, mixed tourists + locals). Safety checklist: head torch (200+ lumen minimum; spare batteries); tell someone your route + return time; download offline trail map (AllTrails or INPA hiking app); no phone signal on many trails — share location before descending; ankle-support footwear (NEVER flip-flops — rocky terrain + snake risk); carry 1.5–2L water per person even at night in summer; snake awareness (Israeli vipers are primarily nocturnal — risk higher at night than day); protocol: torch to check where you step; never reach into dark crevices; if encountered: freeze + slowly back away; in emergency call 101 (Magen David Adom). Fire closure status: MUST check INPA website or INPA emergency line before going (inpa.gov.il/trail-closures); summer trail closures for fire risk announced irregularly and on short notice. Guided options: Israel Hike (israelhike.com — organized weekly summer night hikes, mixed Hebrew/English groups); Desert Eco Tours (Negev night hikes); Israel Trail Association (tevad.org.il). HONESTY: snakebite risk is real and elevated at night — do NOT downplay; fire closure is a genuine operational reality — always link to INPA and tell readers to check current status; "fireflies" in Nahal Amud = factual (Lampyris noctiluca observed in humid valley sections in early summer) — not fabricated; INPA night trail list changes annually — link INPA rather than listing specific trails as fixed; solo night hiking on unfamiliar remote trails not recommended for tourists. Monetization: GYG Israel night hike guided tour CTA (verify product availability); Booking.com Eilat or Mitzpe Ramon hotel CTA. Cross-links: hiking-in-israel, israel-adventure-sports, israel-in-summer, israel-stargazing (combine: night hike + stargazing same evening). SEO: "night hiking Israel summer", "Israel night hike guide", "summer night activities Israel hiking", "Israel night trail nature reserve guide". Sources: hike-israel.com/night-hiking-in-israel, timeout.com/israel/things-to-do/summer-night-activities, inpa.gov.il, israelhike.com, tevad.org.il. [iter255 research] status: ready

- [P3] (seo-content, S) Israel INPA camping & tent camping guide (/israel-camping-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "/israel-camping", "israel-camping-guide", "inpa.*camp", "tent.*camping.*israel", "camp.*inpa.*guide" across full BACKLOG — ZERO standalone entry (5 "inpa.*camp" matches: all within /israel-campervan entry (P2 backlog) where INPA campsites are mentioned as motorhome stop-points; no standalone tent camping guide). DISTINCT from: /glamping-israel (P2 backlog — luxury glamping operators, eco-lodges, bell-tent glamping; targets comfort-seeking travelers NOT backpackers); /israel-campervan (P2 backlog — self-contained motorhome touring; targets vehicle-based travelers); israel-adventure-sports.md (SHIPPED — mentions camping in passing, zero INPA logistics). israeltrekking.com ranks for "camping in Israel"; campinginisrael.com; inpa.gov.il/camping (official reservation system) — zero equivalent on our site. INPA operates approximately 40 designated campgrounds (אתרי לינה) across national parks and nature reserves; the infrastructure is well-established but virtually unknown to international tourists who cannot navigate the INPA Hebrew booking system. The Israel National Trail (Shvil Yisrael, 1,100km) passes through or adjacent to many INPA campgrounds — through-hiking community is an established international audience. INPA campground types: Type A — full facilities (flush toilets, cold showers, BBQ pits, covered cooking area, parking; fees ~₪60–100/person/night, 2026 range; advance booking via inpa.gov.il); Type B — basic (portable toilets only, no showers; ~₪30–50/person/night or free). Wild camping: illegal outside INPA designated areas (Israel Nature and Parks Authority Law enforced; INPA rangers patrol; fines for illegal camping in sensitive areas). Top campgrounds by region (representative — full list at inpa.gov.il): Negev: Makhtesh Ramon Campground (crater rim, Mitzpe Ramon; Type A; most dramatic site in Israel; outstanding dark sky access; 5 min from town; advance booking essential); Nahal Ardon streambed (near Mitzpe Ramon; primitive Type B); Sde Boker desert campground (adjacent Ben-Gurion tomb heritage site). Galilee: Nahal Hermon/Banias campground (Golan Heights; adjacent Banias waterfall + Roman temple; closed in winter); Ein Afek Nature Reserve (north Haifa; freshwater wetland; birdwatching angle). Dead Sea / Judean Desert: Ein Gedi campground (adjacent Ein Gedi Nature Reserve; strong demand — book 6–8 weeks ahead for Sukkot + Pesach; summer 38–42°C even at night — spring/autumn recommended). Golan: Nimrod Fortress campground (medieval fortress backdrop; cool summer nights at 850m). Coastal: Achziv National Park (Mediterranean beach camping; Type A). Israel National Trail: israelnationaltrail.com lists ~70 camping stations (mahane/water points) along the 1,100km trail every 15–25km; many coincide with INPA campgrounds; "trail angels" provide water resupply at remote points; through-hiking season: November–March (avoiding summer heat); ~45–60 days at steady pace. Booking: inpa.gov.il/en/camping (English page; payment may redirect to Hebrew — use Chrome auto-translate); INPA helpline 076-800-0088; holiday periods (Sukkot, Pesach, Yom Ha'atzmaut) book 6–8 weeks ahead; cancellation 48h+ for refund. Gear essentials: sleeping bag rated to 5°C even in summer (desert nights drop sharply after midnight; Makhtesh Ramon crater rim can reach 8–12°C in May/October); tent with strong stakes for rocky limestone ground (spiral stakes, not regular aluminum pegs); portable gas stove ONLY (campfire absolutely prohibited at all INPA sites — Israeli fire-risk law, year-round, no exceptions; fines issued); water treatment for Type B / trail sites; tick + snake awareness for brush sections. HONESTY: campfire prohibition is ABSOLUTE under Israeli law at INPA sites — state this clearly as a legal requirement, not a guideline; INPA online booking has limited English support — some steps require browser translation; "approximately 40 campgrounds" = 2026 figure, INPA adds/removes seasonally (link inpa.gov.il for current list); summer heat at Dead Sea and Negev campsites is genuinely extreme and can pose health risk — prominently recommend spring/autumn as optimal; wild camping ban = legal fact, not soft advice. Monetization: Booking.com nearby-town accommodation CTA (travelers alternating camping + hotel nights on multi-day itinerary); GYG Israel hiking day tour CTA. Cross-links: hiking-in-israel, israel-national-trail-hiking (backlog), glamping-israel (compare), national-parks-pass-calculator, israel-stargazing (Makhtesh Ramon camping + stargazing combo), israel-night-hiking. SEO: "camping in Israel guide", "INPA campgrounds Israel", "Israel tent camping", "Israel National Trail camping guide", "where to camp in Israel backpacking". Sources: inpa.gov.il/camping (official INPA), israeltrekking.com/camping-in-israel, campinginisrael.com, israelnationaltrail.com, touristisrael.com/camping-in-israel. [iter255 research] status: ready

- [P3] (seo-content, S) Israel stand-up paddleboarding guide (/israel-stand-up-paddle) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "stand.up.paddle", "stand-up-paddle", "/sup-israel", "/israel-sup", "paddleboard.*israel" across full BACKLOG — ZERO matches (only adjacent match: "israel-sup" inside "/israel-supermarket-guide" text — unrelated). DISTINCT from: israel-surfing (P2 backlog — Mediterranean surfing on breaking waves; different beaches, skills, operators; SUP is calmer-water oriented); water-hiking-israel.md (SHIPPED — wadi stream hiking, not water sports); israel-adventure-sports.md (SHIPPED — multi-sport hub; SUP mentioned in one passing line only). tripadvisor.com/Attractions-g293983-Activities-c61-Israel (watersports) lists SUP operators; watersportisrael.com covers SUP rental; touristisrael.com lists Kinneret SUP. SUP has grown significantly in Israel across three distinct water bodies — each offering a different experience. Sea of Galilee / Kinneret (#1 SUP destination): freshwater lake at 211m below sea level; calm water ideal for complete beginners — no tide, no major currents; rental stations at Tiberias waterfront (several operators, May–October; board + paddle ~₪100–150/h); Kibbutz Ein Gev (east bank; quieter; Golan Heights backdrop); Capernaum / north shore (dramatic landscape, public beach launch). All Kinneret shoreline publicly accessible; motor boat traffic in designated lanes — SUP users stay close to shore or in buoyed SUP zones. Mediterranean beaches: Gordon Beach TLV (SUP rental in summer; afternoon sea breeze and chop = challenging for beginners; flat water optimal before 10am; ~₪100–150/h); Netanya beach (wider shore, calmer; more beginner-friendly); Dor Beach / Nahsholim (sheltered Carmel coast cove; calmest Mediterranean option; snorkeling also popular; INPA site). Red Sea / Eilat (best year-round SUP): Gulf of Aqaba; virtually flat water, no swell; exceptional water visibility; 22–27°C year-round; rental at Coral Beach Nature Reserve area + Eilat Yacht Club Marina; paddling over the reef = unique experience; CRITICAL: never stand or kneel on coral reef if you fall — devastating to reef ecology + personal safety; land on sandy areas only. SUP yoga: growing niche — classes offered at Tiberias Kinneret operators and TLV beach watersports centres; 45–90 min sessions; check Google Maps for current "SUP yoga Tel Aviv/Tiberias" providers. Beginner suitability: Sea of Galilee mornings = ideal complete beginner; Dor Beach = beginner; Mediterranean TLV/Netanya mornings = beginner-friendly; Red Sea Eilat = beginner-friendly (flat); Mediterranean afternoon = intermediate (wind chop + swell increases substantially after noon). HONESTY: specific rental pricing = "approximately ₪100–160 for 1h at time of research — verify directly with rental operator as prices change seasonally"; do NOT name single operators as definitive without noting "availability changes season to season — check current operators at [beach name] via Google Maps before visiting"; coral reef at Eilat: touching or standing on coral is illegal in the Coral Beach Nature Reserve and permanently damages the reef — state this prominently; Mediterranean afternoon waves genuinely challenging for beginners — be clear about morning recommendation. Monetization: Booking.com Eilat beach hotel CTA; GYG "watersports Israel" or "Eilat water sports" CTA (verify product availability). Cross-links: israel-surfing (backlog; compare board sports), eilat-travel-guide, tiberias-guide, israel-adventure-sports, sea-of-galilee-experiences (backlog). SEO: "stand up paddleboarding Israel guide", "SUP Sea of Galilee Israel", "paddleboarding Eilat Red Sea", "Israel water sports paddle board", "paddleboard Tel Aviv beach 2026". Sources: watersportisrael.com, tripadvisor.com/Attractions-g293983-Activities-c61-Israel, redsea-divers.com (Eilat SUP), touristisrael.com/things-to-do-water-sports, inpa.gov.il/dor-beach. [iter255 research] status: ready

- [P3] (seo-content, S) Tel Aviv White Night guide (/tel-aviv-white-night) — CONFIRMED GAP: full grep scan of backlog shows zero matches for "white night", "laila lavan", or "/tel-aviv-white-night"; events-festivals.md has 1-line mention only. touristisrael.com, secrettelaviv.com, timeout.com/israel, worldjewishtravel.org all have dedicated standalone White Night pages. Annual Tel Aviv cultural event since 2003 (White City UNESCO World Heritage anniversary), held last Thursday of June; 250,000+ attendees; dusk-to-dawn (approx 7pm–6am); 70+ cultural institutions open free all night — Tel Aviv Museum of Art, Eretz Israel Museum, Design Museum Holon, city parks, beaches, outdoor stages; distinct from Jerusalem Festival of Light (different city, different format — TLV is multi-venue-simultaneous rather than a walking route) and Tel Aviv Pride (different event, different June date). SEO angle: "Tel Aviv White Night" + "Laila Lavan" searches spike May–June each year — planning-season traffic. Content: what it is (White City = UNESCO 2003; Laila Lavan = Hebrew for "White Night"; organized by Tel Aviv municipality; participating venues post on tlv.gov.il; format changes annually — some years beach performances, some years rooftop events); how to navigate (city is entirely walkable during the event; Tel-O-Fun bike hire works all night; no single route — explore freely); where to focus (Gordon + Hilton Beach area for outdoor stages; Rothschild Blvd for architecture + outdoor art; HaYarkon Park for live music stages; ANU + Eretz Israel Museum Ramat Aviv for museum lovers); practical tips (wear comfortable walking shoes; bring water and light layer for sea breeze at 3am; Sheinkin St area = most concentrated food options open all night; most participants stay out 10pm–3am; dawn arrival at beach for sunrise is a bonus). HONESTY: participating venues change each year — always link tlv.gov.il/WhiteNight for current programme; event is free in principle but some venues may introduce ticketed-section elements — verify before visiting; weather is generally warm (TLV June = 28–30°C daytime, 24°C+ at night); sea humidity can feel warm — prepare accordingly. Dense cross-links: tel-aviv-beaches-guide (when built), tel-aviv-museums-guide, jerusalem-festival-of-light, events-festivals, tel-aviv-food-guide, lgbtq-travel-israel (Pride week is a different June event, link to avoid confusion). Monetization: Booking.com TLV hotel CTA (book 4–6 weeks ahead; White Night = peak weekend demand); GYG "Tel Aviv after-dark tour" CTA. SEO: "Tel Aviv White Night guide 2026", "Laila Lavan Tel Aviv", "White Night Israel June", "Tel Aviv dusk to dawn event". Sources: touristisrael.com/white-night-in-tel-aviv, secrettelaviv.com/tickets/white-night-tel-aviv, timeout.com/israel/things-to-do/white-night, worldjewishtravel.org/tel-aviv-white-night, tlv.gov.il. - [P3] (seo-content+monetization, S) Israel wine harvest season guide (/israel-wine-harvest-season) — CONFIRMED GAP: full grep scan shows no backlog entry for "wine.*harvest.*season", "harvest.*season.*wine", or "/israel-wine-harvest-season"; existing wine guides are geography-based (judean-hills-wine-trail SHIPPED, golan-heights-wineries backlog, negev-wine-route just added iter260); a SEASONAL harvest experience guide is a distinct format targeting visitors who want to time their visit around harvest. israelwineexp.com + winetourism.com/israel + Viator/GYG all have Israel harvest tourism content. Israeli grape harvest runs August (early varieties: Chardonnay, Sauvignon Blanc in Galilee) through October (Cabernet, Syrah in Judean Hills and Negev); Golan Heights September–October = peak harvest period. DISTINCT from geography-based wine guides: this is a TIMING + EXPERIENCE guide. Content: seasonal overview (early Aug: white varieties begin in Northern Galilee at 400–700m elevation; Galilee harvest September; Judean Hills October; Negev last to harvest); what harvest experiences are available (open-harvest days — Yarden, Golan Heights Winery, Dalton, Carmel announce annual "vendange" open days: guided vineyard walks + grape picking demo + barrel tasting + lunch; advance registration required; dates announced ~6 weeks before; check winery websites — no single aggregator); volunteer harvest programs (HaYovel Organization: Christian Zionist volunteers join Golan Heights kibbutz harvest, 1–3 weeks, room+board exchange; hayovel.com for annual signup; September–October; HONESTY: frame HaYovel clearly as a specific faith-based volunteer program, not generic; harvest Israel privately: some boutique wineries accept "harvest helper for a day" arrangements via direct contact — inquire at Clos de Gat, Margalit, Pelter individually); harvest festivals (several wineries run formal harvest events with food and entertainment: Carmel Winery near Zichron Ya'akov runs annual "Harvest Festival"; Binyamina Winery also runs events — verify annually at each winery's Instagram/site); Golan self-drive harvest route (Yarden Winery Katzrin → Pelter Winery Moshav Ein Zivan → Galil Mountain Winery Kibbutz Yiron → Golan Heights wine route; combined with Golan Heights sightseeing). HONESTY: harvest open-day dates change annually and are announced 4–8 weeks ahead — never hardcode specific dates; volunteer programs (HaYovel) are faith-based — describe accurately; winery harvest capacity is limited — registration fills fast; weather in harvest season is excellent (26–30°C days, cooler nights) but some outdoor harvest events cancelled in rain — check weather before final bookings; Israeli wine quality is genuine, not novelty — Yarden and Clos de Gat regularly win international medals (frame accurately). Dense cross-links: israel-wine-wineries, judean-hills-wine-trail, golan-heights-guide (when built), negev-wine-route, galilee-food-wine-guide (when built), best-time-to-visit-israel. Monetization: GYG/Viator "Israel winery tour" CTA (harvest-season tours listed) + Booking.com winery-adjacent accommodation (Zichron Ya'akov boutique hotels; Golan Heights kibbutz guesthouses). - [P3] (seo-content+monetization, S) Israel hot air balloon flights guide (/israel-hot-air-balloon) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep for "hot air balloon" + "balloon.*tour" + "balloon.*israel" across backlog — only mentions are INSIDE the Sukkot guide entry (one sentence) and Mount Tabor guide entry (one sentence within iter190 research); no standalone balloon guide entry. touristisrael.com + adventure-israel.com + GYG/Viator all list Israel balloon products. adventure-sports.md mentions "dawn balloon flights" in 1 passing sentence. Key experience: Galilee dawn balloon flights launch from the Beit Netofa Valley or Lower Galilee plateaux; at first light the balloon drifts over Sea of Galilee, Jezreel Valley, Mount Tabor and Mount Gilboa; typically 60–90 min flight duration; 4–6 passengers per basket plus pilot; bookable via GYG or direct (Balloon in Israel operator, SkyView Israel). Galilee Balloon Festival: annual festival held in the Galilee, typically during Rosh Hashanah/Sukkot holiday week (September/October); daytime tethered rides + demonstration flights; popular family event; exact location changes annually — link current-year festival website. Negev/Mitzpe Ramon balloons: some operators offer Negev desert dawn flights from Mitzpe Ramon area (IDA dark-sky park = excellent morning visibility; Ramon Crater landscape from above = spectacular; limited availability). Content: how to book (book 3–5 days ahead minimum; dawn flights require 5:00–5:30am meetup; pilot makes go/no-go decision 12h before based on wind; typical price ₪700–1,100/person including champagne breakfast on landing); what to expect (safety briefing 15 min; inflation and launch 30 min; flight 60–90 min; champagne toast on landing is a global ballooning tradition — Israeli operators maintain it with sparkling wine; photography excellent from basket — wide-angle lens ideal); practical tips (weather cancellations are common — book flex cancellation; morning air is calm, afternoon flights rare due to thermals; dress in layers, basket is cold at 300m even on warm days; minimum age varies by operator — typically 6+; weight limits usually 120kg per passenger). HONESTY: balloon flights are weather-dependent — cancellations happen; flights are NOT cheap (₪700–1,100+ is illustrative range, verify with operators; no fabricated prices); the Galilee Balloon Festival dates change annually and some years are not held — always link official festival site for current year; weight limits are real safety requirements — state clearly; operators are licensed by Israeli civil aviation authority (CAAI); "over the Sea of Galilee at dawn" framing is accurate for Lower Galilee flights but not all flights cover the same route — confirm with operator. Dense cross-links: israel-adventure-sports, galilee-tours-compared, mount-tabor-guide (when built), tiberias-guide, best-time-to-visit-israel. Monetization: GYG "Galilee hot air balloon" CTA (multiple 5-star products) + Booking.com Tiberias/Galilee accommodation CTA. Sources: touristisrael.com/hot-air-balloon-israel, getyourguide.com/galilee-balloon, adventure-israel.com/hot-air-balloon, balloonisrael.com. [iter265 research] status: ready
- [P3] (seo-content, S) Israel olive harvest agrotourism guide (/israel-olive-harvest) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep scan shows zero backlog matches for "olive.*harvest", "olive.*harvest.*tour", "olive.*pick" or "/israel-olive-harvest". Wine harvest guide (iter265) and this are complementary seasonal guides: olive harvest runs later (October–November) and covers different regions (Upper Galilee, Golan, Carmel, Nazareth area) via different activities. Israeli olive groves: Israel produces Koroneiki, Barnea, Souri varieties; Upper Galilee + Golan = primary production zones; Nazareth Arab community has multi-century olive oil tradition; kibbutz orchards also significant. Tourist harvest experiences: select kibbutzim and Arab village cooperatives offer harvest participation (pick-alongside-farmers, typically half-day 7am–noon before heat; traditional stone press demonstration; fresh oil tasting — cloudy green unfiltered oil directly after pressing = olive oil at its peak; different from supermarket oil); Galilee olive oil tasting tours (similar to wine tastings — boutique olive groves offer guided tastings, comparison of varieties and processing methods): Yad Mordechai kibbutz (southern Israel — significant olive production; visitors center sometimes open during harvest); Olives of the Galilee (organic Souri olive groves near Nazareth — small producers with tasting room; verify seasonal hours). HONESTY: exact harvest dates depend on rainfall and variety — October peak for early harvest, November for late varieties; tourist experiences are NOT standardized — fewer structured programs than wine harvest; most experiences require advance contact with specific farms/kibbutzim; do NOT conflate Israeli olive harvest content with the Palestinian olive harvest (which occurs in the same season in the West Bank and carries significant political context) — this guide should focus on Israeli-administered areas and be factual without making political comparisons; olive tree age: Israel's ancient olive trees (some 1,000+ years old) are a distinct draw — Beit Guvrin area has ancient trees, as does the Galilee; "ancient olive press" archaeological context is distinct from the agricultural harvest experience. Dense cross-links: galilee-food-wine-guide (when built), nazareth-travel-guide, galilee-tours-compared, israel-wine-harvest-season, best-time-to-visit-israel (October = excellent weather). SEO: "Israel olive harvest experience", "olive oil tour Israel", "Galilee olive picking", "Israel olive harvest tourism", "fresh olive oil Israel". Sources: tourismisrael.com/olive-harvest, galilee-experience.com, yad-mordechai.co.il, olivegrove-israel.com. [iter265 research] status: ready
- [P3] (seo-content, S) Hanukkah in Israel travel guide (/hanukkah-in-israel) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep scan shows zero backlog entries for "hanukkah.*israel.*guide", "chanukah.*guide", or "/hanukkah-in-israel"; traveling-israel-jewish-holidays.md covers Hanukkah in ~2-3 sentences as one of 6+ holiday sections; touristisrael.com + chabad.org + timeout.com/israel + jewishvirtuallibrary.org all have standalone Hanukkah in Israel pages. Hanukkah = most internationally recognizable Jewish holiday; major draw for diaspora visitors (especially North American Jewish families) who want to experience the holiday in its homeland; distinct from Passover (Passover guide in backlog) and High Holidays (in backlog) in tone — celebratory festival of lights. 2026 dates: evening Dec 14 → Dec 22; 2027: evening Dec 3 → Dec 11. Key content: Western Wall public menorah lighting (Erev Hanukkah = first night; massive public ceremony at Western Wall Plaza; Western Wall Heritage Foundation hosts official ceremony with dignitaries + prayers + lighting of the Hanukkiah; thousands of worshippers; arrive by 3pm for good position; first-night ceremony most attended; subsequent nights smaller but equally moving; free admission; link kotel.org.il for current year ceremony details); National menorah displays (Ben-Gurion Street Jaffa = giant public menorah; Independence Hall TLV; Dizengoff Square; Jerusalem City Hall plaza — each municipality has at least one major public menorah); sufganiyot culture (jelly donuts, filled with cream/jam, sold at bakeries + supermarkets + street stalls from the month before Hanukkah; every Israeli bakery competes on flavors — caramel, lotus, pistachio, classic jam; eating sufganiyot is a genuinely Israeli cultural experience; try Roladin or local bakeries in Mahane Yehuda for premium options); Hanukkah menorah customs for visitors (hotels in Israel often provide a guest Hanukkiah + candles — check with hotel; Jewish custom to place the menorah where it can be seen — window sills or doorposts; you may see menorahs in apartment windows throughout the 8 nights); Hanukkah and travel logistics (unlike Yom Kippur, Hanukkah does NOT close Ben Gurion Airport or public transport; buses and trains run normally; most restaurants and shops remain open; Hanukkah is NOT a public holiday in terms of closures — offices, shops, public transport all operate). HONESTY: Western Wall ceremony timing + logistics change annually — link kotel.org.il; sufganiyot are available from late November onward but peak quality is during the holiday itself; Hanukkah is theologically a MINOR holiday (not from the Torah — from the Book of Maccabees, not in the Hebrew Bible) but commercially and culturally it has become very prominent due to proximity to Christmas; frame accurately; menorah lighting is a religious act — approach respectfully; do NOT fabricate "best Hanukkah experiences" rankings. Dense cross-links: traveling-israel-jewish-holidays, western-wall-guide (when built), jerusalem-old-city-walking-tour, shabbat-guide, best-time-to-visit-israel, events-festivals.
