- [P3] (seo-content, S) Lag BaOmer in Israel guide (/lag-baomer-israel) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep scan shows zero backlog entries for "lag baomer", "lag.*omer", or "/lag-baomer"; traveling-israel-jewish-holidays.md covers Lag BaOmer in ~2 lines; touristisrael.com has a dedicated Lag BaOmer guide; aish.com covers Meron pilgrimage extensively; Israel's most visually spectacular domestic holiday is barely covered on our site. 2026 date: evening May 15 → May 16; 2027: May 4 → May 5. Lag BaOmer = 33rd day of the Omer counting between Passover and Shavuot; celebrated with bonfires nationwide and pilgrimage to Mount Meron. Key experiences: (1) Mount Meron pilgrimage — Rabbi Shimon bar Yochai's tomb (Rashbi, Talmudic sage, associated with Kabbalah and the Zohar); largest annual gathering of Jews in Israel (100,000+ concentrated on eve of Lag BaOmer); midnight bonfire lighting ceremony = emotionally powerful for observant Jews; CRITICAL SAFETY NOTE: after the 2021 crowd crush at Meron (45 deaths), Israeli government implemented mandatory pre-registration and sectored access (mount-meron.co.il for annual registration; must register weeks in advance; capacity limits enforced); recommend Safed as a safer alternative for tourist visitors unfamiliar with crowd dynamics; (2) Safed (Tzfat) Lag BaOmer — mystical city holds major bonfire celebration with Kabbalistic significance; far more manageable crowds than Meron; combines with general Safed tourism (Artists' Quarter, Kabbalist synagogues, Ha'Ari Sephardic Synagogue); 30-minute drive from Meron; recommended for visitors wanting the holiday experience without extreme crowds; (3) National bonfire tradition — entire country holds bonfires (neighborhood parks, open fields, beaches) on the eve of Lag BaOmer; bonfire smoke is a distinctive Israeli sensory experience; children start collecting wood weeks before; municipal areas designate bonfire zones; HONESTY: National bonfire tradition creates significant air pollution in Israeli cities; some health authorities advise staying indoors on Lag BaOmer eve — frame factually for health-conscious visitors; Meron experience is VERY crowded and entry is controlled — registration system described above; it is NOT appropriate to simply show up at Meron without registration (safety enforcement active since 2022). Dense cross-links: traveling-israel-jewish-holidays, safed-tzfat-guide, galilee-tours-compared, events-festivals, best-time-to-visit-israel (May = excellent weather). SEO: "Lag BaOmer Israel guide", "Mount Meron Lag BaOmer", "bonfire night Israel", "Israel Lag BaOmer pilgrimage", "Lag BaOmer travel Israel". Strong demand: Israeli wine quality surged internationally 2020–2026 (Golan Heights Winery, Clos de Gat, Flam, Recanati = regular international medal winners); urban wine bar scene has exploded in TLV especially in the natural/biodynamic category since 2022. Content: Tel Aviv wine bars (Norman Wine Bar — Hayarkon 30, TLV's most acclaimed natural wine list, 100+ wines by the glass, Mediterranean small plates, reserve 3+ days ahead; Sputnik Bar — Florentin, natural and orange wines, industrial-chic, open late; Bimat HaAmud — Rothschild area, Israeli-focused wine bar with tasting flights; Shesek — small Jaffa wine and food bar with rotating Israeli boutique labels; wine-focused stalls at Sarona Market); Jerusalem wine bars (Sputnik Jerusalem — Nahalat Shiva, natural wine forward; HaMesila — German Colony, wine and cheese pairings; Yulia — Mahane Yehuda vicinity, Israeli wine + mezze); beer vs wine neighborhoods (Florentin TLV = natural wine bar heartland; Rothschild Blvd = upscale wine-focused dining; Emek Refaim Jerusalem = wine café culture; German Colony = bottle-shop-with-bar format); how to drink Israeli wine well (Israeli wine region overview in 3 sentences — Golan Heights: cooler temps, elegant reds; Judean Hills: limestone altitude, age-worthy reds; Galilee: diverse, fruit-forward; Negev: bold desert reds; Shomron/Carmel: coastal, accessible); natural wine in Israel context (strong natural movement since 2018 — Jonathan Recanati's RCAN label; Lotem Winery; Agur small batch; Dalton Alma cuvée — these appear in TLV natural bars frequently); practical (wine by the glass ₪35–80 typical; flight tastings ₪90–150; Israeli wine bars are generally open Sat night — not Shabbat-observant like most restaurants; reservations for Norman Wine Bar strongly advised). HONESTY: wine bar scene is evolving rapidly — specific venue names and lists may change; never fabricate specific wine scores or ratings; price ranges are estimates from public sources; Israeli natural wine scene is genuine but smaller than French/Italian — frame accurately, not as "Israel rivals Burgundy"; Shabbat closure patterns vary by venue. - [P3] (seo-content, S) Israel sport fishing & angling guide (/israel-fishing-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "fishing.*guide", "angling", "sport.*fishing", "israel.*fish.*tour", "sea.*fishing.*israel" across full backlog — only match was "Ancient Boat Museum at Ginosar (2,000-year-old wooden fishing boat)" in Sea of Galilee boat-tours entry = a museum artifact, NOT sport fishing; no standalone fishing guide. Israel offers 3 distinct sport fishing zones that appeal to different audiences: (1) Mediterranean Sea — charter fishing boats from Herzliya Marina (closest major marina to TLV; ~8 charter operators), Jaffa/Tel Aviv port, and Haifa port; target species: amberjack (palamida), grouper (locus), sea bream (denise), bluefin tuna (seasonal); typical charter: ₪400–700/person 4–6h, includes tackle + bait; best season April–October; (2) Red Sea Eilat — big-game fishing reputation; target: barracuda, mahi-mahi, yellowfin tuna, queenfish; charter boats from Eilat Marina (multiple operators); sport-fishing tourism is a recognized Eilat tourism segment alongside diving; best April–June and September–November (calmer Red Sea); boats leave at dawn ~5am; (3) Sea of Galilee / Kinneret — freshwater fishing; requires INPA fishing license (₪50/year, available at parks.org.il or regional offices); main target: tilapia (musht — the "St. Peter's Fish" of restaurant menus); strict daily catch limits (check current INPA regulations; quotas have been tightened to protect Kinneret stock); boat rentals from Tiberias and Ginosar; hand lines and rods from shore also common at Ein Gev and Tiberias promenade. HONESTY: no fabricated catch rates or "guaranteed species" language; Mediterranean charter fishing = weather-dependent (cancellations common Oct–March); INPA Kinneret fishing regulations change annually — always link parks.org.il for current rules; commercial Kinneret fishing almost completely banned since 2018 to allow stock recovery — sport fishing is subject to strict limits; Red Sea fishing is subject to INPA marine reserve regulations near Coral Beach; Eilat charter prices vary by season and operator — link to marina directly, no hardcoded prices; "St. Peter's Fish" / tilapia served in Tiberias restaurants is mostly farmed today, not wild-caught — a factual note worth including. Cross-links: eilat-diving-snorkeling (Red Sea context), sea-of-galilee-experiences (boat tours + Kinneret), galilee-food-guide (St. Peter's Fish restaurant tradition), tiberias-guide, israel-national-parks-pass (INPA license context). SEO: "fishing in Israel guide", "Israel sport fishing", "Eilat fishing charter", "Sea of Galilee fishing", "Mediterranean fishing Israel". Sources: herzliyamarina.co.il, eilatmarina.co.il, parks.org.il/fishing, getyourguide.com/israel-fishing, touristisrael.com/sport-fishing-in-israel. [iter270 research] status: ready

Context: Israeli artisan cheese scene is internationally significant (voted in top 10 "cheese-producing countries worth visiting" by International Dairy Federation; Kibbutz Sde Eliyahu's organic dairy; Galilee/Golan goat and sheep cheese farms). Content: key cheese regions (Upper Galilee/Golan = Israel's premier cheese zone; Carmel Highlands; Jordan Valley kibbutzim); notable producers and visits (Kibbutz Sde Eliyahu — certified organic dairy; visitor programs available by advance booking; produces Emmental-style, Gouda-style, and regional soft cheeses; HaKevutza Farm near Tiberias — sheep milk cheese, farm-stays available; Har Beit Milk Golan — goat cheese at Moshav Neve Ativ in northern Golan, near Mount Hermon; Galil Mountain area artisan producers — often only open by advance appointment); Chag HaHalav — Israel's dairy festival (Shavuot-adjacent event typically held at Kibbutz Lotan or event grounds in the Arava Valley; celebrates Israel's dairy heritage; artisan cheese tastings, cheesemaking workshops, farm-to-table meals; dates follow Jewish calendar — check annual organizers; 2026 Shavuot = June 1–2 evening); practical cheese tourism circuit (self-drive from Tiberias/Katzrin in Golan: 1 day visiting 2–3 farms; phone-ahead essential — most farms are NOT walk-in friendly; some kibbutz dairies operate small shops open specific morning hours); Israeli cheese at retail (for tastings without driving: Shufersal chain supermarkets carry wide artisan Israeli cheese range; Jerusalem Mahane Yehuda market dairy vendors; TLV Levinsky Market spice stalls often carry specialty cheeses); Druze labaneh connection (Druze villages Daliyat al-Carmel and Isfiya = fresh labaneh + olive oil + za'atar breakfasts = most accessible artisan dairy experience for visitors). HONESTY: farm visit availability is not guaranteed — advance contact required for every listed farm; kibbutz visitor programs change seasonally; Israeli artisan cheese is genuinely high-quality but not comparable in variety to French/Italian — frame accurately; Chag HaHalav dates and location change annually — link official sources when available; never fabricate specific tasting notes or cheese competitions won. - [P3] (seo-content, S) Israel multicultural sites & Jewish-Arab coexistence tourism guide (/israel-coexistence-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: searched "coexistence", "multicultural", "jewish.*arab.*site", "shared.*society", "binational" across full backlog — ZERO matches. golan-druze-villages.md (SHIPPED) covers Druze villages specifically; haifa-travel-guide.md (SHIPPED) covers Haifa as a destination but not specifically its coexistence/multicultural angle as a tourism focus; NO guide frames Israel's multicultural reality as a tourism experience. Growing traveler segment: international visitors (especially Jewish diaspora, educators, interfaith groups, NGO workers) seeking to understand Israel's internal cultural complexity beyond conflict news. Competitors: nativeisrael.com, peaceisrael.org, neveshalom.org (official Neve Shalom tourism page), touristisrael.com/arab-cities-in-israel all have relevant content; zero equivalent editorial on site. Content: Haifa — Israel's most celebrated coexistence city (Jewish-Arab-Druze-Bahá'í populations; Wadi Nisnas Arab neighborhood with Museum of the Unknown Artist; "Holiday of Holidays" multicultural festival December/January = free street festival celebrating Hanukkah + Christmas + Ramadan/Eid together; German Colony Jewish-Arab residential coexistence; Bahá'í Gardens as symbol of religious pluralism; Technion university = Jewish + Arab student integration; HONESTY: Haifa is celebrated for coexistence but still has economic disparities between Arab and Jewish neighborhoods — frame honestly); Neve Shalom / Wahat al-Salam (intentional village 25km from TLV; founded 1970s; ~60 families, half Jewish-Israeli half Palestinian-Israeli; runs School for Peace programs; visitor and volunteer programs via neveshalom.org; open to guided visits — contact in advance; a genuine experiment in living coexistence, not a tourist attraction; HONESTY: the village is small and its community is not a performance — visitor programs are educational partnerships, not tours; book well in advance; the village's own website should be the only reference for booking); Akko/Acre (UNESCO Old City; Arab-majority city with Jewish minority + mixed business district; the city is genuinely multicultural in practice; Crusader-era architecture is equally Arab and Jewish and Christian; the annual Akko Fringe Theater Festival = 5-day festival Oct bringing Israeli Jewish and Arab theater companies to perform together in the Old City alleys); Jisr az-Zarqa (Israel's only coastal Arab city; the contrast between Caesarea's archaeological wealth and Jisr az-Zarqa's poverty is stark and often cited in Israeli public discourse; some tour operators offer "contrast tours" including both — frame honestly the economic dimension rather than merely as "coexistence tourism"); Jaffa / Yafo (rapidly gentrifying Arab historic city absorbed into Tel Aviv municipality; Muslim Quarter around Hassan Bek Mosque; Saint Peter's Church; Arab-Jewish restaurants side-by-side; HONESTY: Jaffa's gentrification is controversial — many long-term Arab residents displaced; this coexistence narrative has significant tension and context; editorial framing must acknowledge complexity). HONESTY: this guide must avoid both "Israel is a multicultural paradise" framing AND "coexistence is impossible" framing — both are reductive; Neve Shalom and Haifa's Holiday of Holidays are genuine achievements; Jaffa and Jisr az-Zarqa show genuine economic disparities; the guide's value is honest complexity; avoid political advocacy; cite official village websites (neveshalom.org, haifa.gov.il, akko.muni.il) as authoritative sources; never fabricate quotes from community members or statistics about coexistence success. Dense cross-links: haifa-travel-guide, jaffa-travel-guide, golan-druze-villages, akko-acre-guide, is-israel-safe (for context on inter-community safety). - [SHIPPED iter277 → adbfd22] Rosh Hashanah in Israel travel guide (/rosh-hashanah-in-israel) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep scan shows zero backlog entries for "jewish new year", "rosh.*hash", or "/rosh-hashanah"; traveling-israel-jewish-holidays.md covers High Holidays briefly; touristisrael.com, secrettelaviv.com, and iTravelJerusalem all have dedicated Rosh Hashanah guides. Rosh Hashanah = most important Jewish holiday and the primary draw for diaspora Jewish travelers timing a visit; Sep 11-13 2026, Sep 2-4 2027. Key experiences: Western Wall for Musaf Rosh Hashanah prayer (tens of thousands; free entry; arrive early for position; both Sephardic and Ashkenazi congregations hold services on the plaza; kotel.org.il for current-year schedule); Kol Nidre at synagogues (Yom Kippur eve, 10 days after Rosh Hashanah — distinct from Rosh Hashanah itself but diaspora visitors often experience both; elaborate choral versions in Jerusalem's historic synagogues; advance tickets required for most synagogues); Tashlich ceremony (symbolic casting of sins into flowing water; popular on the Yarkon River in Tel Aviv, the Sea of Galilee in Tiberias, and the Mediterranean shore; first day of Rosh Hashanah afternoon; public, welcoming, photogenic); Rosh Hashanah food experiences (apple and honey dipping is the iconic custom; round challah symbolizes the year's cycle; pomegranate seeds for blessings; Mahane Yehuda Market and Levinsky Market put on seasonal honey and challah displays; restaurant special menus available — booking essential); hotel advance booking (hotels fill months ahead during the High Holiday season; Jerusalem and Dead Sea resort hotels especially tight; book 6–12 months ahead for Rosh Hashanah period; some hotels run multi-day full board "Yom Tov packages" with all meals included). Practical logistics: national transport runs reduced Friday-ish schedules on Rosh Hashanah eve (similar to Shabbat); Yom Kippur 10 days later = all roads empty (car-free); most government offices closed both for Rosh Hashanah and the intermediate days (Aseret Yemei Teshuva). HONESTY: synagogue services are religious events — dress modestly; non-Jewish visitors are welcome as respectful observers at most synagogues and at the Kotel plaza; Kotel Tunnels tours are suspended on Rosh Hashanah itself; hotel prices surge 40–80% during Yom Tov — always book early; Tashlich is a public outdoor custom open to all observers. Dense cross-links: traveling-israel-jewish-holidays, western-wall-guide, shabbat-guide, jerusalem-old-city-walking-tour, best-time-to-visit-israel, mahane-yehuda-market-guide. Monetization: Booking.com Jerusalem hotels CTA (advance booking messaging) + GYG Rosh Hashanah Jerusalem tour CTA. - [SHIPPED iter281 → e1f9d2a] Kerem HaTeimanim (Yemenite Quarter) Tel Aviv guide (/kerem-hateimanim-tel-aviv) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep scan shows zero backlog entries for "kerem.*hateimanim", "hateimanim", or "yemenite.*quarter"; tel-aviv-neighborhoods-guide.md has broad TLV neighborhood coverage but no dedicated Kerem HaTeimanim standalone guide; tel-aviv-carmel-market.md covers the adjacent Carmel Market; beinharimtours.com has a dedicated Kerem HaTeimanim walking guide page, timeout.com/israel has a Kerem neighborhood guide, Fodor's has it, igoogledisrael.com has a dedicated section, itraveltelaviv.com covers it. Historic Yemenite Jewish neighborhood founded in 1904 southwest of Carmel Market; one of TLV's oldest and most distinct neighborhoods; architecture = low-rise 2-story whitewashed buildings with decorative iron balconies; main street HaKovshim; distinct cultural identity from Ashkenazi/Central European TLV. Key content: Yemenite food culture (jachnun = slow-baked rolled pastry, traditionally served Saturday morning with grated tomato, boiled egg, and z'hug chili paste; malawach = pan-fried flaky flatbread; lachuch = spongy Yemenite crumpet; Yemenite soups — hilbe fenugreek soup and merak are weekend staples; kubbaneh = overnight bread for Shabbat morning; the neighborhood has several family-run Yemenite restaurants serving these dishes: Yemenite House, Shaul's Inn, Shlomo and Doron are historically cited — verify current operating before publishing); food market edge (Kerem HaTeimanim's northern edge abuts the Carmel Market — the transition point where the Yemenite Quarter becomes market territory is a distinctive experience; spice shops, dried fruit, date honey specialty); neighborhood atmosphere (narrow lanes; laundry between buildings; cats; old men playing backgammon outside; very photogenic but an actual living neighborhood — photography of individuals requires sensitivity); creative/LGBTQ scene (the neighborhood's affordable rents historically attracted artists and now has a LGBTQ-friendly bar and café scene, particularly HaKovshim Street, distinct from the glitzy Allenby/Dizengoff nightlife scene); walking from Carmel Market (15-minute walk south from main Carmel Market entrance; self-guided walk using HaKovshim + HaArzot + surrounding lanes). HONESTY: the neighborhood is a living residential community — treat respectfully; restaurant names change over years — verify currently operating before publishing; some venues have sporadic hours — call ahead; the LGBTQ-friendliness of the neighborhood is genuine but not universally marketed; Yemenite food in Israel bears little resemblance to Yemeni cuisine in the Middle East context (it developed distinctly in Israel over 100+ years) — frame as Israeli-Yemenite cuisine. Dense cross-links: tel-aviv-carmel-market-guide, tel-aviv-neighborhoods-guide, tel-aviv-food-guide, israel-food-tours-cooking-classes, tel-aviv-nightlife. Monetization: GYG Yemenite food tour CTA (multiple products offered including a "Yemenite Quarter food walk") + Booking.com TLV accommodation CTA. - [P2] (seo-content+monetization, M) Egypt, Jordan & Israel 3-country itinerary guide (/egypt-jordan-israel-itinerary) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep scan for "egypt.*jordan.*israel", "3.*country.*middle", "cairo.*petra.*jerusalem" shows ZERO backlog entries for the 3-country combined route; DISTINCT from: (a) israel-jordan-itinerary (10-day Israel+Jordan only, P2 S backlog) and (b) israel-egypt-guide (P2 M backlog — Israel + Egypt dual entry); the 3-country circuit is a distinct SEO target and a distinct logistical challenge. touristisrael.com has THREE dedicated 3-country packages (10-day, 12-day, 14-day); globalhighlights.com has a full editorial; TourRadar lists 10+ operators; kimkim, pilgrimtours.com, egypttoursplus.com all have complete guides. One of the world's most-searched Middle East travel itineraries; high commercial intent (typical tour packages $5,000–8,000/person for 10-14 days). Content: route overview (2 canonical route options: (A) Israel-first: arrive Ben Gurion → Israel (Jerusalem/Tel Aviv/Galilee/Masada/Dead Sea) → overland to Jordan Aqaba via Wadi Araba crossing or fly TLV-AMM → Petra/Wadi Rum → Amman → fly Amman-Cairo or overland Aqaba-Eilat then ferry/fly from Eilat to Sharm/Cairo → Egypt (Cairo/Luxor/Alexandria); (B) Egypt-first: reverse route; most group tours choose Israel-first to take advantage of Ben Gurion Airport's international connections); border crossings (Israel-Jordan: Wadi Araba crossing near Eilat/Aqaba most used; Allenby Bridge crossing for the West Bank route — note: West Bank crossing adds complexity and is NOT recommended for tourist circuit purposes; Rabin/Yitzhak Rabin Peace Crossing south of Eilat = most tourist-friendly; Israeli passport stamps — Jordan and Egypt both accept Israeli passport holders but a passport with Israeli stamps can cause issues in other Arab/Muslim-majority countries outside the 3 — advise using a separate passport or new pages if possible; Jordan and Egypt border crossing fees; timing considerations — border crossings take 2-4 hours); itinerary frameworks (10-day: Israel 5 days, Jordan 3 days, Egypt 2 days — tight; 14-day: Israel 6 days, Jordan 4 days, Egypt 4 days — recommended minimum for Cairo + Luxor; 21-day: in-depth); tour operators (Abraham Tours runs Israel + Jordan packages; Bein Harim operates Israel-Jordan-Egypt group tours; for Egypt-specific, recommend partnering with Intrepid/G Adventures or local Cairo operators; important: Israeli travel agents cannot legally operate in Egypt — the Egypt leg typically requires a separate Egyptian operator); Egypt entry requirements (Israeli citizens can enter Egypt; travelers with Israeli passport stamps in third-country passports — check entry requirements for Egypt and Jordan annually as policies evolve; Sinai-only visa vs full Egypt visa distinction — most 3-country itineraries require full Egypt tourist visa). HONESTY: border crossing times are estimates — can be longer; policies on Israeli passport stamps in Arab countries CHANGE — always check FCDO/State Dept advisories at time of travel; this itinerary is genuinely complex and usually better done with a tour operator for first-timers; Abraham Tours and Bein Harim are legitimate Israel-side operators but cannot book the Egypt leg independently; flight connections within the circuit (e.g., TLV-AMM-AQJ) may require day-before arrival in Amman — factor into schedule; "3-country in 10 days" is rushed — honest framing essential; Egyptian antiquities tourism is authentic but the pace of a combined trip limits depth. Dense cross-links: israel-jordan-itinerary, israel-egypt-guide, eilat-guide, petra-jordan-day-trip (if exists, else jordan-day-trips), transportation-in-israel, best-tours-in-israel. - [P3] (seo-content, S) Israel travel guide for food allergy sufferers (/israel-food-allergies-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep scan for "food.*allerg", "allerg.*travel", "sesame.*israel", "/israel-food-allerg" shows ZERO backlog matches; DISTINCT from the P2 S celiac/gluten-free Israel guide (separate backlog item, pending); the food allergy guide covers different allergens and different practical challenges. foodallergy.org has an Israel travel page; wanderlux.com has "Traveling in Israel with Food Allergies"; emacare.com has an Israel allergy guide; TripAdvisor Israel forum has many threads; NO Israeli travel editorial site has a comprehensive standalone food allergy guide. Key practical facts: (1) No mandatory EU-style allergen labeling in Israel — Israeli law does not require the 14-allergen box; labeling is in Hebrew and Arabic only; only the largest manufacturers voluntarily add allergy information in English; (2) Sesame is UBIQUITOUS and the highest-risk allergen — tahini (sesame paste) is in hummus, some challah breads, burekas filling/crust, salad dressings, sabich sauce, many pastries; sesame seeds on many breads; nut-free restaurants may still use sesame; important for travelers with sesame allergy (FDA named sesame as a major allergen in 2021 — Israeli labeling does not follow US/EU allergy disclosure standards); (3) Tree nuts in desserts (baklava, halva = sesame + honey but often contains pistachio; Druze sweets use walnut; Moroccan pastries with almond; bakeries do not routinely disclose nut content); (4) Eggs — eggs are in many Israeli pastries, shakshuka (obviously), bourekas, many café items; IMPORTANT: Israeli Passover means EXTRA eggs in traditional foods during that week; (5) Cross-contamination risk — open kitchen counters at market stalls and buffets (Israeli hotel breakfast buffets are elaborate but cross-contamination is common; speak to the dining room manager before buffet service; hotel kitchens are most accommodating for allergen requests in Israel; kibbutz-style communal dining is harder to accommodate); practical tools (allergyisrael.org.il — the primary Israeli food allergy resource; provides Hebrew allergy cards for 12 common allergens; use these printed or on phone screen at restaurants; Israeli chefs generally responsive to clearly-stated allergy concerns; "ani alergi le-..." = "I am allergic to..."; the word "alergi" is understood in Hebrew; some restaurants maintain separate allergen-free preparation areas — ask when booking); restaurant types ranked by allergy safety (hotel dining rooms: most accommodating — advance notice and kitchen separation possible; kosher dairy restaurants: NO meat or seafood, fish allergen risk lower, but still heavy dairy; kosher meat restaurants: NO dairy, but sesame and nut risk in sauces; Arab restaurants in Nazareth/Akko: may use less processed ingredients but limited English communication; market stalls: lowest safety margin — cross-contamination very common). HONESTY: Israel does not have EU-style food safety labeling and travelers must be proactive; cannot guarantee any restaurant is safe — frame as practical guidance, not guarantees; allergyisrael.org.il is an independent resource — link to it rather than claiming its content as our own; sesame allergy is particularly challenging in Israel because sesame is culturally central to Israeli cuisine; travelers with severe anaphylactic allergies should consult an allergist before traveling; EpiPen/adrenaline auto-injectors are available in Israeli pharmacies (called "EpiPen" in Hebrew too; no prescription required for purchase). Dense cross-links: israel-food-guide, israel-kosher-food-guide (when built), traveling-israel-jewish-holidays (Passover food context), israel-travel-tips, is-israel-safe. SEO: "food allergies Israel travel", "sesame allergy Israel guide", "traveling Israel food allergy", "Israel allergy cards Hebrew", "Israel restaurant food allergy 2026". Sources: foodallergy.org/israel-travel, wanderlux.com/israel-food-allergies, allergyisrael.org.il, emacare.com/israel, tripadvisor.com/ShowForum-g293977-i2055-Israel.html (allergy forum threads). [iter275 research] status: ready
- [P3] (seo-content, S) Tisha B'Av in Israel travel guide (/tisha-bav-in-israel) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep scan shows ZERO backlog entries for "tisha", "Tisha", "9th of av", "ninth of av", or "/tisha-bav"; traveling-israel-jewish-holidays.md covers Tisha B'Av in passing; goisrael.com (official Ministry of Tourism) has a dedicated Tisha B'Av page; planitisrael.com has "Experiencing Tisha B'Av in Jerusalem: A Spiritual Journey"; thekotel.org has multiple dedicated Tisha B'Av pages. DISTINCT from Yom Kippur (different character — mourning vs atonement) and from High Holidays guide (different context). 2026 date: July 22-23 (begins sundown July 22); 2027: August 11-12. Tisha B'Av commemorates the destruction of both Temples and other Jewish tragedies; a 25-hour fast; Jerusalem's most solemn annual day. Key experiences for visitors: Western Wall Plaza on Tisha B'Av eve (Eicha — the Book of Lamentations — is chanted publicly at the Wall; starts after sundown; thousands gather; sitting on low chairs or the ground (as in mourning) throughout the service; haunting, incredibly moving atmosphere for any visitor; the Old City streets are unusually quiet and solemn; non-Jewish visitors welcome as respectful observers; no photography requested by the community during services — be mindful); Old City atmosphere (the day after — Tisha B'Av daytime — Old City streets are quieter than on any other day except possibly Yom Kippur; most Jewish-owned shops closed; the Muslim Quarter and Christian sites operate normally; a unique opportunity to experience the Old City in deep quiet; some visitors describe it as the most spiritually powerful day to visit Jerusalem even for non-Jews); synagogue Kinot readings (morning service includes Kinot — the lamentation poetry of mourning; Ben Ezra Synagogue in Cairo has ancient Kinot manuscripts; in Jerusalem, many synagogues open their doors — Hurva Synagogue holds a particularly large morning service); practical logistics (all public transport runs normally on Tisha B'Av itself; unlike Yom Kippur, roads are NOT empty; Ben Gurion Airport operates fully; restaurants outside the Old City and Jewish neighborhoods operate normally; it is inappropriate to eat or drink in the Western Wall Plaza area during the fast; most Israeli Jews who are not strictly religious eat normally on Tisha B'Av; the degree of observance varies enormously; the day is NOT a public holiday in the sense of transport shutdowns). HONESTY: the atmosphere at the Western Wall on Tisha B'Av eve is deeply religious — visitors should follow the lead of the community; photography during Eicha services is strongly discouraged; dress modestly (shoulders and knees covered for both men and women; head covering for men at the Wall); the commemoration is of genuine historical tragedy — approach with appropriate seriousness; Eicha chanting times at the Wall change annually (sundown = variable by Jewish calendar date) — link kotel.org.il for current-year timing. Dense cross-links: traveling-israel-jewish-holidays, western-wall-guide, jerusalem-old-city-walking-tour, shabbat-guide, best-time-to-visit-israel. SEO: "Tisha B'Av Israel guide", "Tisha B'Av Jerusalem Western Wall", "9th of Av Israel experience", "Eicha reading Jerusalem", "visiting Jerusalem Tisha B'Av". Sources: goisrael.com/tisha-bav, planitisrael.com/tisha-bav-jerusalem, thekotel.org/tisha-bav, kotel.org.il (for annual schedule). [iter275 research] status: ready
- [P3] (seo-content, S) Mea Shearim ultra-Orthodox Jerusalem neighborhood guide (/mea-shearim-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep scan shows zero standalone backlog entry for "mea.*shearim", "mea she.*arim", or "/mea-shearim"; ONLY current site mentions are: (a) SHIPPED israel-film-tv-tourism.md — Shtisel filming location reference (1 paragraph); (b) Jerusalem neighborhoods context (brief mention in a broader guide); no standalone dedicated guide. beinharimtours.com has a dedicated Mea Shearim ultra-Orthodox Neighborhood page; Danny the Digger has a standalone guide; America Israel Tours has a guide; inandaroundjerusalem.com has a detailed walking guide; TripAdvisor lists it as a major Jerusalem attraction with 1,000+ reviews; GYG has "Mea Shearim hidden gems" tours; goguided.tours has a dedicated ultra-Orthodox neighborhood tour. Jerusalem's largest Haredi (ultra-Orthodox) Jewish neighborhood; founded 1874; one of the oldest Jewish neighborhoods outside the Old City; primary daily language is Yiddish (not Hebrew — Hebrew considered sacred for prayer); a genuine living community, not a tourist attraction; visited by both Jewish and non-Jewish travelers. Key content: neighborhood character (approximately 10-minute walk from Damascus Gate; population ~30,000; conservative/Hasidic and Mitnagdic communities; distinctive dress — black coats, hats, white shirts for men; modest dress for women; Yiddish as spoken language; strong anti-Zionist political stance among some communities — not incompatible with Israeli citizenship); visitor guidelines (dress code: ALL visitors must wear long trousers/long skirts below the knee; arms covered to elbow; no shorts, sleeveless tops, or tight clothing; head covering for men is respectful; signs at neighborhood entrances state dress requirements clearly); photography (signs throughout the neighborhood request NO photography; photographing individuals without explicit permission is disrespectful and will cause upset; street scenes and architecture without people may be photographed with sensitivity; some community members will object to any cameras; guided tours usually advise stowing cameras); best visiting times (Thursday evening = busy with Shabbat preparation — challah buying, fish market, fresh produce; Shabbat itself = completely quiet but visitors should understand Shabbat rules: no photographs, no driving through the neighborhood; the transformation between Thursday evening rush and Friday afternoon quiet is remarkable); the shuk/market (Mea Shearim Market on the main street = excellent for traditional Jewish items: menorahs, Shabbat candle holders, kippot, Jewish books, traditional food); cholent culture (cholent = slow-cooked stew; eaten Saturday midday after synagogue; some shops sell Thursday/Friday cholent for pre-Shabbat sale; uniquely Ashkenazi Jewish food experience). HONESTY: this guide MUST be framed as visiting a living community as a respectful guest, not as a tourist attraction or a zoo; some Mea Shearim residents object to tourism entirely — acknowledge this honestly; violations of dress code or photography requests have caused incidents; group tours may disturb residents — smaller groups preferable; some religious extremists have thrown things at immodestly dressed visitors — this should be noted as a safety consideration, not suppressed; the community's anti-Zionist stance (e.g., Neturei Karta) should be acknowledged factually without editorial judgment; Mea Shearim is safe for respectful visitors — frame the precautions as etiquette, not danger; GYG guided tours may offer a safer first experience for unfamiliar visitors. Dense cross-links: jerusalem-old-city-walking-tour, holy-sites-dress-code-etiquette, israel-film-tv-tourism (Shtisel), shabbat-guide, traveling-israel-jewish-holidays. Monetization: GYG "Mea Shearim walking tour" CTA (guided tour recommended given etiquette complexity). SEO: "Mea Shearim guide Jerusalem", "visiting ultra-Orthodox Jerusalem neighborhood", "Mea She'arim etiquette dress code", "Jerusalem Haredi neighborhood visit", "Mea Shearim tour guide". - [SHIPPED iter282 → 8933b7b] Herzliya day trip & beach city guide (/herzliya-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep scan for "herzliya.*guide", "/herzliya", "apollonia.*national", "herzliya.*marina" across full backlog — ZERO standalone entry; netanya-guide.md (SHIPPED) covers the coastal city to the north but NOT Herzliya; day-trips-from-tel-aviv references Herzliya only in passing as one of many options; tripadvisor.com, trip.com, expedia.com, travelocity.com, isrotel.com/exploring_herzliya, touristisrael.com/herzliya all rank for "things to do in Herzliya" with zero editorial from our site. Herzliya = upscale coastal city 20 min from TLV on Green Line light rail; distinct identity from Netanya or Tel Aviv. Key attractions: (1) Apollonia National Park (Arsuf) — INPA site on coastal cliffs; well-preserved Crusader fortress ruins (13th-century Arsuf Crusader castle) + Byzantine and Hellenistic remains; dramatic cliff-edge ruins with Mediterranean sea views; archaeological significance: excavations ongoing; INPA pass valid; moderate hike on clifftop trail overlooking sea; ~2h visit; seasonal hours — verify inpa.gov.il; one of Israel's most visually striking coastal parks; (2) Herzliya Marina — upscale waterfront district; restaurants, cafes, boutiques, and yacht berths; afternoon destination for walking, dining, coffee; distinct from Jaffa Port (historic) — Herzliya Marina is modern Mediterranean; (3) Acadia Beach (Herzliya Pituach beach strip) — Blue Flag beach; lifeguarded summer season; beach chair rental; less crowded than TLV beaches on weekdays; beachside restaurants; (4) Herzliya Museum of Contemporary Art — HerzliyaMuseum.co.il; rotating contemporary art exhibitions; architecturally distinctive building; worth 1h visit for art-interested visitors. Getting there: Green Line light rail Tel Aviv-Center → Herzliya Station (5 stops, ~20 min, ₪6.90); or Egged bus 501/502 from Tel Aviv Central (30 min). From Herzliya Station: Apollonia NP 3km by taxi/bus; Marina 2km; Museum in city center 1.5km. Combining: Apollonia morning (2h, clifftop views + ruins) → Marina lunch (2h, waterfront dining) → Museum afternoon (1h) → Acadia Beach (sunset swim) = full day from TLV. HONESTY: Apollonia Park entry fees and hours change — always link inpa.gov.il; Marina restaurant prices = upscale by Israeli standards (₪80–160 main course range — editorial; verify at each restaurant); Herzliya Pituach area = wealthy suburb, not a typical Israeli city experience; beach sun safety June–August (UV index 8–11); no fabricated attraction rankings. Dense cross-links: day-trips-from-tel-aviv, netanya-guide, israel-national-parks-pass (Apollonia = INPA card valid), tel-aviv-beaches-guide (when built), first-time-in-israel. Monetization: Booking.com Herzliya hotel CTA (InterContinental David + boutique Marina hotels) + GYG/Viator day tour from TLV CTA. - [P3] (seo-content, S) Nachalat Binyamin arts & crafts market guide (/nachalat-binyamin-market) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep scan for "nachalat.*binyamin.*guide", "nachalat.*binyamin.*market.*standalone", "/nachalat-binyamin-market" across full backlog — only references are (a) shopping-in-israel.md (SHIPPED iter276) mentions "Carmel+Nahalat Binyamin+Levinsky" as a TLV market cluster in one sentence, (b) Israel markets hub (P3, M, backlog iter180) refers to "adjacent to Nahalat Binyamin craft fair Tue/Fri" in a Jaffa section cross-reference. Neither is a dedicated standalone guide. beinharimtours.com has a dedicated "Nachalat Binyamin Walking Tour" page; touristisrael.com has Nahalat Binyamin content; secrettelaviv.com covers it; TripAdvisor lists it as a distinct attraction. Israel's longest-running outdoor arts market (founded 1920s); holds a market association inspection to ensure all vendors sell original handmade work; 200+ artists; Tue & Fri only (10am–6pm); located on Nahalat Binyamin Street between Allenby and Carmel Market areas in central TLV. DISTINCT from: Carmel Market (food produce and general goods — different street, different character, different audience); Jaffa Flea Market (vintage/antiques/furniture); Levinsky Market (spices and dried goods); Israel markets hub (hub format covering 5 markets briefly — this standalone guide captures dedicated "Nachalat Binyamin market" search traffic). Content: what makes it unique (jury-selected artists — not commercial imports; diverse media: silver jewelry, hand-blown glass, leather bags, ceramic sculpture, painted silks, hand-printed textiles, wooden toys, Judaica; each artist works their own stall — you can talk directly with the maker; authentic craft vs. tourist-tat distinction); practical visiting (arrive ~11am Thu/Fri for best selection before stalls sell out; lunch at adjacent Carmel Market food stalls — natural combo visit; pay by cash or card — varies by vendor; bargaining is unusual at Nachalat Binyamin unlike at traditional souqs — these are artists pricing their own work; parking difficult — use Tel-O-Fun bike or bus 16/18/239 to Carmel Market stop); what to buy (original silver jewelry = best value vs. import; ceramic items for Jewish holidays; hand-block-printed fabrics; personalized calligraphy as gift; original sketches of Israeli scenes). HONESTY: Market runs TUE & FRI ONLY (critical — many tourists arrive on other days expecting an open market; clarify this prominently); does NOT operate on Jewish holidays even if Tue/Fri; market closed in rain (outdoor stalls); artist lineup changes week to week; no fabricated artist profiles; the "longest-running" claim is editorial consensus, not an officially certified record. Dense cross-links: tel-aviv-carmel-market, jaffa-travel-guide (Jaffa flea market comparison), israel-markets-guide (when built), tel-aviv-neighborhoods-guide, shopping-in-israel. Citrus/orange picking: Nov–Feb in Sharon Plain (Moshavim north of TLV); some farms open self-pick; 3. Cherry picking: Jun in Golan Heights — Moshav Odem area, brief intense season; advance check required; 4. Date picking: Sep–Oct in Arava Valley and Jordan Valley; Kibbutz Ketura, Kibbutz Lotan, Kibbutz Ein Gedi — medjool dates on the tree; 5. Banana farm visits: Jordan Valley year-round; 6. General harvest seasons: pomegranate Oct, fig Aug–Sep); historic agritourism sites (Neot Kedumim — biblical landscape park; ancient agriculture demonstrations including olive-pressing, grape-stomping, grain-grinding; Shfela region near Tel Aviv; pre-booking required; Ein Yael Farm Museum, Jerusalem Hills — ancient farming methods with hands-on activities; Kfar Kedem in Galilee — donkey rides, olive oil pressing, biblical-era agricultural demonstrations in full costume; most authentic of the three); kibbutz farm programs (several kibbutzim accept farm-volunteer visitors for half-day programs — Kibbutz Lotan (Arava) has eco-farming experiences; advance contact via individual kibbutz websites); new 2026 Galilee agro-tourism investment (Ministry-funded visitor centers at select Galilee farms; new English-language picking programs planned for 2026–2027; travelandtourworld.com for current program details). Getting there: farm picking is scattered — rental car essential for berry/cherry/date season circuits; some organized day-trip tours from TLV (check GYG "Israel farm tour" for current products). HONESTY: specific farm opening dates vary by harvest conditions each year — never hardcode "farm X is open Feb 15" (it depends on season and annual weather); 13M ILS investment is announced but programs roll out over 2026–2029 — some planned experiences may not yet be operational; farm self-picking is predominantly Hebrew-language signage and customer base — guide visitors to look for farms explicitly marketing English-friendly programs; some historic sites (Kfar Kedem) require advance booking weeks ahead; not all "farm tourism" in Israel is pick-your-own (distinguish between self-picking orchards and demonstration/educational farm sites). Strong market: Abraham Tours runs a dedicated weekly "Tel Aviv Pub Crawl" (abrahamtours.com/tours/tel-aviv-pub-crawl/, Thu/Fri/Sat 21:30 start, includes free beer + drink specials); D-TLV Pub Crawl (d-tlv.com) has its own brand with TripAdvisor listing and 2026 reviews; Tourist Israel has a "Tel Aviv Nightlife Tour" (touristisrael.com/tours/tel-aviv-nightlife-tour/); GetYourGuide lists "Tel Aviv: Pub Crawl and Nightlife Tour with Shots" (t445735); TripAdvisor 2026 pub crawl reviews 100+. Content: organized pub crawl options (Abraham Tours: weekly Thu/Fri/Sat, ₪80–120/person, 21:30 start, free beer at first bar + shots at subsequent bars + drink specials; tour visits 4–5 bars across central TLV neighborhoods; great for solo travelers meeting others; D-TLV: similar format, check d-tlv.com for current schedule; GYG t445735: "Pub Crawl and Nightlife Tour with Shots" — same social format via GYG booking); self-guided bar hopping (by neighborhood: Florentin = loudest, cheapest, most local energy — best 3–4 small bars in 3-block radius including Orna ve Ella area + Vital St; Rothschild Blvd = Bauhaus backdrop, sophisticated cocktail bars open until 3am, busiest Fri–Sat; Allenby St corridor = eclectic mix of dive bars + live music + LGBTQ venues + late closing; Dizengoff area = neighborhood feel, less tourist, best for people-watching over beer; beach bars = start at sunset, move inland after 10pm; most bar-hoppers start at beach bars and work their way into Florentin or Rothschild by midnight); timing (TLV nightlife starts late; pubs fill 22:00–midnight; clubs 01:00+; Thursday = most local crowd; Friday = most tourists; Saturday = quieter as many Israelis leave city for Shabbat family dinners earlier in evening; do not expect much before 21:00 at any bar); practical tips (most bars cash or card; cover charges rare at pubs, common at clubs; ID for 18+ — Israeli law; Gett app works for 4am taxi; most bars close 3–4am, some until dawn). HONESTY: organized pub crawl operators change and sometimes suspend operations — always verify current schedule before booking; minimum age 18 per Israeli law; pub crawl meetup locations change — check operator website; self-guided route reflects general consensus of consistently popular bar districts as of 2026 research — individual bars open/close; no fabricated ratings or "best bar" rankings. - [P2] (seo-content+monetization, M) Galilee Christian sites touring circuit guide (/galilee-christian-sites-circuit) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep scan for "galilee.*christian.*circuit", "galilee.*christian.*sites", "christian.*circuit.*galilee", "/galilee-christian" across full backlog — ZERO standalone entry; referenced as a cross-link target in STATE.md iter273 (sea-of-galilee-boat-tour: "Dense internal links: tiberias-guide, galilee-christian-sites-circuit, nazareth-sea-of-galilee-day-trip...") but never added as a standalone backlog item. DISTINCT from: christian-pilgrimage-holy-land.md (SHIPPED — national multi-week pilgrim itinerary; not a Galilee day-circuit guide); nazareth-travel-guide.md (SHIPPED — Nazareth city only); sea-of-galilee-boat-tour.md (SHIPPED — on-water experience); galilee-tours-compared.md (SHIPPED — tour operator comparison, not self-drive circuit guide). Competitors: beinharimtours.com/galilee-christian-sites, christiansintheland.com/galilee-tour, holylandsite.com/galilee-circuit, touristisrael.com/christian-galilee, numerous GYG products ("Galilee Christian day tour from TLV" = top-selling Israel GYG product category) — all rank for "Galilee Christian sites" + "New Testament sites Galilee" with ZERO editorial from our site. Content: circuit overview (7 key New Testament sites in the Galilee region drivable in 1–2 days from Tiberias or Nazareth base); sites (1. Nazareth — Basilica of the Annunciation (largest church in Middle East; Mary's Well; White Mosque; grotto; open daily; dress code: shoulders/knees covered; 2h minimum); 2. Cana (Kafr Kanna) — traditional site of the miracle of water into wine (John 2:1-11); two competing chapels (Franciscan and Greek Orthodox); pilgrim masses celebrated; 20 min from Nazareth); 3. Mount Tabor — traditional Transfiguration site (Matthew 17:1-9); 588m summit with panoramic Jezreel Valley view; Franciscan Basilica of the Transfiguration (1924); narrow winding road — large vehicles use minibuses from base; 1h visit; 4. Capernaum (Kfar Nahum) — site of Peter's house (preserved under Byzantine octagonal church remains); ancient synagogue where Jesus taught (John 6:59); Franciscan custody site; excavated ruins + rooftop viewing platform; open daily except Jewish Sabbath; ₪35 entry; 5. Tabgha (Church of the Multiplication of the Loaves and Fish) — mosaic floor depicting baskets of bread and two fishes; modest dress required; free entry; Benedictine monastery; 6. Mount of Beatitudes (Sermon on Mount) — Franciscan chapel overlooking the Sea of Galilee; magnificent Galilee panoramic views from terraced gardens; pilgrimage mass times vary; free entry; 7. Yardenit — commercial baptism facility on the Jordan River south of Sea of Galilee (NOT the biblical baptism site — see Jordan-river-baptism-site guide; but very convenient and spiritually meaningful for many pilgrims; organized baptism services for groups and individuals)); circuit logistics (1-day from Tiberias base: Nazareth 45min → Cana 20min → back north toward Capernaum/Tabgha/Mount of Beatitudes on north shore of Kinneret loop — full day 200km; 2-day: add Mount Tabor on Day 2 + Yardenit; rental car essential; GYG organized tour from TLV also an option); practical planning (dress code applies at all Catholic/Orthodox sites — pack a scarf for women; bring sun protection; opening times midday closure 12:30–14:30 common at monastic sites; INPA national parks pass does NOT cover any of these sites; Nazareth = busiest, get there before 10am; Capernaum + Tabgha = quieter). HONESTY: the biblical sites in Galilee are traditional associations with Gospel events, not scientifically verified archaeological locations (with the partial exception of Capernaum where excavations support the synagogue's 1st-century dating); frame as "traditional site of..." rather than definitive; Yardenit is a commercial facility, not the historically proposed baptism site (Qasr el-Yahud near Jericho is the candidate); midday closures are common and inconsistent — always link official site pages before planning; Mount Tabor minibus from base is compulsory for tour buses (private cars can drive up on weekdays — check current policy at mount-tabor.net). Dense cross-links: christian-pilgrimage-holy-land, nazareth-travel-guide, sea-of-galilee-boat-tour, tiberias-guide, galilee-tours-compared, car-rental-israel, jordan-river-baptism-site (when built). Monetization: GYG "Galilee Christian sites day tour from Tel Aviv" CTA (top-selling GYG Israel product) + Abraham Tours Galilee private tour CTA + Booking.com Tiberias/Nazareth hotel CTA. - [P3] (seo-content, S) Gan HaShlosha (Sachne) warm springs visitor guide (/gan-hashlosha-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep scan for "gan.*hashlosha", "sachne", "gan.hashlos", "warm.*spring.*beit.*shean", "/gan-hashlosha" across full backlog — ZERO standalone entry; referenced in south-israel-itinerary (P2 backlog) as "Gan HaShlosha / Sachne warm spring pools (30 min south; INPA site; 28°C freshwater pools; swimming + picnic; open year-round; one of Israel's most pleasant outdoor sites)" and in megiddo-jezreel-valley-guide (SHIPPED iter258) as a day-circuit addition — but no standalone dedicated guide. DISTINCT from: water-hiking-israel.md (SHIPPED — wadi stream hiking; Sachne is a park with pools, not a hiking trail); beaches guides (sea/lake beaches — Sachne is inland freshwater); Ein Gedi (Dead Sea oasis — completely different geography). Competitors: touristisrael.com has dedicated "Gan HaShlosha National Park" page; laidbacktrip.com has a full guide; tripadvisor.com Beit She'an Valley top attraction; israel.travel/sachne (official). One of Israel's most visited INPA sites year-round; ~500k+ annual visitors. What makes it unique: three pools fed by natural warm springs (Amal/Nahr Jalud stream source; constant 28°C year-round — warm for winter, refreshing in summer; freshwater not salt); surrounded by lush subtropical vegetation (date palms, bamboo, eucalyptus — rare contrast to surrounding dry Jezreel Valley landscape); waterfalls between pools; ancient ruins visible at the site edge (ancient Bet She'an / Scythopolis ruins context — the National Park of Beit She'an is 10 min away; a natural combo); one of the few INPA sites where swimming is the primary activity. Practical visit: (1) Opening: year-round, roughly 8am–5pm (summer extended; check parks.org.il as hours change seasonally); entry: ₪35–55 adult (INPA national parks pass valid — cross-sell); parking on-site; (2) Getting there: Route 669 turn-off from Route 90 north of Beit She'an; 45 min from Tiberias by car; 1h 15min from Haifa; no regular tourist bus — car or Beit She'an sherut then taxi; (3) Crowds: extremely busy Sukkot, Passover, summer weekends — arrive before 9am to guarantee parking and pool space; Tuesday and Wednesday midweek = calmest; (4) Beit Alfa Byzantine synagogue (15 min from Sachne): INPA site, ₪14 entry, not covered by INPA national parks pass; 6th-century CE mosaic floor with complete zodiac wheel — exceptional quality and rare survival; 20-min visit; perfect half-day combo: Sachne swim (2–3h) + Beit Alfa mosaic (1h) + lunch at Beit She'an (the modern town has cafes); (5) National Park of Beit She'an: 10 min from Sachne; massive Roman-Byzantine theater + colonnaded street + tell (Iron Age city layers); Israel's best-preserved Roman city; INPA pass valid. Day circuit potential: Megiddo (1.5h) → Beit She'an Roman city (2h) → Sachne swim (2h) → Beit Alfa mosaic (1h) = full Jezreel Valley archaeology + nature day. HONESTY: swimming pool area can feel crowded and not peaceful during holidays/weekends — set accurate expectations; the springs are genuinely warm (28°C) but not hot-spring level; no sunbeds/umbrellas for hire — bring your own shade; lifeguards present but visitors responsible for children; some pools have depth variation — check area signage; Beit Alfa entry fee is separate and NOT covered by INPA pass (frame clearly). Dense cross-links: israel-national-parks-pass, megiddo-jezreel-valley-guide, water-hiking-israel, galilee-tours-compared, day-trips-from-haifa, day-trips-from-tel-aviv, tiberias-guide (Galilee base). SEO: "Gan HaShlosha Israel visitor guide", "Sachne warm springs Israel", "swimming springs Beit She'an Valley Israel", "natural springs Israel swimming", "Gan HaShlosha INPA park guide". Sources: touristisrael.com/gan-hashlosha, laidbacktrip.com/sachne-warm-springs, tripadvisor.com/Attraction_Review-g1399225-d2395168, parks.org.il/parksAndReserves/gan-hashlosha, israel.travel/sachne. - [P3] (seo-content+monetization, S) Jordan River baptism site visitor guide (/jordan-river-baptism-site) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep scan for "jordan.*river.*baptism", "yardenit.*guide", "/jordan-river-baptism", "qasr.*el.*yahud", "baptism.*site.*jordan" across full backlog — ZERO standalone entry; referenced as a cross-link target in STATE.md iter273 sea-of-galilee-boat-tour note ("Dense internal links: tiberias-guide, galilee-christian-sites-circuit, nazareth-sea-of-galilee-day-trip, galilee-tours-compared, jordan-river-baptism...") — page is expected but not yet documented in backlog; christian-pilgrimage-holy-land.md (SHIPPED) mentions Yardenit in a single sentence with no practical visitor detail; galilee-christian-sites-circuit (backlog iter280) references Yardenit but correctly defers to this standalone guide for depth. Two distinct baptism sites exist that visitors confuse — this guide clarifies both. (1) Yardenit (Sea of Galilee southern outlet, Kibbutz Kinneret) — dedicated commercial Christian baptism facility operated by Kibbutz Kinneret since 1981; purpose-built pools + changing rooms + white robes; organized group and individual baptism services; ~450,000 visitors/year; gift shop + cafe; most organized pilgrim tours use this site; booking via yardenit.com or tour operators; practical, accessible, spiritually meaningful; NOT the historically proposed site of Jesus's baptism but traditional for thousands of pilgrimage groups; open daily except Yom Kippur; ₪25 entry for non-baptizing visitors; baptism itself: advance booking + white robe rental ₪30; (2) Qasr el-Yahud (Jordan River near Jericho, West Bank border area) — the primary scholarly and Eastern Orthodox candidate for the traditional site of John's baptism of Jesus; on the Jordan-Israel border adjacent to the West Bank; INPA-managed access; reopened to visitors 2011 after multi-year mine clearance project (HALO Trust); Israeli-side access via Jericho road (Israeli-administered, accessible from Jerusalem); Palestinian-side also has a site (Al-Maghtas, Jordan — UNESCO WHS 2015); this Israeli-side site is raw riverbank, no facilities, powerful atmosphere; open to visitors free; guided tours available from Jerusalem operators; INPA coordinates access — check current hours via parks.org.il as access can be restricted; separate from: Jericho city visits (Palestinian Authority territory — different access); comparing the sites (Yardenit = organized, modern, accessible, group-friendly; Qasr el-Yahud = historically significant, atmospheric, remote, more appropriate for individual reflection vs. mass baptism groups); which to visit (pilgrimage groups wanting baptism ceremony: Yardenit is purpose-built for this; individual travelers and photographers seeking the historically significant site: Qasr el-Yahud; ideally visit both on a Dead Sea-to-Galilee itinerary). Getting there: Yardenit — 10km south of Tiberias on Route 90; Egged bus 36 from Tiberias (15 min) or tour; Qasr el-Yahud — 40km from Jerusalem via Route 90 toward Jericho/Dead Sea direction; car (no public bus to this specific site); GYG Jerusalem-Jericho day tours often stop here. HONESTY: Yardenit is NOT the biblical baptism site per current scholarly consensus or Eastern Orthodox tradition — state this clearly and gently; Qasr el-Yahud is the Israeli-side site of the traditionally proposed baptism location (the Jordanian Al-Maghtas site across the river is the UNESCO-listed site); Qasr el-Yahud access is currently generally open but has been restricted in past — always verify parks.org.il before visiting; Jordan River water at Qasr el-Yahud is murky and slow-moving in 2026 (heavily diverted for agriculture) — frame expectations honestly; Yardenit white robe pricing and baptism booking requirements change — link yardenit.com. Dense cross-links: christian-pilgrimage-holy-land, galilee-christian-sites-circuit, sea-of-galilee-boat-tour, tiberias-guide, day-trips-from-jerusalem, is-israel-safe. Monetization: GYG "Jordan River baptism tour" CTA (organized Jerusalem-to-Qasr el-Yahud tours) + GYG Galilee Christian tour CTA. SEO: "Jordan River baptism site Israel guide", "Yardenit Israel visitor guide", "Qasr el-Yahud guide", "baptism Jordan River tourist", "Jesus baptism site Israel". Sources: yardenit.com, parks.org.il/qasr-el-yahud, seetheholyland.net/jordan-river-baptism-sites, beinharimtours.com/jordan-river-baptism, tripadvisor.com/Attraction_Review-Yardenit, getyourguide.com/israel/jordan-river-baptism-tours. - [SHIPPED iter286] Neve Tzedek neighborhood guide (/neve-tzedek-guide) → b803fa4 — CONFIRMED GAP: tel-aviv-neighborhoods-guide.md (SHIPPED iter157) covers Neve Tzedek as one of five neighborhoods in a hub; 1-day-tel-aviv-itinerary (SHIPPED iter226) includes a brief Neve Tzedek afternoon block; NO standalone guide exists in BACKLOG or DONE (grep -c "neve tzedek" case-sensitive = 0 in BACKLOG.md). Timeout Israel, Tourist Israel, Expedia, US News Travel all have dedicated Neve Tzedek features. Israel's first Jewish neighborhood outside Jaffa walls, established 1887 by 66 founding families. Distinct angle: the neighborhood embodies the arc from dusty pioneer settlement → urban decay → 1990s artist-led gentrification → today's luxury boutique quarter. Content: History (founded 1887 by 66 Jaffa Jews seeking relief from overcrowded Jaffa Old City; Neve Tzedek = "Oasis of Justice" in Hebrew; the oldest urban neighborhood that became part of modern Tel Aviv; founding families include the Rokach and Chelouche families whose mansions survive as museums); Suzanne Dellal Centre for Dance and Theatre (restored 1989 by architect Ram Karmi in former school building; courtyard with cascading bougainvillea = one of TLV's most photographed spaces; Batsheva Dance Company rehearsals sometimes visible; free courtyard access; ticketed performances from Batsheva and international companies; suzannedellal.org.il for programming); Shabazi Street (main pedestrian strip; boutiques by Israeli designers — Maskit, Idit Barak, Frau Blau — each with different identity; mix of fashion, ceramics, olive wood, contemporary Judaica; avoid Saturdays — most shut; best Thu–Fri afternoon); Nahum Gutman Museum (50 Rokach St; Tel Aviv's pioneer illustrator and painter; vibrant murals of old Jaffa and early 20th-century life; modest admission ~₪30; compact but evocative; Thu extended hours); architecture walk (original late-Ottoman Arab houses with decorative stonework; early Bauhaus-influenced structures from 1920s settlers; contrast between restored mansions and preserved humble workers' cottages — both authentic); where to eat (Shabazi 26 — pioneering bistro credited with anchoring the neighborhood's culinary revival; Orna & Ella — long-running TLV institution; HaBasta — produce-driven weekend brunch; Pastel at Sarona adjacent — fine dining cross-link). Practical: best reached by light rail Green Line → Florentine or Allenby stations, then 10 min walk north; weekend crowds peak Fri morning; parking scarce — public transport preferred; full neighborhood walkable in 2–3 hours including museum visit. HONESTY: Neve Tzedek's boutique scene changes frequently — "check current hours before visiting; some smaller boutiques open Thu–Fri only"; the neighborhood's artistic identity has been partially replaced by luxury retail — frame accurately; Suzanne Dellal courtyard free, but performances ticketed (advance booking essential for popular shows). Monetization: Booking.com TLV Neve Tzedek / Florentin area hotels CTA (premium boutique-hotel density here: Alma Hotel, Shapira House Hotel) + GYG "Neve Tzedek + Jaffa walking tour" CTA (multiple products that include Neve Tzedek). Dense cross-links: tel-aviv-neighborhoods-guide, 1-day-tel-aviv-itinerary, jaffa-travel-guide, tel-aviv-white-city, tel-aviv-coffee-guide, tel-aviv-street-art (backlog). SEO: "Neve Tzedek Tel Aviv guide", "Neve Tzedek neighborhood visit", "things to do Neve Tzedek", "Neve Tzedek shopping boutiques", "Neve Tzedek history". - [SHIPPED iter287] (seo-content, S) Israel hummus trail & best hummus guide (/israel-hummus-trail) — CONFIRMED GAP: 16 occurrences of "hummus" in BACKLOG.md but ALL within other guides' content (food guide, vegan guide, Safed guide, Haifa food guide, 1-day TLV itinerary, etc.); ZERO standalone hummus circuit guide entries. Closest existing content: jerusalem-food-guide.md (SHIPPED iter161) covers Abu Shukri Old City hummus in one section; arab-israeli-culture-guide (BACKLOG iter195, M) covers Abu Gosh tangentially as part of Arab-Israeli culture — this guide is specifically a RESTAURANT CIRCUIT focused on hummus as a craft. The Nosher, Eater, Food & Wine, Time Out Israel, NativeIsrael, Tourist Israel all have dedicated "Best Hummus in Israel" editorial with the Abu Hassan → Abu Shukri → Abu Gosh circuit as the canonical route. Content: What makes Israeli/Palestinian hummus distinct (made fresh daily — never from a can; smooth chickpea base with generous tahini + lemon + olive oil pool + cumin; served warm, not cold; the canonical accompaniment = hard-boiled egg, raw onion, and warmed flatbread; the "argument" between Jerusalem-style (thicker, less tahini) vs. Jaffa/Acre-style (runnier, more tahini) is genuine and the guide's hook); the six essential stops (1. Abu Hassan / Ali Karawan, Jaffa — 1 Dolphin Street; the most legendary hummus in Israel; opens at 7am, often sold out by noon; queue system on busy days; cash only; order hummus + ful (slow-cooked fava bean) + boiled egg; closed Sat; 2. Abu Shukri, Jerusalem Old City Muslim Quarter — 63 Al-Wad Street near Via Dolorosa; legendary since 1950s; whole-chickpea topping; open from 8am, typically close afternoon; busiest Thu–Fri; 3. Afteem, Bethlehem — Manger Square; best hummus in Bethlehem; opens morning; cash only; cross-link bethlehem guide when built; 4. Abu Gosh, highway village 10km from Jerusalem — multiple competing hummus restaurants in the village (Abu Ghosh Restaurant, Caravan Restaurant); the whole village IS Israel's informal hummus capital; 20 min from Jerusalem; 5. Hummus Said, Akko Old City — Ottoman arcade, Akko souq; said to use more tahini than any competitor; open morning only most days; combine with Akko Old City sightseeing; 6. Azura, Jerusalem Machane Yehuda market — Levantine-style hummus with spiced lamb shoulder topping; lunch only; distinct from the pure hummus places); etiquette (arrive early — best hummus depleted by noon; order one dish per person; accept the whole-chickpea topping + olive oil; use bread not cutlery; tips welcomed but not mandatory; places are always busy — accept communal seating); making it a trip (1-day Jerusalem hummus route: Abu Shukri AM + Azura lunch + Abu Gosh on drive back; Tel Aviv day: Abu Hassan AM + head to Jaffa flea market; 2-day circuit with overnight in Jerusalem covers all major stops). HONESTY: specific restaurant opening hours change and closures happen — frame as "widely cited as of 2026 research; always verify by calling ahead"; the "best hummus" designation is genuinely contested — use "widely regarded" not superlatives; Abu Hassan in particular has had many imitators using the same name — specify the Dolphin Street location clearly; Bethlehem stop requires West Bank crossing with passport (cross-link bethlehem-travel-guide + is-israel-safe); the "hummus wars" between places claiming to be original/best are real and entertaining — acknowledge the debate without declaring a winner. No affiliate monetization for restaurant guides (non-commission category); link GYG "Machane Yehuda food tour" for Jerusalem component. Dense cross-links: jerusalem-food-guide, jaffa-travel-guide, akko-acre-guide, bethlehem-travel-guide (when built), arab-israeli-culture-guide (when built), kosher-food-guide (hummus is always parve), vegan-vegetarian-israel. SEO: "best hummus in Israel guide", "Israel hummus trail", "Abu Hassan Jaffa hummus", "Abu Shukri Jerusalem hummus", "where to eat hummus Israel". Distinct from: glamping-israel.md (SHIPPED iter268 — eco-accommodation focus, not therapeutic springs); dead-sea-hotels-guide (BACKLOG — hotel-focused; Dead Sea = flotation/mud/cosmetics, not thermal spring pools); water-hiking-nahal (SHIPPED — cool spring wading, not therapeutic hot springs). Israeli thermal spa culture is a major domestic tourism draw with very limited English-language guide coverage. Content: Hamat Gader (primary destination — on Yarmouk River gorge at Syria-Jordan-Israel tri-point; accessible via Golan Heights Route 98; 38°C volcanic thermal pools + separate cold plunge pools + Roman-era thermal bath ruins; large crocodile park adjacent; indoor and outdoor pools; seasonal opening hours — closed Mon in winter, check in advance; admission ~₪100 adults includes pools and croc park; weekend crowds especially Sukkot + Passover — arrive early; lockers + towel rental; café on site; 60 min from Tiberias by car; INPA does NOT cover this — private operator); Hamat Tiberias National Park (mineral springs at foot of Mount Berenice, Sea of Galilee shore; historically famous ancient thermal baths used by Romans + Herod the Great + crusaders; INPA-managed national park component = archaeological ruins of ancient 18-room bathhouse; outdoor thermal pools accessible to the public adjacent to the park; ₪35 INPA entry for ruins; outdoor pool admission separately priced via private operator; 5 min south of Tiberias center); Hammei Zohar, Ein Bokek (Dead Sea shore; sulfur thermal springs historically used since antiquity; commercially operated thermal pool at several Dead Sea hotels — Dan Hotel, Hod HaMidbar — accessible to non-guests for day use ₪80–150; combine with Dead Sea float for full Dead Sea spa day — distinct from standard hotel pool); Golan Heights hot springs side trips (several smaller mineral springs in the Golan; some accessible via INPA trails, some via private farms). HONESTY: Hamat Gader is privately operated — hours and prices change regularly; link visithamatgader.co.il directly; never publish specific pricing (range ₪80–120 adult for combined pool+croc park as of 2026 research); Hamat Tiberias thermal pools are a mix of public and private operator facilities directly adjacent to the national park — clarify the two components (INPA ruins = national park; pools = private); thermal spring temperatures may vary seasonally; "therapeutic claims" (arthritis, skin conditions) are cited by Israeli tourist literature but this guide should NOT make medical claims. Monetization: Booking.com Hamat Gader / Katzrin (Golan base) hotel CTA + Booking.com Tiberias hotel CTA (overnight base for thermal circuit). Dense cross-links: galilee region, tiberias-guide (when built), golan-druze-villages, dead-sea-guide, dead-sea-hotels-guide (when built). SEO: "Israel thermal baths guide", "Hamat Gader visitor guide", "thermal pools Israel", "Israel hot springs tourism", "Hamat Tiberias spa". Sources: visithamatgader.co.il, parks.org.il/hamat-tiberias, tripadvisor.com Hamat Gader, touristisrael.com/hamat-gader, lonelyplanet.com/israel/sea-of-galilee/activities/hamat-tiberias. [iter285 research] status: ready

- [P3] (seo-content, S) Rishon LeZion city guide (/rishon-lezion-guide) — CONFIRMED LOW BACKLOG COVERAGE: only 2 BACKLOG matches (both brief cross-references). tourism.gov.il, tripadvisor.com, frommers.com, citysightseeing.co.il all have Rishon LeZion content. Israel's 4th-largest city (260,000+ residents), 15 min from Tel Aviv by light rail Red/Green Line — frequently overlooked by international visitors who base in TLV without exploring its southward suburbs. NOT a suburb — a city with its own distinct identity and firsts. Content: Israel's "City of Firsts" (historic frame): Rishon LeZion = "First in Zion" — first moshav (agricultural settlement) of the first aliyah (1882); first Hebrew kindergarten in the Land of Israel (founded 1898 by Leah Berlin); first Hebrew flag raised in Eretz Israel (1891); first Hebrew labor organization; first Philharmonic concert in the land; Beit HaGiborim Museum (free; documents these firsts with artifacts; central old town district); the Carmel Winery (Rothschild Avenue; Israel's oldest and largest winery, established 1882 by Baron Edmond de Rothschild as part of his Zionist patronage; open for tours and tastings Sun–Thu; ₪30–50 tasting entry; wine available for purchase; the building is an original Ottoman-era stone winery; carmelwines.co.il); the Old Neighborhood (Shikun HaOtim — original 1882 Ottoman-era settlers' stone buildings around Herzl Street; Beit Ahad Ha'am museum in writer Asher Ginsberg's original home); Hasharon Park & Municipal Forest (one of Israel's largest urban parks, green family destination between Rishon and neighboring Be'er Yaakov); Weizmann Science Institute (nearby in Rehovot — short bus ride south — Nobel laureate Chaim Weizmann's original lab and museum, plus beautiful campus gardens open to visitors; framing: "Day trip to Israel's Silicon Valley roots" pairing with Rishon's founding era); practical guide (light rail Line 2 [Red] and Line 1 [Purple] from TLV Central: 20–25 min; stop: Rishon LeZion HaRishonim or HaHadasha; walking map from train station to Old Neighborhood to Carmel Winery ~1.5km; combine with Ben Gurion Heritage Institute in Sde Boker context). HONESTY: The Carmel Winery museum is not a world-class museum experience — it is a modest historical display with a working winery context; frame as "the most historically significant" not "spectacular"; Hebrew kindergarten is marked by a modest plaque, not a large museum — verify current status of Beit HaGiborim before sending visitors; specific Rishon LeZion sights outside the Old Neighborhood area are scattered and require a car for efficiency. Dense cross-links: day-trips-from-tel-aviv, tel-aviv-neighborhoods-guide, israel-wine-wineries (SHIPPED), bar-bat-mitzvah-israel (Israeli Jewish heritage + Bar Mitzvah tourism cross-link). Monetization: Booking.com Rishon LeZion hotels (alternative base to TLV for budget travelers; cheaper accommodation); GYG "South Tel Aviv + Rishon LeZion heritage tour" CTA (verify if available product). SEO: "Rishon LeZion city guide", "things to do in Rishon LeZion", "Rishon LeZion day trip from Tel Aviv", "Carmel Winery Rishon LeZion tour", "Israel founding city guide". Sources: tourism.gov.il/rishon-lezion, tripadvisor.com/Tourism-g1542474-Rishon_LeZion, frommers.com/destinations/rishon-lezion, carmelwines.co.il. [iter285 research] status: ready

- [P3] (seo-content+monetization, S) Bat Yam coastal city guide (/bat-yam-guide) — CONFIRMED LOW BACKLOG COVERAGE: only 1 BACKLOG match (brief cross-reference). timeout.com/israel, secrettelaviv.com, tripadvisor.com all have Bat Yam content. Israel's 9th-largest city (250,000+ residents), immediately south of Tel Aviv on the Mediterranean coast — accessible as a 20-min walk south from Jaffa port or 10-min light rail ride (Green Line, Bat Yam stop). Very different cultural character from TLV: blue-collar, Mizrahi-majority, authentic "real Israel" atmosphere without tourist polish. Growing travel niche: visitors seeking an alternative to TLV's gentrified experience. Distinctive for: annual Bat Yam International Street Theater Festival (BISC — one of the largest street theater festivals in the Middle East; annual, free admission, August typically; attracts international troupes; family-friendly; distinctive performative atmosphere unlike anything in TLV); street art (large-scale municipal murals throughout the city, particularly along the beachfront and near HaAtzmaut Square — less curated than Florentin TLV but more raw); the promenade (3km south of Jaffa; connects directly to TLV promenade making it the natural end of the 16km Jaffa-TLV-Bat Yam beachfront walk); affordable eating (no-frills falafel shops, Iraqi-Kurdish restaurants [kubbeh soup], Georgian bakeries, simple café culture; genuine 2026 "off-the-radar" TLV budget traveler destination for lunch or sunset); Bat Yam lighthouse (nautical heritage site visible from promenade; free to photograph from exterior); what NOT to do (area around Bat Yam has higher crime statistics than TLV — focus tourist recommendations on promenade, beach, HaAtzmaut Square daytime rather than inland residential areas; frame honestly). HONESTY: Bat Yam is experiencing gentrification but is NOT yet a polished tourist destination — frame as "authentic urban Israel for curious visitors, not a packaged tourist experience"; the street theater festival dates change annually — link official BISC site; no fabricated "top restaurant" recommendations in a city where the appeal is authenticity; crime context — note that TLV's southern neighborhoods and Bat Yam's border areas require reasonable urban awareness (standard advice, not scare language). Monetization: Booking.com Bat Yam accommodation CTA (significantly cheaper per night than TLV beachfront; useful frame for budget travelers who want beach access); GYG "TLV Jaffa Bat Yam promenade cycling tour" (verify available product). Dense cross-links: jaffa-travel-guide, tel-aviv-beach-guide, tel-aviv-neighborhoods-guide, 1-day-tel-aviv-itinerary (Jaffa → promenade extends to Bat Yam). SEO: "Bat Yam Israel guide", "Bat Yam beach Tel Aviv", "things to do Bat Yam", "Bat Yam street art Israel", "Bat Yam street theater festival". Sources: timeout.com/israel/things-to-do/bat-yam, secrettelaviv.com/neighborhoods/bat-yam, tripadvisor.com/Tourism-g303980-Bat_Yam, batiment.co.il/tourism-bat-yam. [iter285 research] status: ready
