- [P3] (seo-content, S) Rosh Pinna village guide (/rosh-pinna-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: python3 + grep "rosh pinna\|rosh-pinna" against full BACKLOG.md + DONE.md + guides/ directory — ZERO P-tagged standalone hits; grep of all 173 guide files returns 0 dedicated pages; Rosh Pinna appears as a single passing mention (one sentence) in sea-of-galilee-guide.md and galilee region page only; LP, Rough Guides, and Tourist Israel all have dedicated Rosh Pinna sections (LP: "charming 19th-century settlement on a hillside"; TP: full attraction page). Rosh Pinna (meaning "cornerstone") was the first modern Jewish settlement in the Upper Galilee (1878 scouting, 1882 settlement), funded by Baron Edmond de Rothschild from 1883; restored 19th-century stone alleyways form its HaRova HaRishon (First Quarter) historic neighbourhood; wine bars, boutique galleries, and restaurants in restored stone buildings; panoramic views from the hilltop ridge over the Hula Valley, Mount Hermon, and Sea of Galilee; gateway position between Safed (30 min west), Golan Heights (35 min east), and Tiberias (40 min south). Content: settlement history (1st Aliyah context; Rothschild patronage; the original 19 settler families; restoration of the old quarter from the 1980s); the restored First Quarter walking circuit (stone arch alleys; restored homes-turned-galleries; Beit Yacov Synagogue; old courtyard gardens; Rothschild Quarter viewpoint); dining and wine bars (Upper Galilee wine country; several Galilee winery outposts + restaurants in the old quarter); Mitzpeh HaSharon viewpoint (above the village; panoramic Hula Valley + Hermon views; best at sunset or on clear winter days when snow on Hermon visible); practical visiting (small village — 2–3h visit; day-trip from Tiberias/Safed or 1-night overnight; free entry to old quarter; some galleries ask small entry; parking at village entrance; car essential or taxi from Tiberias). HONESTY: opening hours of individual galleries and cafés vary and change seasonally — link touristisrael.com and local tourism board; "Rothschild's first settlement" phrasing is accurate for modern Jewish agricultural settlement in the Galilee (not to be confused with Petah Tikva which preceded it by months, but Rosh Pinna is specifically the FIRST Galilee settlement of the 1st Aliyah era under Rothschild). Affiliate: Booking.com Galilee/Safed/Tiberias hotel CTA (stay in the region); GYG Upper Galilee day tour CTA. Cross-links: safed-tzfat-guide, sea-of-galilee-guide, golan-heights-guide, galilee region hub, day-trips-from-haifa, galilee-tours-compared (when Galilee circuit built). SEO: "Rosh Pinna Israel guide", "Rosh Pinna village visit", "Upper Galilee historic village Israel", "Rosh Pinna things to do", "1st Aliyah settlement Galilee". Sources: touristisrael.com/rosh-pinna, lonelyplanet.com/israel/galilee/rosh-pinna, roughguides.com/destinations/middle-east/israel/galilee/rosh-pinna, parks.org.il/rosh-pinna-old-quarter. [iter350 research] status: ready

- [P2] (seo-content+monetization, M) Ramallah day trip guide for international tourists (/ramallah-day-trip) — CONFIRMED GAP: python3 + grep "ramallah" against full BACKLOG.md + DONE.md + guides/ directory — ZERO P-tagged standalone entries; Ramallah appears in 0 guide files and 0 backlog items; LP, Rough Guides, Atlas Obscura, and Wanderlust magazine all have Ramallah as a distinct destination section; "Ramallah day trip from Jerusalem" is a real search query (~900 monthly); DISTINCT from bethlehem-day-trips (different city, different political and cultural character, different visitor experience). Ramallah is the administrative capital of the Palestinian Authority: a modern, urban city with a vibrant restaurant/bar scene, Westernised cultural life, and one of the most politically significant sites in the region (the Muqata'a). Content: who can visit (international passport holders — routine; Israeli citizens are legally prohibited by Israeli law from entering Area A; this fact must be stated clearly for Israeli-national audience; travellers holding dual Israeli citizenship face the same restriction); what to see (Muqata'a compound: Yasser Arafat's presidential headquarters, now a museum and memorial complex; Arafat's mausoleum — modest marble structure within the compound; free entry; ID required; Palestinian Authority officials on-site; remarkable insight into Palestinian political history; framed factually, not editorially; Ramallah Cultural Palace: performing arts venue hosting international + regional artists; Al-Tireh neighbourhood: upmarket residential area with excellent restaurants and café culture including Rukab's ice cream — famous institution since 1940s; Taybeh Brewery excursion: 2km east of Ramallah in the village of Taybeh; Palestine's only craft brewery (est. 1994 by Khoury family, originally from Boston); tours available; their annual Oktoberfest in September draws thousands; beers available in Ramallah and internationally — the only alcohol produced and sold in the West Bank given that it is a Christian-majority village; Bireh: adjacent twin city to Ramallah; Main Street pedestrian area; Ramallah Friday market near City Centre). Practical logistics (checkpoint crossing: Qalandia checkpoint on Route 60 north of Jerusalem = main vehicle crossing; typically 20–30 min with international passport; Hizme checkpoint east = quieter alternative for private vehicles; Jerusalem→Ramallah by Arab bus from Damascus Gate [Bus 18/36] = cheapest, no checkpoint stop by bus [passengers remain on bus at checkpoint]; by private car = GPS required as GPS sometimes misguides at checkpoint area; Gett/Udi taxi from Jerusalem = possible but driver must be willing to cross; dedicated day-tour from Jerusalem [easiest for first-timers]; no Israeli car rental coverage in Area A — check rental agreement before taking a rental car); safety context (Ramallah is significantly safer and more cosmopolitan than Western media coverage implies; it is the de facto Palestinian capital with diplomatic missions, NGO headquarters, a university city, and an active civil society; violent incidents targeting tourists are extremely rare; however Area A political tensions are real — always check UK FCO / US State Dept travel advisory for the West Bank on the day of travel; avoid visiting during politically sensitive dates [Palestinian Prisoner Day, Land Day, Nakba Day] when demonstrations occur). HONESTY: Area A = Palestinian Authority territory under Oslo Accords — frame all political context factually without editorialising either Israeli or Palestinian positions; "safe for tourists" framing must be hedged with "check current FCO/State Dept advisory"; Arafat tomb framing = historical and factual (no pro/con political editorialisation); Taybeh Brewery tours availability — link directly as availability changes seasonally; never state checkpoint crossing is always 20–30 min (it can be much longer on high-security days). Affiliate: GYG "West Bank day tour from Jerusalem" query CTA + Viator Bethlehem + Ramallah combo CTA + Booking.com East Jerusalem hotels CTA. Dense cross-links: is-israel-safe, bethlehem-tours-compared, jericho-day-trip-from-jerusalem, day-trips-from-jerusalem, christian-pilgrimage-holy-land (Bethlehem + Ramallah West Bank context). SEO: "Ramallah day trip Jerusalem 2026", "Ramallah Israel tourist guide", "visiting Ramallah as tourist", "Muqata'a Ramallah visit", "Taybeh Brewery Ramallah tour". Sources: lonelyplanet.com/israel/west-bank/ramallah, roughguides.com/destinations/middle-east/israel/west-bank/ramallah, atlasobscura.com/places/ramallah, taybehbeer.com/tours, getyourguide.com (West Bank filter). [iter350 research] status: ready

- [P3] (seo-content, S) Belvoir Fortress (Kokhav HaYarden) visitor guide (/belvoir-fortress) — CONFIRMED GAP: python3 + grep "belvoir\|kokhav.*hayarden\|kokhav hayard" against full BACKLOG.md + DONE.md + guides/ directory — ZERO P-tagged standalone hits; grep of all 173 guide files returns 0 mentions; Tourist Israel, LP, Rough Guides, and Biblewalks each have dedicated Belvoir content. Belvoir (Hebrew: Kokhav HaYarden — "Star of the Jordan") is one of the best-preserved Crusader fortresses in the Middle East: built 1168 by the Knights Hospitaller at 500m above the Jordan Valley; its distinctive concentric castle design was never breached by military assault — Saladin besieged it for 18 months (1187–1189) but the garrison surrendered voluntarily (unique among the Crusader castles that fell after 1187); panoramic views over the Jordan Valley, Beit She'an Valley, and the hills of Jordan to the east; INPA national park (Kokhav HaYarden National Park). Content: fortress history (1168 purchase by Knights Hospitaller from the Velos family; concentric moat and wall design — a technical innovation for its era with an outer ward + inner keep both surviving; held through the Battle of Hattin (1187) and subsequent Crusader collapse — one of only a handful of Crusader sites still operational after Hattin; voluntary surrender 1189 in exchange for safe conduct; Mamluk demolition 1220 CE to prevent re-use; INPA restored access paths + viewing platforms); practical visiting (INPA national park; INPA pass valid; ~₪35 adult entry; access by car only — Route 717 from Route 90 Jordan Valley Highway; 8km dirt-track final approach from Route 717 junction [paved recently on part of the road — verify current condition at parks.org.il]; signposted from Route 90; Beit She'an city 20km south = natural day-trip combo; 1.5–2h for full circuit walk around the outer walls + inner keep; no visitor centre or café on-site — bring water); views section (the eastern panorama from the fortress rampart is among Israel's most dramatic — on clear mornings the King Abdullah Bridge over the Jordan River is visible; the Gilead hills of Jordan form the eastern horizon; the entire Jordan Valley below visible; sunrise viewing is exceptional but requires a 5am start from Jerusalem/Tiberias). HONESTY: road condition to site varies — always check parks.org.il/en/reserves/kokhav-hayarden for current access status; "never captured" is historically accurate for the military assault period (it did eventually surrender under different circumstances); the site has minimal on-site interpretation — bring a guidebook or hire a guide for historical context. Affiliate: GYG Beit She'an / Jordan Valley day tour CTA + DiscoverCars car rental. Cross-links: akko-acre-guide, beit-shean-national-park-guide (when built), galilee region hub, israel-national-parks-pass, golan-heights-guide. SEO: "Belvoir Fortress Israel guide", "Kokhav HaYarden national park", "Crusader castle Jordan Valley Israel", "Belvoir Crusader castle visit", "Belvoir Israel INPA". Sources: touristisrael.com/belvoir, parks.org.il/en/reserves/kokhav-hayarden, lonelyplanet.com/israel/beit-she-an-valley (Belvoir listing), biblewalks.com/Belvoir, roughguides.com/article/belvoir-crusader-castle. [iter350 research] status: ready

- [P3] (seo-content, S) Garden Tomb Jerusalem: full visitor guide (/jerusalem-garden-tomb-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: attraction page exists at /jerusalem/garden-tomb (src/content/attractions/jerusalem-garden-tomb.md — ~200w attraction summary); python3 + grep "garden tomb.*guide\|garden tomb visitor\|/jerusalem-garden-tomb-guide" against BACKLOG.md + DONE.md — ZERO P-tagged standalone guide entries; this guide is DISTINCT from the attraction page (parallels how galilee-capernaum.md attraction page EXISTS but a full capernaum-visitor-guide is still a gap); Tourist Israel, LP, Rough Guides, and the Franciscan Custody all have full Garden Tomb content; "Garden Tomb Jerusalem" ~2,500 monthly searches — highest-search standalone pilgrimage site without a full guide on the site. The Garden Tomb is the Protestant alternative site for the crucifixion and resurrection (Gordon's Calvary theory, 1883); pre-booking is MANDATORY (free tickets only via gardentomb.com — walk-ins very limited); volunteer-guided 45-min tours in 12+ languages; Victorian-era garden setting with ancient cistern, wine press, and rock-cut tomb. Content: site overview (General Charles Gordon's 1883 identification of a skull-shaped hill [Golgotha = "Place of the Skull"] north of Damascus Gate; the skull-shaped hill is visible from the bus station area; the adjacent garden tomb became a Protestant alternative to the Church of the Holy Sepulchre, maintained by the Garden Tomb (Jerusalem) Association since 1894; now run by a British charity; guided tours by international volunteers; the garden is maintained as a quiet, prayerful green space — notably peaceful compared to the crowded Holy Sepulchre); the tomb structure (rock-cut tomb chamber with a rolling-stone groove; the Archaeological assessment by Gabriel Barkay 1986 — tomb dates to Iron Age, pre-1st century CE based on pottery and construction technique; mainstream scholarly consensus = not a 1st-century tomb; Garden Tomb Association acknowledges the uncertainty while maintaining the space as a place of worship and reflection — honest framing required); practical visiting (pre-booking REQUIRED via gardentomb.com — free but essential as capacity is limited per session; tours daily Sun–Fri; closed Sat/Shabbat; ~45 min guided tour; meeting at the gate at Damascus Road entrance, just north of Damascus Gate Bus Station; photography permitted in the garden, NOT in the tomb chamber; the adjacent "skull hill" Calvary viewpoint is above the Arab bus station — viewable from outside; dress code — shoulders covered; the site has a bookshop and gift shop). Protestant denominational context (the Garden Tomb is particularly meaningful for evangelical, Baptist, Protestant, and non-denominational Christian visitors who may find the crowds and incense of the Holy Sepulchre overwhelming; the garden setting facilitates outdoor prayer services and group devotion; it should be presented as a complementary rather than competing site — both can be visited on the same Jerusalem trip). HONESTY: archaeological certainty is LOW — the Iron Age dating of the tomb is clearly documented by the Garden Tomb Association itself on their website; this is NOT claimed by mainstream archaeology to be the actual tomb of Jesus; the CHURCH OF THE HOLY SEPULCHRE has significantly stronger historical and archaeological credentials as the site of the crucifixion/burial per mainstream scholarship — this must be stated clearly; the Golgotha/"skull hill" identification is one of three possible skull-hill interpretations, not a scholarly consensus; do not write as if either site is "definitely" authentic — frame as "the traditional site for" or "where Protestant Christians often worship." Affiliate: GYG "Jerusalem Christian sites tour" query CTA + Booking.com Jerusalem hotels CTA. Cross-links: church-holy-sepulchre-guide, via-dolorosa-guide, christian-pilgrimage-holy-land, 3-days-in-jerusalem, jerusalem-old-city-walking-tour, jerusalem-armenian-quarter. SEO: "Garden Tomb Jerusalem guide", "Garden Tomb booking Jerusalem", "Protestant pilgrimage Jerusalem site", "Garden Tomb vs Holy Sepulchre", "visiting Garden Tomb Jerusalem 2026". Sources: gardentomb.com, touristisrael.com/garden-tomb-jerusalem, lonelyplanet.com/israel/jerusalem/garden-tomb, roughguides.com/article/garden-tomb-jerusalem. [iter350 research] status: ready

- [P3] (seo-content, S) Mukhraka (Elijah's Fire) visitor guide (/mukhraka-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: python3 + grep "mukhraka\|mukhraqa\|elijah.*carmel\|carmel.*monastery" against full BACKLOG.md + DONE.md + guides/ directory — ZERO P-tagged standalone hits; grep of all 173 guide files: 0 Mukhraka-specific guides; day-trips-from-haifa.md (SHIPPED) mentions the Carmel range in passing (one sentence about "Druze villages") but ZERO mention of Mukhraka; haifa-travel-guide (backlog P2 M) has not yet been built; Tourist Israel and LP both have Mukhraka content. Mukhraka (Arabic: "the burning place") is a Carmelite monastery on the Carmel ridge above the Jezreel Valley at ~482m elevation: the traditional site of Elijah's contest with the 450 prophets of Baal (1 Kings 18) — where Elijah called down fire from heaven to consume his sacrifice while the Baal prophets' sacrifice remained cold; the Carmelite Order traces its spiritual origins to Elijah and this mountain; the current monastery was built 1883 by the Carmelite friars; a bronze statue of Elijah stands on the roof terrace; the hilltop panorama is one of Israel's best inland viewpoints — on clear days encompassing the Jezreel Valley, Mount Gilboa, Mount Tabor, Sea of Galilee, the Golan Heights, and (very clear days) Mount Hermon with snow. Content: biblical narrative context (1 Kings 18: Israel in a 3.5-year drought; Elijah's challenge to 850 prophets of Baal and Asherah; the fire test; the slaughter of the prophets at the Kishon River below; the cloud "as small as a man's hand" bringing rain; the theological climax of the Elijah cycle); Carmelite connection (the Order of Carmel — the Carmelite monastic order — takes its name from this mountain and considers Elijah their model and "father"; the 1883 Carmelite monastery is a pilgrimage site for Carmelite nuns and friars worldwide; small church inside with traditional stained glass depicting the fire narrative); the Elijah statue (bronze, rooftop terrace; friar carries sword and flame; excellent viewpoint backdrop for photography); panoramic terrace (one of the broadest inland panoramas in Israel; Jezreel Valley — the "bread basket of Israel" — stretches from the Carmel foothills to the Jordan Valley below; Megiddo archaeological site visible on a clear day in the valley 15km south; sunrise and early morning = best visibility before haze); practical visiting (7km from Daliyat al-Carmel Druze village via signposted road; 25 min drive from Haifa; car required; monastery church open Sun–Fri 8am–noon + 2pm–5pm; donation appreciated; free entry; small kiosk sometimes on-site; combine with Daliyat al-Carmel Druze village market [10 min east] for a full Carmel circuit; En Hod artists' village [15 min west] also possible as a half-day circuit). HONESTY: the specific rock of the Elijah's altar is unknown — the tradition identifying Mukhraka as the site is based on topographic fit (high point overlooking the Kishon Valley) rather than archaeological confirmation; any "this is where Elijah stood" language must be framed as "traditional site" or "the site where, by tradition..."; opening hours of the monastery church may vary on feast days — link Carmelite Order website for current schedule; panoramic views depend on weather and haze levels — winter/spring mornings = clearest. Affiliate: GYG Mt Carmel/Druze village day tour CTA + Booking.com Haifa hotels CTA. Cross-links: day-trips-from-haifa, christian-pilgrimage-holy-land, haifa-travel-guide (when built), galilee region hub, hiking-in-israel. SEO: "Mukhraka Israel guide", "Elijah fire site Mount Carmel", "Mukhraka monastery Israel visit", "Mount Carmel Elijah altar", "Carmelite monastery Israel pilgrimage". Sources: touristisrael.com/mukhraka, lonelyplanet.com/israel/haifa (Mukhraka section), carmelites.info/mt-carmel, parks.org.il. [iter350 research] status: ready

- [P3] (seo-content, S) Nabi Samuel (Tomb of Samuel) visitor guide (/nabi-samuel-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: python3 + grep "nabi samuel\|nebi samuel\|nabi-samuel\|prophet samuel.*tomb\|samuel.*tomb.*jerusalem" against full BACKLOG.md + DONE.md + guides/ directory — ZERO P-tagged standalone hits; grep of all 173 guide files: 0 dedicated pages; site appears 0 times in existing guides; Tourist Israel, Rough Guides, and Biblewalks all have dedicated Nabi Samuel content; LP lists it as an attraction near Jerusalem. Nabi Samwil (Arabic) / Nebi Samuel (Hebrew) / Nabi Samuel: a prominent 885m hilltop 5km north of Jerusalem's Old City, dominated by a mosque-synagogue complex built above the traditional tomb of the prophet Samuel (שמואל הנביא). Content: significance to three traditions (Judaism: Samuel was the last Judge of Israel and first king-maker — he anointed both Saul and David; his death and burial are recorded in 1 Samuel 25; the tomb chamber on the lower level of the complex is a Jewish prayer site venerated for centuries; Muslims: Nabi Samwil is a prophet in Islamic tradition; the 12th-century Crusader church built here was converted to a mosque by Saladin in 1187; the Ottoman-era mosque still stands, incorporating Crusader architectural elements — pointed arches, stonework — visible in the exterior; Christians: Samuel is a prophet in all three traditions); the complex (entering: Jewish prayer hall = ground-floor chamber beneath the mosque floor; tomb of Samuel surrounded by Jewish prayer books and candles; access via the side entrance; women/men separated prayer sections; the mosque = upper level; non-Muslim visitors can typically view the exterior of the mosque and the Crusader architectural elements — access policy for interior varies; the Crusader church remains incorporated in the building's walls — look for the half-rounded apse and the pointed-arch windows); hilltop viewpoint (885m elevation; from the roof terrace or the adjacent INPA viewing area, a sweeping panorama over northwest Jerusalem, the Ben-Gurion Airport visible on clear days, the Judean Hills, and the Ramallah area to the north; one of the few elevated points where the scale of the Jerusalem plateau is visible); practical visiting (6km north of the Old City; Route 436 from Ramot neighbourhood or Route 44 from Ben-Gurion Airport direction; INPA national park surrounding the hill (Nabi Samuel National Park); parking at the park entrance; 1.5–2h for the complex + viewpoint; mosque visit access: confirm current policy before visiting — approach respectfully, knock at mosque entrance; Jewish prayer hall access: any Jewish visitor or interested tourist; dress modestly; no entry fee for the complex itself; INPA park entry fee for the archaeological area around the hill). Archaeological context (Crusader "Castellum Emmaus" / "Mons Gaudii" — named "Mount of Joy" by Crusaders as it was the first place from which Jerusalem's towers became visible to pilgrims from the north; 12th-century fortress + church; Mamluk-Ottoman conversion; the Crusader monastic presence was significant here 1141–1187). HONESTY: the identification of this hilltop as Samuel's burial site is a medieval Jewish and Islamic tradition, not archaeologically confirmed — the 1 Samuel 25 text says Samuel was buried "in his house" at Ramah, whose location is disputed; present as "the traditional site of Samuel's tomb" or "venerated by Jewish tradition since the medieval period as..."; mosque upper level access policy varies — do not guarantee interior access in the guide; the surrounding INPA park has excavations from multiple periods — Israelite, Hasmonean, Byzantine — that are partially visible on guided walks. Affiliate: GYG Jerusalem extended day tour CTA + Booking.com Jerusalem hotels CTA. Cross-links: 3-days-in-jerusalem, day-trips-from-jerusalem, jewish-heritage-israel, christian-pilgrimage-holy-land, city-of-david-jerusalem, 1-day-jerusalem-itinerary. SEO: "Nabi Samuel Jerusalem guide", "Tomb of Samuel Jerusalem visit", "Nebi Samuel Israel", "Nabi Samwil mosque synagogue", "Jerusalem hilltop viewpoint". Sources: touristisrael.com/nabi-samuel, parks.org.il/nabi-samwil, biblewalks.com/NabiSamuel, roughguides.com (Nabi Samuel listing), lonelyplanet.com/israel/jerusalem. [iter350 research] status: ready

- [P3] (seo-content, S) Capernaum full visitor guide (/capernaum-visitor-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: attraction page exists at /galilee/capernaum (src/content/attractions/galilee-capernaum.md — ~200w attraction summary); python3 + grep "capernaum.*guide\|capernaum.*visitor.*guide\|/capernaum-guide" against BACKLOG.md + DONE.md — ZERO P-tagged standalone guide entries; this guide is DISTINCT from the attraction page (same rationale as jerusalem-garden-tomb-guide above); LP, Rough Guides, Tourist Israel, the Franciscan Custody, and Christiansintheland.com all have comprehensive Capernaum guides; "Capernaum Israel" = ~3,500 monthly searches (one of the highest-traffic Holy Land attraction queries without a dedicated guide on the site). Capernaum is identified in all 4 Gospels as the base of Jesus's Galilean ministry: Peter's house, the synagogue where Jesus taught, the healing miracles (paralytic lowered through the roof; Peter's mother-in-law; the centurion's servant). Content: biblical significance (all 4 Gospels identify Capernaum as Jesus's Galilean home base — "his own city" per Matthew 9:1; Peter, Andrew, James, and John were Capernaum fishermen; healings performed here: paralytic through the roof [Mark 2:1–12], centurion's servant [Matthew 8:5–13], Peter's mother-in-law [Mark 1:29–31]; the synagogue in which Jesus taught is referenced in Mark 1:21–28; Jesus's lament over Capernaum [Matthew 11:23] implies he expected them to respond to his miracles — "if the miracles done in you had been done in Sodom, it would have remained to this day"); Peter's house excavation (the Franciscan church at Capernaum is elevated on concrete pillars above the excavated insula (residential block) identified since 1968 as Peter's house — the octagonal Byzantine church built over it in the 5th century [an octagon = "special site" church type in Byzantine architecture, as at the Nativity in Bethlehem] is visible below the modern church; pilgrimage grafitti on the plaster walls dates to the 2nd–4th centuries referring to Peter and Jesus — the earliest textual evidence of the site's veneration; the excavation is viewable through the glass floor panels of the modern church); the two synagogues (White Synagogue = the visible limestone synagogue ruins are 4th–5th century CE — this is frequently misdescribed as "the synagogue where Jesus taught" but the limestone construction dates to centuries after Jesus; the Black Synagogue = basalt foundation stones beneath and around the White Synagogue ARE earlier — possibly Herodian-era, consistent with a 1st-century synagogue; the basalt foundations are visible in situ under and beside the white limestone ruins; understanding both is key to appreciating the site); practical visiting (Franciscan Custody site: northern side of the excavated town; entry fee ~₪25; the modern Franciscan church on pillars above Peter's house; the two synagogues visible from ground level; no photography inside the Franciscan church; dress code; Sundays often crowded with pilgrimage groups — weekday morning preferable; 1–2h for full site; accessible year-round; Sea of Galilee shoreline adjacent for post-visit walk; combine with Tabgha [Church of the Multiplication of Loaves and Fishes, 5km south] and Mount of Beatitudes [8km south] for a full Sea of Galilee pilgrimage circuit [one of Israel's most rewarding half-day combinations]). HONESTY: "this is where Jesus taught" framing requires hedging — the White Synagogue dates to centuries AFTER Jesus; the archaeological case for an earlier Black Synagogue beneath it is strong but scholarly discussion is ongoing; Peter's house identification is the strongest archaeological claim on the site (pilgrim graffiti + octagonal church pattern); the Franciscan Custody maintains ownership and access to the site — their current hours and entry fee should be confirmed at custodia.org; do not fabricate miracles or suggest the site is "definitely" the authentic location of any specific Gospel event — frame as "identified as" or "the traditional site of." Affiliate: GYG "Sea of Galilee Jesus tour" query CTA + Booking.com Tiberias hotels CTA. Cross-links: sea-of-galilee-guide, christian-pilgrimage-holy-land, nazareth-travel-guide, galilee region hub, galilee-tours-compared. SEO: "Capernaum Israel visitor guide", "Capernaum Peter's house guide", "Sea of Galilee biblical sites", "Capernaum synagogue Jesus", "Capernaum pilgrimage guide 2026". Sources: touristisrael.com/capernaum, lonelyplanet.com/israel/galilee/capernaum, franciscan.org/capernaum, custodia.org (Franciscan Custody Holy Land), christiansintheland.com/capernaum, biblewalks.com/Capernaum. [iter350 research] status: ready

- [P2] (seo-content+monetization, M) Jericho complete destination guide (/jericho-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: jericho-day-trip-from-jerusalem.md (SHIPPED iter213) covers logistics only (checkpoint crossing, entry formalities, what to expect); zero destination content on the major Jericho sites. Competitors: elijahtours.com/complete-jericho-guide, touristisrael.com/jericho/27731, lonelyplanet.com/israel/west-bank/jericho, roughguides.com all have standalone Jericho destination pages. World's oldest continuously inhabited city (settlement from ~9,000 BCE). Area A Palestinian Authority — same entry situation as Bethlehem (Israeli citizens cannot legally enter; foreign tourists enter freely with passport). Key sites: (1) Tell es-Sultan — the biblical Jericho mound (Neolithic 9000 BCE wells, Bronze Age city walls, Joshua's siege per Joshua 6; INPA co-managed; ₪20 entry); (2) Mount of Temptation (Jebel Quruntul) — where Jesus fasted 40 days per Matthew 4; Greek Orthodox monastery carved into the cliff face (7th-century origin); cable car from Jericho center to monastery entrance (₪30–40 return; runs 08:00–17:00 daily); dress code required; (3) Hisham's Palace (Khirbet al-Mafjar) — Umayyad winter palace 724–743 CE; the Tree of Life mosaic is one of the finest surviving early Islamic artworks; (4) Zacchaeus' Sycamore Tree — ancient sycamore in Jericho center (Luke 19:1–10); free; pilgrimage tradition; (5) Ein es-Sultan (Elisha's Spring) — biblical healing-of-the-waters spring (2 Kings 2:19–22); accessible within Ein es-Sultan park; (6) Jericho cable car viewpoint over Wadi Qelt gorge. Audience: Christian pilgrimage + archaeology + West Bank cultural tourism. HONESTY: Area A entry → always link border-crossings.md + is-israel-safe.md; Israeli citizens cannot legally enter; cable car hours vary seasonally — link visit-jericho.ps; Hisham's Palace mosaic access may change — reference RIWAQ (Palestinian heritage NGO); no fabricated entry prices. Affiliate: GYG Jericho + Bethlehem combined day tour CTA, Booking.com Jericho hotels CTA. Cross-links: jericho-day-trip-from-jerusalem.md (upgrade with destination content pointer), christian-pilgrimage-holy-land.md, bethlehem-day-trip.md, border-crossings.md. SEO: "Jericho Israel guide", "what to see in Jericho", "Mount of Temptation cable car", "Hisham's Palace Jericho", "Jericho ancient city 2026". Sources: elijahtours.com/jericho-guide-2026, touristisrael.com/jericho, lonelyplanet.com/israel/west-bank/jericho, parks.org.il, riwaq.org. [iter355 research] status: ready

- [P2] (seo-content, S) Israel dark tourism guide — memorial sites, war history & difficult heritage (/israel-dark-tourism) — CONFIRMED GAP: yad-vashem-visitor-guide.md (SHIPPED) covers Yad Vashem in depth but no thematic hub exists for the broader dark tourism circuit; zero backlog P-tagged entries for "dark tourism". Dark tourism is a recognised travel niche (dark-tourism.com, responsibletravel.com both have dedicated Israel sections ranking well). Content: framing section (dark tourism = educational travel to sites of tragedy; respectful, not voyeuristic); major sites: (1) Atlit Detainee Camp (near Haifa) — British Mandate detention center 1938–1948 where Jewish "illegal" immigrants were interned; preserved original barracks, watchtowers, immigrant ship replica; INPA-managed; under-visited relative to its historical significance; (2) Ghetto Fighters' House Museum (Kibbutz Lohamei HaGeta'ot, Western Galilee) — Israel's only Holocaust museum outside Jerusalem; founded by Warsaw Ghetto Uprising survivors; Yad Layeled Children's Memorial; (3) Beit Theresienstadt (Givat Haim Ihud) — documentation center for Jews deported from Terezín; small, significant; (4) Ammunition Hill, Jerusalem — site of the fiercest battle of the June 1967 Six-Day War; preserved trenches + bunkers + audiovisual exhibit; free entry; (5) Acre (Akko) Prison — British Mandate military prison; Irgun prisoner escape 1947; underground cells; INPA; cross-link akko-acre-guide.md; (6) Latrun Armored Corps Museum — 1948 battles + tank collection; cross-link latrun-guide.md; (7) Kfar Etzion Field School museum (Gush Etzion; 1948 massacre site; West Bank). HONESTY: do not sensationalise or treat as entertainment; respect visiting protocols; some sites require advance booking; link official sites for hours; Kfar Etzion is in the West Bank (Area C) — note context; no fabricated admission prices; frame Atlit as still emotionally charged. Affiliate: GYG "Holocaust remembrance and memorial Israel" tour CTA, Booking.com Haifa/Galilee hotels CTA. Cross-links: yad-vashem-visitor-guide.md, akko-acre-guide.md, latrun-guide.md, jewish-heritage-israel.md, haifa-travel-guide.md. SEO: "dark tourism Israel", "Israel memorial sites guide", "Holocaust remembrance sites Israel beyond Yad Vashem", "Atlit Detainee Camp guide". - [P2] (seo-content+monetization, M) Ashkelon visitor guide — ancient Philistine city, national park & beach resort (/ashkelon-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: no Ashkelon content anywhere on the site; zero backlog P-tagged entries. Ashkelon is Israel's largest coastal city south of Tel Aviv (pop. ~140,000) and one of the world's most archaeologically significant sites. Competitors: lonelyplanet.com/israel/ashkelon, touristisrael.com/ashkelon/27731, roughguides.com/middle-east/israel/ashkelon all have standalone city guides. Dual appeal: (1) Ashkelon National Park (INPA) — oldest surviving arched gateway in the world (Canaanite gate, ~1800 BCE; completely preserved); Roman forum + Byzantine-era basilica ruins; Philistine occupation layers; the world's largest ancient dog cemetery (1,000+ dogs buried by Phoenicians 5th century BCE, documented in Journal of Archaeological Science 2011); park also contains Herodian-era colonnaded street fragments; (2) City history — Canaanite Bronze Age (Egyptian Execration Texts 19th c. BCE), Philistine Iron Age (one of the five Pentapolis cities; linked to Delilah and the Gaza narrative of Samson), Herodian (Herod's purported birthplace), Byzantine port, Crusader maritime hub, Ottoman town; (3) Modern beach city — 3 km of sandy beaches; Barnea Beach + Delilah's Lagoon Beach (free entry); coastal promenade; beach cafés; (4) Afridar quarter — descendants of 20th-century West African/Nigerian immigrants; unique cultural community. Proximity to Gaza (20 km) requires honest safety framing. HONESTY: always link is-israel-safe.md for current security situation; national park hours via parks.org.il/ashkelon; dog cemetery archaeological framing (published science, not legend); no fabricated prices; Herod's birthplace is a matter of historical tradition, not certainty. Affiliate: GYG "Ashkelon day trip from Tel Aviv" CTA, DiscoverCars car rental CTA (easy self-drive 45 min from TLV), Booking.com Ashkelon hotels CTA. - [P3] (seo-content, S) Circassian villages of Israel guide — Kfar Kama & Reyhaniye (/circassian-villages-israel) — CONFIRMED GAP: zero backlog entries for "circassian" in any form; zero guide files. Circassians are a ~4,000-person Sunni Muslim minority in Israel (Adyghe people from the North Caucasus, resettled in Ottoman Palestine 1878 after the Russo-Circassian War 1864). Only two villages: Kfar Kama (Galilee, near Tiberias, pop. ~3,200) and Reyhaniye (Upper Galilee near Golan, pop. ~1,300). Unique tourism angle: Circassians are one of the few Muslim communities in Israel who serve voluntarily in the IDF (alongside Druze). Content: cultural background (distinct from Arab population in language, customs, cuisine, and ethnic origin; Adyghe language + Hebrew + Arabic trilingual; traditional dress preserved for festivals); Kfar Kama visit — Circassian Heritage Museum (open by arrangement; covers the 1864 diaspora, traditional tools, textiles); traditional food (Circassian chicken = walnut sauce poached chicken; layers of pastry + walnuts; brined white Circassian cheese); Nowruz celebration (late May/early June — traditional Adyghe New Year; open to visitors; community performances + food); folk dance troupe (Adyghe national dance performances; contact Kfar Kama Cultural Center); Reyhaniye (smaller, more isolated; traditional Adyghe crafts + weaving). Practical: no formal tourism office in either village — group tours available via Galilee operators (Abraham Tours, Nazareth Experience tours list Kfar Kama); individual visits welcome but must contact community center in advance (kfarkama.co.il). HONESTY: neither village has commercial tourist infrastructure; this is genuine community-based cultural tourism, not a packaged experience — frame honestly; IDF service is voluntary and stems from community decision, not imposition — describe accurately; no fabricated population statistics (use ~4,000 for the Israeli Circassian community per CBS data). Cross-links: druze-villages-carmel.md, galilee region page, haifa-travel-guide.md. SEO: "Circassian Israel", "Kfar Kama Israel visit", "Circassian village tour Galilee", "minority culture Israel". Sources: touristisrael.com/kfar-kama, kfarkama.co.il, jewishvirtuallibrary.org/circassian, abrahamtours.com Galilee tours. [iter355 research] status: ready

- [P3] (seo-content+monetization, S) Cremisan Winery & Beit Jala guide — Salesian monks, Palestinian wine & Jerusalem hilltop views (/beit-jala-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: zero backlog entries for "cremisan" or "beit jala"; zero guide files. Beit Jala is a predominantly Christian Arab town 2 km west of Bethlehem with an internationally known wine tourism draw. Cremisan Winery is operated by the Salesians of Don Bosco since 1885 — one of the oldest continuously operating wineries in the Holy Land — producing ~400,000 bottles/year; the monastery's hilltop terrace has panoramic views extending to Jerusalem's Old City on clear days. Content: Beit Jala overview (majority Christian Arab population; hilltop at 895m — noticeably cooler than Bethlehem; historic olive oil culture; Church of St Nicholas (Latin Patriarchate); hilltop café culture with valley views); Cremisan Winery tour logistics (45-min guided tour of barrel cellar + fermentation tanks + garden terrace; wine tasting of 3–4 varieties; group tours preferred — book via salesian-cremisan.com; advance reservation 2–3 weeks recommended; accessible by taxi from Bethlehem Manger Square ~15 min or bus service from Bethlehem junction); wine varieties (Cremisan Blanc — Hamdani + Jandali indigenous Palestinian grape varieties + Gewürztraminer blend; Cremisan Rouge — Shiraz + Carignan; Cremisan Sparkling El Bandak — native grape revival; white and red both available internationally); combine with Bethlehem day trip. HONESTY: Area A (Bethlehem municipality) — Israeli citizens cannot legally enter; foreign tourists enter freely with passport; always reference border-crossings.md + is-israel-safe.md; Cremisan Winery grapes described as "locally grown indigenous varieties" — not claiming exclusivity; prices and tour times from salesian-cremisan.com (do not hardcode prices); Cremisan is a Palestinian winery, NOT an Israeli producer — make this distinction clearly in israel-wine-wineries.md cross-link note. Affiliate: GYG "Bethlehem + Cremisan wine tour" CTA, Booking.com Bethlehem hotels CTA. Cross-links: jerusalem-bethlehem-day-trip.md, israel-wine-wineries.md, border-crossings.md. SEO: "Cremisan Winery guide", "Beit Jala visit", "Palestinian wine tour", "Bethlehem wine tasting", "Cremisan monastery tour". - [P3] (seo-content, S) Samaritan community guide — Mount Gerizim, Kiryat Luza & Passover ceremony (/samaritan-community-israel) — CONFIRMED GAP: zero backlog entries for "samaritan" as a tourism subject (backlog has no related P-tagged entry; samaritan references elsewhere in content are to the biblical Good Samaritan parable, not the actual Samaritan people); zero guide files. The Samaritan people (~840 worldwide in 2020 census — one of the world's smallest religious minorities) live split between Kiryat Luza village on Mount Gerizim (West Bank, near Nablus) and the Holon/Tel Aviv coastal area. Content: background (Samaritans = ancient Israelite sect descended from the Northern Kingdom of Israel; accept only the Pentateuch / Torah; do not accept Jerusalem as holy — Mount Gerizim is their sacred site; maintain a distinct Hebrew script older than the Assyrian square script used in standard Hebrew today; separate Samaritan Torah with ~6,000 textual variants including the 10th commandment referencing Gerizim); Kiryat Luza village visit (Mount Gerizim summit, Area B — both Israeli citizens and foreign tourists can access; ~400 Samaritans; distinctive dress; community bilingual in Hebrew and Arabic; Samaritan Museum; ancient synagogue site; community is hospitable to arranged visits); the Passover ceremony (world's most ancient continuous Passover celebration; traditional sheep sacrifice on Passover eve at Mount Gerizim summit at sunset; hundreds of international tourists, media and researchers attend annually; Samaritan Passover date follows their own calendar — typically 1 to 4 weeks offset from Jewish/Western Passover — verify annually via thesamaritanupdate.com); Holon Samaritans (some families also maintain residence in Holon near Tel Aviv; easier access alternative). HONESTY: do not confuse the real Samaritan people with the biblical parable character (the "Good Samaritan" of Luke 10:25–37 was a fictional figure — explicitly clarify this distinction); Passover sheep sacrifice is graphic — warn readers honestly; Mount Gerizim is in Area B (joint Palestinian Authority/Israeli security control) — Israeli citizens can enter; no fabricated population data (use ~840 figure from reliable sources); community visiting requires advance coordination — do not suggest walk-in tourism; frame as cultural education, not curiosity about a relic community. Cross-links: border-crossings.md, is-israel-safe.md, christian-pilgrimage-holy-land.md, jewish-heritage-israel.md. SEO: "Samaritan Israel guide", "Mount Gerizim visit", "Samaritan Passover ceremony", "Kiryat Luza guide", "ancient Samaritans Israel". Sources: thesamaritanupdate.com, mt-gerizim.com, haaretz.com/samaritan-passover, touristisrael.com/samaritans, timesofisrael.com samaritan articles. [iter355 research] status: ready

- [SHIPPED iter363 → 236efef] Nimrod Fortress National Park — largest medieval castle in Israel (/nimrod-fortress-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep "nimrod-fortress\|nimrod fortress guide\|nimrod national park guide" against full BACKLOG.md returns only cross-references embedded in other items (northern-israel-road-trip P2, mount-hermon-skiing P2/P3); zero standalone P-tagged Nimrod entry; DONE.md and all guide files contain zero mentions at guide-file level. Competitors with dedicated Nimrod Fortress guides: beinharimtours.com/nimrod-fortress, touristisrael.com/nimrod-fortress/6010, israel-in-photos.com/nimrod-fortress, igoogledisrael.com/nimrod-fortress, laidbacktrip.com/nimrod-fortress-israel, tripadvisor.com (500+ reviews) — all rank for "Nimrod Fortress Israel" and "best castles in Israel". Nimrod Fortress (Qal'at Nimrod) is the largest intact medieval castle in Israel, located at 800m elevation on a ridge at the foot of Mount Hermon in the northern Golan Heights, 40 km northeast of Tiberias. Built 1229 CE by the Ayyubid ruler Al-Aziz Muhammad as a strategic fortress protecting the route to Damascus; reinforced by the Mamluk sultan Baibars in 1275 (inscription still visible near the western gate). Features: 21 towers; outer + inner defensive walls; seven-sided "beautiful tower"; 4-storey south-western tower with panoramic views of the Hula Valley; water cisterns; the "keep" (highest point — views of Mount Hermon); a 27-metre secret staircase passage descending from the keep to the moat; Baibars inscription 1275 (one of few surviving Ayyubid/Mamluk historical inscriptions in situ in Israel). Natural pairing: Banias Nature Reserve (Hermon Stream) is 5 km southwest — same access road, same day trip; Tel Dan Nature Reserve is 15 km west. INPA national park: INPA pass valid; ₪22 adults; open daily 08:00–17:00 summer / 08:00–16:00 winter. Trailhead has a 2-hour circuit; not recommended for young children (steep spiral staircases). HONESTY: Nimrod Fortress is NOT a Crusader castle — it was built by Ayyubid Muslims (important historical distinction that many tour sites get wrong; the Crusaders built other Golan forts but not this one); the "Nimrod" name is a later appellation, not historical; verify Baibars inscription readability at current INPA condition before committing to specific text. Affiliate: GYG Northern Israel Golan day-tour CTA, DiscoverCars car rental CTA (no public transport to site). Cross-links: golan-heights-guide.md (add Nimrod bullet), day-trips-from-haifa.md (Golan option upgrade), banias-nature-reserve-guide.md (when built — same-day circuit note). SEO: "Nimrod Fortress Israel guide", "medieval castle Israel Golan", "Golan Heights castle", "Nimrod Castle visitor guide", "Qal'at Nimrod". Sources: beinharimtours.com/nimrod-fortress, touristisrael.com/nimrod-fortress/6010, israel-in-photos.com/nimrod-fortress, en.parks.org.il/nimrod-fortress, igoogledisrael.com/nimrod-fortress. [iter360 research] status: ready

- [SHIPPED iter432 → fbd6201] Hai Bar Yotvata Nature Reserve — self-drive biblical wildlife safari near Eilat (/hai-bar-yotvata-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep "hai.bar\|hai-bar\|yotvata\|hai bar yotvata" against full BACKLOG.md returns only a single cross-reference inside the birdwatching hub item (P2 in backlog, not shipped); zero standalone P-tagged entry; zero guide files mention the reserve at all. Competitors with dedicated Hai Bar Yotvata guides: nativa.co.il/hay-bar-yotvata-nature-reserve-guide, israel-in-photos.com/hai-bar-yotvata, touristisrael.com/yotvata-hai-bar-nature-reserve/8806, lonelyplanet.com/israel/the-arava/hai-bar-yotvata, beinharimtours.com/hay-bar-yotvata, worldjewishtravel.org/hai-bar-yotvata — all rank for "Hai Bar Yotvata" and "wildlife reserve near Eilat". The Yotvata Hai-Bar Nature Reserve is a 3,000-acre (12 km²) INPA biblical wildlife breeding and reacclimation centre administered jointly with Israel's Wildlife Protection Unit, located 35 km north of Eilat on Highway 90, between Kibbutz Yotvata and Kibbutz Samar. Mission: breed endangered and locally extinct animals mentioned in the Bible for reintroduction to the Negev and Arava. Animals: white oryx (Oryx leucoryx; formerly extinct in the wild; the reserve's landmark species); onager / Asiatic wild ass (Equus hemionus onager; the biblical pere — "wild donkey" of Job 39:5–8); ostrich (reintroduced; historically native); Arabian sand gazelle; Dorcas gazelle; spotted eagle owl (nocturnal aviary section). TIMELY NEWS HOOK (March 2026): 33 white oryx calves and a rare scimitar oryx (Oryx dammah; virtually extinct in the wild) were born at the reserve, the largest single-season birth cohort in its history — a strong 2026 content hook. Self-drive format: visitors drive personal vehicles through the open-range section (no buses/tours in open range); guided minivan tour of nocturnal section (predators: leopard-cat, caracal, sand cat; separate booking). Summer: arrive early (08:30 opening) as midday temperatures exceed 40°C in the Arava. HONESTY: animals are in a large fenced open range (not "wild" in the unconfined sense); the leopard and lion cannot be reintroduced to Israel currently — do not suggest this; the "biblical animals" framing is a conservation narrative — frame it honestly as inspired by the textual record, not proven zoologically in every case; prices from eilattickets.com (do not hardcode — verify on-site). Affiliate: DiscoverCars car rental CTA (Highway 90 drive from Eilat is required; no public transport), Booking.com Eilat or Kibbutz Yotvata accommodation CTA (Kibbutz Yotvata adjacent has a working guesthouse and the famous Yotvata dairy). Cross-links: eilat-travel-guide.md (add Hai Bar as day-trip option from Eilat), eilat-diving-snorkeling.md (alternative non-beach activity), car-rental-israel.md (required transport note). SEO: "Hai Bar Yotvata guide", "wildlife safari near Eilat Israel", "white oryx Israel", "Arava wildlife reserve", "Hai Bar Yotvata 2026". Sources: nativa.co.il/hay-bar-yotvata-nature-reserve-guide, israel-in-photos.com/hai-bar-yotvata, touristisrael.com/yotvata-hai-bar, en.parks.org.il/hay-bar-yotvata, lonelyplanet.com/israel/the-arava/hai-bar-yotvata. [iter360 research] status: ready

- [P2] (seo-content+monetization, S) Achziv National Park — Mediterranean sea lagoons, camping & Phoenician ruins (/achziv-national-park-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep "achziv\|akhziv\|achziv national park\|achziv-guide" against full BACKLOG.md returns only: (a) a passing Achziv mention inside the western-galilee-guide P2 item, and (b) a one-line mention in the Mediterranean diving guide; zero standalone P-tagged Achziv entry; best-beaches-israel.md (SHIPPED iter262) mentions Achziv in a single table row only. Competitors with dedicated Achziv guides: nativa.co.il/achziv-national-park-guide, israel-in-photos.com/achziv, touristisrael.com/achziv/8771, tripadvisor.com (400+ reviews), beinharimtours.com/akhziv-national-park, worldbeachguide.com/israel/achziv — all rank for "Achziv National Park" and "Israel sea lagoons" queries. Achziv National Park is a 5 km stretch of pristine Mediterranean coastline 5 km north of Nahariya in the Western Galilee, administered by INPA, featuring a completely unique natural feature: sealed sea lagoons formed where kurkar limestone ridges trap seawater, creating shallow warm pools that are ideal for families with young children (30°C in summer) while deeper areas offer Mediterranean snorkelling over rocky reef. Archaeological: within the park is the site of the ancient Phoenician city of Achziv (biblical Achzib — Joshua 19:29; Judges 1:31; Micah 1:14); visible stone foundations of fishing village + olive oil press remains (pre-Hellenistic); Phoenician burial caves (some accessible). Natural: largest remaining sea turtle nesting beach in Israel (Mediterranean loggerhead Caretta caretta; nesting June–August; INPA wardens protect nests — do not disturb); sea anemones, stone crabs, blennies in tide pools; kurkar ridge coastal plants including Yellow Horned Poppy (rare in Israel). Camping: massive campground (25 dunams; INPA managed; must pre-book via parks.org.il — limited capacity introduced post-COVID); summer capacity fills weeks ahead. HONESTY: advance reservations at INPA website required (do NOT suggest walk-in camping); sea lagoons are natural features that vary by season (summer = full; winter = partially dried); sea turtle nesting = do not approach nests; Achziv is not a snorkelling dive site (Mediterranean visibility 5–15m; best by Israeli standards but not Red Sea quality). Affiliate: Booking.com Nahariya accommodation CTA (5 km south; nearest base), DiscoverCars car rental CTA (Achziv is not well-served by public transport). Cross-links: day-trips-from-haifa.md (add Achziv to Western Galilee section), best-beaches-israel.md (upgrade Achziv bullet with guide link), nahariya-guide.md (when built — same corridor), western-galilee-guide.md (when built — include Achziv). SEO: "Achziv National Park guide", "Israel sea lagoons", "Israel Mediterranean camping", "Achziv beach Israel", "Nahariya day trip 2026". Sources: nativa.co.il/achziv-national-park-guide, israel-in-photos.com/achziv, touristisrael.com/achziv/8771, en.parks.org.il/akhziv-national-park. [iter360 research] status: ready

- [P3] (seo-content, S) Mount Arbel National Park — cliff hike above the Sea of Galilee (/mount-arbel-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep "mount arbel\|mount-arbel\|arbel national park\|arbel cliff guide" against full BACKLOG.md returns only cross-references inside the Jesus Trail hiking guide (P2) and best-hikes-israel guide (P2) — neither has Mount Arbel as a standalone guide item; zero P-tagged standalone entry; zero guide files in src/content/guides/ for Arbel. Competitors: hike-israel.com/arbel (standalone guide + trail map), igoogledisrael.com/mount-arbel (dedicated guide), touristisrael.com/mount-arbel/2602 (dedicated page), hikingintheholyland.com/mount-arbel (multi-photo guide), slavaguide.com/mount-arbel — all rank for "Mount Arbel hike" and "Arbel cliff Israel". Mount Arbel National Park is an INPA national park in the Lower Galilee, 3 km west of Tiberias, rising 390m above the shore of the Sea of Galilee (Kinneret) in near-vertical limestone cliffs. The panoramic view from the summit (and especially from the cliff descent) is one of the most dramatic in Israel: the entire Sea of Galilee, Tiberias city, the Golan Heights plateau, Mount Hermon in the north, and the Jordan Valley extending south all visible simultaneously. Key features: (1) Cliff path descent with iron handrails and carved rock steps — not via-ferrata level but adventurous; the tightest passages require sideways movement and careful footing; 45–90 min descent; (2) Cave fortress in the cliff face — Herodian-period cave complex used by Jewish rebels (Zealots?) and later converted to defensive positions; Josephus records Herod the Great's soldiers descending in baskets on ropes to clear the caves (Jewish War 1.16.4 / Antiquities 14.15.5 — primary source); archaeologically significant as a rare in-situ Herodian military cave complex; (3) Ancient synagogue ruins at the summit — Byzantine-era village synagogue foundations; (4) Jesus Trail passes through Arbel — pilgrimage trail starting at Nazareth and ending at Capernaum; Arbel cliffs are the dramatic finale of the 60-km trail's Galilee segment; (5) Magdala proximity — ancient Magdala (Mary Magdalene's birthplace) is at the base of the cliffs, 2 km from Tiberias; natural day-trip cluster. HONESTY: summit path and cliff descent NOT recommended for children under 10 or those with vertigo or mobility challenges; summer heat extreme (exposed trail with no shade for 2 km; temps 35–38°C July–August — morning-only hiking strongly recommended); trail closes after rain (slippery rock; check parks.org.il before visiting); the cave fortress attribution to Herodian period is archaeologically established but literary accounts in Josephus are the primary source (not physical inscriptions). Affiliate: GYG Tiberias Galilee day-tour CTA (Arbel can anchor a Tiberias/Galilee tour), Booking.com Tiberias/Sea of Galilee accommodation CTA. Cross-links: tiberias-guide.md (add Arbel as main day-trip option), sea-of-galilee-guide.md (add Arbel hiking note), hiking-in-israel.md (add Arbel description), galilee-christian-sites-circuit.md (Magdala + Arbel natural pairing). SEO: "Mount Arbel hike", "Arbel cliff Israel", "Sea of Galilee hike", "Tiberias day trip hike", "Mount Arbel National Park guide". Sources: hike-israel.com/arbel, igoogledisrael.com/mount-arbel, touristisrael.com/mount-arbel/2602, hikingintheholyland.com/mount-arbel, en.parks.org.il/arbel. [iter360 research] status: ready

- [P3] (seo-content, S) Chagall Windows at Hadassah Hospital Jerusalem (/chagall-windows-jerusalem) — CONFIRMED GAP: ein-kerem-jerusalem-guide.md (SHIPPED iter331) mentions the Chagall Windows 3 times as a key Ein Kerem attraction but has no space for a deep treatment; zero standalone P-tagged backlog entry; zero guide files specifically for the Chagall Windows. Competitors: tzurtours.com/chagall-windows, funinjerusalem.com/chagall-windows, dannythedigger.com/chagall-windows, worldjewishtravel.org/chagall-windows, beinharimtours.com/chagall-windows-at-hadassah — all rank for "Chagall Windows Jerusalem" and "Hadassah Hospital synagogue visit". The 12 stained-glass windows are one of the most significant works of 20th-century religious art in the world: Marc Chagall was commissioned by the Hadassah Women's Organization in 1959 and donated 12 windows (each ~11 feet / 3.3m tall × 8 feet / 2.4m wide) to the synagogue of Hadassah Hospital Ein Kerem in 1962. Each window represents one of the 12 Tribes of Israel (12 sons of Jacob), with animals, colours, symbols, and text from the Twelve Blessings (Genesis 49, Deuteronomy 33) as the iconographic programme. Significance: one of only three buildings worldwide housing a complete set of Chagall stained-glass windows (the others are All Saints' Church, Tudley, Kent, UK and the Cathedral of Metz, France); Hadassah Ein Kerem is the only Chagall stained-glass cycle outside Europe; entirely non-figurative human representation (no human faces — respecting the Jewish prohibition on graven images); Chagall collaborated with master glazier Charles Marq, developing a new 3-colour-per-pane technique. Story hook: during the 1967 Six-Day War, several windows were damaged by Jordanian artillery shells and Chagall himself returned to Israel to personally repair the Tribe of Benjamin window — a widely shared anecdote with documentation. Visiting: open Sun–Thu 08:30–15:30 (guided + self-guided); last guided tour 13:30; English, Hebrew, French, German guided tours; audio guides in Dutch + Spanish; ₪30–40 entry; Hadassah Hospital Ein Kerem (specify Ein Kerem — there are TWO Hadassah campuses: Ein Kerem and Mount Scopus); bus 19 from Jerusalem Central Bus Station (40 min); taxi from Mahane Yehuda ~₪50. HONESTY: this is a functioning hospital — dress respectfully, follow hospital rules; no independent wandering in the hospital outside the synagogue/visitor area; the windows show battle + death imagery as part of tribal blessings narrative — note this so visitors aren't surprised; 1967 repair story widely told but verify specific details (the Tribe of Benjamin damage) before committing to specifics. Affiliate: GYG Jerusalem Old City + Ein Kerem combined tour CTA, Booking.com Jerusalem accommodation CTA. Cross-links: ein-kerem-jerusalem-guide.md (upgrade Chagall paragraph → guide link), christian-pilgrimage-holy-land.md (add Hadassah/Chagall as Jewish sacred art point), best-museums-jerusalem or jerusalem-museums backlog item (cross-reference). SEO: "Chagall Windows Jerusalem guide", "Hadassah Hospital synagogue visit", "Jerusalem stained glass Chagall", "Ein Kerem things to do". Sources: tzurtours.com/chagall-windows, dannythedigger.com/chagall-windows, jerusalem-insiders-guide.com/chagall-windows, publicdelivery.org/chagall-jerusalem-windows, hadassahmagazine.org/chagall-windows. - [P2] (seo-content, S) Arava Desert Mountain Biking — Yahel-Timna singletrack trail guide (/arava-mountain-biking) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep "yahel.timna\|yahel-timna\|timna.*singletrack\|arava.*MTB\|arava.*bike\b" against full BACKLOG.md returns ZERO matches. A new 100+ km continuous desert singletrack trail opened late 2025 / early 2026, connecting Kibbutz Yahel to Timna Park through the Arava Valley in the southern Negev. Built with a ₪20 million investment by the Eilot Economic Development Corporation; brings the Arava's total bike route network to 350+ km (combining the Israel Bike Trail southern section + Yahel-Timna singletrack + 200+ km of gravel and asphalt loops). The singletrack winds through volcanic black basalt ridges and limestone wadis before descending to Timna Park's copper-mining landscape (ochre, sienna, and white sandstone formations). TIMELY HOOK (2026): the route opened to public promotion in early 2026 as part of the Eilot Regional Council's outdoor tourism push; no English-language standalone guide exists on any major travel publisher. Competitors with relevant Israel MTB content: bike-israel.com/self-guided-yahel-timna (logistics map only), alltrails.com (Yahel Bike Loop GPS track only, no context guide), viagginbici.com (Italian language only), parktimna.co.il/bicycle (Timna Park side only). Content: what the Yahel-Timna trail is (new infrastructure angle); trail character (desert singletrack through volcanic + limestone landscape; mix of technical rocky sections and fast gravel traverses; suitable for intermediate MTB riders + above; hardtail and full-suspension bikes both viable); logistics (start point: Kibbutz Yahel, accessible from Eilat by car, 55 km north on Highway 90; end: Timna Park bike exit; shuttle recommended for one-way; no bikepacking camping on singletrack section — day ride format; bring 3+ litres water); combining with Timna Park (option: enter from the bike trail end, spend 2 hrs at King Solomon's Mines + Mushroom Rock + Egyptian temple ruins); Arava network context (350+ km total routes; Eilot council tourist app for route maps); best season (October–March; avoid May–September as Arava valley temps reach 38–45°C). HONESTY: trail infrastructure is new — conditions may evolve; trail not suitable in wet weather (flash-flood risk in desert wadis; ims.gov.il check before riding); no bike rental in Kibbutz Yahel — rent from Eilat bike shops or bring own; no emergency rescue on trail outside Timna Park boundary — carry phone + hydration beyond minimum. Affiliate: DiscoverCars car hire CTA (Highway 90 drive from Eilat required; no public transport to Kibbutz Yahel), GetYourGuide Eilat adventure tours CTA (secondary). Cross-links: eilat-travel-guide.md (add Yahel-Timna MTB as day-trip option), red-canyon-eilat.md (same-day Red Canyon → Yahel combination possible for strong riders), timna-park-guide.md (when built). SEO: "Arava mountain biking guide", "Yahel Timna trail Israel", "desert mountain biking Israel", "Eilat MTB singletrack", "Israel bike trail southern section". Sources: bike-israel.com/self-guided-yahel-timna, i24news.tv (Yahel-Timna opening coverage), israelvalley.com/2026/02/12 (investment announcement), alltrails.com/trail/israel/southern-district-hadarom/yahel-bike-loop, parktimna.co.il/bicycle, eilot.org.il. - [P2] (seo-content+monetization, M) Best hotels in Mitzpe Ramon — crater rim accommodation guide (/best-hotels-mitzpe-ramon) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep "best-hotels-mitzpe\|mitzpe-ramon-hotel\|mitzpe-ramon.*hotel.guide\|hotel.*mitzpe-ramon" against full BACKLOG.md returns ZERO standalone matches (mitzpe-ramon-guide.md is a general destination guide, not a hotel-focused money page). TIMELY HOOK (2026): two major new luxury developments under construction on the crater rim — VERT Hotel by Africa Israel Hotels (260 rooms; opening targeted 2027–2028) and EDGE Hotel by Geshem Group (260 rooms; adjacent site); both represent the largest planned expansion of Mitzpe Ramon accommodation in the town's history. Current accommodation options: (1) Beresheet by Isrotel Exclusive (5-star; the iconic property; 110 rooms + 5 private pool suites; perched on crater rim with private infinity-view pools; MICHELIN Guide Israel 2023 inclusion for its restaurant; internationally recognised; advance booking required months ahead for peak season); (2) Daroma by Isrotel (4-star; same management group; more affordable with same Makhtesh Ramon location; family-friendly; pool); (3) Ramon Suites by Smart Hotels (3-star; 40 suites; central Mitzpe Ramon; budget-to-mid range; breakfast included); (4) Mid-range guesthouses (Mitzpe Ramon has ~20 family-run guesthouses and glamping sites; best clustered on Nahal HaMakhtesh strip overlooking the crater; prices ₪300–700/night). Selection framework: for top-tier crater-edge luxury → Beresheet; for family + crater views at lower price point → Daroma; for central town access + walkability → Ramon Suites; for authentic atmosphere + low cost → crater-rim guesthouses. HONESTY: Beresheet prices typically ₪1,500–4,500/night depending on season and room type; link Booking.com for current rates; the crater-rim guesthouses require a car for evening dining (Mitzpe Ramon town center is 2 km from the rim); VERT and EDGE hotels are not yet open — do not present as bookable options; the MICHELIN Guide listing for Beresheet's restaurant is confirmed from the 2023 MICHELIN Guide Israel; Beresheet also hosts the MICHELIN Green Star (sustainability). Monetization: Booking.com hotel CTA per tier; DiscoverCars car rental CTA (public transport to Mitzpe Ramon is very limited — Beer Sheva bus connection only). Dense cross-links: mitzpe-ramon-guide.md, negev-stargazing.md (dark sky designation makes Beresheet's stargazing terrace a highlight), negev-desert-hiking.md, israel-glamping-guide.md (overlap with glamping guesthouses). SEO: "best hotels Mitzpe Ramon", "where to stay Makhtesh Ramon", "Beresheet hotel guide", "Mitzpe Ramon accommodation 2026", "Makhtesh Ramon luxury hotel". Sources: ynetnews.com/travel/article/sjphzx8oxx (VERT+EDGE hotel announcements), booking.com/city/il/mitzpe-ramon, secrettelaviv.com/best-hotels-in-mitzpe-ramon, myisraelstay.com/the-17-hotel-openings, beresheet.isrotel.com.
