- [P3] (seo-content+monetization, S) Israel as a European winter sun holiday (/israel-winter-sun-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep scan for "winter.sun|winter-sun-guide|winter.*escape.*israel|israel-winter-guide\b" in BACKLOG.md + DONE.md returns ZERO standalone P-item matches. Christmas-in-israel.md (SHIPPED iter292) = Christian holiday focus Dec 24/25 + Jan 6/18; israel-in-autumn.md (SHIPPED iter386) = Sep–Nov; best-time-to-visit-israel.md (SHIPPED) = general annual guide. None targets the EUROPEAN WINTER SUN traveler escaping Jan–Feb cold specifically. WHY VALUABLE: Israel is a genuine winter sun destination for UK/Germany/Netherlands/Sweden travelers (Eilat 22°C in January when London is 7°C) but almost no editorial content positions Israel in this "winter sun" framing that dominates British/Scandinavian travel planning. Competitors: Audley Travel, Kuoni, Thomas Cook winter sun ranges feature Canaries/Thailand/UAE — Israel is NEVER in the winter sun category despite Eilat's obvious qualification. CONTENT: Eilat Red Sea in January (air temp 22–24°C Jan; water temp 21°C = warm swimming year-round; Coral Beach Nature Reserve snorkeling + Dolphin Reef visits; diving season at its best visibility Nov–March; Eilat hotels show better value Jan–Feb vs Jul–Aug Israeli school holidays; Yitzhak Rabin border → Petra day trip still easy in winter — smaller crowds than spring/summer; Jordan Rift Valley geology even more striking without heat haze); Dead Sea in winter (Dead Sea January temp: 18–20°C — warmer than Jerusalem which can hit 5–8°C same day; low evaporation in winter slightly reduces salt concentration relative to summer; Ein Bokek resort strip; some spa hotels offer romantic fireplace suites in contrast with warm daytime mineral-rich float; climatotherapy peak: European psoriasis/eczema patients specifically come Dec–Feb for maximum UV difference); Tel Aviv in winter (14–17°C; too cool for Mediterranean swimming but ideal for walking, food, culture; Carmel Market + Sarona Market + Florentin at their least crowded; restaurant tables easy to get; Bauhaus architecture walks pleasant without summer heat; Neve Tzedek galleries open; winter = best time to explore Jaffa flea market leisurely; Hayarkon Park bird watching); Jerusalem winter magic (6–12°C average Jan; possibility of snow 1–2 times/decade; Old City alleyways atmospheric in winter light; Jewish Quarter free of summer crowds; Yad Vashem and Israel Museum at their calmest; Christmas spirit still present in Christian Quarter through Epiphany Jan 6); Golan/Hermon snow (Mount Hermon ski resort: Israel's only ski resort; lifts typically Jan–March depending on snow; short season with quirky Israeli ski culture; day trip from Tiberias or overnight Golan). Practical tips: packing for Israel's climate split (Jerusalem 8°C + Eilat 22°C = can be same day trip; bring layers); January flights often cheapest of year (TLV is rarely "winter sun" in flight deals — savvy travelers find excellent fares); Israel vs Canaries/Tenerife comparison (Israel: more cultural depth, day-trip options like Petra/Masada, but less reliable sun in Jerusalem + Tel Aviv; Eilat: as warm as Lanzarote, with more things to do off the beach). HONESTY: Eilat is warm but it is not a classic "beach resort strip" comparable to Cancun — be honest about the style; Dead Sea float in winter is warm but water temperature varies; Hermon skiing is snow-dependent — never guarantee; Israel winter weather in Jerusalem/Tel Aviv includes rain; Israel is not mainstream "winter sun" because it's not all-inclusive resort territory — editorial framing should be honest about what kind of traveler finds Israel perfect in winter. CTAs: Booking.com Eilat winter hotels + Booking.com Dead Sea resorts + GYG Eilat snorkel tour. 6 FAQs (is Eilat warm in January, is Tel Aviv beach weather in winter, Hermon skiing reliable, can I visit both Jerusalem and Eilat in one winter trip, Israel vs Canaries for January, is Dead Sea cold in winter). Dense cross-links: eilat-travel-guide.md, dead-sea-guide.md, israel-in-autumn.md, best-time-to-visit-israel.md, christmas-in-israel.md, golan-heights-guide.md, israel-5-vs-7-vs-10-days.md. SEO: "Israel winter sun holiday", "Israel January holiday", "Eilat January February holiday", "Israel winter escape Europe", "Israel Red Sea holiday winter 2026". Sources: eilat.city/en (weather data), israel.travel/eilat (official tourism), uk-travel-consumer-research showing winter sun gap. [iter445 research] status: ready

Sephardic Heritage Museum route offers Spanish visitors a uniquely personal connection to Israeli history; Sephardic quarter of Jerusalem's Old City + the Sephardic synagogues (Rambam, Ben Zakkai complex) have direct Spanish-exile connection; El Ghriba synagogue in Israel vs the broader Sephardic narrative; frank pilgrimage angle: Spain = 5th largest Catholic-sending nation globally and the Holy Land is the pilgrimage mother ship; many Spanish Catholic groups visit via Abraham Tours + Bein Harim; Spanish-speaking tour guides available in Jerusalem (coordinate via local operators). Zero accessible-travel content on our site. MARKET IMPORTANCE: disability travel is one of the fastest-growing travel segments globally; US ADA/UK Equality Act requirements mean many Western corporate-travel buyers require accessibility info. FORMAT: practical guide, not inspirational (specific information over aspirational framing). CONTENT: Transportation (accessible public buses with kneeling feature + low floor in major cities; Israeli Railway Authority (rakevet.co.il) — wheelchair accessible trains with advance booking; Ben Gurion Airport — full accessibility, dedicated assistance desks, TDD services; taxis/accessible minivans — Kav Naki special taxis available in main cities; in Jerusalem light rail + Egged accessible buses; Gett app — accessible vehicle filter); Jerusalem (Old City: note cobblestone areas + stepped passages — many sections ARE accessible but not all, detailed by quarter; Jewish Quarter = most level; Muslim Quarter souk = difficult for wheelchairs; Western Wall plaza fully paved and accessible; Israel Museum fully accessible; Tower of David accessible ground level; Church of Holy Sepulchre: partially accessible — entry-level chapels accessible, upper areas steep); Tel Aviv (best Israeli city for accessibility: flat terrain, beaches have accessible beach wheelchairs via municipal programme, Hilton Beach + Gordon Beach have matted paths to sea; Tel Aviv Museum of Art fully accessible + advance-booking guided tours; ANU Museum fully accessible; Sarona Market fully accessible; Tel Aviv Light Rail fully accessible; Florentin + Neve Tzedek cobblestones note); Haifa (Bahá'í Terraces: upper section only accessible from top (Ben Gurion Ave entrance) with advance coordination — lower terraces require steps; HaMeginerim Center has accessible viewing area; cable car from port to Carmel area; Haifa Museum of Art accessible); Dead Sea (Ein Bokek resort strip = purpose-built flat accessible terrain; resort hotel pools + spa facilities = generally accessible; adaptive flotation aids available; Dead Sea float itself fully possible for all mobility levels; wheelchair beach-access mats at Ein Bokek public beach); Eilat (generally very accessible resort area; Red Sea snorkeling — operators have adapted gear for divers with mobility impairments; Coral Beach Reserve = paved accessible path to coral reef observation area; Dolphin Reef = accessible main area); National Parks accessibility (INPA maintains accessible trails guide at parks.org.il — link directly; Masada: cable car = fully accessible to summit; snake path = not accessible; Ein Gedi accessible botanical garden at kibbutz; Gan HaShlosha/Sachne = partially paved pool area); Accommodation (Booking.com accessible filter; most hotel chains (Dan, Isrotel, Fattal) have accessible rooms — advise booking directly and specifying needs; kibbutz hotels often more difficult; contact Access Israel for curated accessible property list); Specialist tour operators (Access Israel: aisrael.org — nonprofit accessibility NGO with tour booking; Atij.org — specialist accessible tour operator; Ayalon Tours — adaptive tourism; Wheel the World — global accessible travel platform covering Israel). HONESTY: accessibility in Israel is improving but uneven — Old City cobblestones are genuinely difficult; some nature sites require moderate mobility even on "accessible" routes; always call ahead to confirm current access; no accessibility standard is perfect — frame as best-available information + link official sources + specialist operators. CTAs: Booking.com accessible hotel filter CTA + GYG "accessible Jerusalem" tour CTA + Access Israel direct link. Cross-links: visa-information, first-time-in-israel, is-israel-safe, transportation, ben-gurion-airport-guide. SEO: "accessible travel Israel 2026", "wheelchair Israel guide", "disability travel Israel", "accessible Israel tourist sites", "Israel wheelchair holiday guide". WHY VALUABLE: Japan is the world's third-largest outbound travel market (~20M outbound pre-COVID; recovering strongly 2025-2026); growing evangelical/Protestant Christian minority in Japan (~1% of population but organized pilgrimage market; CRASH (Christian Research Association) estimates 300,000+ practicing Protestants); Japanese Buddhist/spiritual travel tradition = strong affinity for pilgrimage destinations; Japan Airlines (JAL) and ANA operate TLV routes via connections; El Al has historically operated seasonal TLV-NRT service. FORMAT: proven origin-market playbook (mirrors israel-for-british-travelers format). Japanese-specific content: (1) MOFA advisory link ONLY — mofa.go.jp/mofaj/toko/index.html — never paste advisory text; (2) ETA-IL: Japan is on visa-exempt list; since January 2025 ETA-IL required (₪25 / ~¥1,000 equiv; iaa.gov.il; apply 72h+ before travel; 2-year multi-entry) — many Japanese travelers unfamiliar with this requirement; (3) Flights: JAL NRT/HND → TLV via AMS/CDG/FRA (various connections ~15–17h); ANA NRT → TLV via connections; El Al seasonal TLV-NRT direct when operating (verify current schedule); Cathay Pacific HKG connection popular routing; (4) Voltage + plug: Japan = 100V/50-60Hz → Israel 230V/50Hz — Japanese appliances NEED both a voltage converter AND plug adapter (Type A Japan → Type H Israel); Japanese travelers with modern electronics (phones/laptops with universal PSU) need plug adapter only; older Japanese appliances (hair dryers, curling irons) need voltage converter; (5) Health insurance: Japanese National Health Insurance (kokumin kenko hoken) does NOT cover abroad; Japanese travelers need kaigai ryoko hoken (overseas travel insurance); most Japanese travel with JTB or similar package tours that include insurance — remind independent travelers to purchase separately; (6) Banking: Japanese bank cards (particularly bank cards with "銀聯/UnionPay" marking) may have limited acceptance at Israeli ATMs; Visa/Mastercard credit cards from major Japanese banks (SMBC Visa, MUFG Visa, Rakuten Card) work at Israeli ATMs; IC card (Suica/Pasmo) does NOT work in Israel; 7-Eleven-branded ATMs in Israel: do NOT exist (7-Eleven bank unique to Japan); Japan Post Bank international ATM withdrawal possible in Israel; (7) Language: Hebrew and Arabic signage; English commonly spoken in tourist areas; Japanese-language audio guides available at major sites (Israel Museum, Yad Vashem, Masada — confirm current availability); Japanese-speaking guides bookable via Israel MOT licensed guide registry (link); some Jewish-Japanese cultural connections (Japanese Zeroth Church support for Israel in some denominations); (8) Embassy of Israel in Tokyo: 3 Nibancho, Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo 102-0084; Japanese Embassy TLV: 4 Ibn Gabirol Street; (9) Pilgrimage framing for Japanese Protestant market: Jordan River baptism (Yardenit site near Kinneret) — organized Christian pilgrimage tours from Japan frequently include baptism ceremony; evangelical church groups often travel with pastor-led delegations (typically 7–14 day programs); coordinate via specialist operators (Tower of David, Abraham Tours, Bein Harim); (10) Cultural note: Japanese travelers typically appreciate precision in information, organized tours, quiet contemplative moments (Garden of Gethsemane, Ein Kerem Ein Karem church, Yad Vashem silent zones — note these explicitly); toilets: Israel has generally good public facilities though not up to Japanese standards — note in practical section. CTAs: GYG Jerusalem private tour CTA (strong for Japanese organized groups) + Booking.com Israel hotels + DiscoverCars (for independent travelers). - [P3] (seo-content, S) The Dead Sea is shrinking: environmental guide & visit urgency (/dead-sea-shrinking) — CONFIRMED GAP: python3-regex scan for "dead.sea.*shrink|dead.sea.*dying|dead.sea.*environment\b|dead-sea-shrinking" in full BACKLOG.md + DONE.md returns ZERO P-item matches; no file at src/content/guides/dead-sea-shrinking.md. WHY VALUABLE: The Dead Sea is losing approximately 1 metre of water level per year due to water diversion from the Jordan River (agriculture + municipal use); the shoreline has receded roughly 40km over 70 years; sinkholes are forming along former shoreline at an accelerating rate; this is both a compelling environmental story and a powerful "visit before it's gone" travel hook that competes differently from any other guide on the site. LINK-BAIT potential: environmental urgency stories consistently attract links from news sites, academic publications, and NGO resources. No competitor has a dedicated editorial piece combining the environmental context with practical travel implications. ISRAEL21c.org + BBC + Guardian have news articles; no travel brand has a comprehensive guide. CONTENT: What is happening (Dead Sea level at ~-430m in 2025; was ~-396m in the 1960s; losing ~1m/year; the Jordan River naturally flows from Sea of Galilee south to Dead Sea but 95%+ is now diverted before reaching the Dead Sea for agriculture and municipal water; saline springs from the Dead Sea bed create mineral formations but accelerating evaporation exceeds natural input); Visual evidence (comparing historical shoreline photos: 1960s resorts now 500m from water; old hotels demolished; concrete jetties now surrounded by land; salt pillars visible along former shoreline); What this means for visitors (the Ein Bokek resort strip at the southern basin IS a managed basin — water is pumped from the northern basin to maintain tourist levels; the southern basin experiences maintained levels for resort purposes; the northern basin has more dramatic shoreline retreat evidence); Sinkholes (karst sinkholes forming along the former western shoreline especially between Kibbutz Ein Gedi and the southern basin — some roads have closed; sinkhole fields are actually a striking geological tourist sight — Dead Sea Works offers Sinkhole Safari tours; visitors MUST stay on marked paths; sinkhole formation is unpredictable); What can be done (Jordan-Israel Red-Dead Canal proposal: pumping water from the Red Sea to the Dead Sea — debated since 1980s; desalination byproduct pipeline; environmental concerns about mixing seas; project status ambiguous — do NOT assert as certain; link official project documentation); Jordan Valley Rehabilitation Initiative and NGO efforts to restore the Lower Jordan River; HONESTY: visit-urgency framing ("visit before it's gone") is valid because the shrinkage is real and ongoing — but the Ein Bokek managed resort area is not shrinking in the visitor experience sense (southern basin maintained by potash plant pumping); distinguish clearly between northern basin (shrinking) and southern basin (managed); never overstate immediacy — "if current trends continue..." framing; sinkhole danger is real — always direct to marked paths and INPA guidance; do NOT enter closed areas. Dense cross-links: dead-sea-guide, dead-sea-hotels-guide, dead-sea-medical-tourism, dead-sea-israel-vs-jordan, hiking-in-israel (Ein Gedi trails near sinkhole zone), israel-national-parks-pass. SEO: "Dead Sea shrinking 2026", "Dead Sea water level dropping", "Dead Sea environmental crisis", "visit Dead Sea before it disappears", "Dead Sea sinkholes guide", "why is the Dead Sea shrinking". Sources: israel21c.org/dead-sea-shrinking-whats-being-done, bbc.com/future/article/dead-sea-shrinking, nationalgeographic.com/dead-sea, friends-of-the-earth-middle-east (Friends of the Earth advocacy for Jordan River restoration). [iter455 research] status: ready

- [P3] (seo-content, S) Tel Aviv and Israel street art guide (/israel-street-art) — CONFIRMED GAP: python3-regex scan for "street.art|graffiti.*israel|florentin.*art.*guide|street-art-guide\b" in full BACKLOG.md + DONE.md returns ZERO standalone P-item matches (Florentin was mentioned as context in iter70 de-dupe notes but NOT added as a standalone BACKLOG item; iter70 noted it as DISTINCT from existing Florentin guide but didn't add it). No file at src/content/guides/israel-street-art.md. WHY VALUABLE: Tel Aviv is internationally recognized for its street art scene (Time Out, Monocle, and Lonely Planet have all featured it); Florentin district in particular attracts urban art tourists; global street art tourism is a growing segment (MOCA tours, street art walking tours consistently top-5 TripAdvisor activity categories in major cities); timeout.com/israel, secrettelaviv.com, israel21c.org all have street art coverage; GYG + Abraham Tours sell dedicated street art walking tours. Zero street art content on our site. CONTENT: Florentin neighborhood (developed from 1990s wave immigration; warehouse district between TLV and Jaffa; high density of street murals on abandoned warehouse walls; Levinski Street spine; graffiti ranging from political commentary to hyper-realist portrait work; mix of Israeli and international artists; Thursday market atmosphere; evolving rapidly — always frame as "dynamic, change frequently"); Tel Aviv port (Old Port = regenerated industrial area; curated murals on warehouse facades; more polished public art vs Florentin's raw graffiti; Hangar 26 cultural centre); Jaffa (cross-cultural street art — Hebrew, Arabic, English mixed in Old Jaffa lanes; flea market area has concentrated mural spots; "Flying Carpet" Rami Meiri installation at Kdumim Square = iconic Jaffa photographic landmark); Rabin Square + HaKarmel area (political public art tradition; large-scale official murals commemorating leaders); Key artists + context (Neta Elkayam: Israeli mixed-media street artist with gallery + street work; Kelekelo: known for large-scale animal portraits on TLV walls; ROSH: social commentary urban art; Yazan Halwani: Palestinian calligraphy artist who has painted in Jaffa = cross-cultural angle; HONESTY: attribution of unsigned works is speculative — never definitively attribute without documented source); The "Tachana" (Old Railway Station) = curated public art space + weekend market + outdoor murals in a historic Ottoman-era train station; Walking tour route suggestion (Florentin → Levinsky Market → Old Port murals → Jaffa flea market: 3–4h circuit); Photography tips (early morning Florentin = best light + least crowded; always ask permission for photos featuring living people in murals/studios; Florentin Thursday evening art events occasionally scheduled — check localeve.co.il or Facebook); Practical notes (Florentin neighborhood is NOT a tourist district by default — go during daytime; cafés/restaurants excellent; generally safe but some blocks industrial; parking limited — taxi/bike recommended). HONESTY: street art is inherently ephemeral — specific mural locations change constantly; frame all specific examples with "as of 2026" or "typically" language; never assert a specific mural will be visible; link GYG walking tours for curated up-to-date experience vs self-guided (potentially finding nothing in a specific spot). CTAs: GYG "Tel Aviv Street Art Walking Tour" CTA + Booking.com Tel Aviv hotels (Florentin/South TLV area). Cross-links: tel-aviv-neighborhoods-guide, neve-tzedek-guide, jaffa-travel-guide (when built), tel-aviv-food-guide, tel-aviv-things-to-do. SEO: "Tel Aviv street art guide 2026", "Florentin street art Tel Aviv", "Israel street art Jaffa", "Tel Aviv graffiti tour", "urban art Israel". WHY VALUABLE: Philippines is the world's third-largest Catholic country (~92M Catholics; ~80% of population); strong organized Catholic and evangelical/charismatic pilgrimage tradition — El Shaddai, Couples for Christ, and numerous Catholic movements organize regular Holy Land pilgrimages from the Philippines; Israel also has approximately 30,000 Filipino OFWs (Overseas Filipino Workers) in caregiving + domestic work sector — their families visit; Philippine inbound to Israel is a measurable tourist segment; no major English-editorial brand has a Philippines-specific Israel guide. FORMAT: proven origin-market playbook. Filipino-specific content: (1) DFA advisory link ONLY — dfa.gov.ph/travel-advisory — never paste text; (2) ETA-IL: Philippines is on visa-exempt list; since January 2025 ETA-IL required (₪25 / ~₱700 equiv; iaa.gov.il; 72h+ before travel; 2-year multi-entry) — many Filipino travelers and OFW visitors unfamiliar; (3) Flights: Philippine Airlines (PAL) MNL → TLV via AUH/DOH/Dubai or BKK connections (~16–19h); Cebu Pacific MNL → TLV via connections; Emirates MNL → DXB → TLV (popular PAX routing); Etihad MNL → AUH → TLV; Qatar MNL → DOH → TLV; no direct Manila–TLV service; (4) Voltage: Philippines = 220V/60Hz → Israel 230V/50Hz — same voltage range; plug: Philippines Type A/B (flat/round pins) → Israel Type H (230V) — plug adapter needed, no voltage converter needed for most appliances; (5) Health insurance: Philippine PhilHealth does NOT cover abroad; Filipino travelers need international travel insurance; local insurers (Manulife Philippines, AXA Philippines, Pioneer Insurance) have international travel policies; OFWs should note OWWA (Overseas Workers Welfare Administration) offers limited travel insurance for documented OFWs only; (6) Banking: Philippine bank cards (BDO, Metrobank, BPI Visa/Mastercard) generally work at Israeli ATMs; GCash international does NOT work in Israel; PayMaya/Maya international Visa card may work at POS — verify before travel; remittance awareness: some OFWs send money home via Western Union/Remitly — Israeli branches exist in TLV; (7) Embassy of Israel in Manila: 23/F Trafalgar Plaza, 105 H.V. dela Costa Street, Salcedo Village, Makati; Philippine Embassy TLV: 11 Abba Hillel Silver Street, Ramat Gan; (8) Catholic pilgrimage angle: Filipino Catholic pilgrimage typically organized through diocese-sponsored tours or Catholic travel agencies (Holy Land tours marketed to Filipino community typically include: Via Dolorosa + Church of the Holy Sepulchre; Basilica of Annunciation Nazareth; Sea of Galilee boat; Jordan River renewal of baptism vows; Bethlehem Church of the Nativity; Mount of Olives; Yad Vashem — a deeply meaningful visit for Filipino Catholics given WWII historical parallels); (9) OFW caregivers/workers context: Israeli-Filipino community is substantial; Israeli families often speak warmly of Filipino caregivers; Filipino workers may be able to facilitate authentic local connections for visiting family members; (10) Cultural fit: Filipino traveler tends to prioritize group touring with extended family; souvenir shopping (Jerusalem Old City souvenirs, Dead Sea cosmetics, olive wood carvings = popular choices among Filipino travelers); excellent food photography culture = align recommendations with Instagram-friendly spots. CTAs: GYG Jerusalem Christian Quarter tour CTA (strong for Filipino Catholic pilgrim) + Booking.com Israel hotels + SafetyWing travel insurance. ## iter460 additions (2026-07-11 RESEARCH)

- [P3] (seo-content+monetization, M) Israel for Nigerian travelers (/israel-for-nigerian-travelers) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep scan for "nigerian.*travel|/israel-for-nigerian|israel.for.nigerian|nigeria.*guide" in full BACKLOG.md + DONE.md returns ZERO P-item standalone matches; no file at src/content/guides/israel-for-nigerian-travelers.md. WHY VALUABLE: Nigeria is Africa's largest country (220M population) and home to Africa's largest Christian population (~110M Christians, ~50% of population); major evangelical/Pentecostal denominations (RCCG — Redeemed Christian Church of God, Winners Chapel/Word of Faith, Mountain of Fire Ministries, Living Faith Church) all organize annual Holy Land pilgrimages; Nigerian evangelical pilgrimage tourism is one of the fastest-growing segments in Israel inbound tourism. CRITICAL DISTINCTION: Nigerian passport holders are NOT on Israel's visa-exempt list — they require an eVisa B2 (similar to India; apply via embassies.gov.il/nigeria; ₪50 fee; 3-week minimum processing; apply 4+ weeks ahead; approval rate reportedly high for tourism; NOT guaranteed). This visa-requirement context is the key differentiator that makes this guide M-effort and highly distinct from Ghana/Kenya/South Africa guides. FORMAT: proven origin-market playbook (mirrors israel-for-indian-travelers.md for visa-complexity sections; mirrors israel-for-korean-travelers.md for pilgrimage sections). Nigerian-specific content: (1) MNRE (Ministry of Foreign Affairs Nigeria) advisory link ONLY — mfa.gov.ng — never paste text; (2) eVisa B2 process: embassies.gov.il/nigeria; requirements include valid passport 6mo+ ahead of travel, passport-size photos, bank statements proving financial stability, confirmed return flights, hotel bookings, travel insurance; online application; fee ₪50 (~$14); processing 2–3 weeks; pickup at Israeli Embassy Abuja or Lagos; for organized church group pilgrimage, the tour operator often handles visa applications collectively; (3) Flights: Ethiopian Airlines LOS (Lagos)/ABV (Abuja) → ADD → TLV (best routing, Ethiopian has strong Nigeria frequency); Turkish Airlines LOS → IST → TLV; Qatar Airways ABV → DOH → TLV; other connections via CDG/AMS possible; no direct Nigeria–TLV service; typical journey time 12–18h with connections; (4) Health insurance: NHIS (Nigeria National Health Insurance Scheme) does NOT cover international travel; AIICO Insurance, Leadway Assurance, and AXA Mansard (Nigeria) offer international travel policies; organized pilgrimage tour packages from Nigerian operators (Elyon Tours, Segun Travel, KOS Travel) typically include group travel insurance; (5) Banking: GTBank, Zenith Bank, First Bank, Access Bank Nigeria Visa/Mastercard work at Israeli ATMs but may have 3–5% international fee; Naira → ILS conversion context (ILS is approximately 460–480 NGN at market rate — verify before travel; don't hardcode); Wise debit card strongly recommended to avoid high exchange fees; Nigerian travelers should inform their bank before travel; chip+pin required at most Israeli ATMs; (6) Voltage + adapter: Nigeria Type D/G (250V) → Israel Type H (230V) — same voltage range (250V appliances are fine at 230V outlets); plug adapter needed (Type D/G 3-pin → Israel Type H rounded 3-pin); no voltage converter needed for standard electronics; (7) Embassy of Israel in Nigeria: Plot 1088, Aguiyi Ironsi Street, Maitama, Abuja; Israeli Consulate in Lagos: currently closed for visa services — Abuja embassy handles all Nigerian visa applications; Nigerian Mission in Israel: 16 Abba Hillel Silver Street, Ramat Gan; (8) Organized pilgrimage logistics: Nigerian evangelical/Pentecostal pilgrimages typically run through specialist tour operators who handle eVisa, accommodation (usually 3–4 star Jerusalem/TLV hotels), guided Christian site circuit (Via Dolorosa, Holy Sepulchre, Nazareth, Sea of Galilee boat, Jordan River baptism renewal at Yardenit, Dead Sea float), and group flights; typical group pilgrimage 10–14 days; group sizes 20–200 people; RCCG, Winners Chapel, and Mountain of Fire each organize annual national pilgrimages; (9) Nigerian cultural fit: Nigerian visitors are deeply devotional at holy sites (expect extended prayer sessions; tour operators allow time for this); social documentation culture (very active photos + videos at all sites); extended family group travel common; souvenir shopping (olive wood carvings, Dead Sea cosmetics, anointing oil from Jordan River, small Crosses from Bethlehem, Israeli flags = most popular items with Nigerian pilgrims); (10) Safety framing: Israel is generally safe for Nigerian visitors; anti-Black racism exists in Israeli society but is not the dominant experience for tourist visitors who are clearly guests; frame carefully, factually, and proportionately — link is-israel-safe.md. HONESTY: eVisa is NOT guaranteed — always state that approval is discretionary; never claim 100% approval rate; organized tour operators reduce complexity but are not a workaround for application requirements; pilgrimage operators should be vetted (check Israeli MOT licensing via tourism.gov.il); baptism renewal at Yardenit is a Baptist/Pentecostal practice — Jordan River at the site near Sea of Galilee, not the historical lower Jordan near Jericho; tourist-oriented site; do NOT fabricate pricing for eVisa guarantors or tour packages. AFFILIATE CTAs: GYG Jerusalem Old City Christian Quarter walking tour CTA + Booking.com Israel hotels CTA + SafetyWing travel insurance CTA (international coverage for when NHIS does not apply). CROSS-LINKS: visa-information.md, christian-pilgrimage-holy-land.md, is-israel-safe.md, cheap-flights-to-israel.md, via-dolorosa-guide.md, jordan-river-baptism.md, israel-after-birthright.md (for Nigerian-diaspora angle). SEO: "Israel travel guide Nigerians 2026", "Nigeria Israel visa eVisa", "Israel Holy Land pilgrimage Nigeria", "visiting Israel from Nigeria", "Israel RCCG pilgrimage guide", "eVisa Israel Nigerian passport". SOURCES: embassies.gov.il/nigeria, mfa.gov.ng (travel advisory), touristisrael.com (pilgrimage content), rccg.org/israel-pilgrimage (RCCG official pilgrimage page), elyon-tours.com (Nigerian pilgrimage operator example — verify operator legitimacy before cross-linking), getyourguide.com/nigeria/israel-tour-packages. NOTE: DISTINCT from israel-for-black-travelers.md (BACKLOG — that guide covers African American/Black British diaspora + Ethiopian Jewish heritage; this is specifically Nigerian logistics/visa/pilgrimage guide). - [P3] (seo-content+monetization, S) Israel for Mexican travelers (/israel-for-mexican-travelers) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep scan for "mexico.*travel|mexican.*traveler|/israel-for-mexican|israel.for.mexico" in full BACKLOG.md + DONE.md returns ZERO P-item standalone matches; no file at src/content/guides/israel-for-mexican-travelers.md. (israel-for-spanish-travelers in BACKLOG is a distinct guide for Spain, NOT Mexico; mexico-specific ETA-IL tip, flights, and banking are entirely different.) WHY VALUABLE: Mexico is the world's second-largest Catholic country by total population (~130M Catholics, ~90% of 144M population); Mexican Catholic pilgrimage tradition is among the world's most fervent (Guadalupe basilica = largest pilgrimage site in the Americas with 20M+ annual visitors; Holy Land = natural extension for highly devout Mexican pilgrims); Holy Land pilgrimages from Mexico organized through Diocese of Guadalajara, Diocese of Mexico City, Franciscan pilgrimage groups, and specialist operators (Tours Terra Santa Mexico); no major English editorial brand has a Mexico-specific Israel guide. FORMAT: proven origin-market playbook (mirrors israel-for-spanish-travelers.md for Catholic pilgrimage sections; distinct Mexico-specific logistical content). Mexican-specific content: (1) SRE (Secretaría de Relaciones Exteriores) advisory link ONLY — sre.gob.mx/travel — never paste text; (2) ETA-IL: Mexico IS on Israel's visa-exempt list; since January 2025 ETA-IL required (₪25 / ~MXN 25 equiv; iaa.gov.il; apply 72h+ before departure; 2-year multi-entry; very few Mexican travelers aware of this requirement — key content differentiator); (3) Flights: No direct Mexico City–TLV service; best routing: Aeromexico MEX → CDG → TLV (codeshare with Air France); Iberia MEX → MAD → TLV (most popular — frequent Iberia MEX hub); LATAM MEX → SCL/GRU → connections → TLV; journey time typically 18–22h; (4) Voltage + adapter: Mexico = 110V/60Hz → Israel 230V/50Hz — both voltage converter AND plug adapter needed for older Mexican appliances (hair dryers, curling irons, blenders); modern electronics (phones, laptops) have universal PSU — check the charger's rating (100–240V = safe; 100–127V only = needs converter); Type A/B plug → Israel Type H adapter; practical advice: bring a universal power strip + voltage converter for Mexico-bought appliances; (5) Health insurance: IMSS (Instituto Mexicano del Seguro Social) does NOT cover international travel; private Mexican insurers (AXA Mexico, GNP Seguros, Qualitas Seguros) offer travel insurance; Assist Card Mexico = widely used Mexican travel insurance brand; international coverage essential for Israel; (6) Banking: BBVA Bancomer, Santander Mexico, Citibanamex Visa/Mastercard work at Israeli ATMs; SPEI (Mexico's bank transfer system) does NOT work in Israel; CoDi (digital payment) does NOT work in Israel; Wise debit card strongly recommended for best exchange rates; ATM withdrawal limits typical — plan cash for markets/small vendors; (7) Embassy of Israel in Mexico City: Paseo de la Reforma 1431-3, Col. Santa Fe, CDMX 05109; Mexican Embassy TLV: 6 Ben Yehuda Street, Tel Aviv (verify current address before publishing); (8) Franciscan connection: Mexico was evangelized by Franciscan friars from the early 16th century onward; the Franciscan order maintains Custody of the Holy Land (franciscan friars from the Terra Sancta Custody serve at the Church of the Holy Sepulchre, Church of the Nativity Bethlehem, and other major sites); Mexican Catholic pilgrims visiting these sites encounter Franciscan friars who share their religious order's long history in the Holy Land — powerful connection for Mexican devout Catholics; Via Crucis (Stations of the Cross) at Via Dolorosa resonates deeply with Mexican Catholic tradition; (9) Sephardic heritage note: Some Mexican colonial families are descendants of conversos (crypto-Jews who converted during the Inquisition) from 15th–16th century Spain; growing genealogy tourism interest from Mexicans tracing possible Jewish heritage (distinct from mainstream pilgrimage). HONESTY: ETA-IL is required but many Mexican travel operators unaware — frame clearly; voltage converter need is real for Mexican 110V appliances — do not omit; Franciscan friars at Holy Sites are predominantly non-Mexican but share the order's Ibero-American tradition; Sephardic heritage angle is real but niche — frame proportionately; no fabricated tour prices. AFFILIATE CTAs: GYG Jerusalem Old City private guided tour + Booking.com Israel hotels + SafetyWing travel insurance (IMSS gap). - [P3] (seo-content+monetization, S) Israel for Argentinian travelers (/israel-for-argentinian-travelers) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep scan for "argentin.*travel|/israel-for-argentin|israel.for.argentina|argentina.*tourist.*israel" in full BACKLOG.md + DONE.md returns ZERO P-item standalone matches; no file at src/content/guides/israel-for-argentinian-travelers.md. (israel-for-spanish-travelers BACKLOG covers Spain; israel-for-brazilian-travelers BACKLOG covers Brazil; argentina = distinct in both logistics AND heritage angle.) WHY VALUABLE: Argentina has the world's 5th largest Jewish community (~180,000 practicing Jews; many more with Jewish heritage / secular identification) and the largest in Latin America; heritage travel from Buenos Aires to Israel is a significant and consistent market; Buenos Aires → TLV is one of the longest-running Latin American–Israel airline routes; the 1994 AMIA bombing in Buenos Aires is deeply felt among Argentine Jews, many of whom visit Yad Vashem with a specific personal connection; Argentina also has a strong Catholic pilgrimage tradition (Buenos Aires archdiocese regularly organizes Holy Land tours). FORMAT: proven origin-market playbook. Argentine-specific content: (1) Cancillería (Ministerio de Relaciones Exteriores, Comercio Internacional y Culto Argentina) advisory link ONLY — cancilleria.gob.ar/viaje — never paste; (2) ETA-IL: Argentina IS on Israel's visa-exempt list; since January 2025 ETA-IL required (₪25 / ~ARS 30,000 equiv — note Argentine peso depreciation is severe; always frame as the NIS amount, not ARS — rates change weekly; iaa.gov.il; 72h+ before travel; 2-year multi-entry); (3) Flights: Aerolíneas Argentinas EZE → TLV (has operated seasonally; verify current route status at aerolineas.com.ar — this is the ONLY potential direct service and may be suspended depending on season/year); LATAM EZE → GRU or SCL → connections → TLV; Air France EZE → CDG → TLV (very popular routing); Lufthansa EZE → FRA → TLV; typical journey 20–24h with connections; (4) Voltage + adapter: Argentina = 220V/50Hz → Israel 230V/50Hz — same voltage; Argentina primarily uses Type I plug (2 flat angled pins + 1 flat ground pin — the distinctive triangular 3-pin plug); Type I → Israel Type H = plug adapter needed; no voltage converter required; some older Argentine buildings still have Type C European plugs — these also need adapter; (5) Health insurance: Argentine PAMI (Programa de Atención Médica Integral) = senior health coverage, does NOT cover internationally; OSDE, Medicus, Swiss Medical Group are private Argentine health insurers — check travel rider; Assist Card Argentina = widely used Argentine travel insurance brand specific to Latin American travelers; (6) Banking: Argentine peso (ARS) NOT commonly exchangeable in Israel — bring USD or EUR in cash, exchangeable at Israeli cambio offices at favorable rates; GTech Visa, Naranja X Mastercard, Mercado Pago Visa work at Israeli ATMs when card has international feature enabled; Wise debit card excellent for Argentine travelers to avoid dual exchange rate complexity; NOTE: Argentina's currency controls are complex — editorial team should verify status before publishing specific banking advice; (7) Embassy of Israel in Buenos Aires: Arcos 1880 Piso 11, Belgrano, CABA; Argentine Embassy TLV: 85 Medinat HaYehudim Street, Herzliya Pituach; (8) Jewish heritage angle: Argentine Jewish community (Ashkenazi waves from Eastern Europe 1880s–1930s; Sephardic immigration from Syria/Morocco/Egypt 1950s–70s; Ashkenazi Holocaust survivors post-1945); many Argentine Jewish families have extended family in Israel following the 2001–2002 economic crisis which drove a wave of aliyah; AMIA bombing memorial visit: Yad Vashem has a section honoring the AMIA victims; Argentine Jewish visitors often make the AMIA–Yad Vashem connection a highlight of their Israel visit; Sephardic Heritage route in Jerusalem (Ben Zakkai synagogue complex, Sephardic quarter of Old City) resonates with Argentine Sephardic families; (9) Catholic pilgrimage: Buenos Aires Archdiocese organizes regular Holy Land pilgrimages; Pope Francis (Argentine, Jesuit) visited the Holy Land in 2014 — Argentine Catholics feel personal connection through their compatriot Pope; (10) Argentine food connections in Israel: surprising culinary connection — Israel has an active Argentine Jewish restaurant scene in Tel Aviv (multiple parrilla-style restaurants; milanesa and empanada-adjacent dishes); mention for authenticity-seeking Argentine travelers. HONESTY: ETA-IL is required; peso depreciation means cost-in-ARS changes frequently — ALWAYS reference costs in USD/ILS never ARS; Aerolíneas direct service is seasonal and may be suspended — always verify; AMIA memorial visit is emotionally significant — frame sensitively and correctly (AMIA building rebuilt at its original location in Buenos Aires; Yad Vashem Israel has dedicated exhibition; do NOT conflate the two locations); no fabricated prices. AFFILIATE CTAs: GYG Jerusalem Jewish heritage private tour + Booking.com Israel hotels + SafetyWing travel insurance (PAMI gap for seniors). - [P3] (seo-content+monetization, S) Israel for Ghanaian travelers (/israel-for-ghanaian-travelers) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep scan for "ghanaian.*travel|/israel-for-ghana|israel.for.ghanaian|ghana.*israel.*guide" in full BACKLOG.md + DONE.md returns ZERO P-item standalone matches; "ghana" appears only as context within /israel-for-black-travelers (BACKLOG), not as a standalone guide. CRITICAL DISTINCTION from Nigerian guide: Ghana IS on Israel's visa-exempt list — Ghanaian passport holders only need ETA-IL (no separate visa required), unlike Nigerians who need eVisa B2. This makes the Ghanaian guide substantially simpler (S effort) and targets a distinct audience. WHY VALUABLE: Ghana is ~71% Christian (~23M Christians in a country of 33M); major evangelical/Pentecostal denominations (International Central Gospel Church/ICGC, Assemblies of God Ghana, Church of Pentecost, COGIC Ghana) organize regular Holy Land pilgrimages; Ghana is West Africa's most stable democracy with a growing middle class; organized pilgrimage tourism from Ghana to Israel is an established market; no major English editorial brand has a Ghana-specific Israel guide. FORMAT: proven origin-market playbook. Ghanaian-specific content: (1) Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Regional Integration advisory link ONLY — mfa.gov.gh — never paste text; (2) ETA-IL: Ghana IS on Israel's visa-exempt list; since January 2025 ETA-IL required (₪25 / ~GHS 340 equiv; iaa.gov.il; apply 72h+ before travel; 2-year multi-entry) — many Ghanaian travelers unaware; (3) Flights: Ethiopian Airlines ACC (Accra) → ADD → TLV (primary route; strong Ethiopia–Ghana frequency); Turkish Airlines ACC → IST → TLV; Qatar Airways ACC → DOH → TLV; ASKY Airlines (West African regional) occasionally connects to ADD for Ethiopian connection; typical journey 12–16h; no direct Accra–TLV service; (4) Voltage + adapter: Ghana Type G (UK-standard; 230V) → Israel Type H (230V) — SAME voltage; Type G 3-pin → Israel Type H 3-round-pin: plug adapter needed; no voltage converter required; same setup as UK travelers; (5) Health insurance: NHIS (National Health Insurance Authority, Ghana) does NOT cover international travel; Donewell Insurance, Enterprise Insurance (Ghana), AXA Mansard (West Africa) offer international travel policies; group pilgrimage operators typically include basic group insurance; (6) Banking: GCB Bank, Fidelity Bank Ghana, Ecobank Ghana Visa/Mastercard work at Israeli ATMs; Mobile Money (MTN Mobile Money Ghana, Vodafone Cash, AirtelTigo) do NOT work in Israel; Wise debit card strongly recommended for best exchange rates; GHS → ILS exchange: approximately 1 ILS = 3.5–4.0 GHS (varies; never hardcode); (7) Embassy of Israel in Accra: Plot 11, Sugarbush Close, North Labone, Accra-North; Ghanaian High Commission in Israel: 12 Ahamadia Street, Ramat Gan (verify current); (8) Church pilgrimage logistics: Ghanaian evangelical/Pentecostal pilgrimages typically 10–14 days; common itinerary mirrors Nigerian pilgrimage pattern: Via Dolorosa → Holy Sepulchre → Nazareth → Sea of Galilee boat → Jordan River (Yardenit) baptism renewal → Bethlehem → Dead Sea float; organized by church tour committees or specialist Ghanaian travel operators; ICGC, Assemblies of God Ghana, and Church of Pentecost Ghana all organize national-level annual pilgrimages; (9) Anglican heritage: Ghana has significant Anglican (Church of Ghana, Province of the Church of Ghana) community from British colonial tradition; Anglican pilgrims have historical connection to Jerusalem (Anglican Bishop in Jerusalem has been stationed since 1841; St. George's Cathedral Jerusalem = prominent Anglican presence); Ghanaian Anglicans visiting Jerusalem feel a strong denominational connection; (10) Cultural note: Ghanaian travelers are typically group-oriented (extended church family + community travel); documentation culture (photos + prayer videos at holy sites is standard); warm + formal social style appreciated in Israeli service interactions; Ghanaian pilgrims often purchase olive oil, blessed water from Jordan River, olive wood crosses, prayer shawls as spiritual gifts for church community back home. HONESTY: ETA-IL is new requirement (Jan 2025) — frame clearly as many Ghanaian travel agents have not yet updated their pre-departure checklists; Mobile Money does NOT work in Israel — emphasize; Yardenit baptism = tourist-oriented site near Sea of Galilee (River Jordan headwaters area), not the historical Jordan River near Jericho (important distinction for theologically aware pastors leading pilgrimages); pilgrimage operators in Ghana should be vetted (Israeli MOT licensed guide requirement applies). AFFILIATE CTAs: GYG Jerusalem Christian Quarter tour CTA + Booking.com Israel hotels CTA + SafetyWing international travel insurance CTA. modern electronics (phones, laptops with 100–240V universal PSU) need only a plug adapter; Type A/B (flat pins, 110V) → Israel Type H adapter; (5) Health insurance: EPS (Entidad Promotora de Salud — Colombia's contributory health insurance) does NOT cover internationally; private insurers AXA Colombia, Sura (Suramericana), Bolivar Seguros offer international travel insurance; Assist Card Colombia = widely used in Latin American travel; (6) Banking: Bancolombia, Davivienda, BBVA Colombia, Banco de Bogotá Visa/Mastercard work at Israeli ATMs; Nequi and Daviplata (Colombian digital wallets) do NOT work in Israel; Tuya (Falabella Colombia) Mastercard does work internationally; Wise card recommended for best exchange rates; COP → ILS exchange: approximately 1 ILS = 1,300–1,500 COP (varies; frame as a relative magnitude, never hardcode exact rate); (7) Embassy of Israel in Bogotá: Calle 35 No. 7-25, Piso 14, Edificio Torre Bancafé, Bogotá DC; Colombian Embassy TLV: 4 Arba'a Hamea Street, Tel Aviv (verify current address); (8) Franciscan pilgrimage connection: Colombia was evangelized by Franciscan and Dominican friars from the 16th century; Franciscan Custody of the Holy Land administers key sites in Jerusalem, Nazareth, and Bethlehem; Colombian Franciscan missionaries feel a historical connection to Tierra Santa; organized Colombian pilgrimage groups frequently travel with Franciscan spiritual directors; (9) Semana Santa pilgrimage tradition: Colombia's Semana Santa is extraordinarily devout (Popayán Semana Santa processions = UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage; Mompox Semana Santa; Tunja colonial religious art; Colombian Catholics who participate in national Semana Santa have particularly deep affinity for walking the Via Dolorosa in person); timing note: Semana Santa in Colombia falls during Holy Week (April); visiting Israel during Israeli Holy Week (which largely coincides) is both meaningful AND requires very advance booking; (10) Cultural note: Colombian travelers tend toward warm, expressive social style; group travel with extended family and church community common; souvenirs: Dead Sea cosmetics, olive wood carvings, Jordan River anointing oil are popular; coffee comparison interest (Colombian + Israeli specialty coffee scenes are both strong; some Israeli cafés source Colombian beans — genuine commonality). HONESTY: ETA-IL required since Jan 2025 — many Colombian travel agents not updated; voltage converter essential for 110V Colombian appliances; never hardcode COP exchange rate; Semana Santa timing overlap with Israel's Holy Week is genuine but accommodation surge in Jerusalem during Easter/Passover is severe — always advise 3–4 months advance booking. - [P3] (seo-content+monetization, S) Israel dog-friendly & pet travel guide (/israel-dog-friendly-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep scan for "pet.friend|dog.friendly|pets.*israel|dog.*israel.*guide|bring.*pet" in full BACKLOG.md + DONE.md returns ZERO P-item matches; no file at src/content/guides/israel-dog-friendly-guide.md. WHY VALUABLE: Tel Aviv is globally famous for its dog culture — approximately 500k dogs in a metro of 4M people, one of the world's highest per-capita dog ownership rates; Hilton Beach has an official dog beach zone; Yarkon Park (TLV) is dog-friendly; Dan Hotels, Isrotel, and Fattal chains each publish explicit pet-acceptance policies; BringFido.com has a dedicated "Dog Friendly Israel" country page ranking for "dog friendly Israel" + "pet friendly hotels Israel" keywords; TripAdvisor lists "10 Best Pet-Friendly Hotels in Israel 2026"; zero pet/dog travel content exists on our site. Growing segment: traveling with pets is now ~30% of US/UK leisure trips. FORMAT: practical guide combining hotel policies, beaches/parks, vet access, and border-entry requirements. CONTENT: (1) Bringing pets to Israel (MARD-PRIMAL import permit from Israel Ministry of Agriculture; rabies vaccination + microchip required; blood-titer test 30 days before entry; airport-of-entry quarantine inspection; advance-permit application 4+ months ahead; HONESTY: framing that this guide helps travelers who live in Israel OR who are planning a future move — short-holiday pet import is genuinely complex; most tourists do NOT bring pets on a 1-week trip; guide is primarily for long-stay/expats + a smaller tourist segment planning extended stays); (2) Dog-friendly areas in Tel Aviv (Hilton Beach official dog zone north section + dog water fountains + waste bags provided; Yarkon Park full-length riverbank trail; Reading Port area; Jaffa beach south section generally tolerant though not official dog zone); (3) Jerusalem pet situation (less dog-friendly than TLV; Old City largely off-limits for dogs due to religious sensitivities; West Jerusalem parks generally dog-tolerant with leash rules; Mount Scopus campus area trails); (4) Haifa + north (Carmel forest trails dog-friendly; Haifa beaches: Bat Galim promenade dog-tolerant off-peak); (5) Hotel pet policies (Dan Hotels: pets accepted at select properties, fee applies; Isrotel: select properties, advance notice; Fattal: select properties; independent boutique hotels: variable — always call ahead; kibbutz guesthouses: extremely variable — rural setting but animal territory concerns; vacation apartments (Airbnb/Booking.com): best bet for pet travelers — filter by pet-friendly); (6) Vet access in Israel (Israel has a high vet-to-pet ratio; veterinary clinics abundant in TLV and Jerusalem; 24h emergency vet clinics in TLV: link to idc-vet.co.il equivalent — do NOT fabricate specific clinic names, link to Google Maps search query instead; medications — Israeli vet clinics stock common medications but may use different brand names than US/EU — bring documentation of prescriptions); (7) Dog food + supplies (Zap.co.il (Israeli Amazon equivalent) + Super-Pharm pet section + Pet Planet chains in TLV; international brands (Royal Canin, Purina) widely available; raw food diet supplies at select TLV pet shops). HONESTY: pet import to Israel is genuinely complex — don't downplay requirements; dogs are NOT permitted in all restaurants (some TLV restaurants tolerate dogs at outdoor tables — frame as variable; always ask); guard dogs (Rottweilers, Pit Bulls, and related breeds) face additional entry restrictions — note clearly; Western Wall plaza, religious sites, and Arab-market sections of Old City are NOT appropriate for dogs; never assert a specific beach/park allows dogs without citing an official source. AFFILIATE CTAs: Booking.com pet-friendly hotels filter CTA + DiscoverCars (car travel with pets angle). CROSS-LINKS: tel-aviv-beaches-guide, tel-aviv-neighborhoods-guide, first-time-in-israel, israel-cost-budget. - [CLOSED — FALSE GAP] Tel Aviv airport layover guide (/tel-aviv-layover) — iter465 research incorrectly identified this as a gap. The content ALREADY EXISTS at src/content/guides/layover-tel-aviv.md (shipped ~iter350, DONE.md SHA 0b8c26b, URL /layover-tel-aviv). The iter465 grep pattern ("layover.*tel.aviv|tel.aviv.*layover") missed the DONE.md title "Layover in Tel Aviv guide" because the file slug uses "layover-tel-aviv" order. DO NOT IMPLEMENT — would create duplicate content. Closed in iter466. WHY VALUABLE: "Tel Aviv layover" gets substantial search volume from transit passengers through Ben Gurion Airport (BGA is the connection hub for Africa↔Europe routes on El Al, Ethiopian Airlines, Turkish Airlines connections); Tourist Israel publishes both "Layover in Tel Aviv" AND a separate "Airline Crew Layover in Tel Aviv Guide"; BookingRadar has "Exactly What to Do with 4, 8, 12 or 24 Hours in Tel Aviv"; BeinHarim sells airport layover tours; Sleeping in Airports has a dedicated TLV airport guide; DISTINCT from our Jerusalem layover guide (different city, audience, transport, and experiences). FORMAT: time-segmented guide (4h / 8h / 12h / 24h) — proven layover content structure. CONTENT: (1) Transport from BGA (Tel Aviv Express (Rakevet) from Airport Terminal 3 to HaHagana/Savidor/HaShalom stations: ~16 min, ₪16; trains every 20–30 min; CRITICAL: Shabbat warning — trains do NOT operate Friday sundown to Saturday night; taxi ~₪200 from BGA to city centre ~45 min; Gett app or metered Yellow Taxi; bus options for budget); (2) What to do by duration (4h = time for one activity + coffee: Carmel Market (HaCarmel Shuk) 10 min by train; vendor stalls, fresh juice, street food; HaCarmel closes ~16:00; good for market-lover transit; OR Sarona Market (food hall, covered, AC, 5 min walk from Kaplan Station) = better on hot days / Shabbat alternative; 8h = add Jaffa: clock tower, Jaffa Port, Jaffa Flea Market (open Sun-Thu 09:00-17:00); 1.5h Jaffa walk; Old Jaffa Hostel free luggage storage; OR Gordon Beach or Hilton Beach (30 min walk from HaShalom station; 2h at beach; shower facilities at beach entrance); 12h = add White City architecture walk (Bauhaus houses along Rothschild Blvd, Dizengoff Square, Bialik Square; self-guided free; or book GYG Bauhaus architecture tour); 24h+ = add evening dinner on Dizengoff Street or HaTachana (Old Railway Station market); sunrise swim + beach breakfast; Florentin neighbourhood coffee); (3) Luggage storage (BGA Terminal 3 Level 1: secure storage facility; Gordon Beach Lockers; Old Jaffa Hostel); (4) Food and drink on layover (Sarona Market: 50+ food stalls; Gordon Beach bars: beach snacks + cocktails; Shuk HaCarmel: street food; Jaffa Port restaurants: fish focus; budget: falafel + hummus at Carmel Market ~₪20); (5) Shabbat warning (critical logistics: from Friday ~17:00 to Saturday ~21:00 the train does NOT run, Carmel Market is closed, many restaurants/bars closed; on Shabbat layovers take a taxi, visit the beach on foot, or eat at HaCarmel-adjacent 24h convenience stores; Tel Aviv has more Shabbat-open venues than Jerusalem but plan ahead); (6) Airline crew section (specific to crew on extended layover stays 12h–48h: crew discount at Dan Hotel Tel Aviv; crew rates at Sheraton Tel Aviv; Dizengoff Square boutique shopping for spare time). HONESTY: ETA-IL is required even for transit passengers who exit the airport — many guides omit this; verify in advance since denied entry on transit would be problematic; Shabbat train disruption is a genuine risk — emphasize clearly; Tel Aviv Express departure times from airport can shift — link to rakevet.co.il for current schedule rather than hardcoding; never assert a restaurant is open on Shabbat without verification. AFFILIATE CTAs: Booking.com Tel Aviv airport hotels CTA (for transit with overnight) + GYG "Tel Aviv city layover tour" CTA + DiscoverCars (rare but airport car rental for 12h+ layover). CROSS-LINKS: ben-gurion-airport-guide, tel-aviv-things-to-do, jaffa-travel-guide (when built), tel-aviv-food-guide, visa-information (ETA-IL for transit note). - [P3] (seo-content, M) Israel archaeology dig-for-a-day and volunteer excavation guide (/israel-archaeology-dig-experience) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep scan for "archaeology.*dig|dig.for.a.day|volunteer.*dig|excavation.*tour|volunteer.*excavat" in full BACKLOG.md + DONE.md returns ZERO P-item matches; no file at src/content/guides/israel-archaeology-dig-experience.md. WHY VALUABLE: "Experiential tourism" is the fastest-growing travel segment globally; archaeology volunteer programs are a unique Israel differentiator that NO competitor has an editorial guide on (confirmed via web search — zero results from Tourist Israel, LP, Rough Guides, GYG); programs span budget to premium; strong appeal to Christian pilgrims (City of David is a deeply religious site), history buffs, families, and academic tourists; high shareability/linkbait from universities, archaeology departments, and Christian media. FORMAT: practical guide covering the main dig programs with specific booking info and seasonal windows. CONTENT: (1) IAA Friends Volunteer Program (Israel Antiquities Authority; free volunteer excavations at rotating Israeli sites; apply via iaa-archaeology.org.il/friends; no Hebrew required; physical fitness required; 2–4 week minimum commitment; typically runs spring + summer; dormitory accommodation available at some sites; genuinely free — application review + placement); (2) VFI Archaeology Volun-Tour (Volunteer for Israel; in partnership with Tel Aviv University archaeologists; 9-day City of David / Emek Tzurim wet-sieving program; $1,800–$2,500/person all-in; biblical Jerusalem context; sifting station on slopes of Mount of Olives with Jerusalem views; artifact ID training included; runs multiple sessions March–November; bookable at vfi.org.il; group size 8–20; includes Jerusalem accommodation + most meals); (3) Emek Tzurim National Park Sifting Project (standalone — not VFI; public wet-sieving of Temple Mount debris removed illegally in 1999; walk-in sessions ~₪55/person; no advance booking required; 2h activity; archaeological educator on site; finds from the sifting have included artifacts from the First Temple period; excellent family activity; open Sunday–Thursday + Friday morning); (4) Shiloh Archaeological Dig (biblical Shiloh/Tel Shiloh north of Ramallah in Area C; run by American Friends of Shiloh / Shiloh excavation committee; 1-week to 5-week programs from $1,150 (1 week) to $4,900 (5 weeks); includes accommodation at Shiloh community; biblically significant site — believed to be location of the Tabernacle; organized through shilohisrael.org; 2026 season: June–July; NOTE: Shiloh is in the West Bank/Area C; access is via organized convoy from Jerusalem or Route 60; security context must be addressed honestly; program is generally conducted safely with security coordination); (5) Tel Megiddo (Armageddon) Excavations (University of Tel Aviv-run excavation; historically significant Bronze/Iron Age site at Jezreel Valley; seasons typically June–July; volunteer registration via archaeology department website; more demanding academic volunteer program vs above; site overlooks Jezreel Valley with Carmel views; no accommodation typically included — stay Haifa or Afula); (6) Tel Shimron Excavation (New Orleans Baptist Theological Seminary partnership; Christian volunteer program; 2-week stints; Tel Shimron is Bronze Age Canaanite city in Jezreel Valley near Sea of Galilee; telshimron.com; $1,500 approx; includes accommodation + meals + guide); (7) Dig for a Day (commercial; several Jerusalem operators run 2–3h commercial sifting experiences aimed at families and day-trippers; less academically rigorous but accessible; GYG lists operators; ~$40–$60/person; Ir David / City of David visitor complex has an archaeological park component); (8) Practical advice (physical requirements: most programs require ability to kneel/crouch on uneven terrain; sun protection essential; water minimum 2L/day; work gloves; closed-toe shoes; morning sessions preferred to avoid peak heat; archaeological sites close or limit access in extreme heat 38°C+; Israeli summer (July–August) very hot for outdoor dig work — spring (March–May) and fall (September–November) are ideal; many programs peak in July despite heat due to academic calendar). HONESTY: City of David / Emek Tzurim dig is operated by Elad Foundation (an Israeli nonprofit associated with Jewish settlement expansion in Silwan/East Jerusalem); acknowledge this context factually and let travelers decide without editorializing; West Bank sites (Shiloh, Tel Shimron) require travelers to understand security protocols and travel restrictions; never assert artifact finds are guaranteed; dry-sieving yields more artifacts than wet-sieving but both are valid; do NOT claim volunteers will discover significant artifacts (statistically unlikely; educational experience is the primary value). AFFILIATE CTAs: Booking.com Jerusalem area hotels CTA (for VFI/Emek Tzurim day access) + GYG "Emek Tzurim sifting experience" tour CTA. CROSS-LINKS: israel-national-parks-pass, first-time-in-israel, dead-sea-day-trip-comparison (Masada/Qumran archaeology angle), christian-pilgrimage-holy-land, jerusalem-old-city-guide. SEO: "Israel archaeology dig experience", "volunteer excavation Israel 2026", "dig for a day Jerusalem", "Israel archaeology volunteer program", "Temple Mount sifting project", "Emek Tzurim sifting Israel". SOURCES: vfi.org.il (VFI Archaeology Volun-Tour), iaa-archaeology.org.il/friends (IAA Friends volunteer program), emektzurim.org.il (Emek Tzurim sifting project), shilohisrael.org (Shiloh dig), telshimron.com (Tel Shimron program). - [P3] (seo-content+monetization, S) Israel for French travelers (/israel-for-french-travelers) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep scan for "for-french|french.*travel.*israel|israel.*french.*guide|/israel-for-french" in full BACKLOG.md + DONE.md returns ZERO P-item matches; no file at src/content/guides/israel-for-french-travelers.md. CRITICAL DISTINCTION from fr/ locale content: the fr/ locale localizes site pages into French — this origin-market guide covers French-citizen-specific logistics (MAEE advisory, French health insurance gap, French banking, Type E→H adapter). WHY VALUABLE: France is consistently a top-3 inbound market for Israel; France has the largest Jewish diaspora in Europe (~500,000 Jews), generating significant heritage and Birthright travel; Air France CDG→TLV is one of the highest-frequency routes (3–4 daily flights, ~4h30m); Catholic pilgrimage tradition strong (Notre-Dame de France hospice operates in Jerusalem; French Franciscan pilgrimage groups); French Jewish diaspora (largest in Europe) produces Birthright + heritage + aliyah tourism; no English competitor has a France-specific Israel guide — a gap that allows us to rank for French-speaking English bilingual travelers AND French diaspora worldwide; market is high-value (France is among top-10 outbound-spend countries globally). FORMAT: proven origin-market playbook (parallel to israel-for-british-travelers.md structure). French-specific content: (1) MAEE (Ministère de l'Europe et des Affaires étrangères) advisory link ONLY — france.fr/conseils-aux-voyageurs/israel — never paste text; (2) ETA-IL: France IS on Israel's visa-exempt list; since January 2025 ETA-IL required (₪25 / ~€7; iaa.gov.il; apply 72h+ before departure; 2-year multi-entry) — many French travelers unaware despite ETA-IL being widely discussed in French media; (3) Flights: Air France CDG → TLV (3–4 daily; 4h30m; AF partner El Al codeshares; Orly departure also available with some Air France scheduling); Transavia France (Air France subsidiary; CDG→TLV on select days; budget option); El Al TLV → CDG (competitor to AF; direct; multiple daily); Vueling BCN → TLV (French travelers routing via Barcelona; not recommended over direct AF but relevant for south-of-France travelers); Air Corsair (charter; marketed to French Caribbean diaspora — not mainstream French travelers); (4) Plug: France Type E (round 2-pin + earth hole in socket face; 230V) → Israel Type H (round 3-pin 230V) — SAME voltage; plug adapter only (Type E to Type H; widely available at BHV, Leroy Merlin, or Amazon.fr before departure); some multi-adapters sold at Ben Gurion arrival; (5) Health insurance: French travelers are NOT covered abroad by Assurance Maladie (AMELI/CPAM); CPAM reimburses care received in EU countries ONLY via CEAM card — this does NOT extend to Israel; French travelers need assurance voyage or assurance rapatriement; popular French options: MAAF Assurances, AXA Voyage, Chapka Assurances, Heyme, April International; EU residents in France may check their MUTUELLE for international medical riders; (6) Banking: French bank cards (Visa/Mastercard from BNP Paribas, Société Générale, Crédit Agricole, LCL, La Banque Postale, Crédit Mutuel, CIC) universally accepted at Israeli ATMs; Lydia / Lyf Pay (French digital wallet) does NOT work in Israel; PayLib does NOT work in Israel; N26 and Revolut widely used among French travelers — both work in Israel with no FX fee; notify bank before travel; (7) Embassy of Israel in Paris: 3 rue Rabelais, 75008 Paris; French Embassy TLV: 112 Promenade Herbert Samuel, Tel Aviv; French Consulate Jerusalem: 5 Paul Émile Botta Street, Jerusalem; (8) French Jewish diaspora angle: French Jewish community is the world's third-largest after USA and Israel; strong aliyah motivation and heritage travel; Sephardic French Jews (North African origin: Algerian, Tunisian, Moroccan Jewish families who came to France post-decolonization 1950s–70s) have family and ancestral connections to Middle Eastern Jewish heritage; French Ashkenazi Jews have Polish, Russian, German ancestral connections; both communities visit Yad Vashem, the Jewish Quarter, and ancestral-community synagogues in Israel; Federation of Jewish Communities (CRIF) organizes educational missions to Israel; (9) Notre-Dame de France (Jerusalem): French Catholic presence in Jerusalem; the 19th-century Latin-rite hospice and chapel at 3 Via Dolorosa serves French pilgrims specifically; operated by the Institut Catholique de Paris; French Catholic pilgrimage groups commonly stay here; (10) Passover / High Holy Days timing for French Jewish travelers: French schools have spring school holidays (vacances de Pâques/Printemps) which typically coincide with Passover; this generates a surge of French Jewish family travel to Israel; Jerusalem accommodations sell out months in advance; frame booking lead-time clearly. HONESTY: CPAM absolutely does NOT cover Israel — never imply Sécurité Sociale coverage extends beyond the EU; MAEE advisory wording changes frequently — link only, never quote; ETA-IL is required but many French travel agents have not updated their pre-departure checklists; Notre-Dame de France hospice bed availability varies — recommend verifying well in advance. AFFILIATE CTAs: Air France TLV booking CTA (via GYG or affiliate-safe link) + Booking.com Israel hotels CTA (French-speaking interface available on Booking.com) + SafetyWing travel insurance CTA (CPAM gap). CROSS-LINKS: visa-information.md, christian-pilgrimage-holy-land.md, jewish-heritage-israel.md, is-israel-safe.md, cheap-flights-to-israel.md, israel-cost-budget.md. - [P3] (seo-content+monetization, S) Six Senses Shaharut luxury resort guide (/six-senses-shaharut-guide) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep scan for "six.sense|shaharut|luxury.*negev.*resort|arava.*luxury.*hotel" in full BACKLOG.md + DONE.md returns ZERO P-item matches; no file at src/content/guides/six-senses-shaharut-guide.md. CRITICAL DISTINCTIONS: israel-wellness-spa.md (SHIPPED iter263) is a general wellness/spa roundup — this is a standalone deep-dive for one specific world-class property; luxury-travel-israel.md (SHIPPED iter146) is a general luxury overview — this is property-specific; best-hotels-mitzpe-ramon (P2 BACKLOG) covers Makhtesh Ramon area, which is a different desert location (Negev crater vs Arava Valley 170km south). WHY VALUABLE: Six Senses Shaharut is one of Israel's most internationally acclaimed luxury properties — MICHELIN Guide hotels listed, Virtuoso preferred, Mr & Mrs Smith rated, World's 50 Best Hotels shortlisted; starting rates ~$600–$1,000/night positions it as a premium affiliate opportunity; luxury travel content converts at lower click volume but significantly higher affiliate commission per booking; "Six Senses Shaharut" is a branded keyword that captures high-intent luxury travel searchers; zero dedicated guides exist on any Israel travel publisher — GYG/Viator don't cover it, Tourist Israel has no deep-dive, Lonely Planet has no property-specific guide. FORMAT: standalone resort guide (similar to structure we'd use for a destination hotel, not a roundup section). CONTENT: (1) Property overview (Six Senses Shaharut: opened 2020; located in the Arava Valley in southern Israel, 30 min north of Eilat / Yotvata Junction; 46-acre desert property; 60 suites and villas in honey-colored Arava sandstone; architecture blends into the desert landscape at the edge of Shaharut village above the Arava rift valley; Six Senses brand = GHM Hotels Group subsidiary; part of IHG Hotels & Resorts loyalty program (IHG One Rewards points accrue and can be redeemed)); (2) Spa + wellness (Six Senses Spa with 6 treatment rooms + presidential spa villa suite; signature treatments include camel milk treatments, Dead Sea mineral wrap, olive oil massages — all locally sourced ingredients; 25m outdoor lap pool; 18m indoor heated pool; yoga studio with panoramic desert view; cryotherapy; hammam; ice baths; sauna; biohacking lab; wellness practitioners on staff (nutritionist, physiotherapist, sleep specialist)); (3) Dining (Shaharut Restaurant: Mediterranean + Levantine cuisine; Friday Shabbat dinner (special menu); Vineyard Bar: wine from Negev Desert wineries — Tulip, Yatir, Carmey Avdat (all South Negev/Arava region producers); poolside snacks + light menu); (4) Activities (stargazing: Arava Valley has some of Israel's darkest skies; Six Senses runs guided stargazing; camel trek in the Arava; Timna Valley visit (ancient copper mines, unique geology, Visitors Center 20 min south — include day-trip note); Uvda Valley ancient rock engravings; jeep + hiking expeditions organized by resort; Eilat Red Sea diving/snorkeling day trip 30 min south); (5) Accommodation types (Deluxe Room: entry; Cliff Suite: desert-edge with panoramic view; Garden Suite: private plunge pool; Sky Villa: ultra-premium with rooftop terrace; presidential suite available; group bookings for wellness retreats); (6) Pricing context (~$600–$1,000+/night for base rooms as of 2026 high season; subject to availability and dynamic pricing — frame as range, never specific rates; high season = Passover + High Holidays + Chanukah + European summer; shoulder = winter except Chanukah; note packages vs room-only; IHG One Rewards redemption option for points travelers); (7) Getting there (fly TLV → ETH (Eilat Ramon Airport) — Arkia + El Al serve TLV-ETH; ~45 min flight; then drive 30 min north to resort — DiscoverCars Eilat car rental link; OR drive from TLV: 4h south on Route 1 → Route 40 → Route 90; scenic drive through Negev recommended for first-timers; resort transfer service available — book in advance); (8) Responsible tourism note (Six Senses brand has global sustainability commitments: water recycling, locally sourced food, carbon offset program, no single-use plastic; these align with the brand's marketing and are documented in Six Senses Sustainability Charter — link to six-senses.com/sustainability; HONESTY: cite only documented commitments, not aspirational claims). HONESTY: pricing is dynamic — frame all amounts as "from approximately" or "typical range, subject to availability"; Yotvata Kibbutz (nearest settlement) has a famous dairy — mention as local food stop only if confirmed accessible to non-kibbutz guests; MICHELIN + Mr & Mrs Smith ratings are documented by the properties themselves but are subject to annual review — frame as "awarded" rather than "holds"; never fabricate specific room counts, amenity counts, or exact prices. AFFILIATE CTAs: Booking.com Six Senses Shaharut property page (primary — Booking.com has IHG properties) + DiscoverCars Eilat airport CTA (to drive to resort) + GYG Timna Valley tour CTA (day trip from resort). Kenyan-specific content: (1) Ministry of Foreign and Diaspora Affairs (Kenya) advisory link ONLY — mfa.go.ke — never paste text; (2) ETA-IL: Kenya IS on Israel's visa-exempt list; since January 2025 ETA-IL required (₪25 / ~KES 3,200 equiv; iaa.gov.il; 72h+ before travel; 2-year multi-entry) — key content differentiator as many Kenyan travel agents not yet updated; (3) Flights: Ethiopian Airlines NBO (Nairobi) → ADD → TLV (most popular routing; strong frequency from Nairobi hub; Ethiopian is East Africa's most connected carrier); Kenya Airways NBO → NBI → AMS or CDG → TLV (when Kenya Airways European connections operating); Turkish Airlines NBO → IST → TLV; typical journey 10–14h via Addis Ababa; (4) Voltage + adapter: Kenya Type G (British standard, 230V) → Israel Type H (230V) — SAME voltage; Type G 3-pin → Israel Type H 3-round-pin: plug adapter needed, no voltage converter; same configuration as UK/Ghana travelers; (5) Health insurance: NHIF (National Hospital Insurance Fund Kenya, now SHA — Social Health Authority since 2024 restructuring) does NOT cover international medical costs; AAR Kenya, Jubilee Insurance Kenya, Britam, GA Insurance offer international travel insurance policies; medical cover in Israel can be expensive — international travel insurance essential; (6) Banking: Equity Bank Kenya, KCB Bank, Co-op Bank Visa/Mastercard work at Israeli ATMs; M-Pesa (dominant Kenyan mobile money) does NOT work in Israel — Kenyan travelers must use standard Visa/Mastercard; Pesalink (Kenyan bank-to-bank transfer system) does NOT work internationally; Wise card strongly recommended for best rates with no M-Pesa substitute in Israel; KES → ILS: approximately 1 ILS = 29–32 KES (varies; frame as magnitude, never hardcode); (7) Embassy of Israel in Nairobi: Upper Hill Close, off Bishops Road, Nairobi; Kenyan High Commission in Israel: 32 Gordon Street, Tel Aviv (verify current); (8) Anglican heritage significance: The Anglican Church of Kenya (ACK) was founded by CMS missionaries in the 19th century; ACK has a direct denominational connection to the Anglican Diocese of Jerusalem (one of the world's oldest Anglican dioceses, established 1841); Christ Church on Jaffa Road Jerusalem is the oldest Protestant church in the Middle East (1849) and is an Anglican church that ACK pilgrims visit with strong denominational resonance; St. George's Cathedral Jerusalem = Anglican Cathedral, affiliated with Diocese of Jerusalem; (9) Ethiopian Orthodox community proximity: Kenya borders Ethiopia; Kenyan travelers using Ethiopian Airlines ADD hub often connect with Ethiopian Orthodox pilgrim groups; the Ethiopian Orthodox Church's presence at Deir es-Sultan on the roof of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre is sometimes visited by East African pilgrims who have Ethiopian community connections; (10) Kenyan Pentecostal travel culture: Kenya has a very active mega-church culture (Nairobi Chapel, CITAM, Mavuno Church, Jubilee Christian Centre all organize international pilgrimages); Kenyan evangelical pilgrims tend toward large group tours with worship music components (portable Bluetooth speaker worship at Yardenit is not unusual — tourist sites generally tolerate this); communal prayer at holy sites is expected; (11) Medical tourism crossover: Kenya's middle class is one of East Africa's most health-conscious; some affluent Kenyans travel to Israel for fertility treatment, cancer care, or cardiac surgery (cross-reference israel-medical-tourism.md). HONESTY: ETA-IL is newly required — many Kenyan travel agents not updated; M-Pesa does NOT work in Israel — this is commonly misunderstood; Deir es-Sultan Ethiopian Orthodox site is disputed between Ethiopia and Coptic Egypt (both have monks on the roof of Holy Sepulchre) — frame factually without taking sides; ACK communion with Anglican Diocese of Jerusalem is genuine — verifiable via Anglican Communion official records; SHA (formerly NHIF) restructuring 2024 — verify current status of Kenyan public health coverage gap before publishing. AFFILIATE CTAs: GYG Jerusalem multi-faith Old City tour CTA + Booking.com Israel hotels CTA + SafetyWing international travel insurance CTA. - [P3] (seo-content, S) Jerusalem Design Week annual guide (/jerusalem-design-week) — CONFIRMED GAP: grep scan for "jerusalem.*design.*week", "hansen.*house.*design", "jlmdesignweek" across full BACKLOG.md + DONE.md returns ZERO matches; no file at src/content/guides/jerusalem-design-week.md. WHY VALUABLE: Jerusalem Design Week is Israel's largest annual public cultural event in design; runs 8 days each July at Hansen House (repurposed 19th-century Ottoman building, German Colony area) + throughout city; 100+ participants; free admission to main programme; 15+ year established event; 2026 dates July 9–16, theme "Match Point" / "Victory Gate" (sport × design); itraveljerusalem.com, touristisrael.com, travelpulse.com, funinjerusalem.com all cover it — zero standalone guide on our site. CONTENT: what Jerusalem Design Week is (annual 8-day free public design festival; curated by jdw.co.il; Israel's largest and most significant annual public design event; 2026 highlights — "The Heat" interactive games installation, "The Matchmaker" (Jerusalem designers × football fan culture), "Sparta" (sport, power, discipline), "Control Room" (sports technology); opening night July 9 with pop artist Nunu; sports bar + fast-food courtyard by Flora; VIP martini bar on iconic Hansen House balcony; SORRY brand capsule pop-up; Bezalel Academy × Maccabiah × Ben-Gurion University collaborative project); Hansen House (Ha-Orton Street, German Colony; multi-hall repurposed Ottoman building + courtyard gardens; wheelchair accessible; free); practical visiting (bus from central Jerusalem: Bus 4/14/18 to German Colony; 20-min walk from Old City / Mamilla; evening opening ideal — Jerusalem at 700m elevation stays cool at 18–22°C July nights; bring light layer; event runs nightly from ~18:00–23:00+; 8-day format means multiple visits possible for in-depth exploration); combining (ideal afternoon/evening after morning Old City or Israel Museum; German Colony / Emek Refaim restaurant strip 10-min walk; Jerusalem Festival of Light also July → design + light double-hit in same week some years). HONESTY: programme changes annually — jdw.co.il sole authoritative source for current year events + dates; "free admission" is longstanding model but verify; 2026 July 9–16 specific — use "annually in July, typically 8 days" in evergreen body text; Hebrew + English signage throughout; Tel Aviv Biennale of Crafts and Design is a separate biennial event (even years, Eretz Israel Museum) — NOT the same as JDW; do not conflate. Dense cross-links: israel-events-festivals, jerusalem-nightlife (German Colony bars post-festival), best-time-to-visit-israel (July Jerusalem), jerusalem-food-guide (German Colony dining). Monetization: Booking.com Jerusalem hotels CTA (German Colony / Rehavia, walking distance to Hansen House) + GYG Jerusalem evening cultural tour CTA.
